  • is there any eval of synthesis quality today?
  • are tools driving the process, or the methodology?
Build
  • institute two-perspectives synthesis as the default habit
  • make the compare step cheap (templates / side-by-side)
  • add lightweight AI evals + raw-interview spot-checks
  • frame agents as assist-only — set up the P1–P5 scale (P5 = anti-pattern)
Three sentences to remember P1 by

Discovery decides the right task, so it is where the product harness begins — and where AI is a second perspective, never a replacement for the PM's own synthesis. The anti-pattern is lazy AI synthesis (transcript → summary → move on), which by Torres's estimate loses 20–40% of detail and captures ~5% of the value; the fix is the two-perspectives method, where the value is the delta between the PM's synthesis and the AI's. AI evals are the product-layer analogue of the verification loop — weaker than tests because the oracle is human judgment, which is exactly why the product scale caps lower than engineering's.

7Further reading

Teresa Torres — Continuous Discovery Habits
Product Talk · producttalk.org

The canonical source for continuous discovery, the opportunity solution tree, and weekly touchpoints — and Torres's positions on where AI helps and where it erodes discovery. Start here.

Report 2 — the product harness & P1–P5 (this workbook)
module M2, discovery section

The full research behind this topic and the assistance scale it feeds into; read alongside P2 (PRDs) and P5 (the scale itself).

Teresa Torres — AI-era writing on research synthesis
Product Talk · producttalk.org

Concrete workflows for running your own synthesis against an AI's and mining the differences — the practitioner source behind the mechanics of §3.

E4 — Verification-first (this workbook)
module M1, topic E4

The engineering verification loop that AI evals are the product-layer analogue of — the tie that explains why the product scale caps lower.

Continuous discovery tooling — Dovetail, Notion AI
vendor docs

The secondary layer: useful for transcription and clustering, but chosen to fit the methodology, never to define it.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (P1-ai-assisted-discovery-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are P2 (PRDs & requirements), P3 (decomposition into agent-ready work), P5 (the P1–P5 assistance scale), M3 (the handoff contract discovery feeds), and E4 (the verification loop AI evals mirror).

P2 · DEEP DIVE

PRDs & Requirements with AI

Study companion · module M2

0Why the PRD is the hinge of the whole system

Discovery (P1) decides what is worth building; the handoff contract (M3) is what an engineering agent can actually execute. The PRD sits between them — it is where fuzzy product intent becomes written requirements. That makes it the hinge: the quality of this one artifact decides how autonomously the engineering harness downstream can run. Investment in the product layer pays off in the engineering layer, and this is the topic where that investment is made or lost.

It is also where AI assistance is at its most seductive. Nothing looks more like PM work than a well-formatted PRD, and nothing is easier for an LLM to produce than a plausible one. P2 is about telling those two apart: what AI genuinely does well in requirements work, what it systematically fails at, and how to structure the collaboration so you get the first without the second.

The one idea to internalise

Marty Cagan's rule: use AI to amplify your thinking, not abdicate it. The productive frame is AI as the best junior PM you have ever had — tireless at drafting, structuring, consistency and edge cases, but with no product judgment of its own. A junior's draft is a starting point a senior edits; the moment the draft ships unedited, the senior has abdicated.

1What works — the junior-PM jobs

The 2025–2026 practitioner consensus (Cagan/SVPG, Torres, Anthropic internal teams — practitioner synthesis, not controlled studies) is consistent about where AI drafting genuinely helps. The common thread: every job on this list is either expansion (more edge cases, more structure, more consistency from the same input) or challenge (find what is wrong with this) — never judgment (decide what to build).

What AI does well in PRD work
  • drafting from rough input — notes, a call transcript, a whiteboard photo
  • structuring and consistency across a long document
  • generating edge cases the author did not think of
  • red-teaming a draft before stakeholder review (§4)
  • generating acceptance criteria from stories (feeds M3)
What must stay human
  • the why — rationale is exactly where LLMs fail hardest (§2)
  • the kill/keep decision on an idea (§3)
  • trade-offs between users, stakeholders, strategy
  • final ownership of every sentence that reaches engineering

For our rollout: the assess question at a client is not "do PMs use AI to write PRDs?" — most already do. It is which of these jobs they use it for, and whether a human edit pass stands between the AI draft and the backlog. AI drafting with human editing is P2 on the assistance scale — today's healthy mainstream. The same output without the edit pass is drift toward P5, the anti-pattern.

2What doesn't work — and the evidence

The failure modes are as consistent as the successes: hallucinated requirements (capabilities or constraints the product does not have), generic "thin" output (a PRD that could describe any product in the category), and — the deepest one — missing rationale. This is not anecdote; it is the best-measured result in the field.

Strong independent evidence — Quattrocchi et al., arXiv:2507.15157

The largest academic study of LLM-generated user stories to date: 13,958 generated stories, 153 of them triple-annotated by humans against the Quality User Story (QUS) framework. Result: LLM stories are rejected far more often than human ones — 77.34% rejection for GPT-3.5, 63.47% for GPT-4 — and the failures concentrate systematically on one quality: Rationale Clarity, the why. GPT-4 scored 1.54 where human stories score 2.66–2.68. Point-in-time caveat: these are 2025 numbers on 2024-era models; re-verify before quoting, but the pattern (form outpacing rationale) has held across model generations so far.

Why the rationale gap hurts precisely at handoff: intent/why is the first component of the handoff contract (M3, H1). An engineering agent given a story with form but no purpose will faithfully implement the form — and every decision the spec does not pin down defaults to a guess made without knowing what the feature is for. The Jama Software traceability audit found teams deploying coding agents without a specification layer produced code with no traceable link to any documented requirement. Weak rationale upstream becomes untraceable code downstream.

The honest caveat we carry to clients: most published "AI in PM" case studies (ChatPRD and similar) are vendor marketing, and the academic numbers come from benchmark and industrial-dataset contexts whose transferability to any given team is not guaranteed. The Quattrocchi result is the strongest independent evidence available; treat everything glossier with suspicion.

3The PR/FAQ principle — AI should speed up killing ideas

Amazon's Working Backwards process starts a product idea as a written press release + FAQ, reviewed before anything is built. Its famous property: most PR/FAQs do not get approved — and that is a feature. The document exists to kill weak ideas cheaply, on paper, before they consume a team.

This reframes what AI drafting is for. If AI makes writing a PRD ten times cheaper, the naive conclusion is "we can produce ten times more PRDs" — which floods the backlog with plausible, thin, unkilled ideas and moves the cost downstream to engineering. The productive conclusion is the opposite: AI should speed up killing bad ideas on paper. Cheap drafting means an idea can reach its kill/keep decision in hours instead of weeks; the kill rate should go up, not the shipping rate of half-thought features.

Requires on input
  • a real product idea with a named customer problem
  • discovery evidence behind it (P1 — not a substitute for it)
  • an explicit kill/keep review step with a human owner
Produces on output
  • a fast, cheap paper prototype of the idea (PR/FAQ or PRD draft)
  • a kill/keep decision made on evidence, not sunk cost
  • for keepers: a draft already structured for red-teaming (§4)
Where it sits
  • Requirements — between discovery (P1) and decomposition (P3); the last point where an idea is cheap to kill.

For our rollout: a useful health metric for the product harness is the proposal kill rate. If AI-drafted PRDs are almost never killed, the review step has degenerated into rubber-stamping (P4's automation bias) and the drafting speed is being converted into backlog noise rather than decision speed.

4Requirements red-teaming — the highest-evidence quick win

Of everything in this topic, adversarial review of requirements is the job with the strongest quantitative support — and it is P3 on the assistance scale, one step beyond today's mainstream, which makes it the natural next move at a client.

EvidenceWhat was measuredResult
Bashir et al., ICSME 2025LLM classification of ambiguous requirements on industrial data (Alstom / Westermo; RISE-led)+20.2% average improvement (10-shot vs 0-shot); best F1 67–76% by dataset; expert rating 3.84/5 (four criteria) — an assistant, not an arbiter
ClarifyGPT, Mu et al., FSE 2024Detecting ambiguity and asking clarifying questions before code generationGPT-4 Pass@1 on MBPP-sanitized 70.96% → 80.80%; average +11.52% (relative) across benchmarks
Quattrocchi et al., 2025QUS quality of LLM-generated stories (§2)Generation is weak on rationale — which is exactly why the critic role outperforms the author role

The asymmetry is the lesson: models are measurably better at finding problems in requirements than at authoring requirements. Finding ambiguity is a pattern-matching task with a checkable answer; authoring rationale requires the product judgment the model does not have. So point the model at the job it is good at: after the PM writes (or edits) a draft, an AI red-team pass hunts for ambiguity, contradictions, missing edge cases, untestable acceptance criteria, and unstated assumptions — and the PM decides which findings matter.

Packaging it — the backlog-refinement gate

The deployable form is a checklist gate in backlog refinement: no story leaves refinement until it has had an AI red-team pass and a human disposition of the findings. This is deliberately the same shape as E4's verification-first loop — a challenge step between draft and done — implemented with human judgment where engineering has tests. It is cheap (minutes per story), it produces its own evidence trail, and F1 in the 67–76% range means the human disposition step is structural, not optional.

5From PRD to handoff — where P2 meets M3

The PRD is not the terminal artifact; the handoff contract is. The practical flow at a client: discovery insight (P1, two-perspectives) → PRD drafted by AI from grounded context — company context, user research, a PRD template — and edited by the PM with kill/keep applied (§3) → red-team gate (§4) → decomposition into agent-ready work items (P3) → each item gets machine-verifiable acceptance criteria, context links, and requirement traceability (M3). Every stage hands the next one exactly what it cannot generate for itself: the why survives because a human wrote it and the contract carries it.

This ordering also fixes the measurement. The tempting metric for AI-assisted PRD work is drafting speed; the meaningful one is downstream: the share of tasks the engineering harness completes without follow-up questions. A PRD process that doubles drafting speed but leaves engineering agents guessing at intent has optimised the wrong end of the pipe — the same lesson E7 teaches about output metrics generally.

6Takeaways for our engagement

Assess
  • are PRDs AI-drafted, and does a real human edit pass follow?
  • do stories carry rationale, or form without a why?
  • what is the proposal kill rate — does paper ever die?
  • is there any adversarial review of requirements today?
Build
  • institute draft-and-challenge: AI drafts + edges, PM edits + owns the why
  • add the red-team gate to backlog refinement (P3 — the quick win)
  • adopt the PR/FAQ frame: cheap drafts exist to kill ideas faster
  • measure handoff quality (no-follow-up-questions rate), not drafting speed
Three sentences to remember P2 by

AI is the best junior PM you have ever had — excellent at drafting, structure, edge cases and red-teaming — but the evidence (Quattrocchi: 63–77% rejection, Rationale Clarity 1.54 vs human ~2.7) shows it systematically fails at the why, which is the first component of the handoff contract. Use cheap drafting to kill bad ideas faster (PR/FAQ), not to produce more unkilled ones, and put the model in the critic's chair where the evidence is strongest (red-teaming: +20.2% ambiguity detection). Amplify your thinking, don't abdicate it — the PM edits, owns, and supplies the rationale; the measure of success is downstream handoff quality, not drafting speed.

7Further reading

Quattrocchi et al. — LLM-generated user stories under the QUS framework
arXiv:2507.15157

The strongest independent evidence in this topic: 13,958 generated stories, triple-annotated sample, the rejection rates and the Rationale Clarity gap. The paper behind §2.

Marty Cagan / SVPG — AI in product management
svpg.com · talks 2025–2026

The "amplify, don't abdicate" frame, the junior-PM analogy, and the empowered-PM argument that P4 builds on. Practitioner synthesis, consistently sober.

Working Backwards — the PR/FAQ process
Bryar & Carr · Amazon practice

The origin of "most PR/FAQs don't get approved — that is a feature", and the kill-ideas-on-paper discipline that §3 adapts for AI drafting.

Bashir et al. — ambiguity detection in industrial requirements
ICSME 2025 · Alstom / Westermo (RISE-led)

The +20.2% red-teaming evidence and the F1 range that makes the human disposition step non-optional. Pairs with ClarifyGPT (Mu et al., FSE 2024) on clarifying questions.

Report 2 — the product harness & the handoff contract (this workbook)
module M2 PRD section · module M3

The full research notes behind this topic, and the contract anatomy the PRD must ultimately feed — read H1 next.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (P2-prds-with-ai-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are P1 (discovery — the evidence a PRD rests on), P3 (decomposition into agent-ready work), P4 (automation bias and the editor role), P5 (the assistance scale), H1 (the handoff contract the PRD feeds), and E4/E7 (the verification and measurement principles it mirrors).

WORKBOOK · VERSIONS

Version history & changelog

Every released version of this workbook

v1.492026-07-18

NEW MODULE M9 — Playbook: from theory to practice. First runbook PB1 Assess. The practice layer lands inside the workbook: five pb- topics ordered by the engagement journey (Assess → Bootstrap → Harness → Handoff → Pilot), built for the internal team to carry the methodology to a brownfield client. PB1 ships complete — a runbook companion in both copies (PB1-assess-runbook.html + overlay) with nine checklist steps across five phases (verification base & delivery baseline, information architecture, governance minimum, team readiness, evaluation), each step carrying the action, the theory link that justifies it, and a DONE criterion; plus two copy-paste templates (readiness questionnaire with four non-compensable [GATE] criteria, assessment-report skeleton with a delivery-baseline section), the score→L1–L5 recommendation table and the F0 go/no-go with STOP criteria (the scoring model is our synthesis, flagged). The delivery baseline (DORA keys + cost per merged PR) is captured at assess time — a pilot without it can be neither proven nor disproven. NEW interaction layer: runbook checkboxes persist through the existing store (agentic-study-v1, new pb field, mirrored into cross-device sync), with a per-runbook progress chip and copy buttons; the standalone copy is deliberately non-persistent. PB2–PB5 are visible as in-preparation topics (one runbook per future version; PB3's templates will be the harness starter kit — runnable hook/permission/CI configs, not prose). Validator extended: pb- prefix recognised, checklist step ids checked for uniqueness (they are frozen progress keys, like topic ids).

v1.482026-07-18

SYNC — delta sync: pushes and pulls now carry only the changes, not the whole document. Until now every sync push uploaded the full encoded study state — and since that encoding grows with edit history, even ticking one section as read sent everything. The workbook now keeps the server's last known state vector and sends only the Yjs diff the server is missing (tens of bytes for a small change); refreshes likewise fetch only the missing diff via a new POST /r/:code/sync endpoint. Every 25th push sends full state as a self-healing reconciliation. Fully backwards compatible: an already-linked device migrates silently on its next push, and older app versions keep working against the updated server. No content or topic-id changes.

v1.472026-07-18

Added the D2 deep-dive companion (Design tokens as the visual contract, DTCG) — token anatomy and the tier model (community practice, not spec; credited to Curtis 2016 options/decisions), the 2025.10 release read precisely (Format + Color stable; theming/modes in the "do not implement" Resolver draft — the earlier "theming shipped" wording corrected in the topic and Report 5 notes), the mid-2026 pipeline (Style Dictionary v5 shipped with 2025.10 coverage converging, Terrazzo, Tokens Studio paid git sync, Figma Variables API Enterprise-gated + native DTCG export not GA, Penpot first open-source native-DTCG tool), code-first sync direction as governance (Sopelnik and Builder.io/Sewell verbatims; Adobe Spectrum designer-authored/code-stored), the agent contract (Pandya closed-set line, DESIGN.md with next-day cross-vendor uptake, the Stylelint/ESLint/drift enforcement stack), the evidence file with corrected dating (zeroheight 56→84 = 2024→2025 report n=294, 86% in 2026 n=147, the 40% pipeline gap, CHI 2026 registry-based 95.08%, and the honest null: no token-specific causal study) plus a do-not-cite list, and the six-step SCSS→DTCG migration (our synthesis). Fact pass: 4 research teammates + 3 adversarial spot-verifiers (13th session in a row with real catches: DESIGN.md tool roster = third-party commentary, mis-dated adoption stat, two paraphrases de-quoted, quote trims).

v1.462026-07-18

NEW DEEP DIVE B6 — Roadmap F0–F3, metrics and STOP criteria. Sixth M5 companion (B6-roadmap-f0-f3-deepdive.html + overlay) — module M5 complete. The roadmap as a permission ladder with measured gates (Report 4 synthesis, honestly analogue-checked: no published canonical equivalent as of July 2026 — nearest cousins stage oversight per task, not access over time), the gate table with deterministic checks and automatic demotion (borrowing GAIE’s risk-routed tiers and the Digital Apprentice’s dual-key promotion / asymmetric demotion), the metrics chapter with the self-report trap (METR) and the vendor-telemetry file (Faros 2026 “Acceleration Whiplash”: review time +441.5%, 31.3% more PRs merging without any review), secrets from day 0 on the 2026 evidence (GitGuardian: Claude Code-assisted commits at 3.2% vs 1.5% baseline — ~2×; MCP configs as a leak surface), STOP criteria anchored to safety standards (ISO 26262 / IEC 62304 / DO-178C traceability via vendor guidance, flagged), the day-1 recipe and the F0–F3 × R1/R2/R3/R6 master-plan table (our synthesis) answering the topic’s open question. Key swarm corrections: DORA’s deployment rework rate re-dated to its true 2024 introduction (fixed across E7, E8, H4, B1 in both copies), the MIT NANDA “95%” pinned to its real wording and methodology (300+ initiatives / 52 org interviews / 153 leaders — the circulating “150/350” is a misquote), DX dataset corrected to 425 organizations, unverifiable sub-figures dropped. New load-bearing sources: Microsoft’s instrumented rollout study (arXiv:2607.01418, ~24% sustained PR-merge lift, peer-network diffusion), DX Q4 impact report (enablement evidence, onboarding 91→49 days), DORA AI Capabilities Model, Swarmia autonomy levels, KPMG stall mechanism, Sharma’s D³ weighed as the nearest published cousin. Topic brown-roadmap enriched (metrics, secrets pin, gates bullet, new check); GitGuardian src updated to the 2026 report.

v1.452026-07-18

NEW DEEP DIVE B5 — Strangler fig, the heatmap and picking the first battlefield. Fifth M5 companion (B5-strangler-heatmap-deepdive.html + overlay), built on an 8-agent fact-verification + discovery swarm. The rewrite question honestly framed (Spolsky, Brooks, the Basecamp counterexample that is really strangler economics), the strangler fig with its real origin story (Fowler 2004, Stevenson/XP-2004 credit), the plumbing toolbox (seams, ACL, branch by abstraction, expand/contract, dark launching, feature flags), the churn × complexity heatmap with caveats, a scored pilot-selection model with the exclusionary criticality gate (our synthesis, flagged), Hyrum's Law, god classes vs the context budget, the database (outbox/CDC, Stripe's four-phase playbook), and what agents reprice in 2026. Key swarm corrections: branch-by-abstraction attribution pinned (Curl coined, Hammant first detailed publicly 2007, Fowler popularized); Code Red printed as issues taking 124% LONGER to resolve in the worst code-health band, not the marketing "faster" reframe; the hotspot-concentration folklore corrected ("4% of code, 70%+ of defects" is a single-system anecdote — the defensible form is Pareto-shaped, ~20% of modules carrying ~80% of defects); and no empirical rewrite failure rate exists (Standish CHAOS figures dismantled by Eveleens & Verhoef 2010 — the case rests on case studies and expert judgment, printed as such). Topic brown-strangler src now opens the overlay first.

v1.442026-07-17

NEW DEEP DIVE B4 — Agent Archaeology: reverse-engineering legacy. Fourth M5 companion (B4-agent-archaeology-deepdive.html + overlay), built on an 8-agent fact-verification + discovery swarm plus a 4-agent spot-verify round (11th session in a row it paid). Corrections: CodeConcise is graph-traversal-AUGMENTED RAG, not "instead of RAG"; the 60,000 person-days headline and the 240 FTE-years figure are the same projection in two units; the 3h-vs-3-weeks anecdote comes from a different client, "best cases"; AWS "~7B lines/year" NOT FOUND → replaced with the verified 1.1B lines analyzed / 810k hours saved, cumulative; Western Union is 2.5M lines, not 250k, and the six weeks run discovery→testing (pilot); the "15–20k lines" module sizing UNPINNABLE → dropped; Siala & Lano's AgileUML zero-spurious result holds for Java (0.14 on Python); the WCA for Z → IBM Bob timeline pinned (GA Mar 2026). Load-bearing new sources: Malykhin's Archaeologist's Copilot (martinfowler.com, Jul 16 2026, verbatims spot-verified), the CROZ Nov 2025 review verbatims, Unum's AVP "if you cannot understand it, you cannot verify", Soliman & Keim ICSA 2025 (0.395 architecture-answer precision), Siala & Lano Frontiers 2025, arXiv:2504.04372 (78% brittle comprehension), Xia TSE 2018 (~58%, 3,148 hours), Corbi IBM SJ 1989, the Glass 2001 attribution fix, Wen ICPC 2019, Uddin & Robillard 2015, Tan SOSP 2007, the Cursor support-bot incident (Apr 2025), Spring REST Docs / Doc Detective / Schemathesis, AgenticAKM arXiv:2602.04445, and the GitHub/Microsoft COBOL framework ($2–5 per 1k LOC). The hypothesis-with-confidence labels, the sampling protocol, promote-through-verification and the F0 workflow are flagged as our Report 4 synthesis. Topic brown-archaeology know/checks/src rewritten with the verified figures.

v1.432026-07-17

UX — sidebar actions restyled as an action menu. The View (expand/collapse all) and Data (export/import data, my notes, sync across devices) controls were laid out as a wrapping row of chips directly under the filter pills, so they read as more filters rather than actions. They are now a vertical menu of full-width rows, each with a leading inline-SVG icon (muted → cobalt on hover) and a hover highlight — echoing the Modules nav’s clickable-row pattern but marked as actions by an icon rather than a code chip. The sidebar now carries three distinct visual languages: nav rows (code chip), filter pills (toggle state), action rows (icon). The Filter by status pills are unchanged. The sidebar is also now height-safe: it caps at the viewport and scrolls internally (max-height:calc(100vh - 100px);overflow-y:auto), so the Data actions are never clipped on short screens or as more modules are added; on mobile it reverts to a static horizontal wrap. No topic-id or content changes.

v1.422026-07-17

NEW DEEP DIVE B3 — Mutation Testing as the Test-Quality Gate. Third M5 companion (B3-mutation-testing-gate-deepdive.html + overlay), built on the three-teammate fact pass + spot-verify round (10th session in a row it paid: the SpecBench quote re-pinned to its verbatim "Oversight therefore collapses onto a single surface: the automated test suite." with the 28 pp/tenfold figure flagged as the abstract headline over a ~27 pp 90th-percentile fit, R²=0.21; the Augment-blog JFreeChart "256K mutants / 109 minutes" figure identified as third-hand — excluded; the ISSTA 2019 flakiness numbers pulled verbatim from the PDF). Theory grounded with verbatims: DeMillo/Lipton/Sayward 1978 (competent-programmer + coupling as stated observations; Hamlet 1977 parallel origin), Just et al. FSE 2014 (coupling for 73% of real faults, 17% not coupled; mutant detection correlates with real-fault detection "independently of code coverage"; Defects4J credited to the ISSTA 2014 companion, not the FSE paper), Inozemtseva & Holmes ICSE 2014 ("should not be used as a quality target" — their word, not "stopping criterion"). LLM-era evidence file made precise: MUTGEN pinned to its real title ("Mutation-Guided Unit Test Generation with a Large Language Model", accepted IEEE TSE; the 53% plateau is one HumanEval-Java subject; 89.5%/89.1% are corpus scores across 1,144/1,900 mutants), Haroon et al. setup clarified (23,977 = the failing subset of 119,163 SAC-generated tests; preprint-flagged), TestGenEval GPT-4o 35.2%/18.8% verbatim, Yoshimoto arXiv:2603.13724 (AI authored 16.4% of test-adding commits; coverage comparable — the premise, not the payoff), Rethinking arXiv:2602.07900 (test volume ≠ outcomes). NEW reward-hacking section: ImpossibleBench 54.0% Conflicting-SWEbench vs 76% Oneoff disambiguated; SpecBench arXiv:2605.21384; Rajan arXiv:2606.16062 (28.5% of a 49-task SWE-bench Verified sample accept a verified-incorrect patch; Pass@1 +14.14 pp on hackable tasks, 123/134 models positive). Feedback-loop evidence: MuTAP (IST 171, 2024: up to 28% more faulty snippets, 17% caught only by it, 93.57%), ACH reused from B2, YATE +21.77% mutants, AdverTest arXiv:2602.08146 (co-evolution verbatims; Defects4J +8.56%/+63.30%), LLMorpheus arXiv:2404.09952. Industry: the Google trilogy with verbatims (one mutant per line; changed/covered/non-arid only; Critique via Tricorder; usefulness 20%→80%; >24,000 developers / 1,000+ projects; ~15M mutants; "developers using mutation testing write more tests") and the fact-check correction that Google’s no-score stance is a practical dismissal ("infeasably expensive… no good way to surface it") not a normative one — distilled into the consumption-mode lesson (survivors-in-review at scale vs module score at the bootstrap boundary). The three taxes with the classic numbers: Schuler & Zeller STVR 2013 (~45% of undetected mutants equivalent; ~15 min/mutant manual triage), TCE ICSE 2015 (7.4% + 21% for C; 5.7% + 5.4% Java), pseudo-tested methods (concept coined by Niedermayr et al. 2016; EMSE 2019: present in all 21 projects, single headline % UNPINNABLE — not cited), ISSTA 2019 (scores vary 4 pp between runs; 9% of mutant-test pairs non-deterministic). Cost ladder + toolbox point-in-time (PIT incremental / Arcmutate PR-scoped / Descartes extreme mutation / StrykerJS --incremental since v6.2; gremlins over stale go-mutesting; Stryker thresholds high:80/low:60/break:null and PIT default 0 — no mainstream tool ships a mandatory score gate, so ~70% is presented as Report 4’s working heuristic). The two-mode gate (diff-scoped advisory survivors + module-level held-out-mutants score as the F1→F2 exit criterion) flagged as our synthesis, with the practice-sheet-vs-exam principle and six anti-patterns. Topic B3 know/concepts/checks enriched (Google-scale bullet, flakiness bullet, held-out-mutants check).

v1.412026-07-17

NEW DEEP DIVE B2 — Characterization & Golden-Master Testing with Agents. Second M5 companion (B2-characterization-golden-master-deepdive.html + overlay), built on the three-teammate fact pass + spot-verify round (9th session in a row it paid). The two pinning techniques stated precisely: Feathers characterization (WELC 2004 ch. 13; verbatim "The purpose of characterization testing is to document your system's actual behavior, not check for the behavior you wish your system had" and "When a system goes into production, in a way, it becomes its own specification") and Falco/Bache approval — golden master — snapshot as one mechanism with four emphases (Bache 2021 anatomy quoted; Rainsberger = popularizer, never "coiner"; Hyrum's Law + Excel-1900/Java-8-HashMap as load-bearing-bug exhibits, pairing flagged as ours). The production evidence file with contexts kept honest: TestGen-LLM (75/57/25 = Reels/Stories eval; 11.5%/73% = test-a-thons; "Assured LLM-SE" = separate paper arXiv:2402.04380), ACH pinned to arXiv:2501.12862 (FSE 2025; 10,795 Kotlin classes → 9,095 mutants → 571 privacy tests, 73% accepted, 36% privacy-relevant; equivalence detector 0.79/0.47 → 0.95/0.96; Messenger/WhatsApp NOT Instagram), Qodo Cover (huggingface/pytorch-image-models PR #2331, 15 tests/168 lines; Friedman quote with its full "…who run fully automated workflows" clause), UnitTenX, YATE (arXiv:2507.18316: repair beats regeneration, +32.06% lines/+21.77% mutants). Five traps, each with measured evidence: coverage vanity (MutGen 100%/4%; TestGenEval arXiv:2410.00752 — GPT-4o 35.2% coverage vs 18.8% mutation, the only benchmark grading mutation), oracle-passing-by-construction (arXiv:2412.14137 read both directions: fatal for bug-finding, definitional for characterization), bug-as-spec measured (arXiv:2607.05139: ~25% → ~14% fault detection when the model sees faulty code), flaky pins (ICSE SEIP 2026 DBMS study: 0.07% baseline vs 0.29–0.71% generated, 63% unordered collections, "prioritize fixing flaky tests" verbatim), and the agent gaming its net (Beck's genie "disabling or deleting tests" verbatim from Beyond the Vibes; ImpossibleBench; ThoughtWorks Radar Vol. 34 "erode approve/deny chokepoints" verbatim) — plus Sapegin's approve-all-fatigue line as the human-scale substrate. The six-step workflow (document-don't-fix → deterministic filter → ~20%+all-suspected sampling → two-door SUSPECTED-BUG triage → ~70% mutation gate → read-only commit) flagged as our Report 4 synthesis. Toolbox table point-in-time (ApprovalTests, Verify licensing change Aug 2026, Jest/Vitest, Texttest, VCR-cassettes, Percy AI reviewer as vendor claim) + four-row deterministic exit-criteria table with the keep-or-discard decision. Fact fixes elsewhere: the Report 4 notes' "there are no wrong answers — just documenting the way things exist" line was Erik Dietrich's gloss misattributed to Feathers — corrected with Feathers' real verbatim. Topic B2 know/concepts/src/checks enriched (bug-as-spec bullet, ACH src, read-only-tests check).

v1.402026-07-17

NEW — cross-device sync. Progress and notes were saved only in this browser; you can now opt in to sync them across devices with no account. From the sidebar Data → sync across devices, "Enable sync" uploads your current state and returns a random 4-character code; every change from then on syncs automatically. On another device, enter the code to pull and merge that state in — that device is then linked too. Merging is handled by Yjs, a conflict-free replicated data type (CRDT), on both the browser and a small Cloudflare Worker — so edits made on two devices reconcile correctly with no data loss and no custom merge logic. Local storage stays primary: the sync engine loads from a CDN and is best-effort; if it is blocked (e.g. inside the Claude preview) or you are offline, saving still works exactly as before, and the top bar shows a synced · CODE / sync error status. Rate-limited server-side. No topic-id or content changes. (v1.39 reserved for the parallel B1 deep-dive branch.)

v1.392026-07-17

NEW DEEP DIVE B1 — The Bootstrap Paradox & Safe Ordering. M5 OPENED. First M5 companion (B1-bootstrap-paradox-deepdive.html + overlay), built on the three-teammate fact pass + spot-verify round. The chapter states the paradox as three separately-true claims — the agent needs a verification loop (E4); legacy is "simply code without tests" (Feathers 2004, Preface verbatim); the agent is the cheapest tool to build the missing prerequisites — and shows how, unanchored, it closes into the bug-as-spec loop. The evidence file with honest weights: DORA 2024 (+25% AI adoption associated with −1.5% throughput / −7.2% stability, with DORA’s OWN mechanism quoted — "small batch sizes and robust testing mechanisms" — and the 2025 sign flip flagged: throughput positive, instability persists, "AI’s primary role is as an amplifier", seven-capability AI Capabilities Model); the METR RCT (16 devs / 246 tasks / 19% slower vs 24% expected & 20% still believed, on 22k+-star / 1M+-LOC repos — the self-report disqualifier); GitClear vendor-flagged (copy/paste 8.3%→12.3%, moved ~24%→9.5%, churn 5.5%→7.9%; the "8×"/"4×" headlines pinned to their dated reports); Stack Overflow 2025 (45.7% distrust vs 32.7% trust; 66% "AI solutions that are almost right, but not quite"). The benchmark greenfield-bias corollary: SWE-bench keeps only fail-to-pass instances (2,294 of ~90k PRs; Epoch AI: ~90% under an hour, Django ≈ half, top-5 repos >80%) and agents drop 72.80% → 25% on evolution-style tasks (SWE-EVO). The five-step dependency chain (read-only comprehension → characterization → minimal CI → AGENTS.md extracted-not-decreed, with the ETH −2%/+23% numbers → scoped writes) is flagged as our Report 4 synthesis and mapped onto Böckeler’s guides/sensors vocabulary; Phase 0 read-only as the paradox-breaker (Xia TSE 2018: 58% of professional time is comprehension; Ehsani MSR 2026 on AIDev-pop: docs/CI/build tasks merge best, Abandoned/Not Reviewed = 38% of failed agentic PRs; the 2024 "pipe dream" → 2025 UnitTenX arc, 0%→100% line coverage on the djbdns case study); four wrong-order failure modes (WO1–WO4) with detection signals; per-step exit-criteria table. Spot-verify (8th session in a row it paid): a fabricated Böckeler mutation-testing quote killed (her real line: acceptance tests "give us a false sense of security in test effectiveness — mutation testing helps us monitor that gap"); UnitTenX "characterization tests" is NOT the paper’s term (it says "resolve bugs and document unknown interfaces") and its 0%→100% is scoped to one case-study codebase (djbdns, 202 functions); the Stack Overflow 66%-vs-45.2% split settled (top frustration vs debugging time). Fact fixes elsewhere: TestGen-LLM "useful:generated 1:4 vs 1:20" is UNPINNABLE (not in arXiv:2402.09171) — dropped from topic B2 and flagged in the Report 4 notes; topic B1’s "DORA mechanism" know bullet reframed to DORA’s own batch-size/testing-discipline hypothesis + the 2025 reversal; MutGen 89.5% pinned to HumanEval-Java (89.1% LeetCode-Java) in topic B3. Topic B1 know/concepts/src/checks enriched (greenfield-bias bullet, METR self-report check).

v1.382026-07-16

NEW DEEP DIVE I7 — Linking Product ↔ Engineering: IDs, Maps & the MCP Bridge. M4 COMPLETE. Seventh and final M4 companion (I7-linking-product-engineering-deepdive.html + overlay), built on the three-teammate fact pass + spot-verify round. The chapter joins the two planes with one boring foreign key: the unified ID scheme with per-platform mechanics (Linear auto-links from branch names; Jira smart commits need key + command in the commit message; GitHub auto-close is same-repo only) and an enforcement ladder from CLAUDE.md convention through prepare-commit-msg to server-side rulesets + CI grep (E5); the missing-links evidence (Bird et al. ESEC/FSE 2009 — 8–55% of fixed bugs linked, and the severity inversion: 63% of minor vs 15% of blocker bugs linked in Apache; LinearB verbatim "In 75% of teams, 31% of branches are unlinked"); the ticket-as-prompt pattern (Copilot↔Jira GA Jun 2026, Devin↔Linear, Cursor↔Linear — vendor-flagged) with its independent counterweight (arXiv:2503.15223, ICSE 2026: 29.6% of plausible SWE-bench patches diverge from the real fix; 66.2% of suspicious ones trace to under-specified issue statements); the system map (C4 verbatims incl. the generate-not-draw L4 rule, arc42 CC BY-SA, matklad); one master per artifact + "reference, don't copy" (our synthesis, flagged); the MCP bridge priced (150k→2k vendor verbatim; MCP-Universe distractor evidence) and threat-modeled (lethal trifecta verbatim + three incidents: GitHub MCP toxic flows May 2025, Supabase MCP Jul 2025, GitLost Jul 2026); provenance (multi-method census 3.3%/30×, Agent Trace RFC Jan 2026, Copilot session-log links + Purview streaming, the VS Code co-author-trailer revert) and llms.txt honestly labeled (Mueller verbatim, Ahrefs 97%). Spot-verify (7th session in a row it paid): GitLost "VP of Sales" detail + Agentic-Workflows preview date dropped as uncorroborated; LinearB stat re-pinned to its verbatim (was "a third of git work"); Bird "every project" softened to the manually-verified projects; "Jira has its own version of reality" NOT FOUND — excluded. Fact fix elsewhere: H3's "AIDev census" naming corrected in both copies — arXiv:2606.24429 is the multi-method census (Khosravani & Mockus); AIDev (arXiv:2602.09185) is the separate PR census it compares against; the 30× base tightened to 850,157 one-snapshot commits. Topic I7 know/concepts/src/checks rewritten with verified figures.

v1.372026-07-16

NEW DEEP DIVE I6 — Retrieval: Agentic Search, Embedding Indexes & Knowledge Graphs. Sixth M4 companion (I6-retrieval-deepdive.html + overlay), built on the three-teammate fact pass + spot-verify round. The chapter maps four retrieval lanes (agentic search / embedding index / symbol index / code KG), the vendor board with primary quotes (Cherny's HN + restated reasons — simpler, security, privacy, staleness, reliability; Cursor's +12.5% on internal Context Bench, correctly attached to semantic search's lift; Cody's 2024 embeddings retreat with the consumer-sunset precision; Riptide "3x recall" pinned to the migrated Codeium post), the independent evidence (SWE-bench BM25 1.96% vs oracle 4.80%; Agentless 32.00%/$0.70; CodeRAG-Bench verbatims; the folklore correction — dense code-trained embedders frequently beat BM25, grep wins on systems properties; Codebase-Memory 83%-vs-92% at 10× fewer tokens), the index tax (I3 drift, Vec2Text 92% embedding inversion, Guo's cost-curve, Blackbird's 2,048-core stat), a GraphRAG anti-miscitation callout, and the L0–L3 escalation ladder + twenty-query bench (our synthesis, flagged). Seed-topic corrections: the "Devin: same pattern" claim was WRONG — Devin indexes (DeepWiki wiki + code graph), now the counterexample; "+12.5% from combining semantic + grep" re-attached; "precision" dropped from Claude Code's stated reasons. Spot-verify kills: a "second Anthropic engineer / outperformed by a lot" quote (NOT FOUND), Riptide "200% recall improvement" (paraphrase; verbatim is "3x the recall"), Codebase-Memory latency/raw-token figures (not in the abstract), Cursor "plaintext never persisted" (unverified on fetchable pages). Topic know/concepts/src/checks enriched to match.

v1.362026-07-16

Highlight notes: write at save, read on hover, edit on click. You can now attach a note to a highlighted passage directly. After "+ save as note" a small popover opens with a text field so you write the note immediately (or skip). Hovering a highlight shows its note as a tooltip (empty notes show a "click to add a note" hint); clicking a highlight reopens the popover pre-filled to edit it. The note is the same thought shown in the per-topic "My highlights" list and the "my notes" modal — one source, three views, kept in sync. Close semantics: save or click-outside persists, skip/Esc discards (Cmd/Ctrl+Enter also saves). Both the popover (z-index:252) and tooltip (253) sit above the deep-dive overlay; marks now use a pointer cursor to signal they are interactive. Workbook-render-only; state.highlights schema unchanged.

v1.352026-07-16

FIX + FEATURE for highlight-notes. Fix: the "+ save as note" button (and the "saved" toast) never appeared when selecting text in a deep dive — a z-index layering bug. Both were appended to document.body at z-index:70/71, below the deep-dive overlay's z-index:200; since they only ever show for selections inside an overlay, they always rendered behind it (present in the DOM, display:block, but visually occluded — confirmed by hit-testing elementFromPoint returning the overlay, not the button). Raised to 250/251. Select-to-note now works end-to-end (button visible → save → persistent tint → toast). Feature: each topic card now shows a "My highlights (N)" section (between "Go deeper" and "My notes") listing the passages you highlighted in that topic's deep dive — quote preview, your note (read-only), and an "open at passage →" jump that opens the overlay scrolled to and flashing the passage. Creating/editing/deleting highlights still lives in the overlay + global "my notes" modal; the per-topic list is a contextual view that stays in sync via renderTopicHls() (hooked into save/delete/import/thought-edit/load). Workbook-render-only; state.highlights schema and the standalone deep dives are unchanged.

v1.342026-07-16

UX — deep-dive links now render as a distinct "Open panel" button, not a citation. In every topic's "Go deeper" section, the in-page deep-dive link was visually indistinguishable from external references: same ↗ "leaving-the-page" bullet, and the anchor even carried target="_blank" (neutralised only by the JS overlay interceptor). Deep dives now render as a bordered button-card (panel-with-lines icon, title, description, "Open panel →" affordance) above a separate "References" list; the ↗ glyph is now reserved for genuinely external citations. Added aria-haspopup="dialog" and dropped the misleading target="_blank" on in-page links. Purely presentational and workbook-render-only: the 26 src arrays are untouched (title/description parsed at render), the #*-deepdive anchor wiring is unchanged, so no progress keys move and no two-copies sync is triggered.

v1.332026-07-16

NEW TOPIC E11 + deep dive — Formal Methods in the Agentic Loop: Vericoding & Runtime Enforcement. Third and last new M1 topic from the July source-evaluation round (54→55 topics; M1 now E1–E11). New companion (E11-formal-methods-deepdive.html + overlay). The chapter’s spine is an anti-conflation rule: Direction A (formal methods on the agent’s OUTPUT — vericoding: arXiv:2509.22908, 82% Dafny / 44% Verus / 27% Lean; Misu FSE 2024 58%; AWS Cedar/Lean shipping) vs Direction B (formal methods on the agent’s BEHAVIOUR — AgentSpec ICSE 2026, the chapter’s only peer-reviewed system; ProbGuard’s measured safety–utility curve 40.63%→2.60% unsafe at 59.38%→10.42% completion). The economics thread runs Newcombe CACM 2015 (cost-perception quote + 35-step DynamoDB bug) → antfly (May 2026, tiered practitioner/vendor; Pebble validation stated precisely — it found a DIFFERENT later-fixed bug) → Vogels×Byron Cook (Feb 2026, neuro-symbolic). Counterweight kept load-bearing: NL-to-TLA+ = 26.6% syntactic / 8.6% semantic across 30 LLMs (arXiv:2606.05792) — generation against a verifier works, unsupervised formalization does not; the human owns intent (Lahiri’s intent gap, arXiv:2603.17150, extending H2’s ladder). Corrections honoured from the fact pass: vericoding venue = none (preprint); Misu = UC Irvine, not MSR, and prompt-based, not repair-loop; ProbGuard never "ProGuard"; AgentSpec "first" only as hedged attribution; Antfly sells retrieval, not verification tooling.

v1.322026-07-16

NEW TOPIC E10 + deep dive — Background & Long-Running Agents: Async Delegation. Second new M1 topic from the July source-evaluation round. New companion (E10-background-agents-deepdive.html + overlay): the execution axis beyond E6 (where/when/who initiates; cross-vendor convergence sandbox→work→PR, launch primaries cited); the evidence both ways (METR TH1.1 — doubling ~4.3 months since 2023, Opus 4.6 ~12 h at 50%, pinned from the raw data file; vs LHTB arXiv:2607.08964 — best model 15.2% pass@1 on sustained tasks); persistence mechanics (Ralph loop primary-sourced to Huntley Jul 2025; initializer/coding-agent split; Managed Agents session-as-append-only-log + wake(sessionId) verbatim; the feature-list protection rule kept I2-precise); verification at a distance (evaluator≠generator; Cursor planner/worker/judge flagged secondhand-via-Osmani; approval fatigue: 93% approvals, 20%→40% auto-approve, 17% classifier false-negatives); governance (Ona: "a guardrail inside the context window is a suggestion, not a boundary" — infra-layer enforcement; cost as governance with token-velocity circuit breakers, runaway-bill dollar folklore excluded); the adoption path (false summit → background agents → software factory; six summit case studies tabled, ALL flagged unaudited vendor self-reports; Ona = ex-Gitpod, OpenAI acquisition agreed Jun 11 2026 — confirmed via openai.com/CNBC/Bloomberg). METR correction applied vs the reading-list drafts: Opus 4.6 is ~12.0 h p50 in TH1.1 data, not the circulating 14.5 h.

v1.312026-07-16

NEW TOPIC E9 + deep dive — Harness Engineering as a Discipline: Eval-Driven Tuning. First of three new M1 topics from the July source-evaluation round (boss reading list, deep-research verification). New companion (E9-harness-tuning-deepdive.html + overlay): the tunable surface (two reference architectures, arXiv:2604.14228 while-loop vs arXiv:2603.05344 compound; six runtime responsibilities, arXiv:2606.20683), the evidence that harness moves scores (arXiv:2605.23950 — harness variance 7.80× model variance, 34–48-pt scaffold swings, the disclosure rule; Nemotron 65–70.4% across five harnesses), the Ratchet & six levers, the eval-driven tuning loop (LangChain/NVIDIA playbook, cost ladder, guidance placement, 94→96/127 middleware ablation — vendor-tiered), and model–harness co-evolution (named Harness Robustness post-training stage). The two Terminal-Bench stories disentangled (Opus 4.6 #33→#5 HumanLayer vs deepagents-cli 52.8→66.5 gpt-5.2-codex Trivedy — two results, both point-in-time). Also: I5 fact refresh against arXiv:2602.11988 v2 — benchmark name CTXBENCH (was misnamed AGENTbench) and developer-written files +2.4% n.s. (was +4%), fixed in both copies.

v1.302026-07-16

I5 deep dive — Progressive Disclosure: Skills, Nested Files & Indexes. New companion (standalone file + in-page overlay) for topic I5: the three tiers (core / map / leaves), Skills' three-level mechanism with the official token economics (~100 tokens per skill, bodies under 5k tokens / 500 lines) and the open standard's cross-vendor reach, nested context files and scoped rules across tools (including the @import illusion and the four-hop limit), the index/map layer (matklad, aider repo map, llms.txt caution), the official 200-line gardening budget, the measured evidence (ETH Zurich arXiv:2602.11988: LLM-generated files −2%/+23% on AGENTbench; McMillan arXiv:2605.10039 structural null; Lulla efficiency deltas), failure modes and a week-one disclosure audit. Topic I5 know/concepts/checks rewritten with verified figures. Fact fix: the I3 Galster citation re-pointed to arXiv:2602.14690 (current title "Harness Engineering for Agentic AI Coding Tools").

v1.292026-07-16

Highlight-notes. Select any passage inside a deep-dive overlay and save it as a note via the floating "+ save as note" button; add your own thought later. A new my notes button (sidebar Data group) opens a modal listing every saved passage grouped by deep dive, each with an editable thought and an "open in deep dive →" jump that scrolls to and flashes the passage. Saved passages stay tinted in the deep dive on every open. Stored in the same per-origin profile; included in export/import.

v1.282026-07-16

Added the I4 deep dive — Memory Systems for Agents: Typologies, Poisoning & the Governed Write-Mode (23rd companion; standalone I4-memory-systems-deepdive.html + in-page overlay): the four-layer typology with the CoALA and MemGPT frames — including a terminology correction propagated to the topic and Report 3 (the "core/recall/archival memory" triad is Letta product vocabulary; the MemGPT paper says main/external context with working context and recall/archival storage); the point-in-time vendor landscape (memory tool memory_20250818 six ops; Claude Code MEMORY.md 200-line/25KB load cutoff; Cline Memory Bank; Cursor Memories GA in 1.2; Copilot Memory public preview — citation re-verification against the current branch, 28-day unused-fact TTL; Devin Knowledge org→enterprise promotion; Mem0/Zep/LangMem with the LoCoMo benchmark dispute kept as a vendor-claims lesson); the three long-horizon techniques with verbatim protocol quotes; memory as the governed fourth write-mode (git-versioned memory, provenance, the promotion path — our synthesis, flagged); the ASI06 threat model with research attacks (AgentPoison >80% at <0.1% poison; MINJA query-only; the mid-2026 systematization arXiv:2606.04329 — 4 write channels / 9 vulnerabilities / 6 attack classes + MPBench) and Rehberger’s two documented demos, plus OWASP’s Agent Memory Guard as the off-the-shelf deterministic stack; the files-vs-store decision criterion (with the GDPR-erasure vs AI-Act-retention wrinkle); and a week-one client checklist. Independent evidence kept separate from vendor claims throughout (Reflexion, LongMemEval, Momento arXiv:2606.00832, cache economics arXiv:2601.06007, Sandelin’s coding-agent memory benchmark with its own-tool caveat). ia-memory know/concepts/src/checks enriched.

v1.272026-07-16

Integrated the session-persistence research (internal artifact, Jul 2026: Recording, Tracking, and Preserving AI Agent Work as First-Class SDLC Artifacts) as targeted summaries across five topics, each with a source link to the full report. ia-taxonomy: the agent session/trajectory as the newest artifact to classify — tiered, not one bucket (durable decision-provenance spine; disposable full payloads on a 7–30-day TTL; link-don't-inline), plus a new check question. hand-traceability: the chain gains the session link — the vendor-neutral Agent Trace spec (agent-trace.dev; Cognition + Cursor, Cloudflare, Vercel, Google Jules, Amp, OpenCode), GitHub Copilot commit→session-log linking (Mar 2026) + SIEM/Purview streaming (preview Jul 2026), the task-centric record (spec → task → session → PR → review), and the layered provenance stack (W3C PROV/PROV-AGENT · SLSA/in-toto · ML-BOM/SPDX 3.0 AI) — flagged point-in-time, adoption nascent. ia-lifecycle: traces extend ADRs down to implementation-level decisions (Lore, arXiv:2603.15566) but are decision provenance, not rationale — CoT unfaithfulness pinned (Anthropic 2025: 25% Claude 3.7 Sonnet / 39% DeepSeek R1; Korbak et al., arXiv:2507.11473), so promoted "why" fields stay human-gated. sec-surfaces: the trace store as a secondary secrets-leakage path and quietly-regulated data store (arXiv:2606.30373: 132,853 HF apps — 14.15% — using 253,755 secrets, 936 logging them at runtime) → redaction at the collector, tiered retention. traj-compliance: EU AI Act Art. 12/19 logging & ≥6-month retention head-start (fines up to €15M/3%, deadline 2 Aug 2026; prEN 18229-1 + ISO/IEC DIS 24970 still drafts). Vendor claims kept separated from independent evidence per house style.

v1.262026-07-16

Added the I3 deep dive — Consistency, Drift & Documentation Debt (topic ia-consistency): embedded overlay + standalone file, written after the three-teammate fact pass (2× verification + 1 discovery) plus the spot-verification round on discovered sources — which earned its keep a fourth time: the scout's context-file population statistics were materially wrong (corrected to the paper's 4,768 files across 2,586 of 2,853 repos, CLAUDE.md leading at 34.4%), a practitioner post was re-dated 2026, and DocPrism's 98%→14% was relabelled a flag rate. Fact correction applied to the topic itself: the 28.9%/4.7-year stale-references figures are pinned to arXiv:2212.01479 (2022), denominator 265/918 — not the EMSE 2023 journal restatement; the DORA "2.4×" folklore multiplier was found unpinnable and excluded (the 2022 amplification framing + published lifts used instead). New load-bearing sources: Treude & Baltes arXiv:2606.09090 (DOCER on agent config files: stale references in 23.0% of 356 repos; "context rot" extended to repo artifacts; the two-snapshot CI grep), Galster et al. (AIware 2026) + Lulla et al. (JAWs 2026: AGENTS.md ↔ ~29% lower runtime), DocPrism (arXiv:2511.00215), DORA 2022 documentation-quality capability + DORA 2025 "AI-accessible internal data", SWE-at-Google ch.10 freshness reminders, Docs as Tests / Doc Detective, Schemathesis/Specmatic (with the Dredd/Optic/driftctl/DeepDocs graveyard flagged point-in-time). The per-category freshness policy flagged as our synthesis. Topic know/concepts/checks enriched.

v1.252026-07-15

Added the I2 deep dive — Lifecycle & Write-Permissions as Governance (topic ia-lifecycle): embedded overlay + standalone file, written after the three-teammate fact pass (2× verification + 1 discovery) plus the spot-verification round on discovered sources — which again earned its keep: the Anthropic harness quote "it is unacceptable to remove or edit tests…" turns out to guard the feature-list entries, not unit-test files (context corrected before use); ThoughtWorks Radar dates pinned (Trial Nov 2016 → Adopt Nov 2017); MADR expanded correctly as Markdown Architectural Decision Records (4.0 adds Rejected); OWASP ASI06 rendered by its canonical name "Memory & Context Poisoning" (here and in I4). New load-bearing sources: ImpossibleBench (arXiv:2510.20270 — GPT-5 cheats 54% under test-vs-spec conflict, Claude models >79% via modifying test cases; mitigation: read-only tests), Anthropic reward-hacking study (12% sabotage of detection code), the Replit database incident (July 2025), Copilot coding agent guardrails as the shipped write-permission matrix, GitHub restrict-file-paths rulesets, the GitLab CODEOWNERS porosity caveat, Rovo/Atlassian MCP identity model, and Lore (arXiv:2603.15566, git-trailer decision records). The write-permission matrix and the ADR code-seam grep test stay flagged as our synthesis. I2 topic know/concepts/checks/src enriched.

v1.242026-07-15

Added the I1 deep dive — Artifact Taxonomy: Durable / Derived / Disposable (topic ia-taxonomy), opening the M4 deep-dive set: embedded overlay + standalone file, written after a three-teammate fact pass — the "Anthropic memory typology (in-context / external / in-weights / cache)" source retired as unpinnable (no primary source; third-party blogs only), the triad confirmed as our Report-3 synthesis and flagged (nearest precedents: Charity Majors durable/disposable code, records-management retention classes, the industry never-hand-edit conventions), Nygard ADR statuses completed with Deprecated, AGENTS.md stewardship corrected to the Agentic AI Foundation / Linux Foundation — plus a spot-verified discovery round (Ahrefs llms.txt study: 97% of 137k domains files at zero traffic; Solmaz SimpleDoc sprawl convention; Kiro .kiro/specs and Spec Kit layouts; a fabricated ".claudeignore" claim caught and excluded). Also fixed a real layout bug found while building: the H2/H3/H4 standalone files lacked the <main> wrapper, so their desktop two-column grid zigzagged content into the TOC column — verified and fixed in-browser. I1 topic know/concepts/checks/src enriched; I2 ADR statuses and feature-list wording corrected.

v1.232026-07-15

Added the H4 deep dive — Feedback Loop & Handoff Anti-Patterns (topic hand-feedback), completing the M3 deep-dive set: embedded overlay + standalone file, written after a three-teammate fact-verification & discovery pass plus a spot-verification round on the discovered sources. Twelve sections: the silence data (HumanEvalComm ACM TOSEM 2025 — >60% code-instead-of-asking; HiL-Bench 67–88%→1–9% when asking is required; Orchid ambiguity costs), the agent as upstream detector (Kiro Requirements Analysis, May 2026 primary source: LLM→formal logic→SMT before code; the ~60%-needed-refinement figure labelled as AWS internal testing via The New Stack), drift triage on Bugaev's reqproof question with verified verbatim wording and three owned exits, the four handoff anti-patterns with detection smells and controls (explicitly divided from E7's rollout-level catalogue), a minimal return-channel protocol (our synthesis, flagged) with the Devin-vs-Copilot/Codex clarification contrast, multi-agent drift (MAST v2 figures ~42/37/21, cited as preprint), loop-health metrics (DORA 2025 Rework Rate as anchor; New Relic survey labelled vendor-commissioned), and five brownfield moves. Fact hygiene: the unpinnable "30-minute feature balloons to 3 hours" line reframed as an unattributed pattern in all copies; Boehm's 100:1 flagged as folklore (Bossavit); a misattributed ClarifyCodeBench figure caught in spot-verification and kept out. H4 topic know/concepts/src/checks and the M3 research notes enriched with the verified evidence.

v1.222026-07-15

Added the H3 deep dive — Traceability & Spec Modes (topic hand-traceability): embedded overlay + standalone file, written after a three-teammate fact-verification & discovery pass. Eleven sections: the thirty-year heritage (Gotel & Finkelstein ICRE '94, RTM/ISO 29148, DOORS/Jama "suspect links", DO-178C/ISO 26262/IEC 62304), the ID chain with the Kiro criterion-level vs Spec Kit feature-folder contrast, structural vs inferred links, a git-native suspect gate (our synthesis, flagged), Böckeler's three spec modes with verified verbatim definitions and the Tessl Jan-2026 pivot, a new provenance section (AIDev census: unstamped agent commits undercounted 30×; Assisted-by vs Co-authored-by trailer debate; EU AI Act horizon), and a five-move minimal ID scheme for brownfield clients. Fact corrections applied across all copies: the unpinnable "~64% ML traceability accuracy" figure retired (now ~60–85% by method; RAG best-case 85.5%, RE:FSQ 2025); "Martin Böckeler" → Birgitta Böckeler in E8 (both copies); the Beck paraphrase replaced by his verified Dec-2025 LinkedIn wording; Kiro "Sync Files" precision (task-completion sync only, no drift detection). H3 topic know/concepts/checks enriched with provenance.

v1.212026-07-15

Deep-dive modal UX pass (design-led, verified in-browser). Full-width surface: the overlay panel widened 1000→1180px and the cramped 74ch cap removed from the wide blocks — .io cards, tables, component cards and figures now span the full content width; prose and callouts keep a comfortable reading measure (a new --rd ≈82ch) so lines stay legible instead of stretching. Section navigator: the black H · DEEP DIVE tag in the bar is now a toggle — click it for a "jump to section" popover listing every §; clicking a row smooth-scrolls the panel to that section. Reading progress: as you scroll, sections you pass are recorded and shown as a "read X/N" chip before the close button plus a thin fill line under the bar, and ticked in the popover; the seen-set persists through the existing store (key agentic-study-v1) so it survives closing and reopening. One generic implementation keyed off the open overlay, so all deep-dive modals (H1, H2, D1, E1–E8, P1–P5, SDLC) get it at once; the version-history modal is excluded. Keyboard/ARIA and reduced-motion supported. No content or topic-id changes.

v1.202026-07-15

Added the H2 deep-dive companion (EARS & Gherkin: machine-verifiable acceptance criteria) — second in the M3 set. The verification gap (ambiguity in / unverifiability out), the six EARS templates with the password-reset worked example, Gherkin as the executable criterion that doubles as the agent's reward signal, the story→EARS→Gherkin→red-tests ladder with the spec-review gate, and the writing craft (declarative what-not-how, BRIEF, failure paths) closing with the BDD trap and the fourth-amigo ownership answer. Three-teammate fact pass: EARS co-authors (Wilkinson, Harwood, Novak) credited and the blue-chip adopter roster reframed as creator-self-reported; Given–When–Then dated to ~2004 (North & Matts); SpecFlow EOL Dec 2024 → Reqnroll (Nagy); Spec Kit's section heading verified as "Acceptance Scenarios"; ClarifyGPT 11.52% confirmed as relative Pass@1 (GPT-4 avg, four benchmarks), Bashir 20.2% reframed as 10-shot-vs-0-shot (ICSME 2025), Wang +23.2 pp confirmed (arXiv:2508.06888). New sources from the discovery pass: Mathews & Nagappan (ASE 2024) tests-in-prompt evidence, SWE-bench Verified's FAIL_TO_PASS oracle as the field's de-facto acceptance contract, AutoUAT/TestFlow (arXiv:2504.07244), QVscribe EARS-conformance tooling, and Automation Panda's Gherkin-guidelines-for-AI. Both new H2 tables ship inside the v1.19 .figscroll wrapper.

v1.192026-07-15

Completed the table-responsiveness sweep started in v1.18: every remaining table.map across the artifact set is now wrapped in the .figscroll container (36 more tables — E1–E6, P1–P5, D1, SDLC-foundations, and the workbook's SDLC/Domain-map and topic sections), so all 50 tables scroll horizontally on narrow screens rather than overflowing. Applied in both copies (standalone files + workbook overlays/sections). No content changes.

v1.182026-07-15

Consistency follow-up to the v1.16 responsive pass: the five table.map tables in E7 (metrics dimensions, pilot data sources) and E8 (Superpowers vs Compound, SDD framework links, SDLC mapping) are now wrapped in the same .figscroll container the diagrams use — on narrow screens they scroll horizontally in their own box instead of overflowing the page, matching how H1 ships its tables. Applied in both copies (standalone files + workbook overlays). No content changes.

v1.172026-07-15

Added the H1 deep-dive companion (Anatomy of the handoff contract) — opening the M3 set. The amnesia test, the six components with per-component failure modes and owners (flagged as Report 2's synthesis; July-2026 re-verification: Spec Kit v0.12 dropped its dedicated non-goals section, amux retired as a reference — it is an agent multiplexer, not a contract format), the spec-review gate with Kiro's Requirements Analysis (LLM+SMT; ~60% of 1,400+ first-draft AC needed refinement, vendor-reported) and Fowler's counter-view, three-amigos ownership with Adzic's verified framing (the unattributable "BDD's second chance" retired), the T0–T2 contract tiers answering the minimal-contract open question, and the annotated TASK-042. New sources: Grove's The New Code, Cognition's Devin task guidance, Osmani's three-tier boundaries, SWE-Bench+ weak-oracle audit, arXiv:2603.15911 as the correct 2026 citation for the 29.6-vs-4.1 tokens/line review density. Also repaired five double-encoded UTF-8 characters from v1.15.

v1.162026-07-15

Responsive & wayfinding pass (design-led review). Mobile diagram legibility: every dense SVG diagram — the SDLC and Domain-map views, and all deep-dive figures — now keeps a legible width and scrolls horizontally on phones (with a right-edge fade + thin scrollbar cue) instead of shrinking to unreadable; captions still wrap to the viewport. Applied in the workbook and mirrored across all 15 standalone deep-dives. Orientation: a scroll-spy highlight marks the module you are reading in the sidebar, a thin reading-position line sits atop the header, and a back-to-top control appears once you scroll. Touch & scale: larger tap targets (status toggles, filter chips, tabs) meeting WCAG 2.2 target-size on touch screens, and fluid module/section headings via clamp(). No content, topic-id, storage, or desktop-layout changes. Dark mode was evaluated and deferred as a separate scoped initiative (it would touch ~150 colour points incl. every diagram fill across 16 files; light stays the right default for a reading-heavy artifact) — see NOTES.md / docs/CHANGELOG.

v1.152026-07-15

Added the D1 deep-dive companion (The design system as the third handoff artifact) — opening the M6 design-harness set. The third-artifact thesis (flagged as Report 5’s synthesis), machine-readable infrastructure with verified Frost quotes, the slop audit + design-homogenization evidence (arXiv:2603.13036), the five layers, the evidence table incl. the Indeed/Wolosin machine-readable benchmark, and conformance-vs-judgment. Fact re-verification pass workbook-wide: unsourced Storybook MCP figures reframed as an unquantified vendor claim, Frost “AI is a new user” corrected to the verified “natural design system consumer”, CHI 2025 a11y finding precisely attributed to CodeA11y.

v1.142026-07-15

Quality-review pass across the whole set (four review agents + a web fact-currency sweep). Factual fixes: stale memory→E4 cross-refs in E2/E3, Devin 3/20 phrasing, Böckeler attribution in E8, Faros-vs-DORA labels, scale-provenance flags (L adapted, P/D original), unsourced numbers softened (Torres 20–40%/~5%, Anthropic −17%, ~70% mutation bar). Currency updates: DORA 2025 (Rework Rate, AI Capabilities Model, "faster but not safer") and the METR Feb-2026 follow-up in E7; Kiro GA + Spec Kit v0.11 + Superpowers v5 in E8; the 2026 skills/plugins harness surface in E3; OWASP Agentic Skills Top 10 + harness-agnostic five-layer control table in E5. New content: E4 "verification beyond the unit-test oracle", E6 operational rubric + circuit-breaker, E2 cache economics, E8 worked 15-line spec, P1 eval checklist + privacy callout + P-ladder. Workbook UX: export/import of progress+notes, suggested study paths, SDLC in the sidebar, T-card research-priority badges, extra self-checks. Validator now also checks CSS-class coverage and CONTENT-MAP id drift.

v1.132026-07-15

Added the P5 deep-dive companion (The P1–P5 assistance scale) — completing the M2 deep-dive set. The five levels with evidence ratings per rung, the verification asymmetry vs L1–L5 (with the Nature Human Behaviour decision-task meta-analysis), why P5 is the anti-pattern (Klarna reversal, Mitchell et al., EU AI Act Art. 14), AI evals as the emerging partial verification loop, and per-artifact level assignment with downshift triggers. P1–P5 flagged as our synthesis with named ancestors. Also tightened Bashir/ClarifyGPT numbers workbook-wide (datasets, relative vs pp).

P3 · DEEP DIVE

Decomposition into Agent-Ready Work Items

Study companion · module M2

0Why decomposition is where throughput is decided

P2 ends with a handoff-ready PRD; the handoff contract (M3) specifies a single work item an engineering agent can execute. Between them sits the least glamorous step in the product harness: slicing an epic into work items. It is also the step that decides throughput, because agents do not work on epics — they work item by item, one fresh session at a time. How you cut decides how many agents can run in parallel, how big the PRs land in review, and how much autonomy each delegation can safely carry.

A perfectly specified epic that is badly sliced still fails: items too big exceed what a model can reliably finish, items too entangled serialise into a queue and merge-conflict against each other, items without their own context force every agent to rediscover intent from scratch. Decomposition is where the product layer either buys the engineering layer its parallelism — or quietly caps it.

The one idea to internalise

Decomposition is a judgment task disguised as a mechanical one. An LLM can propose a split in seconds, and the proposal will look complete. But deciding where the seams are — what is genuinely independent, what is one change pretending to be three, what is out of scope — requires knowing the codebase, the architecture and the strategy. The evidence (§3) says exactly this: AI splitting is a useful completeness checker; the seams and the sizes stay human.

1INVEST, re-read for agents

Bill Wake coined INVEST — Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, Testable — in a 2003 article on xp123.com (a practitioner blog post, later popularised by Mike Cohn's User Stories Applied). It was designed for exactly our problem, twenty years early: handing work across a context gap to someone who was not in the room. Agents widen that gap to its maximum — the recipient now has no shared history at all — which makes INVEST more relevant, not less. Each letter earns a sharper reading:

Letter2003 reading (for humans)Agent re-reading
Independentminimise ordering dependencies between storiesparallelisable across agent sessions — false independence surfaces as merge conflicts; dependency order must be explicit (§5)
Negotiablea story is a placeholder for a conversationdrafts are cheap to regenerate (disposable, I1) — but the decisions in them must be pinned, because the agent won't renegotiate
Valuablea vertical slice of user valuecarries its why and a requirement ID — the rationale gap (P2) means a human supplies it; without it the agent implements form without purpose
Estimableclear enough to estimateright-sized against the model's reliability horizon (§2) — and note: estimability serves the human slicer; AI's own estimates ran at 16% accuracy (§3)
Smallfits in an iterationfits a context window with room to work — the item plus its linked context plus the code it must read, before context rot (E2)
Testableyou know when it's donemachine-verifiable AC (EARS/Gherkin, H2) — the property that separates supervised assistance from delegable work (E4, E6)

For our rollout: INVEST is the vocabulary the client's team already knows. Presenting agent-readiness as a re-reading of it — rather than a new methodology — is the cheapest possible change-management move: same checklist shape, sharpened definitions.

2Right-sizing — the 10–30 line rule and the capability ceiling

Our working heuristic (Report 2 — practitioner synthesis, not a measured result): a work-item spec of roughly 10–30 lines. Under ~10 lines, constraints and non-goals are usually missing — the agent fills the gaps with guesses. Over ~50 lines, you are over-specifying: the spec starts to exceed the code it describes, and review burden has been relocated, not removed (H4 measured AI review commentary at ~29.6 tokens per line of code vs 4.1 human). And tasks under ~15 minutes need no spec at all — a conversational instruction is cheaper than the ceremony.

Both failure directions are real and show up as recognisable smells: under-specification is the 30-minute feature that balloons to 3 hours while the agent rewrites half a module off a misread intent; over-specification is the task whose spec took longer to review than the diff it produced.

Is there independent evidence for a size ceiling? The closest is METR's time-horizon research (arXiv:2503.14499): measure the human clock-time of tasks an agent completes with 50% success. That horizon has doubled roughly every 7 months since 2019, accelerating to ~4 months or faster on 2024+ models; by May 2026 the frontier estimate reached ≥16 hours (Claude Mythos Preview — wide confidence interval; METR itself flags measurements above 16 h as unreliable on the current suite). But two caveats do the real work here. First, the 80%-success horizon is roughly 5× shorter than the headline 50% one. Second, the benchmark is well-scoped, automatically gradable software tasks — real backlog items are messier, so real horizons sit lower still. All figures point-in-time, mid-2026; re-verify before quoting.

The sizing rule that falls out

Size work items to the reliability horizon, not the headline horizon. A delegation that succeeds half the time doesn't halve your work — it doubles your review load and erodes trust in the whole rollout (E7's perception gap). Divide the marketing number by five for the 80% horizon, discount again for messiness, and you land at: hours, not days — which is exactly where the 10–30 line spec heuristic already points. The two rules agree from independent directions.

3The evidence — splitting helps, estimating fails

Decomposition has one controlled experiment on record, and it is worth citing precisely: Pavlič, Saklamaeva & Beranič, Applied Sciences 2024 (University of Maribor; 14(24):12006). Thirteen three-person teams (39 developers analysed) upgraded a Jakarta EE system against 8 user stories; experimental teams had GitLab Duo Chat split the stories into tasks and estimate them, and could adjust prompts but not overrule the tool.

What it found — strong independent evidence

Estimation failed: AI accuracy 16% (SD 9.2) vs 60% (SD 24.5) for human control teams — the paper calls 16% insufficient for industry standards, and 60% industry-typical. The AI overestimated ~4× (13.55 h vs 3.37 h per story) while actual effort was nearly identical across groups. Splitting helped, with a filter: AI decomposition was finer-grained (5.4 vs 3.2 tasks per story) and caught overlooked work — but the lists carried generic, project-irrelevant items, and the authors' recommendation is to start from your own list and use the AI's to spot what you missed. Post-test preference: conventional-only 0%, AI-only 0%, hybrid 100%.

The same group's 2026 follow-up (Splitting User Stories Into Tasks with AI — A Foe or an Ally?, arXiv:2605.07320) sharpens the picture: teams implemented only ~59% of AI-generated tasks versus 100% of manually authored ones, and just 10% of participants found the AI tasks more relevant than their own (55% disagreed) — yet participants still favoured the hybrid workflow. Note honestly: this is the same Maribor group, a follow-up rather than an independent replication — and as of mid-2026 controlled evidence on AI backlog decomposition is essentially this one line of work. Estimation research is broader (zero-shot LLMs now beat older supervised baselines on story-point benchmarks — Shetty et al., arXiv:2603.06276), but benchmarks are not your backlog; treat vendor claims about "AI sprint planning" accordingly.

Read through P2's lens, the result is the same asymmetry: splitting is expansion — generate more candidates from the same input, a job models do well with a human filter; estimation and prioritisation are judgment — they price in context the model does not have, and fail without it. Point the model at expansion; keep judgment human.

4The amnesiac colleague — context links and the agent-ready checklist

The deepest difference between handing a task to a teammate and to an agent: the agent has total amnesia. It was not in the refinement call, does not remember the ticket from March, and will not lean over to ask the senior at the next desk — every clarification is a round-trip that spends the autonomy you delegated for. Report 2's observed failure mode follows directly: given an item without context, agents reverse-engineer intent from code comments — they excavate whatever fragments the repo happens to contain and guess the rest. The fix is not a longer spec; it is context links — pointers to the code, the design, the ADR and the requirement, so the agent assembles fresh, correct context per session (E2) instead of guessing.

That completes the agent-ready checklist — the deployable artifact of this topic, and deliberately the same six components as the handoff contract (H1) at task grain:

Agent-ready = all six present
  • intent/why — one human-written sentence (the rationale gap, P2)
  • scope + explicit non-goals — or the agent over-engineers
  • machine-verifiable AC — Gherkin/EARS (H2)
  • constraints — tech, security, performance
  • context links — code, design, ADR (the amnesia antidote)
  • DoD + requirement ID — traceability travels with the task (H3)
Right-sized = three checks
  • spec ~10–30 lines; under 10, look for the missing constraints
  • well inside the model's reliability horizon (§2) — hours, not days
  • independent of its siblings — parallelisable without merge conflicts
Where it sits
  • Backlog refinement — the same ceremony that carries P2's red-team gate; the checklist is the exit gate: no item leaves refinement incomplete.
Enforce it as a gate, not a guideline

Most of the checklist is deterministically lintable (E5's advisory-vs-deterministic rule): a non-goals section exists, AC parse as Gherkin, context links resolve, a requirement ID is present. A template plus a refinement-bot check makes those structural. What stays human is exactly the judgment part: is the seam right, is the why true, is this really one task. The gate produces its own evidence trail — useful later for E7's measurement story.

5How the SDD toolchains decompose — and what to take

Both major spec-driven toolchains treat decomposition as a first-class, reviewable step (all details point-in-time, mid-2026). GitHub Spec Kit: /speckit.tasks generates a tasks.md — dependency-ordered, grouped by user story, each line - [ ] T001 [P] [US1] description + exact file path, where [P] marks tasks parallelisable because they touch different files with no dependencies; a separate command converts tasks to GitHub issues. Its template states the bar in one line: tasks must be "specific enough for an LLM to complete without additional context" — the tersest definition of agent-ready we have found. Amazon Kiro: requirements.md (EARS) → design.md → tasks.md with review between phases; each checkbox task carries nested sub-tasks and a trailing traceability line (_Requirements: 1.1, 1.5_), and independent tasks can run concurrently.

What we extract is portable without adopting either tool. First, the task list is itself a reviewable artifact — decomposition gets reviewed before any implementation starts, the same inversion E8 applies to specs. Second, independence is made inspectable: explicit dependency ordering and parallel markers turn "these are independent" from a hope into a claim a reviewer can check. Third, traceability travels per task, not per epic (H3). This is what P4 on the assistance scale — AI-driven decomposition with approval gates — looks like when it is done right.

The parallelism trap

Parallel markers make fan-out tempting: ten independent tasks, ten agents. But the binding constraint is not task independence — it is review capacity (E7: never scale agents beyond it). Decomposition sets the ceiling on parallelism; the review bottleneck sets the budget. Slice for independence, then spend the budget deliberately.

6Takeaways for our engagement

Assess
  • who splits epics today, and does AI propose or decide?
  • do items carry non-goals, AC, context links, requirement IDs?
  • typical item size — and how often do agents finish without follow-up questions?
  • are AI-proposed splits filtered, or pasted into the backlog?
Build
  • adopt the agent-ready checklist as the refinement exit gate; lint the structural parts
  • AI proposes the split, a human owns seams and sizes — hybrid won 100% preference
