The three-tier boundary pattern (always / ask first / never) adopted in §2D, the PRD+SRS blend, and "one task focus" — feed the slice, not the binder.

Kiro — specs docs & Requirements Analysis
kiro.dev/docs/specs · GA Nov 17, 2025 · Requirements Analysis announced 2026

The tightest tool coupling of contract to verification: EARS in requirements.md, _Requirements: 1.1_ back-links, property-based testing at GA, and the LLM+SMT contradiction prover (~60% of 1,400+ first-draft AC needed refinement — vendor-reported). Point-in-time; re-verify.

Report 2 — the handoff-contract research notes (this workbook)
module M3 research notes · TASK-042 template in full

The original synthesis this deep dive expands, including the complete task template ready to copy into a client repo, the story→EARS→Gherkin→failing-tests flow, and the phased adoption recommendations.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (H1-handoff-contract-anatomy-deepdive.html). It opens the M3 deep-dive set. Its natural neighbours in the workbook are H2 (EARS & Gherkin — the formats behind component C), H3 (traceability & spec modes — where the IDs travel), H4 (the feedback loop and the anti-patterns §5 diagnoses), P2/P3 (the upstream product artifacts), E2/E4/E5 (the harness layers each component drives), E8 (the SDD workflow that authors and reviews the contract), and D1 (the design system standing beside it when the work has a screen).

H2 · DEEP DIVE

EARS & Gherkin: Machine-Verifiable Acceptance Criteria

0Why the criteria are the contract's engine

H1 dissected the handoff contract into six components and was blunt about which one carries the proof obligation: component C, the acceptance criteria — "machine-verifiable wherever possible". This deep dive is about the two formats that deliver that clause. EARS structures requirements as constrained natural-language rules; Gherkin turns rules into concrete, executable examples. Together they walk a criterion down an altitude ladder — from intent a PM can ratify, to a scenario a reviewer can falsify, to a red test the harness can run — and that last rung is the point. E4's verification-first principle says the reviewer's question is never "does this look right" but "what machine said this is right"; criteria written in prose leave that question unanswerable, and the review gate H1 built becomes theatre.

The reason this matters more for agents than it ever did for humans is the same amnesia argument P3 made for decomposition. A human developer resolves an ambiguous criterion by leaning over to ask; an agent resolves it by guessing with confidence — Report 2 calls this intent hallucination, and it is invisible at exactly the moment it happens, because the agent's guess compiles, passes its own improvised tests, and reads plausibly in the diff. The defence is not more prose. It is criteria structured enough that ambiguity has nowhere to hide (EARS) and concrete enough that the claim "done" is a program output, not an opinion (Gherkin).

The oracle, in one line

Every delegation needs an oracle — the thing that decides pass or fail. A prose criterion makes the reviewer the oracle (expensive, inconsistent, unavailable at 2 a.m. when the agent finishes); an executable criterion makes the harness the oracle. H2 is the craft of moving criteria from the first category to the second, and of knowing when the move is not worth it (§4).

1The verification gap

Put a typical brownfield ticket criterion next to what a machine can evaluate and the gap is obvious. "The system should handle errors gracefully" names no trigger, no observable response, no boundary — it cannot fail, therefore it cannot pass. The INVEST checklist already demanded testable stories a decade before agents; the workbook's M3 overview sharpened the T for the agentic setting: testable is no longer enough, the bar is machine-verifiable, because the whole economic argument for delegation (E6's autonomy levels) collapses if a human must hand-inspect every completion anyway.

The gap has two distinct failure directions, and the two formats of this topic attack one each. Direction one is ambiguity in: the criterion under-specifies, the agent fills the void with priors, and you get the wrong right-looking thing — H1's intent problem at criterion granularity. Direction two is unverifiability out: the criterion may even be clear, but nothing can execute it, so "finished" reverts to opinion and the PR review inherits the entire proof burden — at densities arXiv:2603.15911 measured at 29.6 review tokens per changed line for AI-authored work, that burden is precisely what the contract was supposed to amortize. EARS narrows direction one; Gherkin closes direction two.

One warning before the formats: an executable criterion is only as strong as its oracle. The SWE-Bench+ audit (arXiv:2410.06992, cited at length in H1 §2C) found roughly a third of claimed benchmark passes tainted by solution leakage and another third passing only because tests were weak. Translating a vague rule into a weak scenario produces confident false greens — worse than prose, because it looks machine-verified. The craft sections (§5) and B3's mutation gate exist for exactly this reason.

2EARS — constrained natural language for rules

EARS — the Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax — was developed at Rolls-Royce by Alistair Mavin with Philip Wilkinson, Adrian Harwood and Mark Novak, and published at the IEEE Requirements Engineering conference in 2009 (RE'09, DOI 10.1109/RE.2009.9). The origin is instructive: the team was analysing airworthiness regulations for a jet-engine control system — prose requirements at their most safety-critical and most lawyered — and found that nearly all of them could be rewritten into a handful of sentence templates keyed on when and under what conditions the system must respond. Five core patterns plus a combining form:

PatternTemplatePassword-reset example (Report 2's task)
Ubiquitous — always trueThe <system> shall <response>The service shall log every throttling decision with account id hashed.
Event-driven — triggeredWHEN <trigger>, the <system> shall <response>WHEN a client submits more than 5 reset requests for the same account within 15 minutes, the system shall respond 429 and shall not send a reset email.
State-driven — while a state holdsWHILE <state>, the <system> shall <response>WHILE the throttle flag is off, the system shall preserve existing reset behaviour byte-for-byte.
Optional feature — if the feature is presentWHERE <feature is included>, the <system> shall <response>WHERE the tenant has SSO enabled, the system shall exempt IdP-initiated resets from the counter.
Unwanted behaviour — failure & abuse pathsIF <trigger>, THEN the <system> shall <response>IF the Redis counter is unavailable, THEN the system shall fail open and alert, not block resets.
Complex — combinationsWHILE <state>, WHEN <trigger>, the <system> shall <response>WHILE the flag is on, WHEN the 6th request arrives, the system shall respond 429.
The pattern is the linter

The templates look almost too simple to matter, and that is the trick: a vague sentence does not fit any of them. "Handle errors gracefully" has no trigger for WHEN, no state for WHILE, no observable response for the SHALL clause — attempting the rewrite is the ambiguity detector, and it runs at authoring time, before any agent sees the task. E5's advisory-versus-deterministic rule applies here too: EARS conformance is mechanically checkable (QVscribe has shipped automated EARS-conformance checking since v2.10, alongside INCOSE's 41-rule set), so a client can gate requirements the way CI gates code.

Who uses it? Honestly sourced: Rolls-Royce is the documented origin; beyond that, the adopter roster — Airbus, Bosch, Dyson, Honeywell, Intel, NASA, Siemens — is self-reported by EARS's creator on alistairmavin.com, and we present it as exactly that, a claim by the method's author, not independent verification. What is independently observable is the agentic uptake: Amazon's Kiro generates its requirements.md in explicitly-named EARS notation ("WHEN [condition/event] THE SYSTEM SHALL [expected behavior]") — the tightest coupling yet of a 2009 aerospace syntax to a 2025 coding agent, and a vendor design decision worth reading as evidence of fit (point-in-time, mid-2026; re-verify).

Why does a sentence syntax help a language model? Direct measured evidence is thin — we flag clearly: no study we can cite shows "EARS improves agent code generation by X%", and we refuse to invent one. What exists is adjacent: grammar-prompting work (arXiv:2305.19234, NeurIPS 2023) shows LLMs follow injected formal syntax templates reliably, and requirements-engineering research has begun evaluating EARS as a target template for structuring extracted knowledge (arXiv:2511.15340). The mechanism argument is simpler and older than either paper: EARS removes the degrees of freedom in which intent hallucination lives. The trigger, the state, the actor and the observable response are each forced into a named slot; an empty slot is visible to a reviewer — and to a linter — in a way a fuzzy paragraph never is.

3Gherkin — the executable criterion

EARS produces rules. Rules are still abstractions, and abstractions still leave room to guess — which five requests, what counts as the same account, what exactly does the 429 body say? Gherkin closes the remaining distance with concrete examples. A scenario names real data and a real observable outcome:

The EARS rule from §2, as a Gherkin scenario

Feature: password-reset throttling
  Scenario: sixth request in the window is throttled
    Given account "ada@example.com" has made 5 reset requests in the last 15 minutes
    When the client submits another reset request for "ada@example.com"
    Then the response status is 429
    And no reset email is sent

Given establishes state, When fires the trigger, Then asserts the observable response — the same three slots EARS forced, now bound to data. Note what the scenario adds to the rule: the boundary is now falsifiable (a sibling scenario asserts the fifth request passes), and "no reset email is sent" is a checkable side-effect assertion, not a hope.

The history matters because it explains both the power and the trap (§5). Given–When–Then was worked out around 2004 by Dan North with business analyst Chris Matts, as part of North's behaviour-driven development — BDD — reframing of TDD; the canonical write-up, "Introducing BDD", ran in Better Software magazine in March 2006. Aslak Hellesøy's Cucumber (2008) made the format executable: Gherkin is Cucumber's plain-text language, and step definitions bind each Given/When/Then line to automation code, so the same text is simultaneously the specification a PM reads and the test the harness runs — "executable specification", with "living documentation" as its corollary: documentation that fails CI when it stops being true.

For two decades that double identity was BDD's selling point to humans, and adoption chronically decayed into §5's trap. The agentic setting changes the economics in Gherkin's favour, for a reason E8 already named: the expensive part of BDD was never reading scenarios, it was writing and maintaining the step definitions — mechanical glue code, which is precisely the work coding agents do well and cheaply. And the payoff compounds: a Gherkin set handed to an agent is not just documentation, it is the reward signal. The agent iterates until the scenarios pass; the criteria are the oracle from §0, wired directly into the loop. SDD tooling has converged on the format from the other direction — GitHub Spec Kit's template writes each user story's Acceptance Scenarios section as numbered Given/When/Then items (point-in-time, mid-2026).

4The ladder: story → EARS → Gherkin → red tests

The two formats are levels, not competitors — a distinction Specification by Example (Adzic, 2011) drew as rules versus examples: EARS states the rule at the requirements level; Gherkin illustrates the rule with key examples at the acceptance-criteria level; the failing tests generated from the scenarios are the handoff itself. Report 2's flow, end to end:

Two practical notes on running the ladder. First, the agent drafts, the human ratifies — the same division P4 established for PRDs. Translating a ratified story into candidate EARS rules and candidate scenarios is exactly the mechanical-transformation work agents are good at; the judgement calls — is this rule what we meant, are these the examples that matter, did we cover the failure paths — stay human, at the spec review gate. Second, the ladder is not a mandate: H1's T0–T2 sizing applies rung by rung. A T0 tweak needs no EARS at all; a T1 task might carry two EARS lines and three scenarios (the password-reset task above is exactly this size — fifteen spec lines total, E8's worked example); only a T2 slice earns the full set. Sixteen acceptance criteria on a one-line bugfix is the sledgehammer anti-pattern, not rigour.

5Writing quality — the craft and the trap

Machine-verifiable formats fail in characteristic ways, all of them known for a decade before agents arrived. Three crafts and one organizational trap:

1

Declarative, not imperative

The Cucumber documentation's own rule: scenarios "should describe the intended behaviour of the system, not the implementation — what, not how". An imperative scenario scripts UI mechanics (click this, type that, wait) and welds the criterion to today's interface, so every redesign breaks the spec without the rule having changed; a declarative scenario ("Given an account with 5 recent reset requests…") survives any implementation the rule permits. For agents the distinction is sharper still: an imperative scenario quietly makes design decisions that belonged to the agent's solution space — it smuggles the how into the what, exactly the altitude error P2 warned about in PRDs.

2

BRIEF

Seb Rose and Gáspár Nagy's checklist from Formulation (the BDD Books): Business language, Real data, Intention revealing, Essential, Focused — and the sixth principle, brief itself. "Essential" carries the most weight in the agentic setting: every incidental detail in a scenario ("the button is blue") reads to an agent as a requirement to satisfy, so padding does not just bore reviewers, it actively constrains the solution. The contract-sizing logic of H1 §5 repeats at scenario grain.

3

Failure paths are half the set

H1 §2C's craft, formalized by the formats: EARS gives unwanted behaviour its own pattern (IF/THEN) and Gherkin makes each failure path one scenario — service down, empty result, sixth-versus-fifth request at the boundary. TASK-042's network-failure scenario and the password-reset fail-open rule are the worked examples. A criteria set with only happy paths is a weak oracle (§1) wearing executable clothes; B2's characterization-test discipline is the brownfield version of the same instinct.

The BDD trap — and who writes it at the client

BDD's documented failure mode is organizational, not technical: Given/When/Then adopted as a test-automation syntax, written by test engineers alone, after development, with no conversation — Seb Rose: "I see far more people using G/W/T for test automation than to support BDD… the BDD acronym has become synonymous in the industry with G/W/T powered tests." Adzic's Specification by Example (2011, from 50-plus case studies) found collaboration was the success factor, and his verified framing of the agentic revival cuts both ways: "Specification by Example taken to a new level — or the revenge of Waterfall." Which one a client gets is decided by ownership. The answer this workbook carries (H1 §4): three-amigos lenses, kept — analyst drafts business rules (EARS), tester lens drafts boundary and negative scenarios, tech lead ratifies verifiability — with the agent as the fourth amigo that drafts candidates and step definitions, never the sole author. If only QA touches the Gherkin, you have rebuilt the trap with more expensive tooling.

6What the evidence says — and the 2026 toolchain

The workbook's three headline numbers for this topic all survived a July-2026 re-verification pass, each with a framing correction worth keeping. ClarifyGPT (Mu et al., FSE 2024; arXiv:2310.10996) has the model detect ambiguous requirements and ask clarifying questions before generating code: an average relative Pass@1 improvement of 11.52% for GPT-4 across four benchmarks — concretely, 70.96% → 80.80% on MBPP-sanitized. Relative percent, not percentage points; the mechanism it prices is exactly §0's point that asking beats guessing. Bashir et al. (ICSME 2025, industry track) measured ambiguity classification on three industrial railway requirement sets (Alstom, Westermo): the 20.2% figure is the average gain from giving the model ten in-context examples over zero-shot — the value of showing the model what ambiguity looks like, not of using an LLM at all; the LLM's explanations of why a requirement is ambiguous rated 3.84/5 with practitioners. Wang et al. (arXiv:2508.06888, the RAGcceptance system) generate acceptance criteria grounded in retrieved project data: multi-modal RAG lifts case-level accuracy from 42.8% to 66.0% — the +23.2 pp our topic data cites — on a ~100,000-user education platform.

Read as one argument, the three studies point the same way: requirement ambiguity is measurable, and every mechanism that reduces it before generation pays — asking (ClarifyGPT), showing examples (Bashir), grounding in real project data (Wang). Note the honest limit from §2: none of them tests EARS or Gherkin syntax as the treatment; what they validate is the underlying bet that structure and grounding beat prose.

The test-first rung of the ladder has more direct backing — this is what the discovery pass added to our outline. Mathews & Nagappan (ASE 2024, arXiv:2402.13521) tested TDD-for-LLMs empirically: putting the test cases in the prompt consistently raises solve rates on MBPP and HumanEval — tests-as-spec, measured. And the strongest real-world datapoint is a design decision hiding in plain sight: SWE-bench Verified, the industry's standard coding-agent benchmark, defines "resolved" entirely as a test oracle — hidden FAIL_TO_PASS tests must flip green and no PASS_TO_PASS test may regress, with no human judgement anywhere in the loop. The benchmark the whole field competes on is, structurally, a machine-verifiable acceptance contract; E7's measurement chapter and this topic's thesis meet in that one design choice.

Gherkin-specific agent research is younger — industrial case studies rather than controlled trials, flagged as emerging: AutoUAT and TestFlow generate Gherkin from user stories and executable tests from Gherkin at a reported ~€0.12 per test script (arXiv:2504.07244); the A-TEST 2024 experiments had collaborating LLM agents execute Gherkin scenarios directly; and the practitioner layer is forming — Andrew Knight's Gherkin guidelines for AI (Automation Panda, Apr 2026) is a curated ruleset written to be fed to coding agents as authoring context, §5's craft packaged for the fourth amigo.

Toolchain, point-in-time (mid-2026 — re-verify)
  • Cucumber — Ruby/JS/JVM; open source, stewarded by SmartBear since the 2019 acquisition of Cucumber Ltd
  • pytest-bdd / behave — the Python bindings; pytest-bdd rides the existing pytest harness, usually the lightest brownfield entry
  • SpecFlow is dead — Tricentis EOL'd it Dec 31, 2024 (repos deleted); .NET teams migrate to Reqnroll, the early-2024 fork by Gáspár Nagy, SpecFlow's original creator
  • Kiro — requirements.md in named EARS notation (§2); GitHub Spec Kit — Acceptance Scenarios as Given/When/Then per user story
  • QVscribe — automated EARS-conformance and INCOSE-rule checking for requirements text
How to read the evidence (house rule)
  • Strong independent: ClarifyGPT (FSE 2024), Bashir et al. (ICSME 2025), Wang et al. (arXiv:2508.06888), Mathews & Nagappan (ASE 2024), and SWE-bench Verified's oracle design — peer-reviewed or field-standard, with numbers
  • Vendor claims: Kiro's EARS workflow and Requirements Analysis figures; Spec Kit template design — design decisions, not measurements
  • Practitioner synthesis: the ladder itself (Report 2), the fourth-amigo ownership, BRIEF-for-agents — ours and others' experience, flagged as such

7How H2 connects the framework

TopicThe connection
H1 handoff anatomyH2 is component C in depth — the formats behind "machine-verifiable wherever possible"; the T0–T2 tiers size how much of the ladder a task earns
E4 verification-firstGherkin scenarios are the agent-facing edge of the test pyramid: the failing tests are the oracle the harness runs; weak scenarios = weak oracle = false greens
E5 governanceEARS conformance and scenario linting are mechanically checkable — move them from advisory prose to deterministic gates (QVscribe, CI Gherkin linters)
E8 spec-driven developmentSDD's specify phase emits exactly these formats: Kiro writes EARS, Spec Kit writes Given/When/Then — H2 is the format layer under the E8 workflow
H3 traceabilityEARS rules carry requirement IDs; scenarios tag the IDs; tests inherit them — the chain H3 walks (Kiro's _Requirements: 1.1 back-links are the tool-native version)
H4 feedback loopa production bug re-enters as a new failing scenario — the feedback format equals the handoff format, so nothing is re-explained in prose
P3 / P4 product harnessP3's decomposition produces the stories the ladder consumes; P4's author→editor shift is the authoring model — agent drafts rules and scenarios, humans ratify at the gate
B2 / B3 brownfieldcharacterization tests are Gherkin's brownfield cousin (pin current behaviour before change); B3's mutation gate is how you audit scenario strength

8Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Acceptance criteria are the only part of the handoff contract a machine can enforce, so their format decides whether the spec review gate is real or theatre: EARS forces the rule to name its trigger, state and observable response; Gherkin binds the rule to concrete falsifiable examples; the failing tests are the handoff. The formats are cheap and old — the 2009 aerospace syntax and the 2004 conversation pattern — and the agentic turn fixes their historical cost problem, because step definitions and candidate scenarios are exactly the mechanical work agents draft well, while ratifying rules and choosing the examples that matter stays human at the gate. The trap is organizational, not technical: if Gherkin becomes QA's test syntax instead of the team's shared criteria language, you have rebuilt 2015-era BDD failure with more expensive tooling — keep the three amigos, add the agent as the fourth who drafts but never ratifies.

9Further reading

Mavin, Wilkinson, Harwood & Novak — Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax (EARS)
IEEE RE'09 · DOI 10.1109/RE.2009.9 · templates at alistairmavin.com/ears

The original paper, born from Rolls-Royce airworthiness analysis. The site carries the canonical templates and the adopter list — the latter self-reported by the author, cite accordingly.

Dan North — Introducing BDD
Better Software magazine, March 2006 · dannorth.net/introducing-bdd

The canonical essay; Given–When–Then itself dates to ~2004, worked out with business analyst Chris Matts — the format was a conversation pattern before it was a test syntax, which is the whole point of §5's trap.

Gojko Adzic — Specification by Example
Manning, 2011 · gojko.net

The collaboration evidence base (50+ case-study teams): rules illustrated by key examples, specification as a team conversation. Source of the rules-vs-examples distinction §4 builds on and the "revenge of Waterfall" warning §5 quotes.

Seb Rose & Gáspár Nagy — Formulation (the BDD Books)
Leanpub · BRIEF

The scenario-writing craft: Business language, Real data, Intention revealing, Essential, Focused, Brief. With cucumber.io/docs/bdd/better-gherkin (declarative what-not-how), the two standing style references for §5.

Mu et al. — ClarifyGPT
FSE 2024 · DOI 10.1145/3660810 · arXiv:2310.10996

The ask-before-coding evidence: detecting ambiguous requirements and posing clarifying questions lifts GPT-4 Pass@1 by an average 11.52% (relative) across four benchmarks — the measured version of §0's amnesia argument.

Mathews & Nagappan — Test-Driven Development and LLM-based Code Generation
ASE 2024 · arXiv:2402.13521

The empirical spine of §4's last rung: including test cases in the prompt consistently raises solve rates — tests-as-spec, measured. Read together with SWE-bench Verified's FAIL_TO_PASS oracle, the field's own definition of "done" as a test.

Wang et al. — Multi-Modal Requirements Data-based Acceptance Criteria Generation (RAGcceptance)
arXiv:2508.06888

AC generation grounded in retrieved project data: +23.2 pp case-level accuracy over no-retrieval. The evidence that the agent drafting the ladder's left side (§4) needs grounding, not just a template.

Kiro specs docs & GitHub Spec Kit spec template
kiro.dev/docs/specs · github.com/github/spec-kit · point-in-time, mid-2026

The two SDD tools' native use of the formats: named EARS notation in requirements.md; Acceptance Scenarios as numbered Given/When/Then per user story. Vendor design decisions — evidence of fit, not of effect.

Report 2 — the handoff-contract research notes (this workbook)
module M3 research notes · the story→EARS→Gherkin→tests flow

The original synthesis behind §4's ladder, including the password-reset worked example this deep dive reuses and the phased adoption recommendation for clients.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (H2-ears-gherkin-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours are H1 (the contract these criteria live in), H3 (how the requirement IDs travel from EARS through scenarios into tests), and E4 (the verification stack that runs what this topic writes).

H3 · DEEP DIVE

Traceability & Spec Modes: The Chain from Requirement to PR

0Why it matters

Every artifact in this module exists so that work can be delegated and still be accountable. H1 built the contract, H2 made its criteria machine-verifiable — H3 is about what happens after the handoff: keeping the chain requirement → spec → task → code → test → PR connected while agents generate most of its links. At human speed, weak traceability is an annoyance that surfaces in a quarterly audit. At agent speed it is structural: an agent produces artifacts faster than anyone can reconstruct their provenance afterwards, so the question "which requirement does this code serve?" stops being an audit question and becomes the operating question of the whole delegation loop. Without the link you cannot review by intent (H1), you cannot re-verify after a change (E4), and you cannot triage drift when spec and code disagree (H4).

The failure mode is already documented — though so far as vendor narrative, not measurement. Jama Software describes a systems-engineering team that ran a traceability audit about six months into an AI-assisted initiative and found that "a significant share" of agent-generated code had no traceable link back to any documented requirement, alongside orphaned test cases and specified features that were never built (Mario Maldari, Jama blog, May 2026). No percentage is given, and Jama sells traceability tooling — treat it as an illustration with an incentive, not a statistic. But it names the mechanism precisely: the agent built something, tests passed, PRs merged, and only the audit revealed that nobody could say which requirements the code answered to.

The link is the memory

A human author can be interrogated months later — "why is this here?" gets an answer from memory, however fuzzy. An agent's session is gone the moment it ends (the amnesia argument P3 made for decomposition). For agent-written artifacts the recorded link is not documentation about the knowledge — it is the only knowledge there is. That inversion is why traceability moves from compliance chore to load-bearing infrastructure in an agentic SDLC.

1Thirty years of prior art

Traceability is the least novel topic in this workbook, and that is its strength: the concepts are proven, only the economics changed. The field's founding problem statement is Gotel & Finkelstein's "An Analysis of the Requirements Traceability Problem" (ICRE '94), built on a study of 100+ practitioners, which also drew the still-useful line between pre-requirements traceability (where a requirement came from — stakeholder, regulation, decision) and post-requirements traceability (where it went — design, code, test). The requirements traceability matrix is a standard artifact formalized in ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148; BABOK calls the same thing a coverage matrix. And requirements-management tools solved change propagation decades ago: in IBM DOORS, Jama Connect or Polarion, editing an upstream requirement automatically flags every downstream link as "suspect" until an engineer reviews and clears it — a mechanism we will steal wholesale in §4.

In regulated industries none of this is optional. DO-178C (avionics) requires demonstrable bidirectional trace from system requirements through software requirements to source code, test cases and results; ISO 26262 (automotive) and IEC 62304 (medical device software) mandate the same discipline with different evidence depth. The audit question "show me the requirement behind this code" has had a mandatory answer in aerospace since before most web developers were born.

What the heritage solved
  • the vocabulary: trace links, suspect flags, coverage matrices (29148)
  • the mechanism: upstream change → downstream artifacts flagged for review
  • the governance: bidirectional trace as an audit obligation (DO-178C, ISO 26262, IEC 62304)
What agents change
  • volume — hundreds of artifacts a week, not per release
  • speed — drift accumulates in hours, not quarters
  • authorship — no human memory behind the artifact; the link is the memory (§0)
  • and one thing in our favour: an instructed agent applies ID discipline deterministically — it never finds it tedious (§3)

The practical conclusion for a brownfield client: agentic teams are converging on what regulated industries always did — minus the tooling weight. The job is not to buy an ALM suite; it is to get the suspect-flag idea and the coverage idea running on git primitives (§7).

2The chain — IDs travel forward

Traceability at its simplest is one identifier that survives every hop. The requirement gets an ID at birth; the spec quotes it; the task names it; the branch, the commits and the PR carry it; the tests encode it in their names. Each hop is written down at the moment the artifact is created — by the author, human or agent — which is what makes the whole chain greppable, auditable and free of inference.

The two spec-driven toolchains implement the forward chain at different resolutions — a genuine design contrast, not just branding (tool facts point-in-time, mid-2026; re-verify). Kiro traces at the level of individual acceptance criteria: requirements.md numbers every criterion (1.1, 1.2 …), and each task in tasks.md carries a trailing line naming exactly which criteria it implements — the format Kiro emits is _Requirements: 1.2, 2.1_ under the task entry. GitHub Spec Kit (v0.12.x as of July 2026) traces at the level of the feature: /speckit.specify creates a numbered directory specs/001-feature-name/ holding spec.md, plan.md and tasks.md, plus a matching git branch 001-feature-name — folder- and branch-level linkage, with no per-requirement ID system. Kiro answers "which criterion does this task serve?"; Spec Kit answers "which feature does this branch serve?". For a brownfield client the difference matters: criterion-level trace is what lets a failing test point back at a specific requirement line.

Outside the toolchains, the carriers are ordinary git and forge conventions: the ID in the branch name, a commit trailer (Refs: REQ-12 in the Conventional-Commits footer position), the forge's closing keywords in the PR body ("Closes REQ-12" auto-links and closes the tracker item), and the ID embedded in test names so a red test announces which requirement is failing. This is also where H1's anatomy pays off twice: component E (context links) is where the contract records the chain backwards — the requirement, ADRs and code paths the task descends from — and component F (Definition of Done) is where the tech lead demands the chain forwards: "PR links REQ-12; tests named for it." A T0 micro-task (H1's sizing tiers) may carry nothing but an issue ID; a T2 contract carries the full set.

3Structural links vs inferred links

There are exactly two ways to know which requirement a piece of code serves: write the link down when the artifact is born (structural), or reconstruct it afterwards from textual similarity (inferred — the research field of automated trace-link recovery). The research verdict on inference is remarkably stable across two decades: never reliable enough for unattended use. The retrospective by Cleland-Huang, Hayes and colleagues ("Automating Requirements Traceability: Two Decades of Learning from KDD", arXiv:1807.11454) documents that classic information-retrieval methods never reached uncritical-use quality; current LLM-based recovery lands around 60–70% recall on benchmark datasets, and even the strongest recent result — a retrieval-augmented pipeline for requirements-to-code recovery in automotive reporting ~85.5% recovery accuracy (RE:FSQ 2025) — comes wrapped in the same caveat every paper in the genre carries: residual "phantom links", naming bias, and an explicit requirement for human review, especially in regulated contexts.

So the defensible claim is not "IDs beat ML" absolutely — LLM recovery is improving fast and the gap is narrowing. It is sharper and more useful: structural links are deterministic, auditable and 100%-precise by construction; inferred links are probabilistic and need a human in the loop. A regulated audit (§1) accepts the first kind as evidence and the second only as a reviewed proposal. ReqToCode (Schlathölter, arXiv:2603.13999, March 2026) makes exactly this argument for the agentic setting: requirement IDs embedded structurally in the codebase persist when AI generates or modifies code, whereas ML-inferred associations degrade and need re-training as the code moves under them.

And here the economics tilt further than they ever did for humans, in an inversion worth savouring: humans find ID discipline tedious, so structural traceability historically decayed — which is what created the market for recovery research in the first place. An agent finds nothing tedious. Put the stamping rule in CLAUDE.md and the deterministic gate in CI (§7), and the agent applies the convention on every commit without complaint or fatigue. The author that caused the traceability problem at scale is also the first author that reliably follows the discipline that prevents it.

Where inference does earn its keep: the brownfield bootstrap

Your client's existing codebase has no links — and no discipline retroactively creates them. A one-shot, human-reviewed recovery pass (LLM-assisted, ~60–85% by method) is the realistic way to bootstrap coverage over legacy code, prioritized by the change heatmap (M5). Inference to bootstrap once; structure to maintain forever. What does not work is running inference continuously as a substitute for discipline.

4Invalidation — the suspect flag

A chain of links is only useful if it reacts to change. The mechanism is §1's suspect flag, generalized: when an artifact at one end of a link changes, the artifact at the other end becomes suspect — possibly stale, pending a human (or gate) re-affirming the link. Code changed? The linked spec, requirement and tests are suspect. Requirement changed? Every task, code path and test tracing to it is suspect. In DOORS or Jama this is a built-in red flag cleared by review; the concept transfers to a git-native stack almost embarrassingly cheaply, because markdown specs versioned in the repo (M4's durable tier) already ride the same diff and the same review as code.

Be precise about what today's agentic tools actually do here, because the marketing suggests more than the products deliver (point-in-time, mid-2026). Kiro does not do drift detection. Its one sync feature — "Sync Files" on tasks.md — analyzes the codebase and checks off tasks that turn out to be already implemented (useful when a colleague did the work in another session). That is one-directional and task-completion-only: nothing in Kiro flags that your code has wandered away from requirements.md, and nothing regenerates a spec from code. Spec Kit has no invalidation mechanism at all. If you want suspect flags, you build them — which is fortunately a CI rule, not a product:

SYNTHESIS

A git-native suspect gate

ours — flag it as such to the client

Two deterministic rules (E5's advisory→deterministic principle), implementable as a CI script over the PR diff: (1) code-side: if the PR touches source paths that a spec's context links (H1 component E) name, but does not touch the spec file, label the PR suspect-spec — advisory at first, a failing check once the team trusts it. (2) spec-side: if a spec file changes but no linked code or test changes with it (and the PR is not labelled spec-only), flag the reverse. Both rules are greps over the diff plus the spec's own link list — no ML, no new tooling, and the suspect label lands exactly where H4's triage question picks it up: is the code wrong, is the requirement wrong, or did we learn something?

The deliberate modesty of this design is the point. The heritage tools implement suspect tracking with full link databases and audit trails because their industries must prove it; a product team needs the reflex, not the database — a PR label that forces the drift conversation while the diff is still open, instead of an audit that discovers it two quarters later.

5The three spec modes

Everything so far assumed the spec survives the task. Whether it should — and what role it plays afterwards — is the sharpest open question in spec-driven development, and the cleanest framing is Birgitta Böckeler's (Thoughtworks, martinfowler.com, October 2025), from her analysis of Kiro, Spec Kit and Tessl. Her three modes, verbatim: spec-first — "a well thought-out spec is written first, and then used in the AI-assisted development workflow for the task at hand"; spec-anchored — "the spec is kept even after the task is complete, to continue using it for evolution and maintenance of the respective feature"; spec-as-source — "the spec is the main source file over time, and only the spec is edited by the human, the human never touches the code." A terminology warning her own institution supplies: the Thoughtworks Radar notes that SDD terms are "used inconsistently … without shared definitions" across the industry — you will meet renamed variants (one circulating blog rebrands the third level "spec-as-truth"); anchor on Böckeler's definitions and cite them.

H3's angle on the modes is operational: each mode implies a different traceability contract. That is what the table adds beyond E8's strategic recommendation.

ModeThe spec is…Traceability means…Invalidation burden
spec-firstscaffolding — may not outlive the taskprovenance note: "this PR grew from that spec" — links die young by designnone (nothing durable to drift)
spec-anchoreda living contract beside the codethe full chain of §2, both directions, kept honest by §4's suspect gatethe real cost of the mode — budget for it
spec-as-sourcethe only human-edited artifactgenerative, not documentary: code is rebuilt from the spec (the compiler relationship — you don't trace a binary to its source, you recompile)none in theory; total spec-completeness in practice

Spec-as-source deserves respect as an idea and honesty as a status report. The ambition traces to Sean Grove's The New Code (the manifesto H1 dissects — intent as the durable asset, code as its "lossy projection"). Its boldest commercial bet was Tessl — which called its own approach "spec-centric"; spec-as-source is Böckeler's classification of it — where generated code files carried // GENERATED FROM SPEC - DO NOT EDIT and mapped 1:1 to spec files, backed by $125M of funding. The status report: in January 2026 Tessl repositioned toward an "agent enablement platform" — a versioned registry of agent skills ("Skills on Tessl") — and the pure spec-as-source framing is now its history rather than its headline (point-in-time; re-verify before quoting to a client). The most heavily funded attempt at code-as-disposable-byproduct pivoting away is itself evidence about how far the mode is from production reality.

The principled objection was stated by Kent Beck (LinkedIn, December 2025), on descriptions of SDD that require the whole spec before implementation: this "encodes the (to me bizarre) assumption that you aren't going to learn anything during implementation that would change the specification." That is precisely the assumption spec-as-source hard-codes and spec-anchored relaxes: an anchored spec is allowed to be wrong and get corrected by implementation learning — the suspect gate (§4) plus H4's triage are the machinery of that correction. Hence the recommendation E8 already made and H3 prices out: spec-anchored for a brownfield client — with eyes open that "anchored" is not a free lunch but a commitment to the invalidation discipline of §4. (The tool field keeps moving: the Radar now also lists OpenSpec, a fourth workflow built around change proposals — propose → apply → archive — rather than feature folders. Point-in-time.)

6Provenance — who wrote this code?

Traceability has a second axis the heritage tools never needed: not "which requirement does this serve?" but "who — or what — wrote it?" Review policy (E6's autonomy levels), incident forensics, licensing and emerging regulation all hinge on knowing whether a change was human, assisted, or delegated — and that fact, like the requirement link, must be stamped at creation or it is gone.

The evidence that it is gone by default is now quantified. The multi-method agent census (Khosravani & Mockus, arXiv:2606.24429, June 2026) mined ~180 million repositories for agent activity and found that no single detection method captures more than a fraction of it: bot-account detection alone recovered just 3.3% (28,154) of Claude Code's actual commits — a 30× undercount against the 850,157 commits identified in one snapshot when four trace types are combined (bot accounts, commit-message signatures, author-name suffixes, and config-files-only "silent agents" like Copilot or Cursor, which leave no commit fingerprint at all). The census volumes — Claude Code 886k commits across 17,295 projects, Replit 315k, Google Jules 216k, Devin 98k in its late-2025→early-2026 window — matter less than the method finding: if provenance is not stamped deliberately, it cannot be reconstructed later, even by researchers trying hard. Strong independent evidence, and the provenance mirror of §0's Jama story.

What stamping looks like is currently a convention debate, not a standard (practitioner synthesis, point-in-time). One camp uses git's Co-authored-by: trailer — which forges render as co-authorship; the counter-argument runs that co-authorship implies shared accountability and "a model can't hold accountability", so the Linux-kernel-style Assisted-by: <agent>:<model-version> trailer is more honest, keeping the human's Signed-off-by as the accountability line. The debate has a cautionary tale: Microsoft made VS Code append a Copilot co-author trailer by default and reverted it around May 2026 after backlash (it fired even with AI assistance disabled). GitHub's own coding agent models the middle ground: Copilot-authored PRs are searchable by author, while release notes credit the human who asked for the work — the agent is part of the provenance, the human remains responsible for whether it ships. Regulation is converging on the same demand from above: the EU AI Act's technical-documentation duty (Article 11) requires "traceability throughout the system's lifetime", with high-risk obligations enforceable from 2 August 2026 — though note the popular reading that maps this onto which model generated which code under which spec is so far a vendor gloss on the statute, not its text; verify against the Act before citing it to a client.

Policy beats archaeology

Pick one trailer convention, write it into CLAUDE.md, and enforce its presence with a CI grep (E5). Record agent, model version and — where the harness supports it — a session or task ID, which links the commit back to the contract that authorized it (H1) and the autonomy level it ran at (E6). Three lines of convention at write time versus a research project at audit time: the multi-method census is the measurement of what the research project costs.

7A minimal ID scheme for a brownfield client

Everything above compresses into one deployable scheme. It is our synthesis — assembled from the heritage mechanisms (§1), Kiro's criterion-level idea (§2), the structural-links argument (§3) and the provenance policy (§6) — not an industry standard, and we present it to clients as such.

The scheme — five moves
  • 1 · Registry. specs/ in the repo; one markdown file per requirement cluster, named REQ-012-discount-codes.md; changed only by PR (M4 durable tier)
  • 2 · Contract. IDs live in the contract's components E and F (H1): context links trace backwards, DoD demands the forward chain
  • 3 · Stamping. CLAUDE.md instructs the agent: branch req/012-*, trailer Refs: REQ-012, provenance trailer (§6), tests named test_req012_*
  • 4 · Gates. Two CI greps: PR must carry a REQ id; the suspect rule of §4
  • 5 · Bootstrap. one reviewed inference pass over legacy hot spots (§3), then discipline only
Deliberately skipped — until scale demands
  • RTM/ALM tooling — the registry is the matrix, greppable
  • continuous ML trace recovery — probabilistic answers to a question the scheme answers deterministically
  • per-line provenance — commit granularity suffices outside regulated code
  • the threshold: ID discipline + grep carries you to hundreds of specs; buy tooling when the grep stops fitting in a head

That last bullet answers the topic's open question — how does traceability scale to thousands of agent-generated artifacts? — with a claim we hold with medium confidence: discipline scales further than intuition suggests, because the marginal cost of stamping is zero for an agent (§3's inversion), and the reading side stays cheap as long as links are text in the repo rather than rows in a silo. The genuine scaling wall is human review capacity for suspect flags — which is E7's review-bottleneck finding wearing a different hat, and it is managed the same way: tighten what triggers a flag, not what gets recorded.

8How H3 connects the framework

TopicThe connection
H1 · contractcomponents E (context links) and F (DoD) are the chain's anchor points; T0–T2 sizing decides how much chain a task carries
H2 · EARS/Gherkinnumbered EARS criteria are what the IDs point at — Kiro's _Requirements: 1.2_ names an EARS line; a Gherkin scenario is the criterion's executable end of the link
H4 · feedback loopthe suspect flag (§4) is the trigger; H4's triage — code wrong, requirement wrong, or learning? — is the handler
E4 · verificationre-verification after change needs to know which tests answer for a requirement — that is a trace link, not a convention
E5 · governanceevery rule here is advisory prose made deterministic: the REQ-id grep, the suspect gate, the provenance-trailer check
E6 · autonomyprovenance records which autonomy level produced a change — the audit trail that makes L3 delegation defensible
E8 · SDDE8 recommends spec-anchored strategically; H3 prices it operationally (the invalidation burden is the mode's real cost)
M4 · information architecturespecs in the durable tier make the chain reviewable in the same diff as code; links are structure, not metadata in a silo
M5 · brownfieldthe bootstrap pass (§3) runs over the change heatmap; characterization tests inherit REQ ids as they are ratified

9Takeaways for our engagement

Assess
  • can anyone answer "which requirement does this file serve?" — for human code? for agent code?
  • does any ID survive from tracker to PR today, or do links die at the first hop?
  • where do specs live — repo, wiki, nowhere — and who notices when code drifts from them?
  • is agent-authored work distinguishable in git history at all?
Build (feeds E7 rollout)
  • the five-move ID scheme (§7), advisory first, deterministic once trusted
  • the suspect gate as a PR label wired to H4's triage ritual
  • one provenance trailer convention in CLAUDE.md + CI
  • a metric for E7: % of merged PRs carrying a valid REQ id — the traceability coverage curve
Three sentences to remember H3 by

At agent speed the link between requirement and code is the only memory of intent, so it must be written at creation — structural IDs that travel requirement → spec → task → code → test → PR — because reconstructing links afterwards is probabilistic (~60–85%) while stamping them is deterministic and costs an agent nothing. Change management is the heritage suspect flag rebuilt on git: touch code without its spec and the PR gets flagged, feeding the drift triage. Of Böckeler's three spec modes, recommend spec-anchored and budget honestly for its invalidation discipline; treat spec-as-source as a horizon (its boldest bet, Tessl, pivoted away in January 2026), and stamp provenance now — the multi-method agent census shows unstamped agent authorship is unrecoverable even by researchers trying hard.

10Further reading

Birgitta Böckeler — Understanding Spec-Driven-Development: Kiro, spec-kit, and Tessl
martinfowler.com · October 2025

The spec-first / spec-anchored / spec-as-source framing this section builds on, with tool-by-tool analysis. The definitional anchor in a field the Radar itself calls terminologically inconsistent.

Gotel & Finkelstein — An Analysis of the Requirements Traceability Problem
ICRE '94

The founding problem statement (100+ practitioners; pre- vs post-requirements traceability). Thirty years of prior art in twelve pages — read it to avoid reinventing vocabulary.

Khosravani & Mockus — the multi-method census of AI coding agents in open source
arXiv:2606.24429 · June 2026

180M repos; bot-detection alone recovers 3.3% of Claude Code activity (30× undercount). The quantified case for stamping provenance at write time.

Schlathölter — ReqToCode: traceability as a structural property of the codebase
arXiv:2603.13999 · March 2026

The citable version of §3's argument: embedded requirement IDs persist under AI modification where inferred links degrade.

Kiro spec docs & GitHub Spec Kit
kiro.dev/docs/specs · github.com/github/spec-kit

The two live implementations of the forward chain at different resolutions — criterion-level vs feature-folder-level. Both point-in-time; re-verify versions.

Jama Software — suspect tracking & the SDD traceability-audit story
jamasoftware.com · vendor

The suspect-link mechanism our §4 gate borrows, and the May-2026 audit anecdote — useful narrative, vendor incentive, no numbers.

Report 2 (traceability & feedback) + topics H1, H2, H4
this workbook — module M3

The contract whose components carry the IDs, the criteria the IDs point at, and the triage loop the suspect flag feeds.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (H3-traceability-spec-modes-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are H1 (the contract components that carry the IDs), H2 (the criteria the links point at), H4 (drift triage), E5 (deterministic gates), E8 (the spec-anchored recommendation) and M4 (specs as durable artifacts).

H4 · DEEP DIVE

Feedback Loop & Handoff Anti-Patterns — When Implementation Contradicts the Contract

0Why it matters

Module M3 built the forward path of delegation: H1 the contract, H2 its machine-verifiable criteria, H3 the chain of IDs that keeps the artifacts connected. H4 is the return path — what happens when the implementation contradicts the contract, when a criterion turns out to be impossible, ambiguous or wrong. A contract with no return channel is not a contract; it is a memo thrown over a wall. And the data says the wall is real: when requirements are ambiguous, more than 60% of code-LLM responses generate code anyway rather than ask a clarifying question (HumanEvalComm, ACM TOSEM 2025), and roughly a quarter of agent-authored fix PRs in the wild end up closed without merging — the loop failing at its last hop (arXiv:2602.00164). The feedback loop is where a handoff process either becomes self-correcting or quietly rots.

There is also an asymmetry worth naming up front. The engineering layer is structurally the best-placed detector of bad requirements in the whole SDLC: implementation is where vagueness becomes a concrete fork in the road ("should the discount apply before or after tax?" cannot be deferred once you are writing the line). Agents hit those forks earlier, faster and in greater volume than any human team ever did — the question is whether the discovery travels back to the requirement's owner or dies in a session log. M2 ended with the PM as editor and curator (P4); H4 supplies the mechanism that keeps feeding that editor the one input only implementation can produce: evidence about the requirements themselves.

The division of labour with E7

E7's anti-pattern catalogue (AP1–AP6) covers rollout failures — autonomy without guardrails, review bottlenecks, perception-driven decisions. H4's catalogue covers handoff failures: the four ways the contract itself goes wrong between product and engineering. Same discipline — every named failure maps to a specific control — different layer of the stack.

1The silence is the default — agents don't ask

The naïve assumption behind throw-over-the-wall delegation is that an agent facing a broken requirement will stop and say so. Measured behaviour says otherwise, consistently, across independent benchmarks. HumanEvalComm (Wu et al., ACM TOSEM 2025, arXiv:2406.00215) rewrote 164 HumanEval problems into 762 variants with injected ambiguity, inconsistency or missing information: over 60% of code-LLM responses produced code instead of a clarifying question. "Learning to Ask" (Wang et al., EMNLP 2025) found the same reflex in tool-using agents — faced with an instruction missing a required argument, models hallucinate the missing value rather than ask, a behaviour the authors trace to next-token training itself. And MAST, the UC Berkeley-led failure taxonomy of multi-agent systems (arXiv:2503.13657, preprint), puts a number on it in the wild: "fail to ask for clarification" alone accounts for roughly 12% of observed failures.

The sharpest result is the newest. HiL-Bench (Scale AI, arXiv:2604.09408, May 2026 — preprint, re-verify) gives agents an explicit ask_human() tool and withholds one piece of task-critical information: on software-engineering tasks, pass rates (Pass@3) that sit at 67–88% with full information collapse to 1–9% when success requires recognising the gap and asking. On those same tasks even the best models detected fewer than half of the blockers — the dominant failure signature is confident execution on wrong beliefs. The capability gap is not writing code; it is knowing when not to. The cost of the silence is also quantified: on ambiguous requirements, Pass@1 drops by ~7.2 percentage points on average and by 31 for the worst ambiguity types, and ambiguity nearly doubles a model's internal self-contradiction rate (Orchid benchmark, arXiv:2604.21505 — preprint, re-verify). A newer interactive line measures the questions themselves and finds detection wildly uneven by defect class — some ambiguity types get caught nearly every time, others barely at all (ClarifyCodeBench, arXiv:2607.00711 — preprint).

What the benchmarks establish
  • default behaviour is to proceed, not ask — >60% code-instead-of-question on ambiguous specs (TOSEM 2025)
  • the gap is judgment, not skill: 67–88% → 1–9% when asking is required (HiL-Bench, preprint)
  • detection is uneven by defect class — some ambiguity types are barely caught (ClarifyCodeBench, preprint)
  • guided clarification measurably pays: +11.52% relative from targeted questions (ClarifyGPT, H2)
What follows for the harness
  • never rely on the agent volunteering doubt — instruct it to stop on ambiguity (§5, §8)
  • move contradiction-detection upstream into tooling (§2)
  • make the escalation path cheaper than the guess: a named channel, a label, an owner (§5)
  • treat "no questions asked" on a vague contract as a smell, not a compliment

One caution for client conversations: this is a fast-moving capability, and vendors are attacking it directly — Devin ships confidence-gated question-asking (§5), and 2026 work shows help-seeking is trainable. The load-bearing claim is not "agents will never ask"; it is that asking is not the default, so a process that assumes it is has no floor under it. Point-in-time, mid-2026.

2The agent as detector — verification moves upstream

The same engineering layer that stays silent by default can be made loud by design — and the most concrete existing exhibit is Kiro's Requirements Analysis (announced May 12, 2026, Kiro Applied Science). It runs on the spec before any code is generated, and it is genuinely neurosymbolic rather than "more LLM": in the team's own words, "the analysis automatically encodes the specification into formal logic using LLMs and uses SMT solvers to generate the findings." The pipeline has three stages — refinement rewrites thesis-level sentences into testable ones (emitting new EARS acceptance criteria, the H2 format); auto-formalization translates them into a formal model; logical analysis then finds contradictions and gaps and enumerates concrete counter-examples. This is E4's verification-first principle applied one artifact earlier: the requirement itself gets a failing test.

The headline number travels with a caveat: AWS reports — per The New Stack's coverage, not Kiro's own post — that in internal testing across 35 projects with 1,400+ acceptance criteria, roughly 60% of first-draft requirements needed refinement before they could be reliably implemented. Unaudited vendor internal testing; cite the mechanism as fact and the percentage as a vendor claim. The direction, though, matches the independent research: ClarifyGPT (H2) showed LLMs can profitably interrogate ambiguous prompts (+11.52% relative), and §1's benchmarks show unaided detection is unreliable and uneven by defect class — which is why delegating contradiction-hunting to an SMT solver rather than to the agent's judgment is the architecturally honest move (E5's advisory→deterministic principle, applied to requirements).

The old software-engineering intuition behind all this — defects are cheapest to fix at requirements time — is directionally sound but numerically folkloric: the oft-quoted 100:1 cost-escalation ratio traces, per Laurent Bossavit's The Leprechauns of Software Engineering, to secondary sources citing secondary sources, with reported ratios ranging from ~5:1 to ~100:1. Use the direction, never the multiplier. What has actually changed since Boehm's era is the cost side: a requirements-level check used to mean a review meeting; now it is a solver run that costs cents and minutes. Cheap upstream verification, not a scary multiplier, is the honest argument.

3Drift triage — the three-way question

When implementation and contract disagree — an agent reports an impossible criterion, a suspect label lands (H3's gate), a red test contradicts the spec — the process needs one disciplined move before any fix: deciding which artifact is wrong. The cleanest formulation is Leonid Bugaev's (reqproof blog, "Source of truth: Code, Spec, or Requirement?", May 2026 — a practitioner building a traceability product, so note the commercial interest in the thesis): "is the code wrong, is the requirement wrong, or did we learn something?" — and, crucially, "until that is resolved, something is broken in the process." The question is the whole protocol in one line: every drift event has exactly three exits, each with a different owner and a different artifact to change.

The three exits, operationally. Code wrong — the requirement stands; the criterion becomes or remains a failing test (E4) and the fix is an engineering matter; no product decision needed. Requirement wrong — the code faithfully implements a mistake; the fix is a spec PR owned by the PM (P4's editor role), with the criteria rewritten in H2's formats and every artifact tracing to the requirement flipped suspect (H3). We learned something — the most common and least honoured exit: neither artifact was "wrong" at authoring time, implementation produced knowledge nobody had. This is Kent Beck's point against whole-spec-first development — it "encodes the (to me bizarre) assumption that you aren't going to learn anything during implementation that would change the specification" (Dec 2025). A process that treats every learning as someone's error will hide its learnings; the spec gets updated, the reason gets logged, nobody gets blamed.

Two properties make triage work at agent speed. It must be fast — the queue grows at generation speed, so the decision is a ten-minute conversation at most, default weekly ritual plus an async lane for blockers (§8). And it must be owned — the single most common failure is nobody having the authority to say "the requirement is wrong": engineers patch the code to match a broken spec, or quietly patch the spec to match the code, and both artifacts stay plausible while the drift compounds. Until the question is answered, Bugaev's line applies verbatim: something is broken in the process, not merely in the artifacts.

4The anti-pattern catalogue

Report 2's four handoff anti-patterns, each with its detection smell and its control. The symmetry worth teaching: the first two are the two ways to get the contract wrong (too much, too little), the second two are the two ways to lose the loop (spec abandoned, signal never returned).

HAP1

Over-specification

controlled by H1 sizing · P3

The spec outgrows the code and the review burden is relocated, not removed — the tech lead now reviews markdown instead of diffs. The mechanism is measurable: AI-generated review commentary runs ~29.6 tokens per line of code against a human baseline of 4.1 (arXiv:2603.15911) — density, not quality, and someone must read it. Smells: contracts routinely above H1's 10–30 lines; spec-writing time exceeding implementation time on T0/T1 tasks; PMs "spending more time on markdown than on the product." Control: H1's sizing tiers (T0 micro-tasks need an issue ID, not a contract), P3's decomposition heuristic, and Fowler's skeptical question as the standing review filter: would you rather review this pile of markdown, or the code?

HAP2

Under-specification

controlled by H1/H2 · §1's instruction rule

The contract leaves the fork unresolved, and §1 says what happens next: the agent picks a branch silently and executes it with confidence. On ambiguous requirements Pass@1 drops ~7 points on average and 30+ for the worst ambiguity types (Orchid, preprint); practitioners' war stories are all variations of a quick task ballooning into hours of thrash as the agent rewrites around a misread intent (widely reported; no pinnable canonical source — we cite it as an unattributed pattern, not a statistic). Smells: "confidently wrong" green PRs solving a different problem; follow-up-question count rising after merge instead of before work. Control: intent and non-goals written by humans (H1's two irreplaceable components), EARS-formatted criteria (H2), and the standing stop-on-ambiguity instruction from §5.

HAP3

Stale spec — silent drift

controlled by H3's suspect gate

The spec is written, the feature ships, the spec is never touched again — and six weeks later an agent faithfully implements against a document describing a system that no longer exists. The 2026 SDD literature names this "silent spec-code drift": code evolves, the specification does not, and the divergence stays invisible until it is expensive. No mainstream SDD tool detects it today (H3 §4 — Kiro's Sync Files checks task completion, not drift; point-in-time, mid-2026). Smells: spec directories with no commits since the feature merged; agents citing spec lines that contradict current code. Control: H3's git-native suspect gate — a CI grep, not a product — plus M4's rule that specs are durable artifacts riding the same review as code.

HAP4

Throw over the wall

controlled by §5's return channel

The contract is handed off and nobody is listening for the return signal. Downstream, the loop's failure is visible in the merge data: in a study of 8,106 agent-authored fix PRs, 26.1% were closed unmerged — with test failures (18.1%) and incorrect or incomplete fixes (15.3%) leading the study's qualitative sample of closures — "syntactically plausible fixes alone are often insufficient for successful integration" (arXiv:2602.00164, preprint). The mirror finding: across 33,596 agent PRs, reviewer engagement was the strongest merge predictor, and success concentrated in "actionable review loops" where concrete human feedback let the agent converge — iteration volume alone did not (arXiv:2602.19441, MSR 2026). The wall fails in both directions: unreviewed agent output dies, and agent-discovered requirement bugs drown in session logs. Control: the return channel, formalized — §5.

5The return channel — from session log to owned artifact

The topic's open question — how does an agent-discovered ambiguity reach the PM without a human courier? — now has partial product answers, worth surveying precisely because they disagree (all point-in-time, mid-2026; re-verify). Devin is the most structured: since the 2.1 release it self-assesses confidence (🟢🟡🔴), and "when Devin doesn't have 🟢 confidence, it will ask clarifying questions" and wait for approval before proceeding — Cognition reports green-confidence tasks are twice as likely to end in a merged PR as red (vendor-reported). Its docs also define an escalation ladder for blockers: investigate, fix if adjacent, note and continue, or escalate with an explanation. GitHub Copilot's coding agent and OpenAI's Codex both concentrate clarification in their planning modes — Copilot's plan step emits "open questions for clarification", Codex's plan mode "lets Codex gather context, ask clarifying questions" — but in autonomous execution both largely proceed without asking, and both trackers carry open requests from users asking for ask-by-default. The industry state, compressed: asking exists, but mostly as a mode you must enter, not a reflex you can rely on — which returns the burden to the harness.

SYNTHESIS

A minimal return-channel protocol

ours — flag it as such to the client

Four rules, tool-agnostic, cheap enough for week one. (1) A standing instruction in the agent steering file (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md): on a contradictory, impossible or two-way-interpretable criterion, stop and file a question — never pick a branch silently. §1's evidence says this must be instructed; the same evidence (and H3's ID-discipline experience) says instructed agents comply deterministically. (2) A structured landing place: the question is an artifact, not a chat line — an issue comment or PR label (spec-question, carrying the REQ-ID from H3's chain) that routes to the requirement's owner, so it survives the session that raised it. (3) A named owner and an SLA: every requirement has exactly one person entitled to answer "the requirement is wrong" (P4's editor); blockers get an async answer, the rest queue for the weekly triage (§3). (4) The answer lands in the artifact, not the chat: the resolution is a spec edit, a criterion rewrite or a logged learning — §6's rule that anything decided in a disposable channel gets promoted to a durable one, or it never happened.

Where should the learning exits of triage land? The emerging convention worth watching is the Agent Decision Record — ADRs extended to decisions an agent made autonomously (library choice, pattern choice, interpretation of an ambiguous criterion), so agent reasoning gets the same traceability as human architecture decisions; early-stage, a proposed convention rather than a standard, but it slots exactly into the gap. The division of labour it implies is clean: the steering file says what must never happen; the decision record says what was decided and why; the spec says what must be true. Everything else — the session transcript, the chat thread — is M4's disposable tier, and treating it as a system of record is how ambiguities drown.

6Multi-agent pipelines — drift compounds

Everything above assumed one agent and one contract. Chain agents — a planner feeding an implementer feeding a reviewer — and the feedback problem multiplies, because each hop is a fresh interpretation with no human gate between hops. The best empirical map is MAST (arXiv:2503.13657, preprint — a UC Berkeley-led study of 200+ traces across 7 frameworks, 14 failure modes): roughly 42% of failures are specification issues, 37% inter-agent misalignment, 21% weak verification (v2 figures; version-dependent, cite rounded). Read against M3, that distribution is the whole module restated as a failure budget: bad contracts, broken handoffs between workers, missing gates. Structural work on error propagation adds that failures at hub nodes of an agent graph infect most of the pipeline while leaf failures stay local (arXiv:2603.04474 — measured with injected faults, so cite it for topology, not for natural drift rates).

The practitioner conclusion has not changed since Cognition's "Don't Build Multi-Agents" (E3): keep writes single-threaded, let other agents contribute intelligence rather than concurrent action. H4 adds the contract-shaped corollary: every agent→agent handoff inherits the full H1 discipline — explicit contract, machine-verifiable criteria, IDs, and a return path — because the receiving agent is precisely the "new team member with amnesia" P3 planned for, minus the ability to bump into you at the coffee machine. If a pipeline cannot afford a human triage gate between hops, it must at least afford deterministic verification there (E4/E5); interpretive drift plus seemingly-coherent output is the failure mode with no smell until the end.

7Loop-health metrics — is the feedback loop working?

The loop is invisible in throughput metrics — you inspect it through rework. The anchor is DORA's Rework Rate, the fifth software-delivery metric (introduced in the 2024 report, carried into the 2025 benchmarks): the share of deployments that are unplanned fixes for something that did not work the first time — added precisely because AI-accelerated throughput can look healthy while instability accumulates underneath (strong independent industry research). Around it, three supporting signals, each honestly labelled. GitClear — a code-analytics vendor — reports churn (code revised within two weeks) rising from 3.1% to a projected 5.7% between 2020 and 2024 and duplicated blocks rising ~8×; correlational, no control group, and churn conflates healthy iteration with defect rework, but the direction rhymes with DORA's concern (E7 cites the same data). New Relic's commissioned survey (Hanover Research, June 2026; n=200 US decision-makers — self-reported perception, not telemetry) captures the loop failing in one contradiction: 94% of leaders rate AI code higher-quality at review time, yet 78% report more production incidents and 74% say at least a quarter of AI code needed significant rework within a year — code that grades well at the gate and fails downstream is exactly what a missing feedback loop looks like from above. And the merge-outcome studies from §4 (26.1% of fix PRs closed unmerged; reviewer engagement the strongest merge predictor) give the PR-level view of the same health check.

Track from day 0 (feeds E7's baseline & T2's pilot)
  • rework rate (DORA definition; fifth metric since 2024) — the headline loop metric
  • agent-PR outcomes: merged / closed-unmerged / abandoned, per agent and per team
  • spec-question count and time-to-answer (the §5 channel's own health)
  • spec-change frequency after merge — the "we learned" exits actually landing
Read them together, not alone
  • zero spec-questions on a vague backlog = §1's silence, not clarity
  • rework up + churn up + review praise up = the New Relic contradiction — audit the gate (E4)
  • high closed-unmerged share = the wall (HAP4), not "bad agent"
  • every number here is a trend instrument, not a target — Goodhart applies (E7)

8Five moves for a brownfield client

The loop is the cheapest part of M3 to install — it is mostly agreements, labels and one CI rule. In order:

Move 1 — name the owners. Every requirement gets exactly one person entitled to declare it wrong (usually the PM, P4). Publish the mapping; triage without an owner is a discussion, not a decision.

Move 2 — instruct the agent to stop and ask. One paragraph in the steering file: on contradiction, impossibility or two-way interpretation, file a spec-question with the REQ-ID and stop. This converts §1's measured silence into a deterministic behaviour — the same inversion H3 found for ID discipline: what humans find effortful, an instructed agent does every time.

Move 3 — wire the suspect gate. H3's two CI greps, so drift surfaces as a PR label while the diff is open. The gate is the loop's sensor; without it, triage only ever sees what the agent volunteers.

Move 4 — install the triage ritual. Fifteen minutes weekly over two queues (spec-question, suspect-spec), one agenda item per entry: code wrong, requirement wrong, or did we learn something? Async lane for blockers. The output is always an artifact change — spec PR, criterion rewrite, logged learning — never just a verbal answer.

Move 5 — baseline rework before the pilot. Rework rate, agent-PR merge outcomes, reopened tickets — captured before the first agent task (E7's discipline, T2's pilot design), so six weeks later "the loop works" is a measured claim, not a feeling.

9How H4 connects the framework

TopicRelationship to the feedback loop
H1 · contract anatomythe artifact the loop corrects; intent + non-goals are what triage re-reads first
H2 · EARS/Gherkin"requirement wrong" exits land as criterion rewrites in these formats
H3 · traceabilitythe suspect gate is the loop's sensor; REQ-IDs make the return signal routable
P2/P3/P4 · product layerupstream masters the loop feeds back into; the PM-as-editor is the triage authority
E3 · harness in practicerecurring spec-questions become steering-file rules — the loop that improves the harness itself
E4 · verification-first"code wrong" exits become failing tests; upstream, the requirement itself gets a checker (§2)
E5 · governancestop-on-ambiguity and the suspect gate are deterministic rules, not prose hopes
E7 · metricsrollout-level anti-pattern catalogue (AP1–AP6) beside H4's handoff-level one; rework metrics live here
E8 · SDD workflowthe loop is SDD's missing half: authoring flows forward, learning must flow back (Beck's objection)
M4 · information architectureanswers land in durable artifacts; session logs are disposable — promote or lose
T2 · pilot designloop-health metrics are pilot success criteria, baselined before day one

10Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Agents do not volunteer doubt — across independent benchmarks the default on an ambiguous or contradictory contract is confident silent execution, so the return channel must be instructed, structured and owned, never assumed. Every drift event has exactly three exits — code wrong, requirement wrong, or we learned something — and until someone with authority picks one, the process (not the artifact) is what's broken; the third exit is normal engineering, not failure, and a loop that punishes learning will hide it. The whole apparatus is cheap — a steering-file rule, a label with an owner, two CI greps, fifteen minutes a week — and its health is measurable from day 0 through DORA's rework rate and agent-PR merge outcomes, which is what turns "the handoff works" from a feeling into a number.

11Further reading

Leonid Bugaev — Source of truth: Code, Spec, or Requirement?
blog.reqproof.com · May 14, 2026

The triage question verbatim, and the line this chapter is built on: until it is resolved, "something is broken in the process." Author is building a traceability product — read with that interest in mind.

Wu et al. — HumanEvalComm: Benchmarking the Communication Competence of Code Generation
ACM TOSEM 2025 · arXiv:2406.00215

The peer-reviewed anchor for §1: 762 ambiguity-injected problems, >60% of responses code instead of asking. Communication Rate and Good Question Rate as measurable properties of a coding model.

Trinh et al. (Scale AI) — HiL-Bench: Do Agents Know When to Ask for Help?
arXiv:2604.09408 · May 2026 · preprint

The judgment gap made vivid: 67–88% pass (Pass@3) with full information, 1–9% when the agent must recognise a gap and ask; blocker recall under 50% on SWE tasks. Confident execution on wrong beliefs as the dominant failure signature.

Cemri, Pan, Yang et al. — Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail?
arXiv:2503.13657 · preprint · MAST taxonomy

14 failure modes, 3 categories over 200+ traces: ~42% specification issues, ~37% inter-agent misalignment, ~21% weak verification — M3 restated as a failure budget. Percentages vary by paper version; cite rounded.

Alam, Mondal & Roy — Why Are AI Agent Involved Pull Requests (Fix-Related) Remain Unmerged?
arXiv:2602.00164 · 2026 · 8,106 fix PRs, 5 agents

The loop failing at its last hop: 26.1% closed unmerged, with test failures and incorrect fixes leading — "syntactically plausible fixes alone are often insufficient for successful integration."

Nachuma & Zibran — When AI Teammates Meet Code Review
MSR 2026 · arXiv:2602.19441 · 33,596 PRs / 2,807 repos

Reviewer engagement as the strongest merge predictor; "actionable review loops" beat iteration volume — the empirical case that the human side of the loop is load-bearing.

Kiro — Requirements analysis: catching requirement bugs before they become code
kiro.dev/blog/deep-spec-analysis · May 12, 2026 · point-in-time

The neurosymbolic pipeline (LLM formalization + SMT solving) behind §2, from the team's applied scientists. The ~60%-needed-refinement figure is AWS internal testing reported via The New Stack — vendor claim, label it.

DORA — State of AI-assisted Software Development 2025
dora.dev/dora-report-2025

Rework Rate, the fifth delivery metric (introduced 2024) — unplanned deployments fixing what didn't work first time — catching the instability that AI-era throughput hides. The loop-health anchor for §7.

Laurent Bossavit — The Leprechauns of Software Engineering
leanpub.com/leprechauns · 2015

Why this chapter never quotes the 100:1 defect-cost ratio: the citation trail dissolves under inspection. The model for how to handle folklore numbers — keep the direction, drop the multiplier.

Report 2 — the handoff-contract research notes (this workbook)
module M3 research notes · the four anti-patterns

The original synthesis behind §4's catalogue and the module's framing of the engineering layer as product's best requirements detector.
