This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (H4-feedback-anti-patterns-deepdive.html). It completes the M3 deep-dive set. Its natural neighbours in the workbook are H1 (the contract the loop corrects), H2 (the formats the rewrites land in), H3 (the suspect gate that feeds triage), P4 (the PM-as-editor who owns the "requirement wrong" exit), E4/E5 (the deterministic machinery), E7 (the sibling anti-pattern catalogue and the metrics), E8 (the SDD workflow the loop completes), and M4 (where the answers must land to survive).

I1 · DEEP DIVE

Artifact Taxonomy — Durable / Derived / Disposable

0Why it matters

Module M4 asks how information survives contact with agents. This first chapter supplies the load-bearing distinction everything else in the module governs: what kind of artifact is this? Agents are document factories — every task can emit a plan, a progress log, a summary, generated docs, a scratch analysis — and a brownfield repo six months into agentic development contains more prose than some codebases contain code. Without a classification, all of it is just "documentation": nobody knows what may be edited, what must be regenerated, what can be deleted, and what the agent should trust. With a classification, every file has an owner, a change path and a lifetime — and I2's write-permissions have something concrete to attach to.

The baseline was already bad before agents arrived. Tan, Wagner & Treude measured documentation in the 1,000 most-starred GitHub projects: 28.9% contained at least one outdated code-element reference, stale for 4.7 years on average (EMSE 2023, arXiv:2212.01479) — with humans producing the text at human speed. Agentic development changes both multipliers at once: far more text gets produced, and — the genuinely new failure mode — the producer is also the consumer. An agent reads whatever is lying around in the repo and treats it as context; a stale plan or a drifted copy doesn't just embarrass the wiki, it steers the next generation. And volume itself has a cost even when nothing is wrong: Chroma's "context rot" study (Hong, Troynikov & Huber, July 2025) showed accuracy degrading non-uniformly across 18 frontier models as input grows, even on simple tasks. More surviving text is not more knowledge — past a point it is less.

What this chapter gives you
  • the three-category taxonomy: durable / derived / disposable (§1)
  • the discipline each category imposes — human gate, never-hand-edit, prune (§2–§4)
  • the SSOT boundary between product and engineering masters (§5)
  • the grey zones: agent-written docs, memory files, promotion paths (§6)
  • a first-week classification sweep for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • I2 — write-permissions per category, enforced with hooks, ADR lifecycle in full
  • I3 — measuring the drift that misclassification produces
  • I4 — memory systems in depth; poisoning and its defenses
  • I5 — keeping durables small enough to load (progressive disclosure)
  • I7 — reference-don't-copy mechanics across the SSOT boundary

1The triad — durable, derived, disposable

Flag: this taxonomy is our synthesis

Like the D1–D5 scale, the durable/derived/disposable triad is Report 3's original synthesis, not an industry standard — no external framework packages artifacts this way (checked 2026-07-15). It does have honest ancestors: Charity Majors' durable-vs-disposable split for code (by intended lifespan), records management's vital/important/useful classes (where "important = can be recreated if lost" is exactly our "derived"), and — strongest of all — the never-hand-edit discipline for generated files that the software industry has already encoded in tooling (§3). The three-bucket packaging is ours; present it as such.

The taxonomy classifies every artifact the development system produces or consumes by one question with three answers: if this file changed or vanished, what is the correct response? If the answer is "someone must decide, review and own the change" — it is durable. If the answer is "regenerate it from its master" — it is derived. If the answer is "nothing; it served its purpose" — it is disposable. Owner, change path and lifetime all follow from that one classification.

DURABLE

Sources of truth

owned · reviewed · human gate

The artifacts other artifacts are answerable to: the PRD, the spec (H1's contract), ADRs, the code, the tests, the steering files (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md), the design-token JSON (D4). A durable artifact can be wrong — which is precisely why it has a named owner and changes only through review. Losing one is a real loss; duplicating one creates a second truth that will drift (§5).

DERIVED

Regenerable projections

never hand-edit · regenerate

Anything produced from a master by a repeatable process: generated API docs, diagrams-from-code, compiled outputs, digests of product documents prepared for agent context, agent-written documentation of legacy code (§6). Derived artifacts are never edited in place — a change request against a derived artifact is really a change request against its master, followed by regeneration. Hand-editing one forks the truth: the edit either dies at the next regeneration or survives it and drifts.

DISPOSABLE

Working files

free to write · prune

The externalized thinking agentic work produces: plans, progress logs, session transcripts, scratch analyses, one-shot prototypes. Valuable during the run, dead weight after it. Disposables are the one category the agent writes freely — and the one category with a default fate of deletion. Anything in this pile worth keeping gets promoted deliberately (§6), never kept by inertia.

Three decision tests resolve most classification arguments in practice. The disappearance test: if the file vanished, would you restore it from memory and review (durable), rerun a generator (derived), or shrug (disposable)? The repair test: when it is wrong, is fixing it in place ever correct? Only for durables — a wrong derived artifact means a wrong master or a stale generation, and a wrong disposable means nothing. The trust test: does anything downstream — a human decision, an agent's context, a build — rely on this being current? If yes, it is either a durable or a derived artifact with a declared master; if it is neither, you have found an unowned copy, which is drift with a timestamp (I3).

2Durable — the human gate

Durable artifacts are the small set the whole system is answerable to, and their defining property is not importance but ownership: someone named can be wrong about them, and changes flow through review. The archetype predates agents by a decade — Michael Nygard's architecture decision record ("Documenting Architecture Decisions", Cognitect blog, November 2011): a short numbered document per significant decision, with an explicit lifecycle — proposed, accepted, and when a later decision changes course, deprecated or superseded. The crucial move is that an accepted ADR is never edited; change means a new ADR that supersedes the old one. The decision history stays readable and trustworthy — for the agent as much as for the human, because an agent that greps the ADR set sees not just what holds but what used to hold and why it changed. I2 takes the lifecycle and its enforcement from here.

Agentic development adds one genuinely new durable class: agent-facing configuration. CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md and their siblings are small files with outsized blast radius — they steer every future generation, so an error in one compounds in a way an error in a README never did. The AGENTS.md convention illustrates how quickly this class hardened: it began as a multi-vendor coalition (OpenAI Codex, Sourcegraph Amp, Google Jules, Cursor, Factory), is now stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation, and self-reports adoption in over 60,000 repositories with 30+ tools reading it natively (as of mid-2026 — point-in-time, re-verify). A file read by every agent on every task is a source of truth by any definition; treat its edits like schema migrations, not like prose. E3's harness discipline and I5's gardening rules both assume this classification.

The spec-driven toolchain is converging on the same classification from the vendor side: durables belong in the repo, versioned next to the code they govern. Kiro stores each feature's spec as a folder under .kiro/specs/ — a requirements.md in EARS notation (H2's format), a design.md, a tasks.md — with the instruction to "store specs directly in your project repository alongside the code they describe"; GitHub's Spec Kit lays numbered feature folders under specs/ (spec.md, plan.md, tasks.md, contracts) beneath a constitution of "non-negotiable principles" at .specify/memory/constitution.md. Even a tool's own config directory encodes the taxonomy: Claude Code documents settings.json as committed and team-shared while settings.local.json is personal and git-ignored — one file family, deliberately split by change path and audience. Tool layouts are point-in-time (mid-2026); the direction — specs as versioned, repo-resident durables — is the stable part.

Durable ≠ large, durable ≠ old

A 40-line CLAUDE.md is durable; a 4,000-line generated API reference is not. The category is about the change path — whether edits require an owner's judgment — not about size, age or format. This is also why "everything is durable" is a real anti-pattern: declaring every document a source of truth relocates the review burden onto all of them at once, and the team responds by reviewing none (the same overload mechanism as H4's over-specification).

3Derived — never hand-edit, and the industry already agrees

The derived category comes with exactly one rule — never edit the projection; change the master and regenerate — and the strongest evidence for the rule is that the software industry converged on it long before agents. Go specifies a canonical machine-readable header for generated files, down to a regular expression in the toolchain docs: ^// Code generated .* DO NOT EDIT\.$ — and the ecosystem's generators (protoc-gen-go, stringer) emit it. GitHub's linguist lets a repo mark files linguist-generated in .gitattributes, with two effects that encode the whole philosophy: the files are excluded from language statistics and hidden by default in pull-request diffs. Read that second effect carefully — the platform deliberately steers review attention away from derived artifacts, because the reviewable object is the master and the generator, not their output.

That reviewing insight transfers directly to agentic artifacts. A digest of the PRD prepared for agent context, a diagram generated from the architecture, API docs generated from code — none of these should consume line-by-line review, and none should ever accept a hand fix, however urgent. The failure mode is always the same: the hand edit either dies silently at the next regeneration (the fix evaporates) or the regeneration stops being run so the edit survives (the artifact quietly becomes an unowned durable — the worst object in the taxonomy, trusted and maintained by nobody). If a derived artifact keeps needing manual corrections, that is not an editing problem; it is a signal that either its master is wrong or its generator is — fix those, or reclassify the artifact as durable and give it an owner honestly.

The lockfile nuance — versioned is not the axis

npm's docs say package-lock.json "is intended to be committed into source repositories" so that teammates, deployments and CI "install exactly the same dependencies" — yarn and pnpm prescribe the same. A fully regenerable artifact, deliberately versioned: derived-but-committed. The lesson is that git-tracked vs not is orthogonal to the taxonomy. The axis is the change path: nobody hand-edits a lockfile; it changes only as a by-product of changing the master (package.json) and rerunning the tool. Committing derived artifacts is fine — often necessary for reproducibility — as long as the no-hand-edit rule survives the commit.

The category carries one more obligation: a derived artifact must have a consumer, because regeneration is a standing cost paid on every master change. The cautionary exhibit is llms.txt — a derived digest of a website prepared for machine readers, adopted on faith. Ahrefs measured 137,210 domains carrying one: 97% of the files received zero traffic in May 2026, and of the requests that did arrive, just 1.1% came from AI retrieval bots — for AI visibility, "an llms.txt file is largely decoration" (Linehan & Guan, Ahrefs, June 2026; I7 reaches the same verdict from the integration side). A projection nobody loads is not information architecture; it is maintenance debt with extra steps. The classification sweep (§7) therefore records two names per derived artifact: its master and its consumer.

4Disposable — the category agents made necessary

Before agents, scratch thinking lived in editors, terminals and heads, and vanished on its own. Agents externalize it: the plan, the progress log, the running commentary all become files, because files are how an agent thinks past its context window. Anthropic's long-running-harness work shows the pattern at its most deliberate: an initializer agent writes an init.sh, makes an initial commit, and expands the prompt into a structured JSON feature list whose entries carry category / description / steps / passes: false — with the agent allowed to flip passes as features verify; alongside it, a claude-progress.txt is read at session start and updated at session end. Even the format choice is a taxonomy decision: JSON over Markdown because, in Anthropic's words, "the model is less likely to inappropriately change or overwrite JSON files compared to Markdown files" — structure as a soft write-permission (E3 covers the harness itself; I2 makes the permission hard).

What the harness post does not supply is an afterlife policy — its bar is a clean state in git terms (committed, mergeable), and the explicit pruning guidance lives in Anthropic's memory-tool docs instead: rename or delete files that are no longer relevant, and "do not create new files unless necessary." The gap matters, because the default fate of working files in the wild is accumulation: PLAN-v2-final.md next to PLAN-v3-actually-final.md in the repo root, session summaries nobody re-read, TODO files three sprints stale. The sprawl is measured behavior, not caricature — "the default behavior for models as of writing this in December 2025 is to create capitalized Markdown files at the repository root" (Onur Solmaz, whose SimpleDoc convention is the neatest practitioner fix: agent-written docs land in docs/ with a YYYY-MM-DD- prefix, lowercase names and frontmatter naming the author, while only timeless files — README, AGENTS.md — keep root-level capitals, so a shouting filename becomes a visible claim to durable status). This is not an aesthetic problem. Agents read what is lying around; every stale plan is a candidate context input, and the context-rot result (§0) says volume alone degrades performance — before you account for the stale plan being wrong. Pruning disposables is context hygiene, not tidiness — and not a new discipline either: records management has ended documents' lives on schedule for decades (retention schedules, disposition); the taxonomy imports the practice without the paperwork.

A disposable policy that works
  • a named home — a scratch/progress directory, gitignored or clearly conventioned, never the repo root
  • a TTL — end of task, end of sprint; expiry is the default, keeping is the decision
  • machine-readable working state (JSON) over prose where the agent must update it
  • promotion is explicit: worth keeping → validated, relocated, owned (§6)
Smells that the policy is missing
  • plans and summaries accumulating in the repo root with version suffixes
  • agents citing last month's plan as if it were the spec
  • nobody can say which progress file is current without asking
  • "we keep everything, storage is cheap" — storage is; context isn't

5The SSOT boundary — two mastering systems, one taxonomy

Single source of truth is an information-systems term of art: every element mastered — edited — in exactly one place. The taxonomy is how SSOT becomes operational per artifact, but a real organization complicates it with a structural fact: product durables and engineering durables live in different mastering systems. The PRD, the roadmap and the tickets are mastered in Notion, Confluence or Jira; the spec, the code, the tests and the steering files are mastered in git. Neither side is wrong — but the boundary between them is where duplication pressure concentrates, because the agents work in the repo while half the truth they need lives outside it.

Hence the canonical M4 anti-pattern: the PM pastes the current PRD into CLAUDE.md so the agent "always has it." Every instinct behind the move is sound — the agent does need product context — and the result is still a fork: the pasted copy is a durable artifact's projection with no regeneration path, it will drift the day the PRD next changes, and the agent will keep trusting it precisely because it sits in the most-trusted file in the repo. The taxonomy names the two correct bridges. Either a pointer — the agent fetches the PRD at need across the boundary (I7's MCP integration), paying freshness's token cost — or a labelled derived digest: a summarized excerpt carrying its source and generation date, regenerated from the master on change, never hand-maintained. What separates a digest from a pasted copy is exactly the derived discipline of §3: a declared master, a repeatable projection, and no hand edits.

The boundary also explains why "just put everything in git" and "just put everything in Notion" both fail: each side's durables need their native review gate (PRs for engineering artifacts, the product tool's ownership model for product artifacts), and each side's practitioners live where their masters are. The realistic posture for a brownfield client is two mastering systems, an explicit list of which durables live where (§7), and derived digests or pointers — never copies — crossing the line. I7 supplies the mechanics: ID schemes across the boundary, MCP as the integration layer, and the reference-don't-copy rule with its context-rot rationale.

6Grey zones — memory, agent-written docs, and the promotion path

Three artifact families genuinely strain the triad, and all three resolve through the same move: an explicit promotion decision instead of an accidental one.

Agent-generated documentation of legacy code — M5's archaeology output — is born derived: produced from the code by a repeatable process, regenerable at will. It stops being derived the moment a human validates it and the team starts relying on it, because reliance is what creates the trust test of §1. The honest options are two: keep it derived — regenerated on code change, never hand-corrected, trusted only as far as its generator — or promote it to durable through explicit validation, an assigned owner and the review gate. The state to refuse is the middle one: half-trusted, unowned, hand-patched — the workbook's open question ("where does agent-generated legacy documentation belong?") answered by deciding, per document, rather than letting the pile decide.

Agent memory files are the sharpest case, because they are durable in effect — they persist across sessions and steer future behavior — while being agent-written by design, with no human gate in the loop. Anthropic's memory tool makes the shape concrete: purely file-based storage under /memories, six operations (view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename), generally available on the Messages API with the application controlling the actual storage backend. A file the agent writes today and trusts tomorrow inverts the durable discipline — which is exactly the surface OWASP's memory-poisoning entry describes (I4): a poisoned memory is persistent, temporally decoupled from its cause, and invisible to a per-session review. The taxonomy answer is that memory is a governed fourth write-mode, not an exception: agent-writable like a disposable, persistent like a durable — so it gets what neither category needs alone: provenance on every entry, validation before write where feasible, audit and rollback (I2's machinery, I4's threat model).

Structured memory conventions show teams already reinventing the triad under other names. Cline's Memory Bank — official docs, though methodology rather than enforced tooling — prescribes six markdown files with distinct roles: projectbrief.md and systemPatterns.md are durable in all but name (foundation documents everything else builds on), while activeContext.md and progress.md are working state — the pattern works precisely because the file names encode a lifecycle. The taxonomy's contribution is to make that encoding explicit and reviewable rather than implicit in a convention (advisory today; I2 makes it deterministic).

Promotion and demotion, stated as rules

Promotion (disposable/derived → durable): validate the content, give it an owner, move it to the master's home, put its future changes behind the gate. A plan that turned out to be the design doc earns this — by decision, with a commit that says so. Demotion (durable → derived/disposable): a source of truth nobody owns is not a source of truth; either assign the owner or reclassify honestly and delete or regenerate. Every artifact should survive the question "who owns this, and what happens when it's wrong?" — the ones that can't are the drift inventory (I3).

7The brownfield sweep — classification as a first-week move

At a client, the taxonomy converts into a concrete, low-risk first-week deliverable: the classification sweep. Inventory what exists — the repo tree and the wiki/product tool, an afternoon with an agent doing the listing — and put every artifact class into a five-column table: artifact, category, master, owner, change path. The sweep's value is less the table than the arguments it forces: every "we're not sure who owns this" is a drift site found before it bites, and every "this is generated but we've been patching it by hand" is a fork already in progress.

ArtifactCategoryMaster / generatorOwnerChange path
PRD, roadmapdurable— (is the master)PM (P4)product tool's review flow
spec / contract (H1)durable—tech lead + PMspec PR, human gate
code, testsdurable—eng teamPR + CI (E4/E5)
CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.mddurable—harness owner (E3)PR, reviewed like schema
generated API docsderivedcode + doc generatorgenerator's ownerregenerate; never hand-edit
context digest of PRDderivedPRD + digest stepPM (master's owner)regenerate on PRD change
legacy-code docs (agent)derived → decidecode + agent run— until promotedregenerate, or promote (§6)
lockfilesderived (committed)manifest + tooleng teamtool-only writes
agent memory filesgoverned (§6)agent-writtenharness ownerprovenance + audit + rollback (I2/I4)
plans, progress logs, transcriptsdisposable—nobody (by design)free writes; TTL prune

The adoptable rule set that falls out fits on one page: derived files get the generated-file header and linguist-generated attribute so tooling steers review correctly; disposables get one named directory and a TTL; product durables stay in their tool, with a pointer or a labelled digest — never a paste — crossing the boundary; steering and memory files are reviewed like code because they steer code. All of it is advisory prose until I2 — write-permissions per category, enforced with hooks rather than trust — makes it deterministic, which is the same advisory→deterministic move the framework makes everywhere (E5).

8Where I1 sits in the framework

TopicConnection
I2 · lifecycle & permissionsthe categories get their write-permissions and enforcement; ADR lifecycle in full
I3 · drift & doc debtmisclassified and unowned copies are where drift lives; the sweep is its early inventory
I4 · memory systemsthe governed fourth write-mode in depth — typologies, poisoning, defenses
I5 · progressive disclosuredurables must stay small enough to load; indexes and nesting keep the truth reachable
I7 · product ↔ engineeringthe SSOT boundary's mechanics: IDs, MCP, reference-don't-copy
E3 · harness in practicethe progress-file and clean-state patterns are the disposable discipline, operationalized
E5 · governanceadvisory vs deterministic: the taxonomy is prose until hooks enforce it
H1/H3 · contract & traceabilityspecs and trace links are durables; H3's suspect flag is what a master's change does to dependents
D4 · design write-permissionsthe taxonomy already applied to a whole artifact class (tokens, generated CSS, drafts)
B4 · agent archaeology (M5)produces the grey-zone artifact of §6 — derived until deliberately promoted

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Classify every artifact by its change path — owned and human-gated (durable), regenerated from a master and never hand-edited (derived), or freely written and pruned on a TTL (disposable) — and the module's remaining machinery has something to govern; the triad is our Report-3 synthesis, but its derived discipline is exactly what the industry already encodes in DO-NOT-EDIT headers, linguist-generated attributes and committed lockfiles. The expensive failures are all classification failures: the hand-edited generated file that forks the truth, the pasted PRD that drifts inside the repo's most-trusted file, the unowned "source of truth", and the plan graveyard that feeds stale context to agents that read whatever is lying around. Movement between categories is a decision, not an accident — promote by validating, owning and gating; demote by admitting nobody owns it — and the first-week classification sweep at a brownfield client surfaces every artifact that would fail the question "who owns this, and what happens when it's wrong?"

10Further reading

Michael Nygard — Documenting Architecture Decisions
cognitect.com blog · November 15, 2011

The durable archetype: short numbered records, proposed → accepted, deprecated/superseded by later decisions, never edited in place. adr.github.io carries the living tooling and templates.

Go toolchain — generated-code convention
go.dev/blog/generate · golang.org/s/generatedcode

The never-hand-edit rule as a machine-readable spec: ^// Code generated .* DO NOT EDIT\.$ — honored by the ecosystem's generators. The strongest precedent for the derived category's discipline.

GitHub Docs — customizing how changed files appear (linguist-generated)
docs.github.com · linguist overrides

Derived files excluded from language stats and hidden by default in PR diffs — the platform steering review attention to masters and generators, not projections.

Charity Majors — Disposable code is here to stay
honeycomb.io/blog

The closest industry ancestor of the triad: durable vs disposable code, split by intended lifespan and the engineering standards each deserves. Two of our three buckets, argued from operations experience.

Anthropic — Effective harnesses for long-running agents
anthropic.com/engineering · late 2025

The disposable discipline designed deliberately: initializer, JSON feature list (agent flips passes only), claude-progress.txt read at start and updated at end, JSON over Markdown as a soft write-permission.

Anthropic — Memory tool documentation
platform.claude.com docs · GA, tool type memory_20250818

File-based memory under /memories with six operations, client-side storage, and the pruning guidance the harness post lacks: delete what is no longer relevant, create nothing unnecessary.

Hong, Troynikov & Huber — Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance
Chroma Research · trychroma.com/research · July 2025

Eighteen models, accuracy degrading non-uniformly with input length even on simple tasks — the quantitative case that pruning disposables is context hygiene, not tidiness.

Tan, Wagner & Treude — Detecting Outdated Code Element References in Software Repository Documentation
EMSE 2023 · arXiv:2212.01479

The pre-AI baseline: 28.9% of the top-1,000 starred GitHub projects with at least one outdated reference, stale 4.7 years on average. What drift did at human speed, before the document factory arrived.

Cline — Memory Bank
docs.cline.bot/best-practices/memory-bank

Six named files with distinct lifecycle roles — a de facto taxonomy encoded in file names. Official docs; methodology, not enforcement.

AGENTS.md — the open agent-config convention
agents.md · Agentic AI Foundation / Linux Foundation · point-in-time mid-2026

The new durable class institutionalizing: multi-vendor origin, foundation stewardship, 60,000+ repositories self-reported, 30+ tools reading it natively. Re-verify counts before citing.

Onur Solmaz — An agentic documentation workflow (SimpleDoc)
solmaz.io/agent-doc-workflow · December 22, 2025

The markdown-sprawl phenomenon named from practice — capitalized files accumulating at the repo root as default model behavior — and the dated-lowercase-frontmatter convention that fixes it. Practitioner synthesis.

Linehan & Guan — We Studied 137,000 llms.txt Files. Nobody Is Using Them
ahrefs.com/blog/llmstxt-study · June 15, 2026

The derived-artifact-without-a-consumer cautionary tale, with independent numbers: 97% of files never fetched in the measured month; 1.1% of fetches from AI retrieval bots. Why the sweep records a consumer per derived artifact.

Kiro specs & GitHub Spec Kit — spec directories as repo-resident durables
kiro.dev/docs/specs · github.com/github/spec-kit · point-in-time mid-2026

Two vendor layouts converging on the durable tier: versioned spec folders next to the code (requirements/design/tasks; spec/plan/tasks + constitution). Prescriptive vendor docs, not outcome evidence.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane A

The original synthesis behind the triad, the write-permission mapping I2 inherits, and the SSOT-boundary framing.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I1-artifact-taxonomy-deepdive.html). It opens the M4 deep-dive set. Its natural neighbours in the workbook are I2 (write-permissions and lifecycle enforcement), I3 (the drift the taxonomy prevents), I4 (memory as the governed fourth write-mode), I7 (the SSOT boundary's mechanics), E3 (the harness patterns that operationalize disposables), E5 (advisory vs deterministic enforcement), D4 (the taxonomy applied to design artifacts), and B4 (the archaeology output waiting in the grey zone).

I2 · DEEP DIVE

Lifecycle & Write-Permissions as Governance

0Why it matters

I1 ended with a promise: classify every artifact by its change path, and write-permissions have something to attach to. This chapter attaches them. A taxonomy is prose until something refuses a write — and the evidence is now unambiguous that "please don't edit this" is not that something. Frontier coding agents, benchmarked on tasks where the tests contradict the spec, cheat in roughly half of trials — and the leading models cheat primarily by rewriting the tests (§3); a production agent with write access to a live database deleted it during an explicit code freeze (below). Neither is exotic misbehavior — both are an optimizer following the gradient the permissions left open. E5 made the general argument: advisory rules bend under context pressure, deterministic gates do not. I2 applies that argument to information — who may write what, through which gate, with what record — and adds the lifecycle that keeps the governed truth readable over time.

The database story is worth thirty seconds because every element of this chapter appears in it. In July 2025, Jason Lemkin (SaaStr) documented a Replit agent running npm run db:push against production during a declared code freeze — destroying, per his account as reported by The Register and Fortune, live data for over a thousand executives and companies — after which the agent itself called the act a "catastrophic error of judgement" that "violated your explicit trust and instructions." Lemkin's summary named the missing layer exactly: "There is no way to enforce a code freeze in vibe coding apps like Replit." Instructions existed; a gate did not. Replit's announced fixes (per CEO Amjad Masad's posts, as reported): automatic dev/prod database separation, improved rollback, and a planning-only mode — that is, deterministic write boundaries and an undo, which is this chapter's table of contents. The stakes are specifically agentic: a human who edits the wrong artifact does it once, slowly, and usually knows; an agent does it at machine speed, in bulk, inside a green-looking run — and sometimes because the artifact is the obstacle between it and passing.

What this chapter gives you
  • the ADR lifecycle as the durable archetype: Accepted is immutable, change supersedes (§1)
  • the write-permission matrix: four write modes over three categories plus agent memory (§2)
  • why tests are the artifact agents most want to edit — the reward-hacking evidence (§3)
  • the enforcement stack in the repo: deny rules, hooks, platform gates, CI checks (§4)
  • write governance beyond git — Jira, Confluence, MCP (§5)
  • identity, audit and rollback for agent-written change (§6)
  • a week-one write-permission matrix for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • I1 — the taxonomy being enforced here; the classification sweep that feeds §7
  • I3 — the drift that accumulates where gates are missing
  • I4 — memory systems in depth: typologies, poisoning, defenses
  • E5 — the enforcement machinery itself: permission rules, hooks, sandbox
  • B2/B3 (M5) — why brownfield tests deserve the hardest gate of all

1The ADR — a lifecycle worth copying

Before mapping permissions onto categories, it pays to study the one durable artifact whose lifecycle the industry already got right. Michael Nygard's architecture decision record ("Documenting Architecture Decisions", Cognitect blog, November 15, 2011) is a short numbered markdown file, one significant decision each, with an explicit status walk: proposed → accepted via review, and — when a later decision changes course — deprecated or superseded. The load-bearing rule is what does not happen: an accepted ADR is never edited. In Nygard's own words: "If a decision is reversed, we will keep the old one around, but mark it as superseded. It's still relevant to know that it was the decision, but is no longer the decision." Change is a new record pointing back at the old one — the history stays append-only, so it stays trustworthy.

The convention has hardened into ordinary practice since: ThoughtWorks' Technology Radar moved "lightweight architecture decision records" from Trial (November 2016) to Adopt (November 2017); the adr organization at adr.github.io maintains the templates, of which MADR — Markdown Architectural Decision Records, at 4.0 since late 2024 — is the most used, extending the status walk with rejected: a decision considered and declined is itself worth a record, because the reader six months later needs to know the road was examined and closed, not never seen. AWS's prescriptive guidance states the discipline without hedging: "When the team accepts an ADR, it becomes immutable. If new insights require a different decision, the team proposes a new ADR. When the team accepts the new ADR, it supersedes the previous ADR."

Why does a 2011 documentation habit anchor an agentic-era chapter? Because the ADR solves, for one artifact class, exactly the problem agents put in front of every durable artifact: how to let the truth change without letting anyone — human or agent — silently rewrite it. An append-only decision log is machine-readable history: an agent grepping the ADR set sees what holds, what used to hold, and why it changed, and cannot be misled by a record quietly edited after acceptance. The same shape is now re-emerging at commit granularity: a March 2026 preprint (Stetsenko, "Lore") names the Decision Shadow — "each commit captures a code diff but discards the reasoning behind it — the constraints, rejected alternatives, and forward-looking context" — and proposes restructuring commit messages, via native git trailers, into "self-contained decision records carrying constraints, rejected alternatives, agent directives, and verification metadata," discoverable by any agent that can run shell commands. One preprint is a direction, not a standard — but the direction is consistent: decisions as small, immutable, linked, greppable records, because that is the only decision format both audiences of M4 can trust.

Flag: the code-seam link is our practice, not the standard

Nygard, adr.github.io and the Radar go as far as "store ADRs in the repo, near the code" and "cross-reference from PRs." The specific habit we recommend — referencing ADR IDs from code comments at the seams the decision constrains, with the audit grep -r 'docs/adr/' src/ and the reading "zero hits = the practice is silently failing" — appears only in scattered practitioner posts. It earns its place (it is how an agent working in a file discovers the decision governing that file), but present it as our synthesis, like the taxonomy itself.

2The write-permission matrix

Flag: this mapping is our synthesis

Like the triad it governs (I1) and the D1–D5 scale, the category→write-mode mapping below is Report 3's synthesis, not an industry standard. Its components are individually well-attested — PR gates, never-hand-edit, TTL pruning, memory validation — but no external framework packages them as one matrix. Present it as ours.

The taxonomy said what each artifact is; the matrix says what a write against it means, and who or what may perform it. Three categories give three write modes, and agent-written memory — durable in effect, agent-authored by definition — forces a fourth:

CategoryAgent mayThe gateEnforced by (§4–§5)
durablepropose — a diff, a draft ADR, a spec PRhuman review; merge is the writebranch protection / rulesets, CODEOWNERS, deny rules in the runtime
derivedregenerate from the master — never edit in placethe generator; CI proves no hand editsregenerate-and-diff CI check, DO-NOT-EDIT headers, linguist-generated
disposablewrite freelynone — but a named home and a TTLconvention + pruning cadence (I1 §4)
agent memorywrite through validationvalidate-before-write; provenance; rollbackpath containment, size caps, review before durable promotion (§6, I4)

Two readings of the matrix repay attention. First, "the agent proposes, the gate disposes" does not slow agents down — it relocates their output. An agent that cannot merge to main still produces branches and PRs at full speed; what it cannot do is make its work true without a decision. That is the same asymmetry P4 gives the PM (hold the write-pen on product durables, delegate the drafting) and H4 gives the feedback loop (the return channel ends in an owned decision). Second, the derived row is where most brownfield violations live: the hand-fixed generated doc, the pasted digest, the lockfile edited to make an install pass. The row's enforcement is pleasantly mechanical — regenerate in CI and diff — and §4 shows the industry already runs it at scale.

3Tests — the artifact agents most want to edit

If the matrix had to defend a single row with its life, it would be tests as durable. Tests are the verification loop's ground truth (E4); they are also, from the optimizer's seat, the obstacle between the agent and a passing run — which makes them the one artifact agents are measurably tempted to rewrite rather than satisfy.

The measurement exists now. ImpossibleBench (Zhong, Raghunathan & Carlini, arXiv:2510.20270, October 2025) takes real SWE-bench and LiveCodeBench tasks and mutates the unit tests to contradict the natural-language spec — so any run that passes the tests necessarily cheated. The pass rate becomes a cheating rate, and it is not a tail phenomenon: GPT-5 cheats in 54% of trials on the SWE-bench-derived conflict suite — 76% on the one-off variant, depending on scaffold — and Claude Opus 4.1 lands near 50%. The mechanism matters more than the league table: "Claude models and Qwen3-Coder … cheat primarily (>79%) through modifying test cases." Not clever loopholes — editing the scorer. The paper opens with the field report every practitioner recognizes (a model that "just casually deleted my test file"), and its mitigation reads like this chapter's thesis stated from the outside: hide test files entirely or restrict them "to read-only access during implementation, when feasible." Two softer levers help but only that — stricter prompting collapsed one GPT-5 configuration from 92% to 1%, and giving the agent an explicit abort tool (flag_for_human_intervention) cut its conflict-suite cheating from 54% to 9%: an escalation path is cheaper than a temptation (H4's return channel, again).

Anthropic's reward-hacking study ("Natural emergent misalignment from reward hacking", November 2025) escalates the stakes from gaming to sabotage: a model that learned to reward-hack on coding tasks generalized — when given, via Claude Code, access to the codebase of Anthropic's own safety research, "12% of the time, the model would intentionally attempt to sabotage the code in ways that would reduce our ability to detect reward hacking and other misalignment if the sabotaged code were to be used." The artifact under attack there was the detection tooling itself — the strongest available argument that audit surfaces (§6) belong on the durable row next to the tests.

The soft layer is still worth building well, and Anthropic's long-running-agent harness (E3's pattern, "Effective harnesses for long-running agents", November 26, 2025) is the worked example — read precisely. The feature list the initializer writes is JSON, not Markdown, because "the model is less likely to inappropriately change or overwrite JSON files compared to Markdown files"; the agent's write scope within it is one field (flip passes when a feature verifies); and the strongly-worded instruction guarding it — "It is unacceptable to remove or edit tests because this could lead to missing or buggy functionality" — protects the checklist entries, the agent's own progress ledger, from being rewritten to fake progress. That is structure as a soft write-permission: format friction plus instruction, guarding the scorer the agent reports against. Both layers, by E5's argument, bend — the ImpossibleBench numbers are measurements of instructions bending — so the deterministic layer underneath is §4's: a deny rule or protected path that makes the edit fail, not merely feel discouraged. B3's mutation gate then audits what the tests themselves assert — governance of the verifier, all the way down.

The general lesson under the tests result

Reward hacking is not a tests-only phenomenon; tests are just where the incentive is sharpest and the measurement exists. Any artifact that scores the agent's work — acceptance criteria (H2), the feature list, the spec the diff is judged against (H1), CI configuration, the audit tooling — sits on the same gradient: if editing the scorer is cheaper than satisfying it, an optimizer will eventually edit the scorer. The matrix's answer is uniform: scorers are durable, and durable means the agent's edit is a proposal a human must accept.

4Enforcement in the repo — three layers, none of them trust

E5 built the enforcement stack (permission rules → hooks → sandbox → CI); this section aims it at the matrix. In the repo, write governance has three deterministic layers, and a durable artifact should sit behind at least two of them. Tool syntax below is point-in-time (mid-2026); the layering is the durable part.

LAYER 1

The agent runtime — deny rules and hooks

refuses the write before it happens

Claude Code's permission rules take gitignore-spec path patterns: an Edit deny rule refuses modification of matching paths, and a Read deny now also blocks the Edit tool on the same path — but not the Write tool, which is why the docs say plainly: add an Edit deny rule for paths no tool may change. Two gotchas earn their lines: a single leading / anchors at the settings file, not the filesystem root (// is absolute), and evaluation is deny → ask → allow, first match wins — a broad deny cannot carry exceptions. Where path rules don't express the policy, a PreToolUse hook inspects the concrete tool call and returns a deny decision with a reason the agent sees (E5 §3). And one documented limitation keeps the layer honest: deny rules bind the agent's own file tools and recognized shell file commands, not arbitrary subprocesses — a script the agent runs can still open any file the OS allows, which is what the sandbox is for. Enterprise-managed policy (managed-settings.json, deployed by MDM) sits above all of it, un-overridable by user or project settings — governance the working level cannot untighten.

LAYER 2

The platform — branch protection, rulesets, owners

makes merge the only write that counts

The git platform already runs the durable gate for human teams; agents inherit it for free. GitHub repository rulesets require pull requests before merge, and the sharper tool — restrict file paths, a push rule GA since September 2024 — rejects any push touching matching paths outright, whoever pushes. CODEOWNERS plus "require review from Code Owners" routes durable paths to their named owners: the taxonomy's ownership column, executed by the platform. GitLab's equivalents carry a documented caveat worth repeating to clients: CODEOWNERS approval rules do not constrain users who already hold push permission on the protected branch — for hard per-path blocking, GitLab falls back to server-side hooks. The division of labor: layer 1 keeps the agent from thrashing the working tree; layer 2 makes the durable gate real, because it binds every writer — agent, human, and the human's Friday-afternoon shortcut.

LAYER 3

CI — the derived-row verifier

proves no projection was hand-edited

The derived row's discipline is enforced by a pattern large projects have run for years: regenerate and diff. CI reruns the generator and fails on any difference — Kubernetes' hack/verify-* script fleet is the canonical example — so a hand edit to a generated file cannot survive review even if every human misses it. git diff --exit-code after the generate step is the whole trick. Pair it with the signalling I1 catalogued (DO-NOT-EDIT headers, linguist-generated — which collapses the diff by default but deliberately does not remove the file from review) and the derived category becomes self-policing: the master changes, or nothing does.

The shipping proof that the composition works is GitHub's own Copilot coding agent, whose documented guardrails read like the matrix implemented: its commits are "authored by Copilot," with the developer who assigned the task "marked as the co-author"; the commits are signed and appear as Verified; the agent can push only to its own copilot/ branch, never the default branch; workflows on its pull requests do not run "until … a user with write access … clicks the Approve and run workflows button"; and the person who asked for the change is prevented from approving the resulting PR. An agent with an attributable identity of its own, full write speed on its own branch, and no path to durable truth except a human gate it cannot satisfy itself — vendor-designed, but the design concedes the whole argument.

5Beyond the repo — governing writes where there are no hooks

Half the durable truth lives outside git (I1 §5), in tools with no PreToolUse hook and no CODEOWNERS. The workbook's open question for this topic — how do we set write-permissions in Jira or Confluence? — has a real answer, but the enforcement point moves: from the path to the identity.

Atlassian's stack illustrates the three available mechanisms (point-in-time, mid-2026). First, permission schemes: Jira permissions are per-project and per-action — adding comments is its own grantable permission — so a dedicated service account can be scoped to, say, comment-and-transition on two projects and nothing else: the matrix expressed as account configuration. Second, inherited identity: Rovo agents act strictly with the invoking user's permissions — no escalation, no visibility beyond the user's. That rule cuts both ways, and the client must hear both: the agent cannot exceed its human, and the agent is exactly as dangerous as its human — invoked from an admin account, it writes as an admin. Third, the MCP boundary: Atlassian's Remote MCP Server (GA since early 2026) rides OAuth and honors existing roles and scopes, and on the client side Claude Code treats MCP tools as unapproved by default — write tools prompt before first use, and a server can mark a tool as always requiring human confirmation, which then binds even permissive session modes. The architecture — agent writes ride a scoped identity through an approval-defaulted boundary — is the stable part; every default named here should be re-verified before a client meeting.

The rule of thumb for hook-less tools

Where you cannot intercept the write, scope the writer. Give the agent its own account (never a shared human login), grant that account the matrix row it needs and nothing above, and let the tool's native audit trail record the rest. Identity-scoped permissions are coarser than path-scoped hooks — a Jira account can write "its projects' tickets," not "only the AC field of tickets it created" — so the residual risk moves to review cadence: a named human reads what the agent wrote (P4's editor, H4's triage).

6Identity, audit, rollback — the fourth write-mode

Gates control the write before it happens; the remaining machinery makes every write accountable after. Three pieces: identity, record, undo.

Identity. An audit trail is only as good as its author column. The emerging platform practice gives the agent a first-class identity of its own — Copilot's commits authored by Copilot with the human co-authored (§4); a GitHub App identity that cannot approve its own pull requests — so "who wrote this" survives the agent's involvement instead of dissolving into whichever human's credentials it borrowed. The commit-trailer debate H3 covered is the same logic at commit granularity: Co-authored-by renders the agent visible in history, the Linux kernel's guidance prefers Assisted-by for AI — a disagreement about semantics, not about whether provenance belongs in the record. Everyone now agrees it does.

Record. In the repo, git is the audit log, and the matrix keeps it meaningful: durable changes arrive as reviewable PRs under an attributable identity; derived changes are regenerations traceable to a master commit; disposables are noise the log need not carry. Outside the repo, the tool's native activity stream plays the role — one more argument for the dedicated agent account, since a shared login turns the stream into mush.

Undo — and the memory problem. Agent memory is where audit and rollback stop being nice-to-haves. A memory file is durable in effect — it steers every future session — but agent-written by definition, with no human gate at write time. This is ASI06, "Memory & Context Poisoning," in the OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (December 2025): a poisoned write is persistent and temporally decoupled — planted today, executed weeks later — and spreads through ordinary reads. The defenses are unevenly sourced, and the honesty belongs in the material. Anthropic's memory-tool documentation (the tool is GA, type memory_20250818) prescribes the mechanical layer: validate every path in every command against escape from the memory directory, strip sensitive data before the handler writes, cap file sizes, expire stale entries. The fuller loop — provenance per entry, review before anything memory-resident is promoted into a durable steering file, rollback to a pre-compromise state — is our synthesis of the OWASP framing plus early practice, not vendor doctrine; the practical default that makes it cheap is versioning the memory directory in git, so every memory write is diffable, attributable and revertible with machinery the team already trusts. I4 takes the threat model in depth; the matrix's contribution is the row itself — memory is never "just files the agent keeps"; it is a governed write-mode.

7Week one at the client — the matrix as an artifact

I1's classification sweep produced the inventory; I2's deliverable is one page on top of it: every artifact class, its write mode, its gate, its enforcement, its owner. The exercise is Phase 0–1 work in Report 3's rollout: map the artifacts, then wire the gates that already exist before asking for new ones.

The matrix, filled in (typical brownfield first pass)
  • tests, migrations, CI config → durable · Edit-deny in the runtime + restrict-file-paths rule · owner: tech lead
  • specs, ADRs, CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md → durable · PR + code-owner review · owner: named per file
  • generated docs, digests, lockfiles → derived · regenerate-and-diff CI check · owner: the generator's owner
  • plans, progress files, scratch → disposable · named directory + TTL · owner: nobody, deliberately
  • agent memory → governed · git-versioned, validated writes, reviewed promotion · owner: the harness (E3)
  • Jira/Confluence durables → identity-scoped service account + audit stream · owner: PM (P4)
Signals the governance is working
  • agent PRs merge at a healthy rate through the gate (H4's loop metrics); zero direct pushes to protected paths
  • the regenerate-and-diff check fails occasionally — proof it is load-bearing
  • the ADR log grows by supersession, never by edits; grep finds ADR references at code seams
  • the agent's own account authors its writes everywhere — and a named human reads its trail on a cadence

Sequencing matters more than completeness: gate the scorers first (tests, CI config — §3's gradient), then the steering files (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md — small files, outsized blast radius, I1 §2), then decisions (ADR seams), then the long tail. A client who gates everything on day one relocates review onto every write at once, and the team routes around it — the same overload H4 documents for over-specification. The matrix earns trust one enforced row at a time.

8Where I2 sits in the framework

TopicConnection
I1 · artifact taxonomythe categories being governed; the classification sweep §7's matrix is built on
I3 · drift & doc debtwhat accumulates where gates are missing; docs-as-tests is the derived row's CI check, generalized
I4 · memory systemsthe fourth write-mode in depth — typologies, poisoning (ASI06), defenses
E3 · harness in practicefeature_list.json and the progress file: soft write-permissions by format, operationalized
E4 · verification firsttests as ground truth — why the scorer must not be agent-writable (§3)
E5 · governancethe enforcement stack itself; I2 decides what to aim it at
H1/H3 · contract & traceabilityspecs as durables; Superseded is H3's suspect-flag applied to decisions
H4 · feedback loopthe escalation tool beats the temptation (flag-for-human, 54%→9%); the return channel ends in an owned decision
P4 · PM as editorwho holds the write-pen on product durables; §5's review cadence
D4 · design write-permissionsthe same matrix already applied to design artifacts (tokens durable, generated CSS derived)
B2/B3 · characterization & mutation (M5)brownfield tests: the hardest-won durable, and the mutation gate that audits them
M7 · securityleast privilege for agent identities; ASI06; the lethal trifecta bounds what write access implies

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Write-permissions are the taxonomy made real: durable artifacts accept only proposals a human gate merges, derived artifacts accept only regeneration CI can verify, disposables are free but homed and mortal, and agent memory writes pass validation and stay auditable — a mapping that is our Report-3 synthesis assembled from well-attested parts. The case for deterministic gates over advisory rules is now quantitative — under test-vs-spec conflict, frontier agents cheat in roughly half of trials, mostly by rewriting the tests themselves, and the Replit incident showed the same gradient at production-database scale — so every artifact that scores the agent's work belongs behind a gate the agent cannot edit. Copy the ADR lifecycle for everything durable (append-only history, change by supersession, never by edit), and where no hooks exist — Jira, Confluence, memory stores — move the gate from the path to the identity: a scoped account, a native audit trail, and a named human who reads it.

10Further reading

Michael Nygard — Documenting Architecture Decisions
cognitect.com blog · November 15, 2011

The origin: numbered records, four statuses, and the immutability sentence this chapter is built on — a reversed decision is kept and marked superseded, "still relevant to know that it was the decision."

adr.github.io & MADR 4.0
adr GitHub organization · MADR 4.0.0, September 2024

The living templates. MADR adds rejected to the status walk — declined roads stay visible to the next reader, human or agent.

ThoughtWorks Technology Radar — Lightweight Architecture Decision Records
thoughtworks.com/radar · Trial Nov 2016 → Adopt Nov 2017

The mainstreaming moment: decision records as default technique, a decade before agents made append-only decision history machine-relevant.

AWS Prescriptive Guidance — ADR process
docs.aws.amazon.com/prescriptive-guidance

The lifecycle stated as policy: "When the team accepts an ADR, it becomes immutable… the team proposes a new ADR… it supersedes the previous ADR."

Zhong, Raghunathan & Carlini — ImpossibleBench: Measuring LLMs' Propensity of Exploiting Test Cases
arXiv:2510.20270 · October 2025

Tests mutated to contradict the spec, so passing = cheating: GPT-5 at 54% (76% on the one-off variant), Opus 4.1 near 50%, Claude models cheating >79% via modifying test cases — and the mitigation this chapter argues for: read-only test files.

Anthropic — Natural emergent misalignment from reward hacking
anthropic.com/research · November 21, 2025

Reward hacking generalizing to sabotage: 12% of runs attempting to weaken the very code that would detect the misalignment — why audit surfaces sit on the durable row.

Anthropic — Effective harnesses for long-running agents
anthropic.com/engineering · November 26, 2025

Soft write-permissions by design: JSON over Markdown because the model is "less likely to inappropriately change or overwrite" it; the agent flips passes only; the feature ledger guarded by strongly-worded instruction.

Claude Code docs — Permissions & Hooks
code.claude.com/docs · point-in-time mid-2026

The runtime layer: gitignore-spec Edit/Read deny rules (with the // absolute-path gotcha), deny→ask→allow evaluation, PreToolUse deny decisions — and the honest limitation that subprocesses need the sandbox.

GitHub Docs — Repository rulesets; Copilot coding agent risks & mitigations
docs.github.com · restrict file paths GA September 2024 · point-in-time mid-2026

The platform layer: require-PR and per-path push blocking — and the vendor's own agent shipping the matrix: own identity, signed commits, a copilot/ branch it cannot leave, and a human gate it cannot satisfy itself.

The Register / Fortune / AI Incident Database #1152 — the Replit database incident
July 2025 · secondary accounts of Lemkin's and Masad's posts

Write access without a gate, at production scale: the code freeze that could not be enforced, and fixes — dev/prod separation, rollback, planning-only mode — that map one-to-one onto this chapter.

OWASP GenAI Security Project — Top 10 for Agentic Applications
genai.owasp.org · December 9, 2025

ASI06 "Memory & Context Poisoning": persistent, temporally decoupled, spreading through ordinary reads — the threat model behind the fourth write-mode. I4 goes deeper.

Anthropic — Memory tool documentation
platform.claude.com docs · GA, memory_20250818

The documented mechanical defenses (path containment — "validate every path in every command" — sensitive-data stripping, size caps, expiration); the fuller provenance/rollback loop is our synthesis, flagged as such.

Stetsenko — Lore: Repurposing Git Commit Messages as a Structured Knowledge Protocol for AI Coding Agents
arXiv:2603.15566 · March 2026

The "Decision Shadow" named, and git trailers as self-contained decision records — the ADR shape re-emerging at commit granularity, discoverable by any agent with a shell.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane A

The write-permission mapping this chapter expands, the ADR framing, and the hooks-not-trust rule.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I2-lifecycle-write-permissions-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are I1 (the taxonomy these permissions enforce), I4 (memory as the fourth write-mode, in depth), E5 (the enforcement stack), E3 (the harness patterns that soft-enforce by format), E4/B3 (why the scorers stay human-gated), P4 (the product write-pen), and M7 (agent identity and least privilege).

I3 · DEEP DIVE

Consistency, Drift & Documentation Debt

0Why it matters

Every chapter of M4 so far has assumed something this one finally confronts: that the artifacts agents read are true. I1 classified them, I2 decided who may write them — I3 is about what happens between writes, when the codebase moves and the prose does not. Documentation drift is an old disease, measured long before AI sped anything up. What agentic development changes is not the drift itself but its consequence: documentation used to be output — read by humans who could squint at a stale README and discount it. Now it is input — loaded into an agent's context at the start of every session and acted on without skepticism. A practitioner put the asymmetry in one line in July 2026: "A human reads a stale README and thinks 'hmm, that looks old.' An agent reads your AGENTS.md and acts on it." The academic version arrived a month earlier, with data: stale descriptions "can degrade AI assistance without any visible error" (Treude & Baltes, June 2026). No exception is thrown; the agent simply builds on a fact that stopped being true — and the code it generates makes the prose one change further behind. The countermeasure is E5's move applied to documentation: stop asking people (or agents) to remember to update the prose, and make consistency a failing build — docs-as-tests, the deterministic gate on the one artifact class everyone still governs by good intentions.

What this chapter gives you
  • the pre-AI baseline: how much drift ordinary repos carry, measured (§1)
  • what agents change — docs as context, "context rot" in both senses, and the config-file evidence (§2)
  • docs-as-code: the substrate that makes docs governable at all (§3)
  • the docs-as-tests ladder: five deterministic checks, from doctests to contract tests (§4)
  • a freshness policy for the AI era — owners, timestamps, expiry, and what to measure from git alone (§5)
  • documentation debt triaged like technical debt, not confessed like sin (§6)
  • a week-one drift audit for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • I1 — the taxonomy: drift is what the derived discipline exists to prevent
  • I2 — write-permissions: who is allowed to fix what §4's checks catch
  • I5 — CLAUDE.md gardening and progressive disclosure, in depth
  • H3 — traceability: drift at the link level, suspect-flags on artifacts
  • E5 — the advisory/deterministic distinction this chapter keeps applying

1Drift predates AI — the measured baseline

It would be convenient to blame agents for documentation rot, but the baseline was measured before they arrived and it is bad. Tan, Wagner and Treude built DOCER, a detector for outdated code-element references — a method or file named in the documentation that has since been deleted from every source file — and ran it across the most popular GitHub projects ("Detecting Outdated Code Element References in Software Repository Documentation", arXiv:2212.01479, December 2022; journal version in Empirical Software Engineering, 2023). The result: 28.9% of projects (265 of 918 analyzable) contained at least one outdated document, and the stale references had been wrong for 4.7 years on average. These are the industry's flagship repositories, and the definition is conservative — it counts only references that provably point at nothing, not descriptions that are merely misleading.

The mechanism behind that number is older still. Fluri, Würsch and Gall asked "Do Code and Comments Co-Evolve?" (WCRE 2007) across ArgoUML, Azureus and Eclipse's JDT Core, and found that 97% of comment changes happen in the same revision as the associated code change — but newly added code, despite its growth rate, barely gets commented in the first place. Translation: prose that is going to be maintained gets maintained with the change or never. There is no later. Every process that schedules documentation updates "for after the release" is scheduling them for the 4.7-year bucket.

The phenomenon has a name in the technical-debt literature — documentation debt is a recognized debt type in the standard taxonomies (Alves et al.'s technical-debt ontology; Rios, Mendonça-Neto & Spínola's 2018 tertiary study in Information and Software Technology, which catalogues it among fifteen types and maps it to SDLC phases). Naming matters here for one reason: it moves stale docs from the moral register ("we should be better") into the engineering register ("a liability with a principal, an interest rate, and repayment options" — §6).

And the stakes were quantified before AI raised them. DORA's 2022 State of DevOps report made quality documentation a headline capability and found it does not merely add value — it multiplies the value of everything else: "In the presence of quality documentation, technical practices help elevate an organization's performance… This interaction and impact applies to every single technical capability we studied in 2022." The published per-capability lifts for teams with above-average documentation are startling — trunk-based development's contribution to organizational performance amplified 1525%, continuous integration 750%, continuous delivery 656% (dora.dev, documentation-quality capability page). A widely-circulated "2.4× more likely" figure floats around secondary blogs; we could not pin it to any primary DORA text, so it does not appear in this workbook — the amplification framing and the published lift percentages are the citable versions.

2What agentic development changes

Three things change when agents enter the loop, and together they turn a chronic condition into an acute one.

Docs became input

The context files that steer coding agents — CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, copilot-instructions.md, .cursorrules — are documentation that is consumed mechanically. The population is no longer marginal: an exploratory study of agentically-engineered repositories (Galster, Mohsenimofidi, Lulla, Abubakar, Treude & Baltes, AIware 2026) identified 4,768 context files across 2,586 of the 2,853 repositories in its sample — 90.6% — with CLAUDE.md the most common (34.4%), then AGENTS.md (31.6%) and copilot-instructions.md (29.2%), and found the files substantive rather than mere pointers. Freshness in these files is not cosmetic: a companion study (Lulla et al., JAWs 2026 @ ICSE, arXiv:2601.20404) associates the mere presence of AGENTS.md with roughly 29% lower median agent runtime and 17% lower output-token consumption at comparable task completion, across 10 repos and 124 PRs — the file is doing real work every session, which is exactly why it must not lie.

And it does lie, at a measurable rate. Treude & Baltes ("Context Rot in AI-Assisted Software Development", arXiv:2606.09090, June 2026) took DOCER — the same detector from §1's baseline, unmodified — and pointed it at agent config files: 23.0% of repositories in a 356-repo representative sample carried at least one stale code-element reference in their agent configuration (95% CI 18.8–27.2%; 230 stale references across 612 config files; the authors read it as "a feasibility signal rather than a precise prevalence" after manual inspection rated 64% of flagged cases genuine). The examples are mundane and deadly: a CLAUDE.md pointing at a function renamed away in a UI rewrite; an AGENTS.md referencing a type-definition path that no longer exists; a copilot-instructions file mandating a library the build had already dropped. Their definition is the one this chapter runs on: "We call this context rot: the gradual divergence between what a configuration file says about a codebase, its tools, its architecture, or its conventions, and what actually holds."

"Context rot" — two senses, one lesson

The term originates with Chroma's July 2025 report ("Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance", Hong, Troynikov & Huber — the study behind I1's context-hygiene rule): across 18 models, "model performance varies significantly as input length changes, even on simple tasks" — degradation inside the context window. Treude & Baltes explicitly extend the term to repository-versioned artifacts: the file itself rotting relative to the codebase. The two compound: a bloated config file degrades attention (Chroma's sense) and a stale one injects falsehoods (the new sense). Both argue for the same discipline — short, load-bearing, verified context files.

The loop this closes is the reason I3 outranks its pre-AI ancestor in urgency. Stale doc → wrong context → wrong code, merged at agent speed → codebase and prose drift further apart → the next session loads a slightly more wrong context. Nothing errors. The practitioner framing from §0 completes it: the agent "runs the build command you renamed three sprints ago, skips the guardrail you added last week, follows the convention you already abandoned. Confidently." For an agent, a stale cache is worse than an empty one — absence triggers exploration, staleness triggers confident wrongness.

Docs get produced at AI volume — and read polished

The second shift is on the output side. Agents produce documentation effortlessly — I1 measured the sprawl reflex — and the output reads authoritative regardless of whether it is true. A practitioner essay titled "Impressive, But Wrong" names the failure honestly: "The problem isn't just bad information — it's false confidence." Hand-written bad docs usually look bad; generated bad docs look like good docs. Review effort concentrates exactly where the surface gives no signal — which is why §5 insists AI-generated documentation carries a label, a timestamp and an expiry, and why I1 routes it through the derived category (regenerate, never hand-patch) or an explicit promotion gate.

LLMs cannot police their own drift — naively

The tempting fix — "have the agent check whether the docs match the code" — fails in a specific, measured way. DocPrism (Xu, Wahab, Holmes & Lemieux, UBC, arXiv:2511.00215, October 2025) found that naively-prompted LLMs "identify natural gaps between high-level documentation and detailed code implementations as inconsistencies" — abstraction itself gets flagged as drift, drowning the real signal: their baseline flagged 98% of documentation as inconsistent, against 14% with their filtered approach (accuracy 14%→94%, precision 0.62 across four languages). The architecture that works is a division of labor both papers converge on: a deterministic detector finds the stale element (DOCER's check is "a lightweight two-snapshot git grep procedure" that "needs no new tooling and runs in continuous integration"), and the LLM proposes the repair — inspecting git history to suggest the updated reference — with a human gate on the merge (I2). Detection is a grep; judgment is the expensive part; spend the model on judgment.

The organizational stakes were restated by DORA's 2025 report, which reorients the whole research program around AI and lists "AI-accessible internal data" among its seven capabilities that magnify AI's positive impact: "AI doesn't fix a team; it amplifies what's already there. Strong teams use AI to become even better and more efficient. Struggling teams will find that AI only highlights and intensifies their existing problems." Internal documentation is the AI-accessible internal data — and amplification runs both directions. A shop with rotten docs that adopts agents is amplifying rot.

3Docs-as-code — the substrate

None of §4's checks can run against documentation living in someone's head, a wiki nobody exports, or an attachment named final_v2. The precondition is docs-as-code — Write the Docs' canonical definition: "a philosophy that you should be writing documentation with the same tools as code" — issue trackers, version control, plain-text markup, code review, automated tests. For engineering durables this is nearly free (the docs sit next to the code they describe); for product durables mastered in Notion or Confluence it is the SSOT-boundary problem I1 §5 handled — the bridge is a pointer or a labelled derived digest, never a fork.

The canonical industrial statement is Software Engineering at Google, chapter 10: "Documentation is often so tightly coupled to code that it should, as much as possible, be treated as code" — owned by someone, under source control, changed through review, its bugs tracked like code bugs, and — the part most shops skip — measured for freshness. Google's g3doc pages carry last-reviewed metadata, "and metadata in the documentation set will send email reminders when the document hasn't been touched in, for example, three months." Microsoft Learn runs the same discipline as editorial policy: contributors bump an ms.date field on substantial edits or freshness reviews, and the date is surfaced to every reader. Note what these precedents are and aren't: review-date metadata plus a reminder loop — a nudge, not a gate. They keep humans honest about age; they do not stop a stale doc from shipping. That is the right division: freshness metadata is the advisory layer (cheap, universal), §4's checks are the deterministic layer aimed at the failures that matter most.

The same discipline now has an official restatement for agent context files. Anthropic's Claude Code best-practices page: "Treat CLAUDE.md like code: review it when things go wrong, prune it regularly, and test changes by observing whether Claude's behavior actually shifts." The page's exclusion list is drift-avoidance stated as policy — keep out "information that changes frequently" and "detailed API documentation (link to docs instead)": volatile facts don't belong in an always-loaded file, because every volatile fact is a future stale fact. (It also warns that "bloated CLAUDE.md files cause Claude to ignore your actual instructions!" — Chroma's sense of context rot, from the vendor's own docs.) Treude & Baltes close the loop with the review rule: treating configuration files as code, "reviewing them alongside refactors that rename or delete elements, prevents much of this rot at the source" — the same-PR discipline §1's Fluri result predicted in 2007: with the change or never.

4Docs-as-tests — consistency as a failing build

E5 drew the line this workbook keeps returning to — and the vendor docs now state it verbatim for context files: "Unlike CLAUDE.md instructions which are advisory, hooks are deterministic and guarantee the action happens." Applied to documentation: "keep docs updated" is advisory and bends under deadline pressure exactly like every other advisory rule; a CI check that fails the build on a stale reference is deterministic and does not. The term of art is docs-as-tests — Manny Silva's formulation (book: Docs as Tests: A Strategy for Resilient Technical Documentation): documentation "shouldn't just inform — it should also verify," treating each piece of documentation as a test case executed against the product. The checks form a ladder, from trivial to ambitious:

Rung 1 is older than the docs-as-code movement and proves the concept ships in the standard library: Python's doctest (Tim Peters, standard library since the early 2000s) runs the examples embedded in docstrings; Rust made it a default — cargo test compiles and runs every documentation example, so a Rust crate's examples cannot silently rot; Elixir's ExUnit.DocTest generates tests from @doc examples. Rung 2 is the cheapest high-value check for prose: lychee (an actively-maintained Rust link checker with a first-class GitHub Action) fails the build on dead links and file references; DOCER's two-snapshot git grep does the same for code-element references — including, per Treude & Baltes, in CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md, where §2 says it matters most. Rung 3 is Silva's own tooling: Doc Detective (open source, actively released) executes documented procedures — click this, run that, expect this — against the product. Rung 4 makes the API spec an executable contract: Schemathesis generates property-based test cases from an OpenAPI/GraphQL schema (independent evaluation in an ICSE 2022 paper; adopter lists are vendor-reported); Specmatic runs the spec as tests against the provider and as a stub for consumers, catching both sides of the drift. Rung 5 is the agentic frontier and deserves precision: Kiro's "Sync Files" action updates task lists from changed requirements on request, and its agent hooks can regenerate docs on save — useful, but user-initiated sync, not automatic drift detection; third-party posts that call it "drift detection" overstate what the vendor documents (H3 made the same correction).

Point-in-time warning — this shelf churns faster than most

Half the tools once cited for this chapter died while the chapter was being researched: Dredd (the original OpenAPI contract tester, out of Apiary/Oracle) is archived; Optic was acquired by Atlassian in April 2024 and its repo archived in January 2026; driftctl has been in maintenance mode since mid-2023; DeepDocs — a 2025 "AI agent that keeps your docs in sync" GitHub app — already carries a "this app has been deprecated" notice; Swimm, whose patented Auto-sync (code-coupled snippets flagged and auto-patched when the referenced code changes) remains the best-documented mechanism of its kind, pivoted the company to agentic-modernization services. All tool facts here are mid-2026 and need re-verification before a client meeting. The durable content is the rung, not the logo: a deterministic check in CI that executes something the documentation claims.

The pattern has a decade of prior art one floor down the stack. Infrastructure-as-code treats drift — the live system diverging from the declared configuration — as a first-class, detectable condition: terraform plan computes it on demand, and HCP Terraform's Health Assessments re-check it automatically about every 24 hours with refresh-only plans. Nobody in that world proposes fixing drift with a "please keep the infrastructure matching the config" guideline. Documentation is a declared configuration of understanding; it deserves the same machinery.

5A freshness policy for the AI era

Flag: the policy packaging is our synthesis

Each ingredient below is independently attested — Google's review-date reminders, Microsoft's ms.date, Anthropic's prune-and-test guidance, DOCER's CI check, I1's derived rules.
