The packaging into one per-category freshness policy with thresholds is Report 3's synthesis, not an industry standard — present it as ours, like the taxonomy and the write-permission matrix it extends.

Detection (§4) needs a policy to enforce. The taxonomy already tells us freshness means something different per category, so the policy writes itself as a third column on I1's table — what "fresh" means, and which check proves it:

CategoryFreshness contractDeterministic checkOn violation
durablenamed owner + last-reviewed date + review interval (90d default, per Google's precedent)rung-1/2 checks in CI; interval breach surfaces on the dashboardowner reviews: re-confirm, revise via PR, or demote honestly
derivedregenerated since its master last changedregenerate-and-diff in CI (I2's check)build fails; regenerate — never hand-patch
agent configzero stale code-element references; volatile facts excludedtwo-snapshot grep + lychee on CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md; reviewed in the same PR as renames/deletesbuild fails; fix in the refactor PR that broke it
AI-generated docslabel + generation timestamp + source + expiry dateexpiry scan in CI (a date comparison)expired = suspect (H3): revalidate and re-stamp, or delete
disposablenone — a TTL, not a freshness contractprune sweep (I1 §4)delete; it was never truth

Two design choices deserve defense. First, expiry means "suspect", not "auto-delete" — H3's semantics: an expired AI-generated doc stops being citable context until a human revalidates it, which is cheap insurance against "impressive but wrong" surviving on its polish. Second, the agent-config row gets the strictest contract — zero stale references, checked every build — because §2 showed it is the highest-leverage rot in the system (loaded every session, trusted completely, measured at 23% of repos) and because the check is nearly free: the references are code elements, and grep is grep.

The client's Phase-1 question — "what staleness threshold triggers an alarm, and can we measure it from day one?" — now has a concrete answer derived entirely from git: (a) stale-reference count in agent config files (alarm at >0 — cheap to hold at zero); (b) dead links/references across durable docs (lychee's count — alarm on increase, burn down the backlog); (c) review-interval breaches per durable (alarm per owner, not per repo); (d) the age gap between each durable doc's last commit and the last commit to the code it describes (a rough but automatable early-warning signal — a doc directory untouched for a year while its module churned weekly is §1's 4.7-year bucket forming in real time). None of these needs tooling the client doesn't already have.

6Documentation debt — triage, not guilt

Everything §4–§5 catch lands in a pile, and a brownfield client's pile is years deep on day one. The technical-debt frame earns its keep here because it forbids both bad responses: pretending the pile isn't there, and declaring bankruptcy ("the docs are useless, ignore them"). Fowler's quadrant (bliki, October 2009) — deliberate vs. inadvertent, prudent vs. reckless — sorts documentation debt as well as it sorts code debt. Prudent-deliberate: "we're shipping without updating the runbook; ticket filed, owner named" — debt as a financing decision. Reckless-inadvertent: the 23% of §2 — nobody decided anything; the drift just accrued, silently, in the files agents trust most. The entire point of §4's machinery is to convert inadvertent debt into deliberate debt: once a stale reference fails a build, ignoring it requires a decision someone owns, and I2's audit trail records who made it.

Triage per drifted artifact is I1 §6's promotion/demotion logic, applied at volume — three honest exits: delete or demote (the doc describes a world that no longer exists and nobody needs the history — most of the pile); regenerate (it's derived, or should be: fix the master, rerun the generator, and add the regenerate-and-diff check so it can't rot again); rewrite and own (it's genuinely durable: validate it, name an owner, stamp a review date, put future changes behind the PR gate). The state to refuse is the one I1 already refused: half-trusted, unowned, hand-patched — the middle state agents keep reading and compounding.

Agents themselves are the labor force that makes repayment tractable — with the division of labor §2 established. The deterministic detector produces the worklist (stale references, dead links, expired stamps); the agent drafts the repair, using git history to find what the reference became (DocPrism's filtered architecture and Treude & Baltes' proposed hybrid are both exactly this shape); a human gate merges, because every one of these repairs is a write to a durable artifact and I2's matrix does not care that the proposer was fixing documentation. M5 extends the same pattern to generating missing docs for legacy code (agent archaeology) — with the I1 rule that generated legacy docs are derived until a human validates and adopts them. What agents must not do is the naive sweep — "review all docs and fix anything outdated" — which DocPrism measured: 98% of documentation flagged, the real signal drowned, and a mountain of confident rewrites for the human gate to review. Point the agent at a grep hit, not at the corpus.

7Week one at the client — the drift audit

I3's week-one move rides on I1's classification sweep (which listed the artifacts) and I2's matrix (which assigned the gates); it adds the freshness column and the first checks. Concretely, in order of effort-to-payoff:

Do in week one
  • run lychee across the repo's markdown; count, don't fix — the number is the baseline
  • run the two-snapshot grep on CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / copilot-instructions; fix these immediately (small, high-leverage, §2)
  • wire both checks into CI as warnings; flip to failing once the baseline burns down
  • stamp every durable doc: owner, last-reviewed, interval (a header template, one PR)
  • label + timestamp + expiry on anything AI-generated the team already trusts
  • write the same-PR rule into CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md stewardship: renames and deletes update the config files that mention them, in the same PR
Refuse in week one
  • "we'll keep docs updated going forward" as the plan — advisory; it is how the client got here (E5)
  • an LLM sweep over the whole doc corpus — the 98% flag rate is the measured outcome
  • a doc-sync tool purchase as the first move — §4's graveyard; principle first, vendor later, if ever
  • rewriting the wiki — triage (§6), don't refinance everything at once
  • contract tests where no OpenAPI spec exists yet — rung 4 needs H1/H2's groundwork first
SDLC placement
  • drift checks live in CI (the E4/E5 verification loop), freshness review lives in the maintenance cadence, and the debt worklist feeds sprint planning like any other debt — documentation stops being a separate hygiene project and becomes part of the same delivery loop as code

8Where I3 sits in the framework

TopicConnection
E5 · governancethe advisory/deterministic distinction, applied to prose; the vendor now states it verbatim for CLAUDE.md vs hooks
I1 · artifact taxonomydrift is the failure mode the derived discipline prevents; §5's policy is a third column on I1's table; §6's exits are I1's promotion/demotion
I2 · write-permissionswho may fix what the checks catch; regenerate-and-diff is the derived row's freshness check; repairs to durables stay behind the human gate
I4 · memory systemsagent memory rots the same way config files do — validate-before-write is freshness at write time
I5 · progressive disclosureCLAUDE.md gardening in depth: short, load-bearing files are also the easiest to keep true
H3 · traceabilitydrift at the link level: suspect-flags are the freshness policy for links; expiry = suspect reuses its semantics
H4 · feedback loopa stale-doc-caused failure routes through the return channel to the doc's owner, not just the code
E4 · verification firstdocs-as-tests extends the verification loop to prose; doctests are literally tests
E7 · metricsDORA 2022's documentation-quality amplification; DORA 2025's AI-accessible internal data; §5's four measurables join the dashboard
P2 · PRDs with AIproduct durables drift too — the same policy applies across the SSOT boundary, enforced by review cadence instead of CI
B4 · agent archaeology (M5)generating docs where none exist — the debt-repayment labor force, with the derived-until-validated rule
M7 · securitya stale config file is an integrity problem: agents act on it — context-rot detection is also poisoning detection (ASI06 neighbours)

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Documentation drift was already endemic before AI — 28.9% of flagship repos carried references stale for 4.7 years on average — but agents change its physics: docs became input, loaded every session and acted on without skepticism, so a stale line no longer decays quietly but is re-injected into generation "without any visible error," and the config files agents trust most are measurably rotten in about a quarter of repositories. The countermeasure is E5's move applied to prose: freshness metadata and same-PR review as the advisory layer, and docs-as-tests as the deterministic one — executable examples, reference and link checks (a two-snapshot grep covers CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md), docs exercised against the product, and contract tests from the spec — each a build-failing check, because "keep docs updated" is how every client got here. Triage the resulting pile like the debt it is — delete or demote, regenerate, or rewrite-and-own; never the unowned middle — with deterministic detectors producing the worklist, agents drafting repairs, and humans gating the merge, since a documentation fix is still a write to a durable artifact.

10Further reading

Tan, Wagner & Treude — Detecting Outdated Code Element References in Software Repository Documentation
arXiv:2212.01479 · December 2022 · journal version in Empirical Software Engineering, 2023

The baseline and the tool (DOCER): 28.9% (265/918) of top GitHub projects with at least one outdated document, stale for 4.7 years on average.

Treude & Baltes — Context Rot in AI-Assisted Software Development
arXiv:2606.09090 · June 2026

DOCER pointed at agent config files: stale references in 23.0% of repos; the definition of config-file context rot, the "without any visible error" failure mode, and the two-snapshot CI grep.

Fluri, Würsch & Gall — Do Code and Comments Co-Evolve?
WCRE 2007 · ArgoUML, Azureus, JDT Core

97% of comment changes ship in the same revision as the code change — prose is maintained with the change or never.

Rios, Mendonça-Neto & Spínola — A tertiary study on technical debt
Information and Software Technology, vol. 102 · 2018 · with Alves et al.'s ontology (MTD)

Documentation debt as a recognized technical-debt type — the frame that moves stale docs from guilt to triage.

DORA — Documentation quality capability & the 2022 State of DevOps report
dora.dev/capabilities/documentation-quality

Quality documentation as an amplifier of every technical capability studied — the published per-capability lifts, and the reason the folklore "2.4×" doesn't appear here.

DORA — 2025 State of AI-Assisted Software Development
dora.dev/dora-report-2025 · September 2025

"AI doesn't fix a team; it amplifies what's already there" — and "AI-accessible internal data" among the seven capabilities that magnify AI's impact.

Winters, Manshreck & Wright — Software Engineering at Google, ch. 10 (Documentation)
O'Reilly 2020 · free at abseil.io/resources/swe-book

Docs treated as code: owned, reviewed, tracked — and freshness metadata that emails the owner when a page goes three months unreviewed.

Write the Docs — Docs as Code
writethedocs.org/guide/docs-as-code

The canonical definition of the substrate: same tools as code — VCS, review, plain text, automated tests.

Silva — Docs as Tests / Doc Detective
docsastests.com · Doc Detective, open source, actively released (v4.30.0, July 2026)

Documentation "shouldn't just inform — it should also verify": every documented procedure executed against the product in CI.

Xu, Wahab, Holmes & Lemieux — DocPrism
arXiv:2511.00215 · October 2025

Why naive LLM drift-detection fails (98% of docs flagged; abstraction read as inconsistency) and the filtered architecture that works — detection deterministic, judgment from the model.

Galster et al. — Harness Engineering for Agentic AI Coding Tools · Lulla et al. — On the Impact of AGENTS.md Files
AIware 2026 (arXiv:2602.14690) · JAWs 2026 @ ICSE (arXiv:2601.20404)

The population (4,768 context files across 90.6% of sampled agentic repos; CLAUDE.md 34.4%) and the payoff (AGENTS.md presence: ~29% lower runtime, ~17% fewer tokens) — why config freshness is worth a build check.

Hong, Troynikov & Huber — Context Rot: How Increasing Input Tokens Impacts LLM Performance
Chroma Research · July 14, 2025 · 18 models

The original sense of the term: degradation with input volume alone — the reason short, load-bearing context files beat exhaustive ones.

Anthropic — Claude Code best practices (CLAUDE.md guidance)
code.claude.com/docs · point-in-time mid-2026

"Treat CLAUDE.md like code: review it when things go wrong, prune it regularly, and test changes" — plus the exclusion list that keeps volatile facts out of always-loaded context.

wolfejam — Your AGENTS.md Is Already Stale — And Your Agent Trusts It Completely
dev.to · July 10, 2026 · practitioner post

The asymmetry in one line: a human discounts a stale README; an agent acts on it. "A stale cache is worse than an empty one."

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane A

The drift framing this chapter expands, and the freshness-policy synthesis flagged in §5.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I3-consistency-drift-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are I1 (the taxonomy drift violates), I2 (who may fix what the checks catch), I5 (context-file gardening in depth), H3 (drift at the link level and suspect semantics), E4/E5 (the verification loop and the advisory/deterministic line), E7 (the DORA evidence), and M5 (agent archaeology as debt repayment).

I4 · DEEP DIVE

Memory Systems for Agents

0Why it matters

Every artifact M4 has governed so far — specs, ADRs, context files, generated docs — shares one property: a human can point at it. This chapter is about the artifact class that grows while nobody is pointing. An agent's defining handicap is amnesia: the context window is working memory, and when the session ends, everything in it is gone. A human engineer compounds knowledge — every debugging session leaves a residue of understanding; a vanilla agent re-learns the codebase every morning, forever. Whatever persists across sessions persists because something wrote it somewhere — a progress file, a memory directory, a managed store — which means memory is not a model property you enable but an information-architecture decision you govern. That framing is the chapter. I1 filed memory under its grey zones (durable in effect, agent-written by definition); I2 gave it a row of its own in the write-permission matrix — the fourth write-mode, "write through validation" — and promised the depth here. The depth has two sides. The capability side: memory is what makes long-horizon work coherent at all — an agent that tracks "for the last 1,234 steps I've been training my Pokémon in Route 1" can pursue a goal for thousands of steps; one that cannot, loops. The liability side: a memory file is the one input the agent trusts completely, wrote itself, and no human reviewed — which is why memory poisoning is a named entry in OWASP's agentic Top 10 (ASI06) and why the write-mode exists. Both sides land on the same desk: whoever owns the harness owns what the agent is allowed to remember.

What this chapter gives you
  • a working typology: four operational layers of agent memory, and the academic frame behind them (§1)
  • the vendor landscape, point-in-time mid-2026 — file-based, database/graph, platform — and what the Mem0-vs-Zep dispute teaches about memory benchmarks (§2)
  • the three long-horizon techniques in depth: compaction, structured note-taking, sub-agents (§3)
  • memory as the governed fourth write-mode: git-versioned memory, provenance, and the promotion path (§4)
  • the threat model: ASI06, the research attacks, the documented real-world demos, and the defense stack (§5)
  • the org-store decision: when a managed memory store earns its keep against plain files in the repo (§6)
  • a week-one memory setup for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E2 — context engineering: the window this machinery exists to protect
  • I1 — the taxonomy; memory as its hardest grey zone
  • I2 — the write-permission matrix this chapter's row comes from
  • I3 — staleness: memory drifts like every other artifact
  • I5 — progressive disclosure: keeping what you remember loadable
  • M7 — the security module; ASI06 in the full threat landscape

1What agent memory is — a working typology

Strip the vendor language and agent memory sorts into four operational layers, distinguished by lifetime and by who can see them:

SESSION

Working memory

one session · the window itself

The context window: the conversation, tool results, scratch reasoning. Lives for one session, dies with it. E2's territory — managed by retrieval and shedding, not by storage.

FILES

Persistent memory files

agent-written · governed (§4)

A directory the agent reads and writes across sessions — Anthropic's /memories, Claude Code's memory directory, a Memory Bank. Survives resets; steers every future session that loads it.

PROJECT

Cross-session project state

progress files · git history

Progress files, plans, NOTES.md — plus git history itself, the memory system every repo already has: every commit is a remembered decision with provenance for free.

ORG

Organizational stores

shared · audited · scale (§6)

Managed stores shared across agents, repos and teams — vector databases, knowledge graphs, platform memory. The layer where scale arrives and where governance gets hardest (§6).

The academic frame underneath is CoALA — "Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents" (Sumers, Yao, Narasimhan & Griffiths, arXiv:2309.02427, TMLR 2024) — which gives language agents four memory types borrowed from cognitive science: working memory ("active and readily available information as symbolic variables for the current decision cycle"), episodic ("experience from earlier decision cycles"), semantic ("an agent's knowledge about the world and itself"), and procedural — which, in CoALA's sharpest observation, comes in two forms: "implicit knowledge stored in the LLM weights, and explicit knowledge written in the agent's code." Read that against the harness and the mapping is direct: a session summary is episodic; a memory file of project facts is semantic; and CLAUDE.md rules, hooks and Skills are explicit procedural memory — the harness itself is a memory layer, which is why I2 guards writes to it so hard. The research lineage behind each type is short and worth knowing: Generative Agents (Park et al., arXiv:2304.03442, UIST 2023) built the canonical episodic design — a "memory stream" of timestamped observations, retrieved by a weighted score of recency, importance and relevance, periodically synthesized into higher-level "reflections"; Reflexion (Shinn et al., arXiv:2303.11366, NeurIPS 2023) showed episodic memory driving capability — agents "verbally reflect on task feedback signals, then maintain their own reflective text in an episodic memory buffer," lifting HumanEval pass@1 to 91% against GPT-4's then-state-of-the-art 80%; and Voyager (Wang et al., arXiv:2305.16291, 2023) built procedural memory as "an ever-growing skill library of executable code" whose skills are "temporally extended, interpretable, and compositional" and — the phrase that matters for harness design — alleviate catastrophic forgetting. Voyager's skill library is the direct ancestor of Skills (I5): remembered procedure stored as reviewable code, not as weights.

The engineering frame is MemGPT (Packer et al., arXiv:2310.08560, October 2023) — "Towards LLMs as Operating Systems" — which treated the context window as RAM and everything else as disk, "virtual context management" as paging. Its two tiers: main context (system instructions, a self-editable working context, and a FIFO message queue) and external context (searchable recall storage and archival storage). The design's real contribution is agency over memory itself: "Memory edits and retrieval are entirely self-directed: MemGPT autonomously updates and searches through its own memory." A terminology note that keeps citations honest: the widely-repeated triad "core / recall / archival memory" is the product vocabulary of Letta — the company the MemGPT project became (UC Berkeley spinout, September 2024) — not the paper's; the paper says "working context" and calls the outer tiers storage. Small thing, but this workbook keeps paper terms with papers and product terms with products.

Three measurements calibrate expectations before any architecture is chosen. Capability: memory demonstrably works — Reflexion's 11-point HumanEval lift is memory and nothing else. Reliability: sustained memory is hard — LongMemEval (Wu et al., arXiv:2410.10813, ICLR 2025) benchmarked five core abilities (information extraction, multi-session reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, abstention) and found "commercial chat assistants and long-context LLMs showing a 30% accuracy drop on memorizing information across sustained interactions." And economics, from the one controlled coding-specific experiment we know of (Markus Sandelin, March 2026 — practitioner-grade: one run per condition, and the persistent-memory tool tested was the author's own, so read it as directional): across three tasks of rising complexity on a real FastAPI codebase, memory left code quality essentially flat, made the simple task slower and costlier than no memory at all, and paid off only as complexity rose — 22–32% cost and 28–40% fewer turns on the hardest task, ~14–16% averaged. Memory is an investment with a break-even point, not a free upgrade — which is also the honest answer to "should every task load everything we know?" The gaps between these results — memory helps, memory systems fail, payoff depends on task shape — are exactly the space §3's engineering and §4's governance exist to close.

2The landscape — three families, and a cautionary benchmark war

Everything on the market in mid-2026 falls into three families, and the sorting matters more than the logos because each family implies a different governance story. All of this section is point-in-time and moves fast; re-verify before a client meeting.

File-based, repo-resident. The family the rest of this chapter defaults to, because its artifacts are already inside the team's git/PR/review machinery. Anthropic's memory tool (type memory_20250818, generally available on the Messages API — "no beta header is required") is deliberately minimal: purely file-based storage under /memories, six operations (view, create, str_replace, insert, delete, rename), executed client-side — "Claude requests file operations, and your application executes them" — so the application owns storage, and the harness owns governance. Claude Code's auto memory applies the shape inside the IDE-agent: a per-project directory with a MEMORY.md index and topic files (§4 takes the layout apart). Cline's Memory Bank is the same idea as a methodology rather than a tool: six prescribed markdown files with distinct lifecycle roles (projectbrief, productContext, systemPatterns, techContext, activeContext, progress) and a documented dependency hierarchy — I1 noted it as the triad reinvented under other names. Cursor's Memories (beta with 1.0 in June 2025, GA in 1.2 that July) adds the missing governance verb: a sidecar model proposes memories from chat, and the user approves — "user approvals for background-generated memories to preserve trust," in the changelog's words. Windsurf's Cascade auto-generates memories to a local per-workspace store. The through-line of the whole family: memory as reviewable text, close to the code it describes.

The coding-agent platforms are converging on the same governance, independently. GitHub's Copilot Memory (public preview, 2026) is two-tier — repository-level facts shared with everyone on the repo, private user-level preferences — and it ships two of this chapter's arguments as product mechanics: facts "are stored with citations pointing to the code that supports them," and when one becomes relevant Copilot "checks those citations against the current branch to confirm the information is still accurate" — I3's staleness check, run at retrieval time — while "any stored fact or preference that goes unused is automatically deleted after 28 days" — TTL as the default, with repo owners reviewing facts and enterprise admins holding export and bulk-delete. Cognition's Devin has Knowledge: items carry a trigger description for retrieval, pin to no repo, one repo or all repos, and scope to the organization — with an explicit promotion step ("if an organization-level knowledge item proves useful enough to share across your entire enterprise, you can promote it directly"), a promotion ladder shipped as UI, rhyming with §4's. Both are vendor designs and point-in-time; the convergence — provenance, re-validation, TTL, scoped promotion — is the signal.

Database and graph engines. Where the file family stores what the agent wrote, this family processes it: extraction, deduplication, retrieval, temporal reasoning. Letta — the company the MemGPT project became — maintains the self-editing memory-blocks design and added the most interesting recent idea, sleep-time compute (Lin et al., arXiv:2504.13171, April 2025): spend model time between sessions — the paper shows offline pre-reasoning cutting test-time compute ~5×; the product frames it as a background agent revising memory while the main agent is idle. Memory that is curated offline instead of mid-task is a genuinely new point in the design space (and, note, a second unattended write path for §5 to worry about). Mem0 (paper: arXiv:2504.19413) is an extraction pipeline over a store; Zep builds on Graphiti, "a temporally-aware knowledge graph engine," so facts carry validity intervals and a query can ask what was true then. LangChain's LangMem SDK (February 2025) ships the CoALA vocabulary as an API — semantic facts, episodic few-shot examples, procedural prompt-updates. All are credible engineering; all quote benchmark numbers, which brings us to the war.

When the vendors benchmark each other — the Mem0/Zep dispute

Mem0's paper claims, on the LoCoMo benchmark, "26% relative improvements in the LLM-as-a-Judge metric over OpenAI" plus "91% lower p95 latency" and >90% token savings versus full-context — and it also benchmarked Zep, unfavorably. Zep's team answered with a rebuttal ("Is Mem0 Really SOTA in Agent Memory?") arguing the comparison misconfigured Zep — timestamps never passed, searches serialized — and re-reporting its own score around 75%; a counter-analysis on Zep's own tracker then argued Zep's headline 84% LoCoMo claim drops to 58.44% once a category designated for exclusion is removed. We do not adjudicate this: the point is that every number in it comes from a party selling the thing measured, the benchmark itself bends under configuration choices, and the honest reading is E7's: memory-vendor benchmarks are vendor claims until a disinterested replication exists. Two later footnotes sharpen the lesson. Sandelin's coding benchmark (§1) names the metric slippage directly: headline "80–95% token savings" figures measure compression of the memory footprint — "not whether a downstream task completes faster." And the benchmark frontier keeps moving — BEAM (third-party, 2026) tests memory at 1M- and 10M-token scales, where every published score so far is, again, vendor-quoted. The independently useful datum stands apart: LongMemEval's ~30% sustained-memory drop (§1) — the problem is real; the leaderboard is marketing.

Platform memory. ChatGPT's memory is the consumer-scale reference: user-directed "saved memories" (February 2024), then automatic "reference chat history" (April 2025) — memory the platform writes for you, stored outside your governance perimeter entirely. For engineering teams it is mostly a contrast object: it demonstrates both the product value of remembering and the governance ceiling of memory you cannot diff, review or gate. The enterprise questions it raises — whose data, what retention, which audit — are §6's.

3The three long-horizon techniques, in depth

E2 introduced the toolkit for work that outlives a context window; this section is the practitioner detail. The source is Anthropic's engineering guidance ("Effective context engineering for AI agents," September 29, 2025 — published, not coincidentally, the same day as the memory tool), and the techniques are three, chosen by task shape.

Compaction summarizes a conversation approaching the window limit and reinitializes with the summary — and the published detail is what makes it copyable: "The model preserves architectural decisions, unresolved bugs, and implementation details while discarding redundant tool outputs or messages. The agent can then continue with this compressed context plus the five most recently accessed files." Note what that list is: a priority order for what deserves to survive a reset — decisions first, open problems second, mechanics last, plus a small working set. Compaction's cost is cache-shaped (E2): it deliberately rewrites history, which invalidates the prompt cache from the first changed byte, so it batches best at milestones rather than eagerly. Its failure mode is subtle — a bad summary is a small memory poisoning of your own making, confidently wrong in exactly the way §5 warns about, which is why milestone compaction plus a durable progress file beats aggressive automatic compression. The cache economics are measured now rather than folklore: across 500+ agent sessions on three providers, prompt caching "reduces API costs by 41-80% and improves time to first token by 13-31%" — but naive full-context caching "can paradoxically increase latency," and the working rule that falls out is one of placement: keep the stable prefix byte-stable, put per-turn-mutating content — memory digests included — at the end of the prompt, and exclude volatile tool results from the cached region (Lumer et al., arXiv:2601.06007, January 2026; E2's Manus rules, now with independent numbers).

Structured note-taking moves state out of the window entirely: the agent writes progress to a file, drops it from attention, and re-reads it when needed. The canonical demonstration is Claude playing Pokémon: the agent "maintains precise tallies across thousands of game steps — tracking objectives like 'for the last 1,234 steps I've been training my Pokémon in Route 1'" — coherence no context window could hold. In engineering work the note file is the progress/plan disposables I1 already classified; what the memory tool adds is a protocol, injected as system-prompt text whenever the tool is enabled, and its two commandments are worth quoting because they encode the whole discipline: "IMPORTANT: ALWAYS VIEW YOUR MEMORY DIRECTORY BEFORE DOING ANYTHING ELSE." — read before you act, so the session builds on recorded state instead of re-deriving it — and "ASSUME INTERRUPTION: Your context window might be reset at any moment, so you risk losing any progress that is not recorded in your memory directory." — write early, because anything un-written is already lost. The curation clause completes it: keep contents "up-to-date, coherent and organized," rename or delete files that are no longer relevant, "do not create new files unless necessary" — I1's pruning discipline, stated by the vendor as agent instructions.

Sub-agent architectures shed context by delegation: "Each subagent might explore extensively, using tens of thousands of tokens or more, but returns only a condensed, distilled summary of its work (often 1,000-2,000 tokens)." The main thread stays clean; the exploration cost is paid in a disposable window. The discipline that makes it work is the summary contract — the subagent's report is the only thing that survives, so its quality bounds the whole pattern (E3's harness patterns formalize this).

Choosing: compaction for a long conversational flow without natural milestones; note-taking for milestone-based iteration, where the progress file survives any reset; sub-agents for parallel exploration and read-heavy research where only conclusions need to return. They compose — a long project typically runs note-taking as its spine, sub-agents for its research bursts, and compaction as the emergency valve — and all three exist for the same reason: the window is the scarcest resource in the system (E2), and memory is how you stop paying window-rent on things that are true tomorrow.

4The governed write-mode — provenance, validation, promotion

I2's matrix gave memory its row: write through validation — agent-writable like a disposable, durable in effect like a spec, therefore governed like neither. This section is that row unpacked into machinery, and the shape to keep in mind is a pipeline with gates, not a directory with rules.

The substrate: files in the repo, versioned in git. The practical default for engineering teams is the simplest one: the memory directory is plain files, and it is committed. Git-versioning memory buys, for free, everything §5's defenses will ask for — every memory write is diffable (reviewable in a PR like any other change), attributable (the commit says which session wrote it), and revertible (rollback to a pre-compromise state is git revert, machinery the team already trusts). This default is our synthesis, not vendor doctrine — Anthropic's memory tool deliberately leaves storage to the application — but every component is standard practice, and the alternative (an opaque store with none of those properties) has to justify itself against it (§6).

The index discipline. Claude Code's documented memory layout is the reference shape for keeping persistent memory loadable: a per-project memory directory containing "a MEMORY.md entrypoint and optional topic files." The entrypoint is a concise index loaded into every session — with a hard cutoff: "The first 200 lines of MEMORY.md, or the first 25KB, whichever comes first, are loaded at the start of every conversation" — while detail lives in topic files (debugging.md, api-conventions.md) read on demand. That is I5's progressive disclosure applied to memory: pay for the index always, pay for the body only when relevant. The cutoff is worth teaching precisely — it is a load limit, not a style suggestion; the 201st line is not "discouraged," it is silently never seen again, the failure mode I3 would call drift by design. (Keep this distinct from the adjacent guidance to target CLAUDE.md files under 200 lines — that one is a style target for a file humans write; this one is a mechanical truncation of a file the agent writes.)

Provenance and validation at write time. The memory tool's documentation prescribes the mechanical layer (§5 gives the threat it answers): validate every path against escape from the memory directory, strip sensitive data before the handler writes, "track memory file sizes and cap how large a file can grow," and "periodically delete memory files that haven't been accessed in a long time." On top of that mechanical floor, the write-mode adds provenance: a memory entry should say where it came from — which session, which task, from whose instruction — because §5's whole threat model turns on the difference between "the user told me" and "a webpage told me." Frontmatter with a source field costs nothing at write time and is the only thing that makes a poisoned entry findable later. This layering — vendor-documented mechanics, provenance and review as the loop around them — is our synthesis, flagged as such.

The promotion path. The most consequential rule in this section: memory files steer sessions, but steering files rule the repo — and the path between them is a one-way gate. An observation born in a session (disposable) may be recorded into the memory directory (governed, validated write); a memory entry that proves durable — a convention, a hard-won constraint, a decision — may be proposed for CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md or an ADR; but that promotion is a PR a human merges, never a write the agent performs. The gradient runs from cheap-and-free toward reviewed-and-durable, and each step up adds a gate. Run backwards it also defines demotion: a memory entry contradicted by the codebase is deleted, not debated — I3's staleness logic applied to the agent's own notes, and the multi-session benchmarks say demotion is not hygiene but the main event: agents "fail primarily through misestimation of user state, treating prior session history as a reliable proxy for current context rather than stale information requiring re-validation" (Momento — Merin, Anugraha, Purwarianti & Winata, arXiv:2606.00832, mid-2026). The dominant failure mode of memory is over-trusting it, which reframes the whole discipline: govern re-validation, not just capture. The promotion path is our synthesis; its parts (I1's categories, I2's gates, the vendor's validation floor) are individually attested.

Run forward at team scale, the promotion path also has a practitioner name: compound engineering. Every's engineering team closes each cycle with a "compound" step they call the money step — "after we do a code review… we'll ask the agent to look at the comments, summarize them, and store them for later," as prompt-based rules living in the codebase and plugins, automatically distributed to the whole team (Shipper & Klaassen, December 2025; they ship the workflow as a Claude Code plugin). Strip the branding and it is exactly §4's pipeline with the loop closed: session experience → reviewed rule → shared steering asset — memory as the mechanism by which the team's harness appreciates, not just the agent's recall.

5The threat model — memory poisoning and its defenses

The reason memory gets a governed write-mode instead of a convention is that it is the one artifact class whose corruption is designed to outlive the attack. OWASP's Top 10 for Agentic Applications (GenAI Security Project, December 9, 2025) names it ASI06 — Memory & Context Poisoning: poisoning "occurs when persistent memory, retrieval, or context is shaped to mislead future steps," and it is "the agentic version of data poisoning, but consequences appear at runtime rather than training time." Three properties separate it from ordinary prompt injection, and each defeats a familiar defense. It is persistent — session-scoped review never sees it, because the malicious line is already in storage when the session starts. It is temporally decoupled — planted today, triggered weeks later, so incident response looks in the wrong time window. And it spreads through ordinary reads — any agent that loads the store inherits the payload, so one write can steer a fleet.

The research attacks put numbers on feasibility. AgentPoison (Chen, Xiang, Xiao, Song & Li, arXiv:2407.12784, NeurIPS 2024) — "the first backdoor attack targeting generic and RAG-based LLM agents" — poisons the agent's long-term memory or knowledge base with a handful of trigger-optimized entries and reports "an average attack success rate higher than 80%" at "less than 0.1% poison rate," demonstrated against an autonomous-driving agent, a knowledge-intensive QA agent and a healthcare EHR agent. One entry in a thousand is not a needle a review will find. MINJA (Dong et al., arXiv:2503.03704, 2025) removes the last comfort — that the attacker at least needs write access: "The attacker injects malicious records into the memory bank by only interacting with the agent via queries and output observations." Crafted queries walk the agent into writing the attacker's "bridging steps" into its own memory (an injection success rate of 98.2% and attack success of 76.8% on average, across GPT-4/GPT-4o agents including an EHR agent). The attack surface is the conversation itself — if the agent writes memory from interactions, every interaction is a potential write. The systematization arrived in mid-2026: "From Untrusted Input to Trusted Memory" (Dash, Ge, Jain, Shah & Shang, arXiv:2606.04329) maps four memory write channels and nine structural vulnerabilities into a taxonomy of six attack classes with a dedicated benchmark (MPBench), and hands designers two conclusions this chapter builds on: "agents designed to write and retrieve memory more aggressively are more exploitable" — §3's auto-capture convenience has a security price — and "existing prompt injection defenses fail to cover memory poisoning attacks," so the defense has to live on the write path, not in the prompt.

And the pattern has left the lab twice, both times documented by Johann Rehberger. SpAIware (September 2024): indirect prompt injection planted persistent instructions in ChatGPT's memory via the macOS app, turning memory into spyware — "the data exfiltration vulnerability became a lot more dangerous as it now spawns across chat conversations," every future session exfiltrating to the attacker until OpenAI patched it (app version 1.2024.247). Gemini memory manipulation (February 2025): a poisoned document plants instructions that fire on the user's own trigger words ("yes," "sure") — the technique Rehberger named delayed tool invocation — writing false long-term memories that Google assessed as "an abuse-related risk with low likelihood and low impact." OWASP's ASI06 write-up cites the Gemini case by name; the loop from lab to standard closed in under a year.

The defense stack is E5's ladder applied to the memory pipeline, and it is honest to say up front that only its bottom layer is vendor-documented; the loop above it is assembled practice (I2 flagged the same). Mechanical (deterministic, vendor-documented): path containment — "validate that all paths start with /memories," canonicalize, reject traversal including URL-encoded forms — plus sensitive-data stripping before the handler writes, file-size caps, and expiry of long-unread files. Structural (deterministic, from I2): the memory directory is the only agent-writable persistent path (deny-rules on steering files), memory is git-versioned so every write is diffable and revertible, and the promotion gate (§4) means nothing memory-resident reaches a durable steering file without a human merge — which converts a successful poisoning from "steers the fleet" to "sits in a reviewable diff." Process (advisory, ours by synthesis): provenance on every entry so an audit can ask "where did this belief come from"; review of memory diffs at promotion time, not per-write; and rollback drills — since memory is in git, recovering from a poisoned week is a revert, provided someone can name the last clean commit. The asymmetry to teach: the attacker needs one unvalidated write; the defense needs the write path to be narrow, the storage to be diffable, and the blast radius to stop at the PR gate.

For teams that want the deterministic version off the shelf, OWASP itself ships one: the Agent Memory Guard project — a separate, official OWASP project positioned as the reference implementation for ASI06 — runs inline on every memory read/write and, in its own words, "validates memory integrity using cryptographic baselines (SHA-256 hashing)," "detects injection attempts, sensitive data leakage, protected key modifications, rapid changes, and size anomalies," "enforces declarative YAML security policies on memory read/write operations," and "captures snapshots for forensic analysis and enables rollback to known-good states" (middleware for LangChain, LlamaIndex and CrewAI; point-in-time mid-2026). Note it is the same four moves this section derived from first principles — integrity baselines, anomaly detection on the write path, policy as configuration, snapshot-and-rollback — which is the reassuring kind of convergence. One honesty note on attribution: the canonical ASI06 entry's own mitigation list was not verbatim-reachable when this chapter was written, so concrete defenses here are attributed to Anthropic's tool docs, the Agent Memory Guard project, and this workbook's I2 machinery — not quoted from the Top 10 entry itself.

6Files in the repo or a managed store — the decision

The open question every client asks once agents multiply: keep memory as plain files in the repo, or adopt a managed store (§2's second family)? The criterion this workbook proposes — our synthesis, assembled from I1–I3's machinery — is a default plus three tests.

The default is files, and it is not a compromise. Files in the repo inherit, at zero integration cost, every property this chapter has needed: diffable writes, provenance by commit, review by PR, rollback by revert, deny-rules by path, staleness checks by grep (I3), and retrieval by the agentic search the coding agent already does best (I6). A managed store starts from zero on each of those and must rebuild them as product features — audit logs for provenance, snapshots for rollback, admin roles for gates — inside a second governance system the team must learn, configure and trust. For a single team on a single repo, the store solves problems the team does not have, at the price of problems it did not have either: a new privileged attack surface (§5, at organizational blast radius), a staleness regime with no same-PR rule (I3's problem, minus its cheapest fix), and vendor numbers from a benchmark war (§2).

The three tests that flip the default. First, the boundary test: does the memory have to cross repos, teams or agent runtimes? Files are repo-scoped by construction; the moment "what we learned about the payments API" must reach five repos and a support bot, a shared store is the honest architecture (the alternative — copy-paste across repos — is I1's unowned-copy drift). Second, the scale test: has retrieval outgrown lexical search — thousands of entries, conceptual queries, cross-referencing — so that I6's hybrid argument applies to memory itself? Third, the compliance test: does memory content fall under retention, audit or privacy regimes (it will, the moment it contains customer or incident data) that demand queryable audit and enforced retention beyond what git provides? Two or more tests positive, evaluate a store; none, keep files and revisit quarterly. And if a store does come in, the write-mode comes with it: I2's rule that where path-gates do not exist, the gate moves to identity — a scoped service account per agent, native audit streams, a named human reading them — plus §4's promotion rule intact: nothing flows from the store into a steering file without a human merge. The store changes where memory lives, never who may promote it.

One compliance wrinkle belongs on the table before any store decision, because it cuts both ways. The moment agent memory contains personal data — a customer name in a debugging note is enough — GDPR's right to erasure applies to it, and not only to raw session logs: a derived memory ("user prefers X," extracted from a deleted conversation) is personal data the erasure must reach, which is an awkward query to run against a memory store that was never designed to answer "everything derived from this person." Pulling the other way, the EU AI Act's logging provisions (Art. 12/19 — automatic recording of events, retention of at least six months for high-risk systems; T4 covers the detail) want agent activity records kept. Classify memory content at write time — personal vs technical, log vs derived fact — or inherit a delete-vs-retain conflict with no per-entry way to resolve it. Framing this as a memory-design requirement is our synthesis; the two regulations it derives from are real and already in force on the timeline traj-compliance tracks.

7Week one at the client

Memory setup at a brownfield client is a half-day of decisions and an hour of mechanics, and it should happen in week one — because the alternative is not "no memory," it is unguided memory: capitalized Markdown files accumulating at the repo root (I1 measured the reflex) and agent-written state nobody classified, versioned or gated. The sequence:

Decide (half a day, with the tech lead)
  • one named memory home per repo — the tool's default if there is one, else .claude/memory/ or equivalent; nothing memory-like lives anywhere else
  • git-versioned: memory commits with the work, so writes are diffable, attributable, revertible (§4)
  • the promotion rule, written down: memory → CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md/ADR only via PR a human merges; never a direct agent write (I2's deny-rules enforce it)
  • the org-store question answered with §6's criterion — for week one the answer is almost always "files, revisit at scale"
Wire (an hour, in the harness)
  • index + topic-file layout (MEMORY.md entrypoint; detail on demand — §4); mind the load cutoff
  • the validation floor in the memory handler: path containment, sensitive-data strip, size caps, expiry (§5, vendor-documented)
  • provenance frontmatter on new entries: source session, task, instruction origin
  • TTL/pruning for session disposables (I1); a quarterly "memory gardening" pass on what remains
  • a rollback drill on the calendar: can the team name and revert to a last-known-clean memory state?

The check that closes the week is the same one I2 and I3 use: try it. Have a session write a memory entry, watch it arrive as a diffable commit with provenance; have the agent propose a CLAUDE.md change from memory, watch it arrive as a PR and not a write; plant a deliberately wrong memory line in a branch, and confirm a later session acts on it — the last one is the demonstration that makes §5 land with a client audience better than any slide.

8How I4 maps to the framework

TopicWhat it contributes to this chapter
E2 · context engineeringthe window as scarce resource; cache economics of compaction; reference-don't-copy
E3 · harness patternsinitializer/progress-file patterns; the subagent summary contract
E5 · governancethe advisory/deterministic ladder the defense stack climbs (§5)
I1 · taxonomymemory as the grey zone: durable in effect, agent-written by definition
I2 · write-permissionsthe fourth write-mode this chapter unpacks; deny-rules and the PR gate
I3 · driftmemory staleness = context rot in the agent's own notes; demotion discipline
I5 · progressive disclosureindex + topic files; Skills as procedural memory kept loadable
I6 · retrievalwhether memory is grepped or embedded is I6's agentic-search argument again
M7 · securityASI06 in the full agentic threat landscape; identity-scoped store access
Report 3the memory-typology and update-protocol research notes this chapter expands

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Agent memory is an information-architecture decision, not a model feature: four layers by lifetime — window, persistent files, cross-session state, organizational store — with the harness itself as procedural memory, and the research base (Reflexion's 11-point HumanEval lift on one side, LongMemEval's 30% sustained-memory drop on the other) saying memory pays and memory systems fail, which is why it gets engineering and governance rather than trust. The working default is files in the repo, git-versioned, with an index kept loadable, a validated write path, provenance on entries, and one non-negotiable rule — memory may propose, only a human merge promotes anything into a steering file — because the threat is measured (poisoning at 80% success from 0.1% poison rates, query-only injection, and two documented in-the-wild demos), persistent, and designed to outlive the session that planted it. Buy a managed store only when memory must cross repo boundaries, outgrow lexical retrieval, or meet compliance-grade retention — and when you do, move the gate from paths to identity and keep the promotion rule intact, because the store changes where memory lives, never who may promote it.

10Further reading

Anthropic — Memory tool documentation
platform.claude.com docs · GA, tool type memory_20250818 · point-in-time mid-2026

The minimal file-based design: six operations, client-side execution, the injected protocol ("ALWAYS VIEW YOUR MEMORY DIRECTORY…", "ASSUME INTERRUPTION…"), and the documented validation floor — path containment, sensitive-data stripping, size caps, expiry.

Anthropic — Effective context engineering for AI agents
anthropic.com/engineering · September 29, 2025

The three long-horizon techniques with the copyable details: what compaction preserves, the Pokémon note-taking demonstration, and the 1–2k-token subagent summary contract.

Claude Code docs — How Claude remembers your project
code.claude.com/docs/en/memory · point-in-time mid-2026

The reference layout for loadable memory: MEMORY.md entrypoint (first 200 lines / 25KB loaded every session) plus on-demand topic files.

Packer et al. — MemGPT: Towards LLMs as Operating Systems
arXiv:2310.08560 · October 2023 · now Letta

The paging frame: main context vs external context, self-directed memory editing. Note the paper says "working context / recall storage / archival storage" — the core/recall/archival-memory triad is Letta product vocabulary.

Sumers, Yao, Narasimhan & Griffiths — Cognitive Architectures for Language Agents (CoALA)
arXiv:2309.02427 · TMLR 2024

The four-type frame — working, episodic, semantic, procedural — including procedural memory as the agent's code: the observation that makes the harness a memory layer.

Park et al. — Generative Agents · Shinn et al. — Reflexion · Wang et al. — Voyager
arXiv:2304.03442 (UIST 2023) · arXiv:2303.11366 (NeurIPS 2023) · arXiv:2305.16291 (2023)

The research lineage: the memory stream with recency/importance/relevance retrieval; the episodic buffer of self-reflections (HumanEval 91% vs 80%); the skill library as procedural memory that "alleviates catastrophic forgetting."

Wu et al. — LongMemEval
arXiv:2410.10813 · ICLR 2025

The sobering measurement: five memory abilities, and a ~30% accuracy drop for commercial assistants on sustained interactions — the independent datum in a field of vendor numbers.

OWASP GenAI Security Project — Top 10 for Agentic Applications
genai.owasp.org · December 9, 2025

ASI06 "Memory & Context Poisoning": persistent, temporally decoupled, spreading through ordinary reads — with the Gemini memory attack cited as the worked example.

Chen et al. — AgentPoison · Dong et al. — MINJA
arXiv:2407.12784 (NeurIPS 2024) · arXiv:2503.03704 (2025)

The feasibility numbers: >80% attack success from <0.1% poison rates; and memory injection through queries alone — no write access required.

Rehberger — SpAIware & Hacking Gemini's Memory
embracethered.com · September 20, 2024 · February 10, 2025

Both documented in-the-wild demonstrations: persistent exfiltration via ChatGPT memory (fixed in 1.2024.247), and delayed tool invocation planting false Gemini memories.

Lin et al. — Sleep-time Compute: Beyond Inference Scaling at Test-time
arXiv:2504.13171 · April 2025 · Letta/UC Berkeley

Thinking between sessions: offline pre-reasoning that cuts test-time compute ~5× — productized as a background agent that revises memory while the main agent idles.

Mem0 (arXiv:2504.19413) · Zep (arXiv:2501.13956) · the LoCoMo dispute
blog.getzep.com rebuttal · getzep/zep-papers issue #5 · 2025

The benchmark war, kept as a teaching object: every number vendor-sourced, the benchmark configuration-sensitive, and the honest posture — treat memory leaderboards as marketing until independently replicated.

Cline — Memory Bank · Cursor — Memories (GA in 1.2) · LangChain — LangMem SDK
docs.cline.bot · cursor.com/changelog/1-2 · langchain.com/blog (February 18, 2025) · point-in-time mid-2026

The convention family: six lifecycle-named files; sidecar-proposed memories with user approval; and CoALA's taxonomy shipped as an API.

GitHub Docs — Copilot Memory · Cognition — Devin Knowledge
docs.github.com (public preview) · docs.devin.ai · point-in-time mid-2026

The governance convergence in product form: repo-level facts with citations re-checked against the current branch and a 28-day unused-fact TTL; knowledge items with trigger descriptions, repo pinning, and an org→enterprise promotion step.

Merin, Anugraha, Purwarianti & Winata — Momento
arXiv:2606.00832 · mid-2026

Multi-session, tool-mediated evaluation; the finding this chapter's demotion discipline stands on — agents treat "prior session history as a reliable proxy for current context rather than stale information requiring re-validation."

Dash, Ge, Jain, Shah & Shang — From Untrusted Input to Trusted Memory
arXiv:2606.04329 · June 2026 · with MPBench

The systematization of memory poisoning: four write channels, nine structural vulnerabilities, six attack classes — and evidence that aggressive memory operations raise exploitability while prompt-injection defenses don't transfer.

Lumer et al. — Don't Break the Cache
arXiv:2601.06007 · January 2026 · 500+ agent sessions, three providers

The measured cache economics behind §3's placement rule: 41–80% cost and 13–31% TTFT improvements, and why naively caching everything can backfire.

Sandelin — a controlled benchmark of AI memory in coding agents
medium.com/@mrsandelin · March 2026 · practitioner-grade; author tests his own tool — directional

The coding-specific calibration: quality flat, simple tasks hurt, complex tasks 22–32% cheaper — and the "compression is not task efficiency" critique of vendor savings claims.

Shipper & Klaassen — Compound Engineering: How Every Codes With Agents
every.to · December 11, 2025

The promotion path run as a team practice: code-review comments summarized into stored, repo-distributed rules — "the money step."

OWASP — Agent Memory Guard
owasp.org/www-project-agent-memory-guard · reference implementation for ASI06 · point-in-time mid-2026

The deterministic defense stack off the shelf: SHA-256 integrity baselines, write-path anomaly detection, YAML policies, snapshots and rollback.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane B

The memory-typology and update-protocol notes this chapter expands, and the promotion-path synthesis flagged in §4.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I4-memory-systems-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are I2 (the write-permission matrix whose fourth row this chapter unpacks), I1 (the taxonomy that makes memory a grey zone), I3 (staleness applied to the agent's own notes), I5 (keeping remembered material loadable), E2 (the context window this machinery protects), E5 (the advisory/deterministic ladder the defenses climb), and M7 (ASI06 in the full security landscape).

MY NOTES

Highlight-notes

I5 · DEEP DIVE

Progressive Disclosure: Skills, Nested Files & Indexes

0Why it matters

Every chapter of M4 so far has decided something about the artifacts an agent works from: what they are (I1), who may write them (I2), how they stay true (I3), what the agent itself remembers (I4). This chapter decides the last variable — how much of it the agent sees at once. The naive answer is "all of it": paste the PRD, the architecture notes, the coding standards and the tribal knowledge into one always-loaded file and the agent can never claim it wasn't told. The measured reality is that this fails twice. It fails on capacity — the context window is the scarcest resource in the system (E2), and Chroma's context-rot report showed accuracy degrading as input grows even on trivially simple tasks, across all 18 models tested (July 2025). And it fails on attention — an instruction buried in a 900-line context file competes with everything around it, which is why the official Claude Code guidance is blunt: files load in full regardless of length, "though shorter files produce better adherence." The engineering answer that emerged across the industry in 2025–26 is progressive disclosure: keep a small always-loaded core, make that core a map of where everything else lives, and let the agent pull the rest just-in-time. Anthropic's context-engineering team states the inversion directly: agents should "maintain lightweight identifiers (file paths, stored queries, web links, etc.) and use these references to dynamically load data into context at runtime" — which "enables progressive disclosure… allows agents to incrementally discover relevant context through exploration." Skills, nested context files, scoped rules and index files are four implementations of that one idea, and this chapter takes them apart mechanism by mechanism — including the evidence that content quality, not raw length, is what the budget is actually for.

What this chapter gives you
  • the principle, honestly attributed — a UX pattern from 1984/2006 turned context discipline (§1)
  • Skills: the canonical three-level mechanism, its official token economics, and the open standard's cross-vendor reach (§2)
  • nested context files and scoped rules — the same idea in every major tool, with the precedence fine print (§3)
  • the map layer: index files as tables of contents, and what happens to indexes nobody reads (§4)
  • gardening the always-loaded core: the 200-line budget, and the studies on what context files actually buy (§5)
  • the failure modes: kitchen-sink cores, dead skills, stale maps, the import illusion (§6)
  • a week-one disclosure audit for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E2 — context engineering: this chapter is its information-architecture face
  • E5 — advisory vs deterministic: skills load guidance; hooks enforce it
  • I1 — the taxonomy: cores and maps are durable artifacts with owners
  • I3 — freshness: a stale map misroutes every session
  • I4 — memory: MEMORY.md is this pattern applied to the agent's own notes
  • I6 — retrieval: the search sibling; disclosure and grep complete each other

1From UX pattern to context discipline

The name is borrowed honestly. Progressive disclosure is a user-interface pattern — show only what the user needs now, defer the rest behind an explicit next step — popularized by Jakob Nielsen's 2006 Nielsen Norman Group article of that name, with research roots in John M. Carroll and Caroline Carrithers' "Training Wheels in a User Interface" (Communications of the ACM, 1984), which found novices learned faster when advanced functions were blocked off. Both findings transfer to agents almost without translation: attention is finite, everything visible competes for it, and the cure is not more information but staged information.

Applied to an agent's context, the pattern gives a three-tier architecture — the vocabulary core / map / leaves is this workbook's framing, but every mechanism in this chapter implements it:

TIER 1

The always-loaded core

every session · paid every time

What enters the context before any work happens: the system prompt, root context files (CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md), skill metadata. Every line here taxes every session, relevant or not — which is why §5's gardening budget applies here and only here.

TIER 2

Relevance-triggered bodies

when the task matches

Content that loads itself when a signal fires: a skill body when the model judges its description relevant, a subdirectory context file when the agent reads files there, a glob-scoped rule when a matching path enters the conversation. The trigger — a description, a path pattern — is the whole interface.

TIER 3

On-demand leaves

pulled by tools · unbounded

Everything the agent fetches deliberately: bundled skill resources, the document an index line points to, any file grep can reach. Costs nothing until touched; effectively unlimited, because the filesystem is the overflow — Manus calls it "the ultimate context… unlimited in size, persistent by nature, and directly operable by the agent itself" (July 2025).

The clearest proof that staging pays comes from the tool layer, where the numbers are largest. MCP tool definitions historically loaded up-front, like a kitchen-sink context file: Anthropic's advanced-tool-use post (November 24, 2025) measures a five-server setup at "58 tools consuming approximately 55K tokens before the conversation even starts." The fixes are progressive disclosure verbatim — a Tool Search Tool that loads definitions on demand (defer_loading: true), reported at an 85% reduction in token usage with accuracy improving, from 49% to 74% on MCP evaluations for Opus 4 and 79.5% to 88.1% for Opus 4.5; and the code-execution-with-MCP pattern (November 4, 2025), which presents tools as files precisely because "Models are great at navigating filesystems. Presenting tools as code on a filesystem allows models to read tool definitions on-demand, rather than reading them all up-front" — the post's worked example drops 150,000 tokens to 2,000, "a time and cost saving of 98.7%."

Vendor numbers, one direction of evidence

Those token and accuracy figures are Anthropic internal testing on Anthropic products — vendor claims by E7's standard, not independent replication. They are quoted here because the direction is corroborated from the independent side: Chroma's context-rot measurements (more input, worse recall — 18 models) and §5's ETH study (redundant context: higher cost, no success gain) point the same way from outside. Loading less is not merely cheaper; past a threshold it is more accurate. Treat the exact percentages as point-in-time marketing precision.

2Skills — the canonical three-level mechanism

Anthropic's Agent Skills (launched with "Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills," October 16, 2025) are the cleanest productized form of the pattern, worth studying even if a client never runs Claude: a skill is just a folder with a SKILL.md file — YAML frontmatter carrying a name (max 64 characters; lowercase letters, numbers, hyphens; the words "anthropic" and "claude" reserved) and a description (non-empty, max 1024 characters) — plus optional bundled scripts, references and assets. The three documented disclosure levels map exactly onto §1's tiers, with official context costs attached:

LevelWhat loadsWhenContext cost (official)
L1 metadataname + description of every installed skillalways — "at startup," into the system prompt"~100 tokens per Skill"
L2 instructionsthe SKILL.md body — "Only then does this content enter the context window"when the model judges the description relevant"Under 5k tokens"; best practice: body under 500 lines
L3 resourcesbundled files — scripts executed, references read"only when referenced" during the work"None until accessed"

Two design consequences deserve more attention than they usually get. First, the description is the entire retrieval interface: at the moment of deciding whether to load a skill, the model sees nothing but those ≤1024 characters — which is why the authoring guidance insists a description state both what the skill does and when to use it, in third person. A skill with a vague description is not a weaker skill; it is an unreachable one (§6). Second, the body budget is not a style preference: a triggered skill spends from the same window as the task, so "under 500 lines," with anything longer split into L3 references, is the same gardening rule as §5's — applied one tier down.

What began proprietary is now — point-in-time, mid-2026 — a cross-vendor surface. Anthropic published the format as an open standard on December 18, 2025 (agentskills.io; spec repo Apache-2.0), and the format's frontmatter-plus-lazy-body shape has been adopted natively: OpenAI's Codex reads SKILL.md folders ("Skills build on the open agent skills standard," scanning .agents/skills upward from the working directory), GitHub Copilot added support on December 18, 2025 (reading .github/skills, .claude/skills or .agents/skills, across cloud agent, code review, CLI and IDE modes), Cursor 2.4 loads project skills from .cursor/skills/, and Gemini CLI reads user and workspace skill directories including .agents/skills — with the spec site's showcase adding VS Code, JetBrains Junie, Goose, OpenCode, Amp, Factory and Kiro. The practical upshot for a client: process knowledge packaged as a skill is not tool lock-in the way a proprietary rules file is, and .agents/skills is emerging as the shared path. All of this list is fast-moving; re-verify before relying on any one tool's support.

Skills execute code — the trust boundary

A skill is not inert prose. The official security guidance is unambiguous: "Use Skills only from trusted sources: those you created yourself or obtained from Anthropic. Skills give Claude new capabilities through instructions and code, which also means a malicious Skill can direct Claude to invoke tools or execute code in ways that don't match the Skill's stated purpose" — with a documented worst case of data exfiltration or unauthorized system access for unaudited third-party skills. Treat skill installation like dependency adoption: review before install, pin what you reviewed, and gate additions through the same PR discipline as any durable artifact (I2). M7 covers the supply-chain framing; I4 already met the ancestor — Voyager's "ever-growing skill library" of executable, reviewable procedures.

3Nested context files and scoped rules

The second mechanism family scopes instructions by location. Claude Code's semantics are the fully documented reference: memory files load in a fixed order — managed policy (e.g. /etc/claude-code/CLAUDE.md), then user (~/.claude/CLAUDE.md), then project (./CLAUDE.md), then ./CLAUDE.local.md for private, uncommitted preferences — and the disclosure rule is verbatim: "CLAUDE.md and CLAUDE.local.md files in the directory hierarchy above the working directory are loaded in full at launch. Files in subdirectories load on demand when Claude reads files in those directories." That one sentence is the monorepo pattern: a small root file with the always-true facts, and per-package files that cost nothing until the agent actually works there — packages/billing/CLAUDE.md taxes only billing sessions.

The import illusion

Claude Code's @path imports (recursive, "a maximum depth of four hops") look like lazy loading and are not: "imported files still load and enter the context window at launch." Imports are an organization feature — splitting one logical core across files — not a disclosure feature. Teams routinely "trim" a bloated CLAUDE.md by moving half of it into imported files and celebrate a diet that changed nothing. Deferral comes only from the mechanisms that actually gate loading: subdirectory files, path-scoped rules, skills.

AGENTS.md, the cross-vendor sibling (stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation; "used by over 60k open-source projects," self-reported), specifies nesting with one sentence: "Agents automatically read the nearest file in the directory tree, so the closest one takes precedence and every subproject can ship tailored instructions." The fine print matters at a client, because implementations diverge: Codex documents walking the tree and concatenating from root down (with AGENTS.override.md for hard replacement), while other tools resolve nearest-first. The defensive authoring rule that works under both: nested files refine their ancestors — they never contradict them, so precedence order cannot change behavior. (Empirically, contradictions may cost less than feared — §5's factorial study found no detectable adherence effect from contradictory adjacent files — but "the agent tolerates our inconsistency" is not an architecture.)

Every major tool now ships the same triggers under its own names — the strongest evidence that this is a convergent pattern, not one vendor's quirk:

ToolMechanismTrigger modesEnforced budget
Claude CodeCLAUDE.md hierarchy + path-scoped rules + skillslaunch (ancestors) · on-demand (subdirectories) · description match (skills)advisory: "target under 200 lines" per file
Cursor.cursor/rules/*.mdcfour modes: always · agent-decides (description) · glob auto-attach · manual @-mention—
Copilot.github/instructions/*.instructions.mdapplyTo: glob frontmatter, comma-separated patterns—
Windsurfrules with a trigger fieldalways · manual · model-decision · globhard caps: 6,000 chars global · 12,000 per workspace rule file
spec: AGENTS.mdnested files per subproject"nearest file… takes precedence" (implementations diverge)—

Read the table's columns against §1: every row is the same three tiers — an always slot, a relevance trigger (glob or description), a manual/on-demand escape hatch. Windsurf's hard character caps are worth quoting to clients precisely because they are deterministic where everyone else's budget is advisory (E5's distinction, applied to context itself). Tool details are point-in-time mid-2026.

4The map layer — indexes as tables of contents

Tiers 2 and 3 share a dependency that is easy to miss: the agent cannot ask for what it does not know exists. Lazy loading without a map degrades into blind search; the always-loaded core earns its cost precisely by telling the agent where things are. That is the index pattern — and it predates agents. Alex Kladov's ARCHITECTURE.md convention (February 2021) built the human version on an observation that transfers to agents unchanged: "it takes 2x more time to write a patch if you are unfamiliar with the project, but it takes 10x more time to figure out where you should change the code." His authoring rules are the anti-staleness discipline this workbook keeps arriving at from other directions: name the bird's-eye modules and entry points, keep it short, and "only specify things that are unlikely to frequently change. Don't try to keep it synchronized with code."

The machine-generated end of the same idea is aider's repository map (Paul Gauthier, October 2023 — before any context-file convention existed): tree-sitter parses every file into definitions and references, a graph ranking algorithm over the file graph picks the most relevant identifiers (personalized PageRank in the implementation), and the result — signatures, not bodies — is packed into a fixed budget (--map-tokens, default 1k). It is a table of contents computed from code, derived and regenerated per I1's rules, and its economics are the whole argument in miniature: a 1k-token map that routes the agent to the right 3 files beats 100k tokens of pasted source. Claude Code's MEMORY.md index (I4) is the same shape for the agent's own notes — a one-line-per-memory index always loaded, topic files on demand.

Two governance corollaries. First, indexes are durable, owned artifacts (I1) that are doubly freshness-critical (I3): ordinary stale prose misleads the session that reads it, but a stale map misroutes every session, silently, at the top of the funnel — so the two-snapshot CI grep from I3 (does every path/name the index mentions still exist?) belongs on index files first. Second, an index needs a consumer. The cautionary tale is llms.txt: of 137,210 domains Ahrefs analyzed (May 2026 data), 28% had published a valid llms.txt — and 97% of those files received zero requests of any kind; AI-retrieval bots accounted for ~1.1% of the few fetches that did happen. An index written for readers who never arrive is decoration. Before writing ARCHITECTURE.md, docs/INDEX.md or a repo map, confirm the loop: the core points to the index, the agent's tools can follow the pointer, and something in CI notices when the pointer breaks.

5Gardening the always-loaded core

Everything above moves content out of the core; this section is about what earns a place in it. The official budget is now explicit — Claude Code's memory documentation: "Size: target under 200 lines per CLAUDE.md file. Longer files consume more context and reduce adherence" — and the failure mode above the budget is not truncation but dilution: "CLAUDE.md files are loaded in full regardless of length, though shorter files produce better adherence." (Earlier editions of this workbook said "under ~300 lines"; that matched the practitioner range — HumanLayer's widely-cited guide says "< 300 lines is best, and shorter is even better," and keeps its own root file under sixty — but the official target is 200, and that is the number to hold a client to.) The same guide supplies the capacity argument: frontier models follow roughly 150–200 instructions with reasonable consistency (citing arXiv:2507.11538), and Claude Code's system prompt already spends ~50 of them before your file adds one.

What earns a line, per the official guidance: facts the agent needs every session — build commands, conventions, project layout, "always do X" rules — plus pitfalls and rationale the codebase cannot reveal. What does not: anything derivable — directory listings, dependency lists, architecture overviews the agent can read from the repo. Claude Code's /doctor now automates exactly that triage, proposing trims of derivable content while keeping "pitfalls, rationale, and conventions that differ from tool defaults." Procedures and per-area material go down a tier: multi-step workflows become skills; area-specific rules become subdirectory files or path-scoped rules. HumanLayer's one-liner for why the core deserves this care is the leverage argument: the file "goes into every single session" — "a bad line of code is a bad line of code. A bad line of an implementation plan has the potential to create a lot of bad lines of code."

The evidence on what context files buy is younger than the practice, and it cuts precisely — three studies, three complementary answers. Presence pays: Lulla et al. (ICSE JAWs 2026, arXiv:2601.20404), across 10 repositories and 124 pull requests, found agent runs with an AGENTS.md present completed comparably while showing "lower median runtime (Δ 28.64%) and reduced output token consumption (Δ 16.58%)" — the file works as an efficiency device. Content decides the sign: the ETH Zurich evaluation (Gloaguen, Mündler, Müller, Raychev & Vechev, arXiv:2602.11988) is the study this chapter leans on hardest — "providing context files does not generally improve task success rates, while increasing inference cost by over 20% on average." Underneath the average: LLM-generated context files reduced resolution rates (−0.5% on SWE-bench Lite, −2% on the authors' CTXBENCH) while raising cost (+20% and +23% respectively), whereas developer-written files managed "an increase of 4% on average" — and the paper's diagnosis is the duplication mechanism this module keeps meeting: generated files reproduce what the agent can discover itself (READMEs, layouts, build tooling), so "context files do not provide effective overviews." Auto-running /init and committing the output is, measurably, paying more for slightly worse results. And structure is not the lever: McMillan's factorial study (arXiv:2605.10039; 1,650 Claude Code sessions, 16,050 function-level observations) varied file size, instruction position, file architecture and contradictions between adjacent files — and found no detectable adherence effect from any of them after multiple-testing correction (Bayes factors 0.05–0.10, evidence for the null); what did move compliance was session depth, roughly "5.6% lower odds of compliance per step."
