Read together: keep the file (Lulla), fill it only with non-derivable knowledge (ETH), and don't expect formatting tricks to substitute for short sessions and deterministic gates (McMillan, E5).

For calibration, the population: 90.6% of 2,853 agentic-tool repositories in the AIware 2026 census carry context files at all — 4,768 of them, led by CLAUDE.md at 34.4% over AGENTS.md at 31.6% (Galster et al., arXiv:2602.14690). The instrument is ubiquitous; the discipline above is what separates the ones that pay from the ones that cost.

6Failure modes

Each mechanism fails in a characteristic way, and all five failures are quiet — nothing errors; the harness just underperforms.

The kitchen-sink core. Everything "important" goes in CLAUDE.md; at 900 lines nothing is important. The signature is instructions the agent visibly ignores while the file's authors add more emphasis — capitals, "CRITICAL", repetition — instead of removing competitors for attention. The fix is §5's triage, and the deterministic backstop is E5's: a rule the team cannot afford to see ignored does not belong in prose at all; it belongs in a hook or a validator.

The auto-generated core. The measured version of the same disease (§5): generated-and-committed context files duplicate the discoverable, cost more, and resolve slightly less. If a generator drafts the file, a human must delete its redundant majority before commit — the ETH numbers are the argument to show whoever asks why the "free" file needs editing.

The dead skill. A skill whose description fails to fire never loads — invisible in any log, discovered only when someone asks why the agent keeps reinventing the workflow the skill encodes. Symmetrically, an over-broad description fires everywhere, taxing unrelated sessions with a 5k-token body. The description is a retrieval query written in advance; it deserves review as such (test: does the skill trigger on the three tasks it exists for, and stay silent on three neighbours?).

The stale map. §4's edge: a renamed package, a moved doc, and the always-loaded pointer now routes every session to a void — or worse, to the old location a lazy git history still contains. Index files get I3's reference checks first, and matklad's rule pre-empts most of it: maps name only what changes slowly.

The import illusion & the nesting mirage. §3's pair of false economies: @imports that reorganize the bill without shrinking it, and nested files that assume nearest-wins semantics one tool honors and another concatenates. Both are verified in minutes — load a session, check what actually entered context (/context in Claude Code) — and both persist for months in teams that never look.

One boundary is not like the others

Dead skills and bloated cores waste tokens; an untrusted skill executes. The failure modes above degrade performance — a malicious or sloppy third-party skill compromises the machine it runs on (§2's warning, M7's supply-chain frame). Disclosure mechanisms move content into the agent's trust perimeter automatically, which is exactly why I2's write-gates guard the directories they load from: whoever can write to .claude/skills/ or the root context file programs every future session.

7Week one at the client

The disclosure audit is a natural early-engagement move: cheap, diagnostic, and it produces its own before/after numbers.

Audit — day one
  • inventory every context artifact: root and nested context files, rules directories, skills, indexes — the census says there will be several
  • measure the always-loaded core: lines and tokens per session before any work happens (Claude Code: /context)
  • classify each core line: needed-every-session · derivable-from-repo · per-area · procedure · dead
  • check the map: does the core say where specs, docs, ADRs and skills live — and do those pointers resolve?
Restructure — the rest of the week
  • trim the core toward the 200-line target: delete derivables (/doctor-style), demote per-area lines to subdirectory files, procedures to skills
  • add the map lines the audit found missing — one pointer per branch, nothing more
  • package the first two skills from real recurring procedures — our own candidates: the task-spec template (H1) and the characterization-test workflow (M5)
  • wire I3's reference check over core + index files in CI, so the map cannot silently rot
  • re-measure: same session type, tokens before work, and instructions-followed spot checks — the before/after pair is week-one evidence for T2's pilot
Rules that keep it healthy
  • every core line answers "which sessions need this?" — "all of them" or it moves down a tier
  • nested files refine, never contradict — safe under both precedence semantics
  • skills from outside the team are reviewed like dependencies, gated like durables (I2)
  • the budget is enforced where possible (CI line count on the core), advised where not (E5)

8Where I5 sits in the framework

TopicThe connection
E2Context engineering names the scarcity; I5 is its information-architecture face — the just-in-time strategy as repo structure.
E5Advisory vs deterministic, twice: skills load guidance while hooks enforce it ("never send an LLM to do a linter's job"), and budgets bite only where a tool or CI enforces them.
H1The handoff contract stays lean by pointing at skills and indexes instead of inlining process — disclosure applied to the contract itself.
I1Cores, maps and skill bodies are durable artifacts with owners; aider-style repo maps are derived — never hand-edited, regenerated.
I2The directories disclosure loads from are write-gated: whoever writes the core or a skill programs every future session.
I3Freshness, prioritized: the always-loaded map is the highest-leverage staleness target in the repo.
I4MEMORY.md's index-plus-topic-files layout is this chapter's pattern applied to memory; Voyager's skill library is the shared ancestor.
I6The retrieval sibling: disclosure stages what is known to matter; agentic search finds what was not — a map makes grep cheaper, not redundant.

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences

Progressive disclosure is the discipline of keeping the always-loaded core small and making it a map — Skills, nested context files, scoped rules and indexes are four mechanisms for the same three tiers: always, on relevance, on demand. The budget is official and concrete — target under 200 lines of core, ~100 tokens per skill's metadata, bodies under 500 lines — but the evidence says the real lever is content: files of non-derivable knowledge pay (+2.4%, −28.64% median runtime), generated duplication measurably costs (+23% for −2%), and formatting tricks move nothing. Guard the mechanism like the infrastructure it is: review skills like dependencies, reference-check the map in CI, and put must-hold rules in hooks, not prose.

10Further reading

Anthropic — Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills · Agent Skills docs
anthropic.com/engineering · October 16, 2025 · platform.claude.com docs · point-in-time mid-2026

The mechanism and its official numbers: three levels, ~100 tokens of metadata per skill, bodies under 5k tokens / 500 lines, frontmatter constraints, and the trusted-sources security warning.

agentskills.io — the Agent Skills open standard
spec + client showcase · standard published December 18, 2025 · Apache-2.0 spec repo

The cross-vendor surface: Codex, Copilot, Cursor, Gemini CLI and others reading the same SKILL.md format; .agents/skills as the emerging shared path. Fast-moving — re-verify the client list.

Anthropic — Effective context engineering for AI agents
anthropic.com/engineering · September 29, 2025

The strategy this chapter structures: just-in-time context via "lightweight identifiers," and progressive disclosure named as what autonomous navigation enables.

Anthropic — Advanced tool use · Code execution with MCP
anthropic.com/engineering · November 24 & November 4, 2025 · vendor-measured

Disclosure at the tool layer: 58 tools ≈ 55K upfront tokens; deferred loading at an 85% token reduction with accuracy gains; tools-as-files because models navigate filesystems (98.7% worked example).

Claude Code docs — Manage Claude's memory
code.claude.com/docs/en/memory · point-in-time mid-2026

The reference semantics: the load hierarchy, subdirectory on-demand loading, four-hop imports that still load at launch, and the "target under 200 lines" budget with its adherence rationale.

AGENTS.md — the open format
agents.md · Agentic AI Foundation / Linux Foundation · adoption self-reported 60k+ repos

Nearest-file-wins nesting in one sentence — and the reason to author nested files as refinements: implementations diverge on precedence.

Gloaguen, Mündler, Müller, Raychev & Vechev — Evaluating AGENTS.md
arXiv:2602.11988 · ETH Zurich · February 2026 (rev. June 2026)

The study that prices context-file content: no general success gain, cost up over 20%; LLM-generated files −0.5%/−2% for +20%/+23%; developer-written +2.4% (short of significance); "context files do not provide effective overviews."

McMillan — Instruction Adherence in Coding Agent Configuration Files
arXiv:2605.10039 · May 2026 · 1,650 sessions, factorial design

The null that disciplines the folklore: size, position, architecture and contradictions produced no detectable adherence contrast; within-session decay (~5.6% lower odds per step) did.

Lulla et al. — On the Impact of AGENTS.md Files · Galster et al. — Harness Engineering for Agentic AI Coding Tools
arXiv:2601.20404 (ICSE JAWs 2026) · arXiv:2602.14690 (AIware 2026)

The efficiency case (Δ 28.64% median runtime, Δ 16.58% output tokens across 124 PRs) and the census (context files in 90.6% of 2,853 agentic repos; CLAUDE.md 34.4% ahead of AGENTS.md 31.6%).

HumanLayer — Writing a good CLAUDE.md
humanlayer.com/blog · November 25, 2025 · practitioner synthesis

The strongest practitioner guide: "< 300 lines is best, and shorter is even better," ~150–200 followable instructions (citing arXiv:2507.11538) with ~50 already spent, progressive disclosure via pointer files, and "never send an LLM to do a linter's job."

Kladov (matklad) — ARCHITECTURE.md · Gauthier — aider repository map
matklad.github.io · February 6, 2021 · aider.chat · October 22, 2023

The two index ancestors: the hand-written codemap ("10x more time to figure out where"), and the computed map — tree-sitter tags, graph-ranked, packed to a 1k-token default budget.

Ji — Context Engineering for AI Agents: Lessons from Building Manus
manus.im/blog · July 18, 2025 · practitioner/vendor

The filesystem as "the ultimate context: unlimited in size, persistent by nature, directly operable" — tier 3 stated as a production lesson, plus todo.md recitation against lost-in-the-middle.

Nielsen — Progressive Disclosure · Carroll & Carrithers — Training Wheels in a User Interface
nngroup.com · December 3, 2006 · CACM 27(8) · 1984

The attribution: the UX pattern this chapter borrows, and the 1984 experiment showing novices learn faster when advanced functionality is staged out of view.

Ahrefs — the llms.txt study
ahrefs.com/blog · June 15, 2026 (May 2026 data) · 137,210 domains

The index-without-a-consumer datum: 97% of published llms.txt files fetched by nothing at all; AI-retrieval bots ~1.1% of the few requests that happened.

Chroma — Context Rot
trychroma.com/research · July 2025 · 18 models

Why the core must stay small even when the window is large: performance degrades non-uniformly as input grows, including on trivially simple tasks.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane B

The progressive-disclosure notes this chapter expands, and the core/map/leaves framing — this workbook's synthesis, flagged as such.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I5-progressive-disclosure-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are E2 (the context window this discipline protects), I1 (cores and maps as owned durables, repo maps as derived artifacts), I2 (the write-gates on what disclosure loads), I3 (freshness — the map first), I4 (MEMORY.md as the pattern applied to memory), I6 (retrieval — the search sibling), E5 (advisory budgets vs enforced caps), and H1 (the contract that points instead of inlining). M4 closes its file-architecture arc here; I6 turns to how the agent finds what no map anticipated.

E9 · DEEP DIVE

Harness Engineering as a Discipline: Eval-Driven Tuning

0Why it matters

E1 gave this workbook its founding equation — Agent = Model + Harness (Böckeler) — and E3 showed what a harness is responsible for. Through late 2025 and 2026 the industry finished the thought: it named the practice, built a method for it, and produced the first hard evidence that the harness is not the junior partner in that equation. The name is harness engineering — attributed to Viv Trivedy of LangChain, and defined by HumanLayer as "the practice of leveraging configuration points to customize and improve your coding agent's output quality and reliability." The evidence is blunter than the definition: a May 2026 position paper measured that harness-induced variance can exceed model-induced variance by 7.8× on average — holding the model fixed and swapping only the scaffold moved SWE-bench-family scores by 9 to 48 points (arXiv:2605.23950). NVIDIA now runs a named "Harness Robustness" stage in post-training; LangChain took an open model to near-frontier eval scores at a tenth of the cost by touching only the harness; and Anthropic ships engineering playbooks for harness design. For our engagement this reframes the client's favourite question. "Which model should we buy?" is the wrong first question — the right one is which harness, configured how, and how will we measure our changes to it. This chapter gives that question a method: the tunable surface (§1), the evidence that tuning it moves outcomes (§2), the authoring discipline (§3), the eval-driven tuning loop (§4), and the co-evolution trap that makes harness choice a strategic decision rather than a convenience (§5).

What this chapter gives you
  • the tunable surface: what a harness is made of, from two verified reference architectures to six runtime responsibilities (§1)
  • the evidence that the harness moves measured outcomes — with the two Terminal-Bench stories properly disentangled (§2)
  • the authoring discipline: the Ratchet, working backwards from behaviour, "success is silent, failures are verbose" (§3)
  • eval-driven harness tuning: the loop, the cost ladder, and the boundary where the harness ends and the model begins (§4)
  • model–harness co-evolution: why models overfit their training harness and what that does to your tool choice (§5)
  • a week-one plan for a brownfield client (§6)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E1 — the equation and the seven components; this chapter is what you do to them
  • E2 — context engineering: the discipline this practice sits inside
  • E3 — harness responsibilities and Anthropic patterns; E9 adds the measurement loop
  • E7 — DORA/METR measure the team; E9's evals measure the agent
  • I5 — progressive disclosure is one family of harness levers
  • T5/T6 — the harness as internal product; vendor strategy once harnesses couple to models

1The tunable surface — anatomy of the thing you tune

You cannot tune what you cannot name. Two independent source-level analyses, published within weeks of each other, give the field its two reference architectures — and they disagree in an instructive way. The first (arXiv:2604.14228, "Dive into Claude Code," Apr 2026) dissects Claude Code and finds deliberate simplicity: "the core of the system is a simple while-loop that calls the model, runs tools, and repeats" — a single ReAct loop with a single state object, explicitly contrasted with graph-based orchestration, delegating to subagents by re-entering the same loop. The second (arXiv:2603.05344, "Building Effective AI Coding Agents for the Terminal," Mar 2026) describes OpenDev, an open-source terminal agent built as the opposite: a compound AI system — "not a single monolithic LLM, but a structured ensemble of agents and workflows," a four-level Session → Agent → Workflow → LLM hierarchy with five separately assignable model roles (execution, thinking, self-critique, vision, fallback). Both are non-peer-reviewed preprints, and OpenDev's description is its builder's own; but together they bound the design space our client will actually choose from: one loop, many levers versus many loops, many models.

Across both designs, the June 2026 survey that treats the harness as a first-class research object (arXiv:2606.20683) decomposes the execution harness into six runtime responsibilities — a useful checklist because every one of them is a place where configuration, not model choice, decides behaviour:

SURFACE 1–2

Observation & context

what the model sees

Tool outputs, file reads, error messages, and the assembled context window: system prompt, context files, skill metadata, tool descriptions. Everything M4 governs — progressive disclosure (I5), memory (I4) — is tuning surface here. This is where the LangChain "guidance placement" result of §4 lives.

SURFACE 3–4

Control & action

what the model may do

The loop itself: turn budgets, retry logic, loop caps, permission gates, hooks, subagent spawning; and the tool inventory — which tools exist, how they are described, what they return. HumanLayer's six levers (system prompts, tools/MCP, context, subagents, hooks, skills) are the practitioner cut of the same surface.

SURFACE 5–6

State & verification

what persists, what pushes back

Session state, checkpoints, progress files, memory writes; and the feedback machinery — tests, linters, typecheckers wired into the loop (E3/E4). Anthropic's long-running-agent harnesses put their most load-bearing rules here (the feature-list protection rule I2 verified; the initializer/coding-agent split of its Nov 2025 playbook).

A practitioner vocabulary worth borrowing sits one level up. Alejandro Piad Morffis ("The Anatomy of AI Coding Agents," Apr 2026 — an informed practitioner synthesis with no benchmarks, from a University of Havana professor who builds his own OpenCode tooling) separates Mode ("the persona the AI adopts… it determines what the agent can do, not just how it thinks" — enforced permissions, not tone), Skill (knowledge "applied implicitly when necessary"), Command (user-invoked workflow: "do this, in this order, using these tools") and Subagent (ephemeral delegation that "returns a summarized result so the main context stays clean"). The explicit-versus-implicit axis — modes and commands are invoked, skills self-activate — is a sharper framing than most tool docs offer, and it maps almost one-to-one onto Claude Code's primitives. His context-budget heuristic — target 40–60% saturation rather than riding to the ~85% compaction threshold — is opinion, but it is the same direction E2's context-rot evidence points.

2Same model, different harness — the evidence

The claim that the harness dominates would be vendor marketing if only harness vendors made it. What changed in 2026 is that it acquired an evidence base with independent entries. The strongest is the position paper "Stop Comparing LLM Agents Without Disclosing the Harness" (arXiv:2605.23950, May 2026). Its authors ran a controlled factorial experiment — three models (GPT-5.4, Kimi K2.6, GLM-5.1) crossed with three harnesses on a SWE-bench Verified subset — and found harness-induced variance exceeding model-induced variance by 7.80× on average. Their collected observational numbers are just as stark: holding Claude Opus 4.5 fixed, SEAL versus Claude Code differ by 9.5 points on SWE-bench Pro; on the HAL leaderboard's SWE-bench Verified Mini, the same Claude Sonnet 4.5 scores 68% under SWE-Agent and 34% under HAL's generalist scaffold — a 34-point swing from scaffolding alone, with a ~48-point swing for o4-mini. Their conclusion is the sentence we should hand the client's evaluation committee verbatim: "Until harness specifications are disclosed, leaderboard comparisons for long-horizon agents should be treated as incomplete and potentially misleading."

The model-builder side confirms it from within. NVIDIA's Nemotron 3 Ultra technical report (arXiv:2606.15007) shows the same model spanning 65–70.4% on SWE-bench Verified depending on which of five harnesses runs it, and its official reproducibility recipe pins not just the model but the harness and sampling parameters — OpenHands for SWE-bench, Terminus-2 for Terminal-Bench, temperature, top-p and turn limits all named — because without them the number is not reproducible. Terminal-Bench itself (a Stanford × Laude collaboration) makes the separation mechanical: agents register as adapters via its Harbor framework, and the CLI takes --agent and --model as independent flags. And Artificial Analysis, benchmarking Nemotron's launch, went a step further — it customized the Terminus-2 harness so that "every run states the turn budget up front… and warns the model as it approaches the limit," on the reasoning that "silently truncating an agent at N turns penalizes models that didn't know the limit was coming." A third-party benchmarker editing the harness to remove harness-induced bias is the practice of §4 arriving in the measurement industry (disclosed caveat: the analysis was NVIDIA-partnered for pre-release access).

The Terminal-Bench story is two stories — keep them apart

Practitioner posts blur two separate results into one meme. Result one (HumanLayer, Mar 2026): Claude Opus 4.6 placed "#33 in Claude Code… #5 in a different harness that wasn't seen during post-training (+/- about 4 positions)" on Terminal-Bench 2.0 — an Anthropic model, cited to argue post-training overfitting (§5). Result two (Viv Trivedy, LangChain, Feb 2026): "we… iteratively improve[d] deepagents-cli 13.7 points from 52.8 to 66.5 on Terminal Bench 2.0. We only tweaked the harness and kept the model fixed, gpt-5.2-codex" — the result Osmani retells as "Top 30 to Top 5." Different models, different teams, same moral. Both are self-reported and point-in-time — the live leaderboard has long since moved on — so cite them as illustrations of harness sensitivity, never as current rankings.

The same discipline applies at the small end of the surface. The ETH Zurich context-file evaluation this workbook leans on in I5 (arXiv:2602.11988) is a harness-tuning result in miniature: adding LLM-generated context files — a one-file harness change — reduced resolution rates (−0.5% SWE-bench Lite, −2% on the authors' CTXBENCH) while raising cost by +20%/+23%, and developer-written files bought only +2.4%, short of significance. Tuning the surface can move outcomes in both directions; that is precisely why §4's loop measures before it keeps. The evidence table, tiered per our house rules:

FindingSourceTier
Harness variance 7.80× model variance (factorial, 3 models × 3 harnesses); 34–48-pt scaffold swings on SWE-bench Verified Mini; "disclose the harness"arXiv:2605.23950 (May 2026)independent research (preprint)
Same model, 65–70.4% SWE-bench Verified across five harnesses; named "Harness Robustness" post-training stageNemotron 3 Ultra tech report, arXiv:2606.15007vendor, self-reported methodology
Six runtime responsibilities; "agent quality emerges from the interaction between model capability, runtime infrastructure, task structure, and evaluation design"arXiv:2606.20683 survey (Jun 2026)independent research (survey)
Benchmark harness customized to disclose turn budgets and avoid penalizing uninformed modelsArtificial Analysis, Nemotron 3 Ultra launch analysisthird-party benchmarker, NVIDIA-partnered
deepagents-cli +13.7 pts on Terminal-Bench 2.0, model fixed (gpt-5.2-codex); Nemotron 0.80→0.84/0.86 vs Opus 4.8's 0.87 at ~10× lower cost ($4.48 vs $43.48/run)LangChain posts (Feb & Jul 2026)vendor claims, internal eval, no external replication
Opus 4.6 #33 in Claude Code vs #5 in an unseen harness (±4)HumanLayer (Mar 2026)practitioner/vendor, point-in-time
Context files as a harness lever cut both ways: −2%/+23% generated, +2.4% (n.s.) developer-writtenETH Zurich, arXiv:2602.11988independent research

3The discipline — the Ratchet and the six levers

If §2 establishes that the surface matters, the discipline question is how to work it without thrashing. The founding move is attributed to Mitchell Hashimoto (mitchellh.com, Feb 2026): "anytime you find an agent makes a mistake, you take the time to engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again." Osmani names the resulting dynamic the Ratchet and gives it teeth as a provenance rule: "Every line in a good AGENTS.md should be traceable back to a specific thing that went wrong." That rule is quietly radical, and it converges with what this workbook found independently: I5's gardening budget said every always-loaded line must be load-bearing; the Ratchet says every line must also have a failure as its birth certificate. A CLAUDE.md line you cannot trace to an incident is a candidate for deletion — and a ratcheted rule that matters should graduate from advisory prose to a deterministic check, which is exactly this repo's own CLAUDE.md-versus-validate.py split (E5's advisory/deterministic distinction, applied to the harness itself).

Two more design laws recur across the practitioner sources, both context-economic in origin. First, "success is silent, failures are verbose" — hooks and verification should say nothing when things pass and speak in detail when they fail. HumanLayer's war story makes it concrete: wiring the full test suite to run after every change flooded the context with 4,000 lines of passing output, and the agent lost the task and hallucinated. The verification machinery E4 builds must be context-efficient or it becomes the failure mode it guards against. Second, subagents as a context firewall — delegate discrete work (locate a definition, trace a flow, run a research errand) so intermediate noise never enters the parent thread, returning condensed answers with filepath:line citations. HumanLayer pairs this with a deliberately contrarian stance: persona-based subagent splits ("frontend engineer," "backend engineer") are ineffective theatre — the value of a subagent is context isolation, not roleplay. That is one vendor's doctrine, not settled fact, but it is a useful challenge to cargo-cult multi-agent setups — and note it targets persona splits, not role separation in the planner/generator/evaluator sense, which Anthropic's own harness work supports (§4).

On attribution, one correction to the sources themselves: HumanLayer credits "context engineering" to its cofounder Dex Horthy. The broader record says the term was popularized by Andrej Karpathy's June 2025 formulation ("the delicate art and science of filling the context window with just the right information for the next step"), with Horthy's 12-factor-agents an influential early articulation. Harness engineering sits inside context engineering, which subsumes prompt engineering — E2's hierarchy, now with named layers.

4Eval-driven tuning — the method

The genuinely new material of 2026 is not any single lever but a method for pulling them: treat the harness as the system under test, and let a task-level eval suite — not vibes — decide what changes survive. LangChain's Nemotron playbook (Jul 2026) is the most explicit published loop, run against its 127-test Deep Agents suite (five categories: files, tools, retrieval, conversation, summarization):

STEP 1–2

Evaluate, then read the traces

diagnosis before prescription

Run the suite; then read the failing trajectories and name the recurring failure pattern. The unit of diagnosis is the behaviour class ("stops reading files at the pagination boundary"), not the failing test.

STEP 3–4

One change, up the cost ladder

cheap screen, expensive confirm

Make one targeted change and re-run — first on a small cheap eval, promoting to the full expensive suite only when the win survives. The ladder mirrors this repo's own spot-verify pattern: screen with a fast check, confirm with a thorough one.

STEP 5

Keep only what repeats — and nothing regresses

the ratchet, quantified

A change is kept when the improvement repeats across trials and no other category regresses. Keep behaviour-class fixes; reject "benchmark tricks" that exploit specific test phrasing — the difference between tuning and overfitting.

Two of the playbook's specific findings deserve to travel. Guidance placement beats guidance content: a "keep reading" instruction did nothing in a tool's description, and started working the moment it moved into the tool's return value — the point in the trajectory where the model was actually failing. Broad system-prompt rewrites, by contrast, "wash out"; short targeted blocks aimed at an observed failure work. That is E2's attention economics operationalized. And the diagnostic boundary: after tuning, four of five categories improved but conversation stayed flat — which the authors read correctly as a model limitation, not a harness one. A category that will not move under harness changes tells you to stop tuning and change (or wait for) the model. That single heuristic — flat under tuning = model problem; moving = configuration problem — is the cleanest available answer to "should we keep engineering or switch vendors?"

The headline result, tiered honestly: harness tuning took Nemotron 3 Ultra from ~0.80 to ~0.84 typical (best 0.86, near Opus 4.8's 0.87) at "roughly 10x cheaper per run… about $4.48 against $43.48 on the full suite." The companion NVIDIA post supplies the one ablated data point in the whole story: a single middleware (a pagination notice appended to read_file output) fixed all three failing file tests and moved the suite from 94 to 96/127. All of it is vendor-run on the vendor's own eval — no arXiv paper, no external replication — which is why §2's independent anchors matter. But the method is corroborated beyond LangChain: Braintrust markets "eval-driven development" as its core practice ("evaluations serve as the working specification"), and Anthropic's harness-design post for long-running app development (Mar 2026) reports the same loop from the vendor side — a GAN-inspired generator/evaluator split (because models grade their own work too generously — E4's evaluator ≠ generator, again), with iteration driving cost per run from ~6 hours/$200 to ~3.8 hours/$125. Eval-driven harness tuning is a practice with multiple independent practitioners, even if every published number is still a vendor claim.

5Co-evolution — when the model learns its harness

The deepest 2026 development is that the coupling now runs both ways: harnesses are tuned to models, and models are post-trained inside harnesses. The Nemotron 3 Ultra report makes it explicit and named: "Harness Robustness is a key part of the post-training stage… we ensure that the model is trained under at least two of the following harnesses: Stirrup, OpenHands, OpenCode, Terminus, Droid, Custom Internal Harnesses," with RLVR data built from "a diverse collection of harness implementations and interaction formats… reducing overfitting to any particular harness design." Read that from the other side: overfitting to a training harness is a real enough failure mode that a frontier lab budgets a named training stage against it. It is the most parsimonious explanation for HumanLayer's Opus-4.6 observation in §2 — a model can be strongest inside the harness it grew up in — and Osmani generalizes it into a co-evolution thesis: better models do not shrink the harness, they shift which scaffolding matters; "anxiety-mitigation" components fall away while higher-level orchestration grows.

Three practical consequences for our engagement. First, benchmark hygiene: a published agentic score is a property of a (model, harness, parameters) triple — when the client's evaluation matrix quotes SWE-bench numbers, require the harness column (§2's position paper gives the citation). Second, vendor strategy (T6): if models perform best in their native harnesses and harnesses encode your accumulated Ratchet rules, then switching costs live in the harness layer, not the model API. The emerging "Harness-as-a-Service" pattern Osmani describes — agent SDKs (Claude Agent SDK, Codex SDK, OpenAI Agents SDK) that ship the loop, tool-calling, state and hooks as a configurable runtime — is the buy-side answer: you configure a maintained loop instead of rebuilding one, and your harness engineering concentrates in the portable layers (context files, evals, hooks, skills). Third, the pruning principle survives: E3 argued a good harness shrinks as models improve; co-evolution refines that — it shrinks in different places than it grows, and only an eval suite tells you which is which. All tool-specific facts in this section are point-in-time (mid-2026) and re-verify before client use.

What we do not claim

Two refuted claims circulate with this material; neither belongs in client decks. LangChain/NVIDIA "harness profiles" are not "first of their kind" — per-model harness adaptation predates the branding. And the viral "only 1.6% of Claude Code's codebase is AI decision logic" figure traces to an unnamed "community analysis" cited secondhand in arXiv:2604.14228 — no primary source, do not cite. The 7.80× variance figure, meanwhile, is one factorial experiment in a preprint: strong, plausibly the most important number in this chapter, and still awaiting peer review and replication. Tier accordingly.

6Week one at the client

Harness engineering is unusually easy to start because the first artifacts are small. Day one — inventory the surface. Which harness(es) are actually in use (IDE, CLI, CI); which of §1's six responsibilities each one exposes as configuration; who owns each lever today (nobody, usually — that is finding one, and T5's internal-product argument). Day two — seed the eval. Collect 10–20 real failed or painful agent tasks from the team's history — not synthetic puzzles — and turn them into a runnable suite with pass criteria. This is the client's Deep-Agents-suite-in-miniature, and it doubles as the pilot's measurement instrument (E7/T2: the agent-level eval complements DORA's team-level metrics; neither substitutes for the other). Day three onward — run the loop. One change at a time up the cost ladder; every kept change logged with the failure that motivated it (the Ratchet, providing I3-style provenance for the harness itself); every incident that recurs twice becomes a candidate for promotion from CLAUDE.md prose to a deterministic hook (E5). Week's end — the boundary check: anything flat after honest tuning goes on the "model limitation" list that feeds the vendor conversation, with §2's disclosure rule attached to any benchmark the vendor quotes back.

7Where E9 sits in the framework

Connects toHow
E1 · the equationE1 names the seven components; E9 is the practice of tuning them under measurement — the equation's operational form
E2 · context engineeringharness engineering is its named subset; guidance placement (§4) is attention economics applied to tool returns
E3 · harness in practiceE3's patterns and pruning principle gain a measurement loop and a co-evolution caveat
E4 · verification-first"success silent, failures verbose" constrains how verification enters context; evaluator ≠ generator recurs in Anthropic's harness design
E5 · governancethe Ratchet's endpoint is promotion from advisory prose to deterministic checks — this repo practises it (CLAUDE.md → validate.py)
E7 · metricsDORA/METR measure the team and the trend; the eval suite measures the agent; the pilot (T2) needs both
I5 · disclosure / I3 · provenancecontext files are harness levers with measured two-way effects; the Ratchet gives every core line a failure as provenance
T5/T6 · platform & vendorthe harness as internal product; co-evolution moves switching costs into the harness layer

8Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

An agentic benchmark score is a property of model plus harness — demand the harness column before believing any number, because measured harness-induced variance can exceed model-induced variance several-fold. Tune the harness the way you refactor code: one change at a time, against an eval suite built from your own failures, keeping only wins that repeat — and read a category that stays flat as a model limit, not a configuration one. Give every always-loaded rule a failure as its birth certificate, and promote the rules that matter from prose to deterministic checks.

9Further reading

Stop Comparing LLM Agents Without Disclosing the Harness
arXiv:2605.23950 · May 2026 · independent position paper (preprint)

The evidence backbone: 7.80× harness-vs-model variance in a controlled factorial; 34–48-point scaffold swings; the disclosure demand for leaderboards.

From Question Answering to Task Completion: A Survey on Agent System and Harness Design
arXiv:2606.20683 · June 2026 · independent survey

The harness as a first-class research object; the six runtime responsibilities taxonomy §1 borrows.

LangChain — Tuning the harness, not the model (+ NVIDIA companion post)
langchain.com · developer.nvidia.com · July 8, 2026 · vendor

The eval-driven loop, cost ladder, guidance-placement result, the 94→96/127 middleware ablation, and the 10× cost story. Method sound; numbers vendor-tier.

NVIDIA — Nemotron 3 Ultra technical report
arXiv:2606.15007 · June 2026 · vendor, self-reported methodology

The named Harness Robustness post-training stage and the 65–70.4% five-harness spread — co-evolution from the model builder's side.

HumanLayer — Skill Issue: Harness Engineering for Coding Agents · Addy Osmani — Agent Harness Engineering
humanlayer.dev · March 12, 2026 · addyosmani.com · April 19, 2026 · practitioner/vendor

The discipline named and systematized: six levers, context firewall, the Ratchet, HaaS, co-evolution. Opinionated; tier as practitioner synthesis.

Mitchell Hashimoto — My AI Adoption Journey
mitchellh.com · February 5, 2026 · practitioner

The founding formulation: engineer a solution such that the agent never makes that mistake again.

Alejandro Piad Morffis — The Anatomy of AI Coding Agents
blog.apiad.net · April 2, 2026 · practitioner synthesis

Mode / Skill / Command / Subagent, the explicit-vs-implicit axis, and the 40–60% saturation heuristic. No benchmarks — vocabulary, not evidence.

Dive into Claude Code · Building Effective AI Coding Agents for the Terminal (OpenDev)
arXiv:2604.14228 · arXiv:2603.05344 · 2026 · independent analyses (preprints)

The two reference architectures: single while-loop ReAct versus compound four-level ensemble.

Anthropic — Effective harnesses for long-running agents · Harness design for long-running application development
anthropic.com/engineering · Nov 26, 2025 & Mar 24, 2026 · vendor

The vendor-side playbooks: initializer/coding-agent split, feature-list protection, generator/evaluator separation, $200→$125 per-run tuning arc. E10 continues here.

Harness engineering is this workbook's own method pointed at itself: the deterministic validator, the spot-verify pattern, the CLAUDE.md whose lines earn their place — all Ratchet artifacts. The chapter's one non-negotiable is the disclosure rule: no agentic number without its harness named. Fast-moving facts here (leaderboards, SDK rosters, model names) are point-in-time as of mid-2026 — re-verify before any client meeting.

E10 · DEEP DIVE

Background & Long-Running Agents: Async Delegation

0Why it matters

Everything in M1 so far assumes a developer watching an agent work. This chapter is about what happens when nobody is watching — agents that run in the cloud, decoupled from anyone's laptop, for hours or days, triggered by events rather than prompts. The pattern converged across every major vendor within about a year: OpenAI's Codex cloud tasks (May 2025), Cursor's background agents (GA June 2025), Google's Jules, GitHub's Copilot coding agent, and Ona — the ex-Gitpod platform whose background-agents guide this chapter dissects, and which OpenAI agreed to acquire in June 2026 precisely "to help Codex take on longer-running work." The capability curve says this is not hype timing: METR's time-horizon data — the E7 metric — now puts the frontier at roughly a 12-hour 50%-success task length (Claude Opus 4.6, TH1.1 data), with the doubling time compressed to ~4.3 months since 2023. But the same measurement discipline cuts the other way: a July 2026 long-horizon benchmark built expressly for sustained work found the best frontier model completing only ~15% of 90-minute terminal tasks at a strict threshold — long-horizon execution is precisely where agents still run out of steam. That tension — capability doubling into a regime the benchmarks say is barely solved — is why this topic needs governance-first treatment, not enthusiasm. Our L1–L5 scale (E6) answers how much an agent may do; this chapter adds the orthogonal axes the scale does not cover: where it runs (your machine vs an isolated sandbox), when (interactive vs async), and who initiates (a human prompt vs a PR, a ticket, a CVE feed, a cron job).

What this chapter gives you
  • the background-agent execution model, and why every vendor converged on sandbox → work → PR (§1)
  • the long-horizon evidence, honestly cut: METR's curve up, LHTB's completion rates down (§2)
  • the persistence mechanics that actually work: Ralph loops, initializer/coding-agent splits, session-as-event-log (§3)
  • verification at a distance: evaluator ≠ generator, delegated approval, and the approval-fatigue numbers (§4)
  • governance: guardrails at the infrastructure layer, cost as a failure mode, the run as the audit record (§5)
  • the adoption path — maturity model, first use cases, traps — with every vendor number flagged as such (§6)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E6 — the autonomy scale this chapter extends with execution axes
  • E9 — the harness being tuned is, increasingly, this async harness
  • E4/E7 — verification and measurement carry the whole model
  • S-module — sandboxing and the lethal trifecta become non-negotiable here
  • I4 — memory systems are the persistence layer's other half
  • T1/T2 — build-vs-buy economics and the pilot that proves any of it

1The execution axis — what a background agent is

Ona's definition is the cleanest on record: "Background agents run in their own development environment in the cloud, with a full toolchain, test suite, and access to internal systems, completely decoupled from your device." The contrast class is the laptop assistant — Claude Code or Cursor in a terminal, synchronous, watched. The background agent inverts every default: it gets an ephemeral, isolated environment provisioned per task; it works asynchronously (you review the result, not the process); and increasingly it is event-initiated — a PR comment, a Slack message, a Linear ticket, a webhook, a published CVE. That last inversion is the conceptually new one for our framework: E6's levels grade how much an agent may decide, but they quietly assume a human pressed enter. Event-driven initiation removes that assumption, which is why §5's governance cannot live in the prompt.

The industry convergence is striking and verifiable from primary launch materials, whatever one thinks of any vendor's numbers: OpenAI's Codex cloud (research preview May 16, 2025) runs each task in its own cloud sandbox preloaded with your repo, works many tasks in parallel, and proposes PRs; Cursor's background agents (v0.50 May 2025, GA in 1.0 on June 4, 2025) spin up an isolated Ubuntu VM, clone the repo, work on a dedicated branch and open a PR — extended in February 2026 to full desktop-and-browser "computer use" per agent; Google's Jules is an async coding agent with a CLI and public API (October 2025) for embedding in native workflows; GitHub's Copilot coding agent picks up issues and works them in an Actions-based sandbox into a PR. Five independent product teams, one shape: sandbox → work → verifiable artifact (PR) → human review. The shape is the finding — it encodes the assumption that agents at this autonomy level must be contained (S-module), must leave an auditable trail (§5), and must terminate in the one artifact engineering organizations already know how to gate: a pull request.

Addy Osmani's companion essay ("Long-Running Agents," April 28, 2026 — high-quality practitioner synthesis, our tiering) contributes the taxonomy this chapter uses throughout, separating three things vendors blur: long-horizon reasoning (planning across many dependent steps), long-running execution (a process that survives hours or days and thousands of model calls), and persistent agency (identity and memory outliving any single task — I4's territory, and the only one of the three that raises S6-style poisoning stakes). A background agent needs the second; it benefits from the first; it does not require the third — and conflating them is how pilots end up buying persistent memory infrastructure to solve what was actually a checkpointing problem.

2The long-horizon evidence — both directions at once

The capability line every deck quotes is METR's (arXiv:2503.14499, the E7 method): "the frontier AI time horizon has been doubling approximately every seven months since 2019" — with the abstract's own hedge, "though the trend may have accelerated in 2024." It did. The TH1.1 revision (January 2026) puts the doubling time since 2023 at roughly 4.3 months (130.8 days, CI 107–161), and METR's current raw data (May 2026 update) places Claude Opus 4.6 at a ~12-hour 50%-success horizon, with an early Claude Mythos preview point at ~17.4 hours. Osmani's essay quotes only the conservative seven-month figure — when citing this material, use the accelerated numbers and name the revision, or the client's planning horizon will be off by a factor of two. The 80%-success horizons remain far shorter than the 50% ones, which is the fine print that matters for unattended work: a coin-flip success rate is delegation-with-review; it is nowhere near fire-and-forget.

Now the other direction. The Long-Horizon Terminal-Bench (LHTB, arXiv:2607.08964, July 2026) was built specifically for sustained autonomous work: 46 terminal tasks across 9 categories, a 90-minute budget per task, hidden fake-proof verifiers, and dense partial credit — because binary pass/fail "collapses almost every model to zero." Agents consumed on average 9.9M tokens and ~231 episodes per task; the best frontier model achieved 15.2% pass@1 at a 0.95 reward threshold (10.9% at a perfect 1.0), with a cross-model mean of 4.3%. SWE-Bench Pro (arXiv:2509.16941) points the same way from the task side: its problems "may require hours to days for a professional software engineer to complete," and they were chosen precisely because current agents mostly cannot. Read together, the two lines say: the frontier horizon is growing fast, and the reliability at that horizon is low. Osmani's "three walls" name the mechanisms — finite context (E2's context rot arrives long before the token limit), no persistent state across sessions, and no trustworthy self-verification (models grade their own work too generously — E4's oldest lesson). Everything in §3 and §4 is engineering around those three walls.

Numbers that do NOT exist (yet)

No independent survey publishes a "share of developers delegating async background tasks" figure. DORA 2025 reports ~90% of developers using some form of AI assistance — general assistance, not background delegation; do not stretch it. The adoption evidence for background agents specifically is vendor case studies (§6), and they are tiered accordingly.

3Persistence mechanics — surviving the context window

The primitive underneath every production pattern is embarrassingly small. Geoffrey Huntley's Ralph loop ("Ralph Wiggum as a 'software engineer'," ghuntley.com, July 14, 2025) is literally while :; do cat PROMPT.md | claude-code ; done — an amnesiac agent re-launched forever, with all state externalized to files (a PRD, a progress file, the repo itself). The insight that makes it more than a joke: the agent is amnesiac, but the filesystem is not. Fresh context each lap sidesteps context rot; durable files carry the plan forward; git carries the work. Every serious system since is a refinement of that split (attribute the pattern to Huntley, popularized with Ryan Carson — a practitioner pattern, not a standard).

Anthropic's two engineering posts supply the refined version, and E9 already met them as harness artifacts. The long-running harness (Nov 2025) splits roles: an initializer agent — "the very first agent session uses a specialized prompt that asks the model to set up the initial environment" — builds the feature list, progress notes and scripts; then a coding agent per session — "every subsequent session asks the model to make incremental progress, then leave structured updates." Its most load-bearing rule guards the work ledger itself: "It is unacceptable to remove or edit tests because this could lead to missing or buggy functionality" — which, per this workbook's I2 verification, protects the feature-list entries the harness tracks (Osmani's gloss "test ratchet" names the family of mechanism; I2/ImpossibleBench cover the protect-the-test-files variant). The Managed Agents architecture (Apr 2026) then virtualizes the whole thing: "a session (the append-only log of everything that happened), a harness (the loop that calls Claude and routes Claude's tool calls…), and a sandbox (an execution environment where Claude can run code and edit files)" — brain decoupled from hands. Recovery becomes an API call: "When one fails, a new one can be rebooted with wake(sessionId), use getSession(id) to get back the event log, and resume from the last event." The reported payoff — p50 time-to-first-token down roughly 60%, p95 down over 90% — is a vendor number, but the design lesson is tier-free: the session log, not the agent process, is the unit of persistence. Harvey's summit formulation says the same thing from the audit side: "the run is the persistent record, not the agent."

Two corollaries complete the mechanics. Context resets as a first-class operation: for very long tasks the working pattern is not rolling summarization but a full teardown — write a structured handoff document, kill the session, start clean from the handoff (the same discipline H1's contract imposes between humans and agents, applied agent-to-agent; and exactly what a Ralph lap does implicitly). Checkpoint-and-resume beats heroic sessions: commit early and often to git, treat each green state as a savepoint, and let recovery mean "reset to last savepoint and replay intent" — brownfield teams already know this discipline as trunk-based development; the agent inherits it for free if the harness enforces small increments (Anthropic's "incremental progress" prompt).

4Verification at a distance — roles, reviews, and the fatigue data

Unattended execution concentrates everything E4 says about self-verification. Both source guides converge on the same two rules. First, the evaluator is never the generator: Anthropic's harness-design post builds a GAN-inspired generator/evaluator pair (E9 §4); Ona's summit synthesis reports every adopting org giving agents the means to verify their own work mechanically — tests, telemetry queries, screenshots — rather than by self-assessment; OpenAI's internal Codex teams route agent output through agent-to-agent review before human review. Cursor reportedly goes further and splits three roles — planner / worker / judge — with different models per role, having found GPT models outlasting Opus for extended autonomous work because Opus "tended to stop early"; that detail reaches us only through Osmani's retelling, so treat it as secondhand practitioner report, not a benchmark. Second, delegated approval with escalation: pre-authorize the reversible (formatting, test additions, dependency bumps inside policy) and route the irreversible (schema changes, deletions, anything security-adjacent) to a human — E6's rubric, made operational because nobody is present to click.

The approval-fatigue telemetry explains why per-action human gating cannot be the answer at this scale. Anthropic's auto-mode post (March 2026) opens with the number: "Claude Code users approve 93% of permission prompts." A gate almost always answered yes is behaviourally no gate at all — and usage data shows auto-approve rates rising with experience, from roughly 20% for new users to over 40% by 750 sessions (partly deliberate trust calibration, per Anthropic, not pure fatigue). Even the mitigation carries a caveat worth quoting in any client conversation: Anthropic's own classifier for dangerous actions reports a "17% false-negative rate on real overeager actions… the honest number." The design conclusion is the S-module's, arrived at from the UX side: move enforcement from the approval dialog to the environment — which is §5.

5Governance — the boundary is the infrastructure

The single most reusable sentence in either source is Ona's: "If the guardrail lives inside the same context window as the agent, it's a suggestion, not a boundary. Security enforcement needs to happen at the infrastructure layer." That is E5's advisory-versus-deterministic distinction and S3's rules-file lesson, restated for the async regime — and every serious deployment in the summit material behaves accordingly: network policies, kernel-level controls, scoped short-lived credentials, air-gapped sandboxes for the most sensitive monorepos, ephemeral workers destroyed after each run with exactly one repo, one tool bundle, one artifact path and one audit trail each. Sandboxing is reported as universal — "every adopting organization sandboxes agents, no exceptions" — which we can believe as a design norm even while tiering the surrounding numbers as vendor claims, because it is the same conclusion the S-module reached from the lethal-trifecta side: an event-triggered agent processing untrusted inputs (PR comments, tickets, CVE feeds!) with repo access and an outbound channel is the trifecta by construction unless the infrastructure breaks one leg.

Cost is a governance surface, not an accounting detail. A background fleet can fail by looping, and a looping agent burns money at machine speed with nobody watching — Uber's summit self-report of AI compute costs up 6× since 2024 shows the baseline growing even when everything works. The practitioner consensus pattern is tiered budgets plus a token-velocity circuit breaker: per-session and per-day caps with automatic termination, and a rate-based trip (one published tunable: a healthy agent runs ~3–4k tokens/minute; tripping at ~10k/min catches a runaway loop inside a minute). Cite the pattern as practitioner synthesis; the viral runaway-bill dollar figures circulating with it trace only to blogs citing each other — folklore until a primary incident report exists (the same discipline that retired Boehm's 100:1 in H4). Add the two remaining rails: credential hygiene — long-lived tokens are the async agent's most dangerous convenience; scope them per run, expire them with the sandbox — and observability as a precondition, not an afterthought: Ona's red-flag list is blunt that fleets without per-run cost and outcome visibility are pilots that will not survive contact with a CFO. Harvey's "the run is the persistent record" closes the loop: the same append-only session log that §3 uses for recovery is the audit artifact compliance will ask for (H3's provenance, T4's Article-12-style logging).

6The adoption path — maturity, first use cases, and the vendor numbers

Ona's maturity model is a useful narrative frame precisely because it is self-serving — read it with that light on. Phase one, laptop code assistants, it calls a "false summit": a local maximum that speeds the individual developer without moving organizational throughput (the same individual-vs-system gap E7's measurement section documents from the DORA side). Phase two, background agents — "you can't skip this phase" — is where isolation, verification and progressive autonomy get built. Phase three, the "software factory," is fleets responding to events end-to-end ("when a CVE is published, agents scan your repos and open a PR"), humans reviewing. The vendor incentive is obvious: phase two is what Ona sells. But the engineering claim underneath survives the discount — you cannot operate event-driven fleets without first having built sandboxing, verification, observability and budgets, and teams that try to jump from laptop assistants to the factory are skipping the harness. The trap list is corroborated by everything this workbook has verified independently: too-ambitious first projects, agents treated as junior devs needing chat supervision, laptop or shared-CI execution as a dead end, no observability, security bolted on in-context instead of infra-layer, and "waiting for a better model" instead of engineering the harness (E9's founding argument).

First use cases: "repetitive, well-scoped work with clear success criteria and real business value" — migrations, flaky-test fixes, dependency upgrades, security patching, documentation. Toil, not greenfield features. This matches our brownfield thesis (M5): the highest-value early work is exactly the work with the strongest mechanical verification and the lowest ambiguity. The build-vs-buy guidance — build only with a dedicated platform team, a 12–18-month horizon, and infrastructure as a differentiator; sandboxed environments alone are a 3–6-month build — is vendor-estimated but directionally consistent with T1's economics. The summit case studies, all self-reported at a vendor-hosted event, none independently audited — quote them as existence proofs of the pattern at scale, never as expected returns:

Org (self-reported)ClaimMechanism detail worth keeping
Stripe1,000+ agent PRs/week on a 100M-line Ruby monorepoair-gapped sandboxes, no internet, no prod creds; pre-warmed devboxes ~10 s cold start; "if it's good for humans, it's good for LLMs"
Ramp30% of merged PRs agent-authored; a security sweep fixed ~100 vulns incl. IDORs pen-testing missedlightweight VMs re-imaged every 30 min; sandbox pre-warmed while the user types; agents query Sentry/Datadog and take screenshots
Spotify1,500+ agent PRs merged; 60–90% time savings on migrationsmigrations that took 20K lines of hand-coded AST transforms now specified in natural language; ran on existing fleet infra
Uber92% of developers use agents monthly; 5,000+ generated unit tests/monthan MCP gateway turning any internal endpoint into an agent tool; AI compute up 6× since 2024 — the cost rail of §5
OpenAI~1M LOC across 1,500 PRs in 5 months, team of 3→7"strict architectural constraints, enforced by linters rather than humans, made agents more productive, not less" — E5 in one sentence
Harvey100% sandboxed runs"the run is the persistent record, not the agent" — ephemeral workers, one repo/tool bundle/credential set/artifact path/audit trail each

7Week one at the client

Day one — locate the client on the map. Almost certainly phase one; the useful question is which phase-two ingredient is furthest along (usually CI as verification) and which is absent (usually per-run observability and budgets). Day two — pick the first background task from toil, not features: one migration, dependency-upgrade or flaky-test class with mechanical success criteria, run through whichever background-agent surface the client's existing vendor already ships (Copilot coding agent, Codex cloud, Cursor — no new procurement for a pilot). Day three — write the delegation contract before the first run: goal, scope, non-goals, tool permissions, stopping condition, budget cap, escalation rule (H1's contract anatomy, pointed at an agent that cannot ask mid-flight). Day four — wire the three rails: sandbox with scoped credentials (infra-layer, not prompt-layer), token/cost cap with automatic termination, and the PR as the only exit channel. Day five — review the run record, not just the diff: the session log is the audit artifact; if the pilot's runs are not reconstructible from it, fix that before scaling — it is also the E7/T2 measurement substrate. Track E6-style operational metrics from day one: interventions per run, escalation rate, cost per merged change, defect escape rate.

8Where E10 sits in the framework

Connects toHow
E6 · autonomyL1–L5 grades decision scope; E10 adds execution axes — where it runs, when, who initiates — and the approval-fatigue data that breaks per-action gating
E9 · harness disciplinethe initializer/coding-agent split, feature-list protection and generator/evaluator pairs are the async harness E9 tunes
E4 · verificationevaluator ≠ generator becomes structural: tests, telemetry and judge agents replace the absent human
E2 · contextcontext rot is the first wall; Ralph laps and context resets are its async countermeasures
S-moduleevent-triggered + repo access + outbound PR = the lethal trifecta unless infrastructure breaks a leg; "a guardrail in the context window is a suggestion"
I4 · memory / H1 · contractpersistent agency is I4's promotion path with S6 stakes; the delegation contract is H1 pointed at an executor that cannot ask
H3/T4 · provenance & compliancethe append-only run record is both the recovery mechanism and the audit trail regulation will ask for
T1/T2 · economics & pilotbuild-vs-buy timelines (vendor-estimated), cost-per-merged-change as the pilot metric, the false-summit argument for measuring org throughput

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

Background agents change the axes, not just the amount, of autonomy — where the agent runs, when, and who initiates — so governance must move from the approval dialog (93% approved, effectively unread) into the infrastructure: sandbox, scoped credentials, budgets, and the PR as the only exit. The frontier task horizon is doubling every ~4 months and sits near 12 hours at 50% success, but purpose-built long-horizon benchmarks show even the best models completing ~15% at strict thresholds — delegate toil with mechanical verification, and treat fire-and-forget as unearned. The unit of persistence, recovery and audit is the append-only run record, not the agent process — design for replay, and the same log serves the pilot's measurement and compliance for free.

10Further reading

Ona — The Background Agents Guide
ona.com/guides/background-agents · mid-2026 · vendor (ex-Gitpod; OpenAI acquisition agreed June 2026) — HIGH bias, all case numbers summit self-reports

The definition, the maturity model, the trap list, and the infra-layer guardrail line. Read for the frame; tier every number.

Addy Osmani — Long-Running Agents
addyosmani.com · April 28, 2026 · practitioner synthesis

The three-way taxonomy, the three walls, five production patterns, and the honest limitations list — cost, credentials, memory drift, auditing a 24-hour run.

METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks + Time Horizon 1.1
arXiv:2503.14499 · metr.org TH1.1 (Jan 2026, data updated May 2026) · independent research

The time-horizon metric E7 borrows: doubling ~4.3 months since 2023; Opus 4.6 ~12 h at 50%. Always cite the revision — the 7-month figure alone is stale.

Long-Horizon Terminal-Bench (LHTB)
arXiv:2607.08964 · July 2026 · independent research (preprint)

The counterweight: 46 sustained tasks, dense reward, best model 15.2% pass@1 at 0.95 threshold, mean 4.3% — where agents run out of steam.

Anthropic — Effective harnesses for long-running agents · Scaling Managed Agents
anthropic.com/engineering · Nov 26, 2025 & Apr 8, 2026 · vendor

Initializer/coding-agent split, the feature-list protection rule (I2-verified), session-as-append-only-log, wake(sessionId), and the TTFT numbers (vendor-tiered).

Geoffrey Huntley — Ralph Wiggum as a "software engineer"
ghuntley.com/ralph · July 14, 2025 · practitioner

The primitive: an amnesiac agent in a bash loop, all state in files. Everything since is a refinement.

Anthropic — How we built Claude Code auto mode · Project Vend (parts 1–2)
anthropic.com · March 25, 2026 · June 27, 2025 / Dec 2025 · vendor research

The 93% approval-fatigue number with its 17% false-negative caveat; and the month-long autonomous-business experiment ("We let Claude manage an automated store in our office…") that grounds the persistent-agency discussion.

Launch primaries for the cross-vendor pattern
openai.com (Codex, May 16 2025) · cursor.com changelog 1.0 (Jun 4 2025) · Google Jules Tools (Oct 2 2025) · GitHub Copilot coding agent docs · vendor

Five product teams, one shape: sandbox → work → PR → human review. The convergence is the evidence.

The background agent is the L4 question made concrete: not "can the model do it" but "what surrounds it when it does." Every load-bearing number here is tiered in place — METR and LHTB independent, everything with a company name attached self-reported — and every tool fact (vendors, features, acquisition status) is point-in-time as of mid-2026: re-verify before client use. E9 tunes this harness; the S-module contains it; T2 measures whether it earned its keep.

E11 · DEEP DIVE

Formal Methods in the Agentic Loop: Vericoding & Runtime Enforcement

0Why it matters

E4 built this workbook's verification hierarchy on tests — code that checks code, sampled one input at a time. Formal methods sit one rung higher: they check all inputs at once, by proof or exhaustive model checking, and for thirty years they stayed in avionics and hyperscaler infrastructure because writing the specifications cost too much. Two 2026 developments put them in this workbook. First, the economics flipped: when agents write both the code and the formal spec, the historically prohibitive authoring cost collapses — that is the argument of the antfly.io essay from the boss's reading list ("Cheap code means formal verification is reasonable now"), and it lands on prepared ground, because AWS documented the payoff back in 2015: "in every case TLA+ has added significant value, either finding subtle bugs that we are sure we would not have found by other means" — including a DynamoDB bug whose shortest error trace was 35 steps long. A benchmark now measures the practice — "vericoding," spec-to-verified-code — at 12,504 formal specifications, with off-the-shelf LLMs succeeding on 82% of Dafny tasks. Second, and separately, a research wave is applying formal methods to the agent itself: runtime enforcement frameworks (AgentSpec, ICSE 2026 — the only peer-reviewed source in this chapter) that watch an agent's actions against declarative safety rules, and predictive extensions that model-check where a trajectory is heading. The honest headline for both directions is the same: real prototypes, quantified trade-offs, and a state of the art that is runtime monitoring, not proofs of agent correctness — the December 2024 roadmap's verdict that "a fully comprehensive methodology for integrating formal methods into LLMs remains elusive" still stands. This chapter keeps the two directions rigorously apart, because conflating them is how formal verification gets oversold to clients.

What this chapter gives you
  • the two directions, kept apart: formal methods on the agent's output vs on the agent's behaviour (§1)
  • the economics that changed, with the AWS precedent and the antfly workflow read honestly (§2)
  • the vericoding evidence: 82/44/27, the 58% Dafny result, and the 8.6% TLA+ counterpoint (§3)
  • the intent gap — why the spec, not the proof, is the bottleneck (§4)
  • runtime enforcement of agent behaviour: reactive, predictive, and the measured safety–utility trade-off (§5)
  • what a brownfield client can actually use this year (§6)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E4 — the verification hierarchy this chapter extends upward
  • H2 — EARS/Gherkin are the low ceremony end of the same formalization ladder
  • E5/S3 — advisory vs deterministic: runtime enforcement is the research frontier of deterministic
  • E10 — unattended agents are why behaviour enforcement matters at all
  • E8 — spec-driven development meets checkable specs
  • B2/B3 — characterization and mutation testing, the brownfield rungs below

1Two directions, one label — keep them apart

"Formal verification of agents" is used in the wild for two nearly unrelated practices, and the first discipline this chapter teaches is to ask which one a source means. Direction A — formal methods on the agent's output: the agent (or the human) writes a machine-checkable specification, and a verifier — Dafny's SMT backend, Lean's kernel, TLA+'s model checker TLC — proves the code or design meets it. The agent's role is authorship; the guarantee attaches to the artifact. The antfly essay lives here: its agent writes TLA+ models of a distributed key-value store and hunts race conditions. Direction B — formal methods on the agent's behaviour: the agent itself is treated as an opaque, stochastic component whose actions at runtime are checked against declarative rules — stop it before it deletes the database, whatever the model weights think. AgentSpec and its successors live here; so, at the deployed-practice end, do the learned guardrail filters (NeMo Guardrails, Llama Guard) that these formal approaches aim to outdo with provable semantics. The academic framing for why Direction B is runtime-only is Ferrando's FMAS 2025 vision paper ("Watchdogs and Oracles"): LLMs are "stochastic, non-deterministic, and continually updated," so static pre-deployment verification of the model's internals is impractical — a widely held position in the formal-methods literature, and the reason every serious system in §5 is a monitor, not a proof. Note what this means for vocabulary discipline: nothing in this chapter certifies "the agent is correct." Direction A certifies artifacts; Direction B bounds behaviours.

2The economics that changed

The cost objection is real and citable. The AWS team's CACM paper ("How Amazon Web Services Uses Formal Methods," Newcombe, Rath, Zhang, Munteanu, Brooker & Deardeuff, April 2015) states the industry's prior plainly: "formal methods have a reputation of requiring a huge amount of training and effort to verify a tiny piece of relatively straightforward code, so the return on investment is only justified in safety-critical domains" — and then reports that seven Amazon teams found "that perception to be quite wrong," with TLA+ surfacing bugs no other technique caught (the DynamoDB replication bug's shortest error trace: 35 steps). They even avoided the words "formal," "verification" and "proof" internally, marketing the practice as "debugging designs." Ten years later the residual cost — a human sitting down to write the spec — is exactly the line item agents attack. Rowan Copley's antfly essay (May 6, 2026) makes the economic argument for the agent era: if generated code is nearly free, the historically expensive step of formal modelling is nearly free too, so why wouldn't we point an agent at TLA+ for anything concurrency-shaped? His workflow is worth keeping precisely because it is so disciplined about ownership: the human scopes hypotheses and boundaries in two files (assumptions.md, boundaries.md); the agent writes and runs the TLA+ models; every bug the model checker finds must be reproduced as a failing unit test before it counts; the fix and the test are kept, and the intermediate TLA+ models are discarded — the deliverable is small, the modelling is scaffolding (I1's disposable tier, applied to proofs).

Tier it honestly: Antfly is a vendor blog (the company sells agent retrieval infrastructure, not verification tooling — the technique is the pitch, not a product), the essay contains one validation anchor and no quantified metrics. And the anchor is weaker than its retellings: Copley took a real race-condition PR from the Pebble repo (CockroachDB's key-value store) recent enough to postdate the model's training data, checked out the commit before the merge — and his workflow "actually homed in on another bug, which I was then able to verify was also fixed in another Pebble PR later." It found a real, independently-confirmed bug; it did not rediscover the bug. That is still a genuine result — and precisely the kind of nuance a client deck will flatten if we let it. The strategic tailwind, meanwhile, is real and primary-sourced: Werner Vogels' February 2026 conversation with Byron Cook (who runs AWS's automated-reasoning group) frames the coupling directly — "When AI systems generate code, make decisions, or provide information, we need efficient ways to verify that their outputs are correct" — with Automated Reasoning checks already shipping in Bedrock Guardrails under the "neuro-symbolic" banner.

3Vericoding — what the numbers actually say

The practice of generating verified code from formal specifications now has a measuring stick: the vericoding benchmark (arXiv:2509.22908; BAIF and MIT authors including Max Tegmark; an arXiv preprint — no peer-reviewed venue, present it as such). It assembles 12,504 formal specifications — 3,029 Dafny, 2,334 Verus/Rust, 7,141 Lean — and coins the term in explicit contrast to its opposite: vericoding generates "formally verified code from specifications… in contrast to vibe coding, which generates potentially buggy code from a natural language description." Off-the-shelf LLM success rates: 82% in Dafny, 44% in Verus/Rust, 27% in Lean — language and toolchain maturity dominate, and the paper notes pure-Dafny verification improving from 68% to 96% over the prior year. The earlier peer-reviewed anchor points the same way: Misu, Lopes, Ma & Noble (FSE 2024, arXiv:2402.00247 — UC Irvine, not Microsoft as often misattributed) got GPT-4 to produce "verified, human-evaluated Dafny methods for 58% of the problems" on 178 MBPP tasks with retrieval-augmented prompting (19% without context) — prompt-engineering, notably, not an agentic repair loop. Re:Form (arXiv:2507.16331) closes the training-side loop: RL with feedback from the Dafny verifier itself, "reducing human annotations" because the verifier is an incorruptible reward signal — the formal-methods version of E4's verifiable-reward argument.

Now the cold water, from the TLA+ side that antfly's essay lives on. A Loyola University Chicago study ("Can LLMs Write Correct TLA+ Specifications?", arXiv:2606.05792, June 2026) tested 30 LLMs across 205 natural-language-to-TLA+ tasks: up to 26.6% syntactic correctness, and only 8.6% semantic correctness — with the sobering conclusion that "current LLMs do not generate reliable TLA+ specifications without expert oversight." SysMoBench (arXiv:2509.23130) reaches the same posture at systems scale, benchmarking AI formal modelling of real concurrent systems (Etcd, Redis, ZooKeeper) and documenting real limitations. The apparent contradiction with the 82%-Dafny number dissolves on inspection, and resolving it is the chapter's most useful technical lesson: Dafny vericoding starts from a formal spec and has a mechanical checker in the loop — the LLM iterates against ground truth. NL-to-TLA+ starts from prose and asks the model to invent the formalization — semantic correctness has no oracle. Generation against a verifier works; unsupervised formalization does not (yet). The antfly workflow quietly respects this: its human scopes the properties, and its bugs must reproduce as failing tests before they count. And the production existence proof for Direction A is AWS again: Cedar, the open-source authorization language, is developed by "verification-guided development" — the model formalized in Lean with mechanized proofs, differentially tested against the Rust implementation across millions of inputs, with the Cedar Symbolic Compiler itself implemented in Lean and proven correct. Formal verification of agent-adjacent infrastructure ships at hyperscaler scale today; what remains research is the agent writing the formalization unsupervised.

4The intent gap — the spec is the bottleneck

If verifiers are mechanical and generation is cheap, everything concentrates in one place: where does the specification come from, and does it say what we meant? Shuvendu Lahiri's grand-challenge paper (Microsoft Research, arXiv:2603.17150) names it: "The gap between informal natural language requirements and precise program behavior — the intent gap — has always plagued software engineering, but AI-generated code amplifies it to an unprecedented scale." His programme, intent formalization, is "the translation of informal user intent into a set of checkable formal specifications" — which readers of this workbook will recognize as H2's EARS/Gherkin ladder extended to its top rung. The ladder view is the practical takeaway: plain-language AC → EARS-structured requirements → Gherkin scenarios that execute → property-based tests → Dafny/TLA+ specifications that verify. Every rung up buys stronger guarantees for more authoring discipline; H2 argued most brownfield teams should climb one rung; this chapter's evidence says the top rungs are becoming climbable because the agent does the transcription while the human owns the intent. The failure mode is climbing the ladder with unowned intent — a formally verified implementation of the wrong requirement is E7's rework problem with a proof attached. That is also the honest frame for E8's spec-driven development: SDD makes the spec the primary artifact; intent formalization makes it checkable; neither makes it correct — only the humans who own the intent do (P-module, H4's clarification loop).

5Direction B — runtime enforcement of agent behaviour

The December 2024 academic roadmap (arXiv:2412.06512 — authors including PAT's creators; toolchain surveyed: Z3, Coq, Isabelle/HOL, PAT, Alloy, PRISM, UPPAAL) is the reference point for expectations: bidirectional ambitions — formal methods to make LLMs reliable, LLMs to make formal methods usable — and the verdict quoted in §0: a comprehensive methodology "remains elusive." What exists eighteen months later is a coherent runtime-enforcement stack, and it is worth teaching because it is the deterministic end-state of E5's advisory/deterministic distinction. Reactive enforcement: AgentSpec (Wang, Poskitt & Sun, SMU — accepted at ICSE 2026, the one peer-reviewed system here) is a lightweight DSL — trigger, predicates, enforcement action — evaluated on every step of the agent loop. The authors report it preventing over 90% of unsafe code-agent executions and eliminating hazardous actions across ten embodied-agent task categories at millisecond overhead. Their most decision-relevant finding is negative: rules auto-generated by an LLM (o1, few-shot) reached 95.56% precision but only 70.96% recall on embodied-agent risks, identified 87.26% of risky code, and prevented rule-breaking in only 5 of 8 autonomous-vehicle scenarios — the guardrail author cannot be the same class of system the guardrail guards, which is E4's evaluator-independence argument arriving in the safety layer. (The paper's "first runtime enforcement framework" claim is hedged "to the best of our knowledge" — NeMo Guardrails and TrustAgent predate it on adjacent ground; never state it as fact.)

Predictive enforcement: ProbGuard — formerly Pro2Guard, and not to be confused with the Java obfuscator ProGuard — extends the idea forward in time (arXiv:2508.00500; same SMU group plus Jiali Wei, XJTU; a preprint whose v3 softened its own title from "enforcement" to "monitoring").
