It learns a discrete-time Markov chain from agent execution traces and model-checks PCTL properties with PRISM at runtime, intervening when the probability of reaching an unsafe state crosses a threshold — reactive rules "lack foresight and struggle with long-horizon dependencies," in its framing (which doubles as its critique of the authors' own AgentSpec; a self-comparison, tier accordingly). Its SafeAgentBench numbers quantify the trade every client conversation about guardrails needs: baseline 40.63% of embodied-agent executions unsafe; AgentSpec-style reactive rules cut that to 19.79%; ProbGuard in hard-stop mode (θ=0.1) reaches 2.60% unsafe — but task completion collapses to 10.42%; its reflect mode lands between, 14.07% unsafe at 47.74% completion. Safety is purchasable; the currency is utility; pick the operating point per blast radius (E6's rubric, now with a measured curve). The wave behind these two is real but young — VeriGuard (Google authors, arXiv:2510.05156) verifies synthesized per-agent policies offline and monitors actions against them online; Agent Behavioral Contracts (arXiv:2602.22302) port design-by-contract with runtime-checked preconditions, invariants, governance and recovery clauses at sub-10ms overhead — and the deployed baseline they all aim to beat is the learned filter (NeMo Guardrails, arXiv:2310.10501; Llama Guard, arXiv:2312.06674), which offers no semantics, only a classifier. Every system in this paragraph except AgentSpec is a non-peer-reviewed preprint; the 2025–26 trajectory is steep, the deployed base is thin, and both facts belong in any client sentence about "provable agent safety."

6Week one at the client — what is usable now

Almost nothing in §5 is procurable today, and saying so is part of the value. What a brownfield client can actually deploy this year, in ascending ambition: (1) The deterministic rails we already teach — E5's hooks, I2's write permissions, S8's rules — are runtime enforcement without the formal semantics; frame AgentSpec-style DSLs as where that stack is heading, not what to buy. (2) Direction A on the narrowest, hottest surface: one concurrency-critical or money-critical component, the antfly workflow with its ownership discipline intact — human-scoped properties in reviewed files, agent-written TLA+ or property-based models, every finding reproduced as a failing test before anyone believes it, models discarded, tests kept. This is a contained experiment with mechanical exit criteria (T2), and the 8.6% semantic-correctness number is the reason the human scoping step is non-negotiable. (3) The intent ladder, one rung: where H2 already put EARS/Gherkin in place, pilot property-based tests generated from AC on one module — the cheapest formalization step with a real oracle. (4) Watch, don't buy, Direction B: track AgentSpec (ICSE 2026) and the behavioural-contracts wave; revisit when something ships with support. The reusable client sentence: proofs for the artifact where it is cheapest, rails for the agent where it is riskiest, and nobody certifies "the agent is correct."

7Where E11 sits in the framework

Connects toHow
E4 · verification-firstthe hierarchy's top rung: tests sample, proofs quantify over all inputs; the verifier as incorruptible reward (Re:Form) is E4's protect-the-oracle argument
H2 · EARS/Gherkinone formalization ladder: EARS → Gherkin → property tests → Dafny/TLA+; the intent gap (arXiv:2603.17150) is H2's problem statement at scale
E5/S3 · advisory vs deterministicruntime enforcement is the deterministic column's research frontier; AgentSpec's 70.96%-recall auto-rules warn against agent-authored guardrails
E10 · background agentsunattended execution is why behaviour bounding matters; the safety–utility curve is E10's delegation contract, quantified
E8 · SDDspec-driven development supplies the artifact; vericoding makes it checkable; neither makes it correct — intent stays human-owned
E9 · harness disciplineenforcement monitors are harness components; ProbGuard's threshold is a tunable with a measured trade-off — E9's eval loop applies
B2/B3 · brownfieldcharacterization tests and mutation gates are the rungs a legacy codebase climbs first; formal models of one hot component come after, not instead
E6 · autonomyblast radius picks the enforcement operating point: hard-stop for irreversible surfaces, reflect for the rest

8Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences to keep

"Formal verification of agents" means two different things — proving properties of what the agent produced (real today where a mechanical verifier sits in the loop: 82% Dafny vericoding, Cedar/Lean in production) versus bounding what the agent does (a fast-moving research stack — AgentSpec, ICSE 2026 — whose state of the art is runtime monitoring, not proofs) — and every source should be asked which one it means. The economics genuinely flipped: agents collapse the spec-authoring cost that kept formal methods in avionics, but the 8.6% semantic-correctness result for unsupervised NL-to-TLA+ says the human still owns the intent and the properties. Safety from runtime enforcement is purchasable and now priced — 40.6% → 2.6% unsafe at the cost of 59% → 10% task completion — so the blast radius, not the vendor deck, picks the operating point.

9Further reading

Newcombe et al. — How Amazon Web Services Uses Formal Methods
CACM 58(4) · April 2015 · peer-reviewed industry report

The precedent and the cost-perception quote: seven teams, TLA+ finding 35-step bugs, "debugging designs" as the internal sales pitch.

Rowan Copley — Cheap code means formal verification is reasonable now
antfly.io/research · May 6, 2026 · practitioner (vendor blog; Antfly sells agent retrieval, not verification)

The economic thesis and the disciplined workflow — human-scoped properties, bugs must reproduce as failing tests, models discarded. One validation anchor (Pebble — found a different, later-fixed bug), zero quantified metrics.

A benchmark for vericoding: formally verified program synthesis
arXiv:2509.22908 · BAIF + MIT · preprint (no peer-reviewed venue — present as such)

12,504 specs; 82% Dafny / 44% Verus / 27% Lean; "vericoding" vs "vibe coding" coined.

Misu, Lopes, Ma & Noble — Towards AI-Assisted Synthesis of Verified Dafny Methods
FSE 2024 (PACMSE) · arXiv:2402.00247 · peer-reviewed · UC Irvine + Noble

GPT-4: verified Dafny for 58% of 178 MBPP problems with retrieval-augmented prompting (19% contextless). Prompting, not an agentic repair loop.

Can LLMs Write Correct TLA+ Specifications? · SysMoBench
arXiv:2606.05792 (Loyola U Chicago) · arXiv:2509.23130 (UIUC/UBC/Nanjing) · independent research

The counterweight: 26.6% syntactic / 8.6% semantic correctness across 30 LLMs; "not without expert oversight." Formalization has no oracle; generation against a verifier does.

Lahiri — Intent Formalization: A Grand Challenge for Reliable Coding in the Age of AI Agents
arXiv:2603.17150 · Microsoft Research · position paper

The intent gap named and the programme defined: informal intent → checkable formal specifications. H2's ladder, top rung.

AgentSpec — runtime enforcement for LLM agents · ProbGuard (formerly Pro2Guard)
ICSE 2026, arXiv:2503.18666 (peer-reviewed) · arXiv:2508.00500 (preprint, same group — self-comparison)

Reactive rules (>90% unsafe-execution prevention; auto-generated rules only 70.96% recall) and predictive DTMC+PCTL monitoring with the quantified safety–utility curve.

Ferrando — Watchdogs and Oracles · the FM-LLM roadmap
FMAS 2025, EPTCS 436 (arXiv:2511.14435) · arXiv:2412.06512 (Dec 2024)

Why Direction B is runtime-only (stochastic, non-deterministic, continually updated), and the sober roadmap: a comprehensive methodology "remains elusive."

AWS Cedar + Lean — verification-guided development · Vogels × Byron Cook on automated reasoning
AWS open-source blog / lean-lang.org use-cases · allthingsdistributed.com · Feb 17, 2026 · vendor

The production proof-point (mechanized Lean proofs + differential testing at scale) and the strategic frame: verify AI outputs with automated reasoning ("neuro-symbolic").

This chapter earns its place by refusing a conflation: proofs about artifacts are shipping, bounds on behaviour are research, and "the agent is formally verified" is a sentence that should never survive our review. The ladder metaphor carries the practice: most clients should climb one rung (H2 → property tests), a few should point an agent at TLA+ for one hot component with human-owned properties, and everyone should know the safety–utility price list before buying enforcement. All tool and paper statuses point-in-time mid-2026; the behavioural-contracts wave is moving fast — re-verify before citing.

I6 · DEEP DIVE

Retrieval: Agentic Search, Embedding Indexes & Knowledge Graphs

0Why it matters

I5 closed on a promise: disclosure stages what is known to matter; retrieval finds what no map anticipated. This chapter is about that finding — where the agent's knowledge of the codebase comes from at query time. Three architectures compete for the job: a live search loop the model drives itself (grep, glob, read — nothing precomputed), a precomputed embedding index queried by similarity (the RAG pattern), and a graph of code entities and relations. The stakes are easy to underestimate because 2023-era folklore settled the question early: "AI needs your code in a vector database" was the default enterprise assumption, and vector-store line items are still in many clients' AI budgets. The production reality of 2025–26 is stranger and more instructive: the most-used coding agent ships no index at all — Boris Cherny, who created Claude Code, put it flatly: "Claude Code doesn't use RAG currently. In our testing we found that agentic search out-performed RAG for the kinds of things people use Code for" — while Cursor runs a hybrid it defends with measurements, Sourcegraph publicly walked its embeddings back, and Augment doubled down on vectors for 100-million-line codebases. Every position on the board is occupied by a serious team with a serious argument, which is precisely why this is a harness architecture decision (E1) and not a tool detail: the retrieval choice determines what your client must build, sync, secure and pay for — an index is a second copy of the codebase with its own freshness problem (I3), its own attack surface (M7), and its own bill. The chapter maps the lanes, reads the evidence, prices the index tax, deflates the knowledge-graph hype, and ends in an escalation ladder you can defend in a client meeting.

What this chapter gives you
  • the four retrieval lanes — agentic search, embedding index, symbol index, knowledge graph — as engineering trade-offs, not religions (§1)
  • what production agents actually shipped, with the primary quotes: the no-index camp, the hybrid, the retreat, the counterexamples (§2)
  • the independent evidence — and the folklore correction about BM25 vs embeddings on code (§3)
  • the index tax: staleness, embedding inversion, and the cost curve (§4)
  • knowledge graphs over code: what is measured, what is self-reported, what has shipped (§5)
  • an escalation ladder with measurable triggers — our synthesis (§6)
  • a week-one retrieval audit for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • I5 — disclosure: the map layer makes search cheap; retrieval finds what the map missed
  • I1/I3 — every index is a derived artifact that can drift
  • I4 — memory: the same retrieval question over the agent's own notes
  • E2 — context engineering: search results are context; loops cost tokens
  • E7 — metrics discipline: every number in §2 is a vendor number
  • M7 — security: embeddings are recoverable; the vector store is an asset

1The four retrieval lanes

Strip the marketing and there are four ways an agent can find things in a codebase. They differ on one axis that matters more than any other: how much is precomputed — because everything precomputed must be stored, synced, secured and paid for, and everything computed live must be paid for in tokens and turns instead.

LANE A

Agentic search

nothing precomputed · the model drives

The model composes searches itself — find files by name pattern, grep file contents, read what looks promising — inspects the results, and refines. Claude Code's tool tier is cost-ordered exactly this way: Glob returns paths for near-zero tokens, Grep (ripgrep underneath) returns matches, Read pays hundreds to thousands of tokens per file — so a competent loop spends cheaply before it spends dearly. Search-relevance veteran Doug Turnbull's diagnosis of why this suddenly works is worth keeping: the win is not grep, it is the loop around grep — an agent that reacts to what it finds, backtracks, and is checked by an outer validation loop. His caveat travels too: "feedback that's not actionable doesn't help an agent" — a tool that returns 4,000 unranked matches has not helped. Zero build cost, zero sync, structurally fresh (it searches the working tree as it is right now); the price is per-query tokens and latency measured in seconds.

LANE B

Embedding index — the RAG lane

chunks precomputed · similarity lookup

The pattern the term RAG named (Lewis et al., NeurIPS 2020, arXiv:2005.11401): split the corpus into chunks, embed each into a vector, store the vectors; at query time embed the question and return the nearest chunks. Its unique power is matching meaning without shared words — "where do we throttle API clients?" can land on RateLimiter even if no file contains the word "throttle". Its unique costs are the pipeline: an index to build, re-embed on every change, host somewhere, and secure — §4 prices all three. Latency, once built, is milliseconds — which §2 shows is exactly why the products that kept embeddings are the ones with millisecond features.

LANE C

Symbol & structure index

derived from syntax · precise relations

Not semantic, not lexical: structural. Tree-sitter or a language server parses the code and answers questions grep answers badly — definitions, references, call sites, "who uses this symbol". Two shipped forms: aider's repo map (tree-sitter symbols, graph-ranked, packed to a 1k-token default budget — I5's computed map), and LSP-backed tool servers such as Serena, which expose find_symbol / find_referencing_symbols to any MCP-capable agent. In I1's taxonomy these are derived artifacts: regenerated from code, never hand-edited, cheap to keep fresh because the compiler toolchain already does the parsing. This lane is the quiet workhorse — most of what a "code knowledge graph" promises, it already delivers without a database.

LANE D

Code knowledge graph

entities + relations · a database of the repo

The maximal precompute: extract entities (modules, classes, functions) and typed relations (calls, imports, inherits) into a graph database the agent queries — CodexGraph (arXiv:2408.03910) is the cleanest research statement of the idea. The pitch is multi-hop structural questions in one cheap query instead of a grep chain. The status — measured results, self-reported gains, and what has actually shipped — gets §5 to itself, because this is where the hype concentration is highest.

Lanes are tools, not religions

Nothing forces a harness to pick one lane. The visible trend of 2026 is that retrieval choices migrate to the tool boundary: Serena ships lane C as an MCP server, Augment ships its context engine as one, research prototypes ship lane D the same way — so the agent picks grep for an exact symbol, the symbol graph for "who calls this", and semantic search for a fuzzy concept, per query. The architecture question then becomes not "which retrieval?" but "which retrieval tools earn a place on the agent's belt?" — which is E9's harness-tuning question wearing different clothes.

2What production agents actually shipped

Positions first, arguments after. Everything in this section is a vendor statement about the vendor's own product — first-person and primary-sourced, but none of it independently benchmarked (E7's standing caveat), and all of it point-in-time mid-2026.

ProductRetrieval architectureStated evidence / reasons
Claude Codeagentic search only — glob/grep/read; no index"agentic search out-performed RAG"; simpler; security, privacy, staleness, reliability
OpenAI Codexagentic search — ripgrep preferred in the core promptno stated anti-RAG position; semantic indexing an open feature request
Cursorhybrid — embedding index (Merkle-synced) + heavy grep+12.5% avg QA accuracy from semantic search (internal bench)
Sourcegraph Codyhad embeddings → removed them (Feb 2024) for native code searchthird-party processing, freshness ops, >100k-repo scale
Devin (Cognition)indexes — DeepWiki auto-generated wiki + code graph over your reposwiki-grounded answers; "advanced code search" on top
WindsurfRiptide — an LLM run over candidates as the reranker, not embeddings alone"3x the recall of state-of-the-art embedding systems" (vendor)
Augment Codeembeddings, doubled down — quantized vector search for 100M+ LOCcompletions latency >40% better; 2GB→250MB via quantization (vendor)
aiderlane C — tree-sitter repo map, graph-ranked, 1k-token budgeta derived map, not a search index

The no-index camp

Claude Code is the flagship datum because the team tried the alternative first. Cherny on Hacker News (February 2025): "Claude Code doesn't use RAG currently. In our testing we found that agentic search out-performed RAG for the kinds of things people use Code for." He later restated the history with the reasons attached: "Early versions of Claude Code used RAG + a local vector db, but we found pretty quickly that agentic search generally works better. It is also simpler and doesn't have the same issues around security, privacy, staleness, and reliability." The Pragmatic Engineer's write-up of the story (March 2026) adds texture: the team tried "local vector databases, recursive model-based indexing, and other fancy approaches," all with downsides ("stale indexes, permission complexity") — "plain glob and grep, driven by the model, beat everything," an approach reportedly inspired by how engineers searched code at Meta when fancier tooling broke. Note what the stated reasons are not: nobody claims grep finds more relevant code than a good embedder — the case is systems-shaped (simplicity, freshness, privacy, reliability), and §3 shows the benchmark literature is consistent with that reading. OpenAI's Codex sits in the same lane with less ceremony: its core prompt bakes in a ripgrep preference ("prefer rg… since rg is faster than grep"), and semantic indexing exists mainly as an open feature request — a default, not a manifesto. Don't cite Codex as an anti-RAG position; cite it as the same architecture arrived at quietly.

The hybrid, defended with a number

Cursor is the measured counterpoint. Its semantic-search post (November 6, 2025) reports that adding embedding search to a grep-capable agent yields "on average 12.5% higher accuracy in answering questions (6.5%–23.5% depending on the model)" — measured on Cursor Context Bench, an internal, unpublished eval — and is explicit that the agent "makes heavy use of grep as well as semantic search, and the combination of these two leads to the best outcomes." Two details are worth repeating precisely: the 12.5% attaches to semantic search's lift on their QA eval, not to some grep-vs-index showdown; and the embedding model is custom, trained on ranked traces from their own agent sessions — a reminder that a serious lane-B commitment includes training and serving infrastructure, not an off-the-shelf vector DB.

The retreat, and the counterexamples

Sourcegraph ran the experiment in the other direction and published the result (February 15, 2024): "Embeddings have been at the backbone of Cody's retrieval stack since we launched… and now that Cody Enterprise is generally available, we're leaving them behind (for now)." Three stated reasons, all of them §4's index taxes in vendor prose: code "has to be sent to a 3rd party (OpenAI) for processing"; "creating embeddings and keeping them up-to-date introduces complexity"; and vector search over ">100,000 repositories is complex and resource-intensive." (Precision on the product's fate: Cody's Free and Pro tiers were discontinued July 23, 2025 as Sourcegraph pivoted to its agentic tool Amp; Cody Enterprise continues — the embeddings retreat was 2024 and architectural, not part of the sunset.)

And the honest board has counterexamples. Devin indexes — its DeepWiki "automatically indexes your repos and produces wikis with architecture diagrams, links to sources, and summaries," and Devin's answers are wiki-grounded with code search layered on top; an earlier draft of this workbook claimed Devin followed the no-index pattern, and that was simply wrong. Windsurf's Riptide attacks the retrieval-quality problem with more compute rather than less: an LLM evaluates candidate snippets for relevance at search time, claiming "3x the recall of state-of-the-art embedding systems" (a Codeium-era post now hosted on Cognition's domain after the acquisitions — treat the details as historical). Augment made the strongest embeddings-at-scale case: quantized vector search serving 100-million-line codebases, with completions latency "improved by over 40%" and index memory cut "8x (from 2GB to 250MB for a 100M LOC codebase)" (June 2025, updated June 2026). Read the counterexamples carefully and a pattern falls out — flag it as this workbook's synthesis, not anyone's official doctrine: the split tracks product shape more than conviction. Millisecond features (completions, real-time navigation, answering over repos you haven't cloned) cannot wait for a grep loop, so they need an index; task agents that think in seconds can afford to search live. Where on that spectrum your client's use case sits is the first question of §6.

3The evidence — what independent sources actually show

Separate the lanes of evidence the way E7 taught: shared benchmarks first, practitioner testimony second, vendor numbers last.

Retrieval caps single-shot performance — and the cap moved the paradigm

The original SWE-bench paper (Jimenez et al., ICLR 2024, arXiv:2310.06770) ran the experiment everyone now forgets it ran: give the model issue text plus retrieved files (BM25), versus issue text plus the oracle files — the ones the real fix actually edited. Claude 2 resolved 1.96% with BM25 retrieval and 4.80% with the oracle; GPT-4, 1.74% even with the oracle (on a 25% subset). Two readings, both load-bearing. First, retrieval quality strictly caps a retrieve-then-edit pipeline — BM25 roughly halved the oracle score. Second, and more important for this chapter: even perfect retrieval left single digits on the table, so the field's answer was not better retrieval, it was abandoning single-shot — letting the model search, read, run and iterate. Retrieval stopped being a preprocessing stage and became part of the loop. That is the deepest sense in which "agentic search won": not grep over embeddings, but iteration over one-shot context stuffing.

Agentless (Xia et al., arXiv:2407.01489) is the cleanest demonstration that the middle ground works too: no autonomous agent, no vector index — a fixed three-phase pipeline whose localization is hierarchical and LLM-driven (repo structure → files → classes/functions → edit locations), scoring 32.00% on SWE-bench Lite at $0.70 average per issue (its 2024 numbers; the leaderboard has moved). Locating code by structure, letting the model read the tree the way a new hire would, needed no embeddings to beat contemporaneous agent frameworks.

The folklore correction: grep does not win on retrieval quality

CodeRAG-Bench (Wang et al., NAACL 2025 Findings, arXiv:2406.14497) benchmarked 10 retrievers × 10 generators on code tasks and reads as a caution in both directions: "notable gains are made in final code generation by retrieving high-quality contexts," but "current retrievers still struggle to fetch useful contexts especially with limited lexical overlap, and generators fail to improve with limited context lengths or abilities to integrate additional contexts." And here the folklore needs correcting: on these code benchmarks, dense code-trained embedders frequently surpass BM25 — notable precisely because in general-domain IR, BM25 usually wins out-of-domain (CoIR, arXiv:2407.02883, is the dedicated code-retrieval benchmark to pin this). So do not repeat "lexical search is just better for code." The defensible claim is different: embeddings retrieve somewhat better on average, and the no-index camp wins anyway — on freshness, simplicity, privacy, and above all on the agent's ability to compensate for a dumb tool by iterating. Colin Flaherty, who built Augment's SWE-bench-topping agent before the industry's agentic turn, says exactly this: "We explored adding various embedding-based retrieval tools, but found that for SweeBench tasks this was not the bottleneck — grep and find were sufficient" — his stated reasons: benchmark repos are modest in size, code is keyword-dense, and a persistent agent recovers from bad first searches.

Benchmark shape is doing work in every direction

SWE-bench repos fit comfortably in the regime where grep thrives (Flaherty's own caveat), so "grep sufficed on SWE-bench" does not settle the 100M-LOC monorepo question — that is Augment's regime, and no shared benchmark covers it. Symmetrically, none of §2's vendor numbers (12.5%, 3× recall, 40% fewer tokens, 99.9% accuracy) were produced on a benchmark anyone else can run. Mid-2026 there is no public, adversarial grep-vs-index benchmark at enterprise scale. When the client asks "which is better?", the honest answer is: for task agents on normal repos, the production consensus and the SWE-bench-era evidence favor search loops; past that, you are choosing between vendors' self-measurements — so run your own (§6).

The one measured head-to-head with structure

The nearest thing to a controlled lane-A-vs-lane-D comparison is Codebase-Memory (arXiv:2603.27277, March 2026 — a young preprint, weight it accordingly): a tree-sitter-derived knowledge graph served over MCP reached "83% answer quality versus 92% for a file-exploration agent, at ten times fewer tokens and 2.1 times fewer tool calls" across 31 real repositories, matching or exceeding the explorer on graph-native queries (hubs, callers) in 19 of 31. That is the honest shape of the whole trade: the grep loop keeps a quality edge; the structure pays a token tax an order of magnitude smaller. Chroma's context-rot report (July 2025, vendor technical report, 18 models) and RepoQA (arXiv:2406.06025) close the loop from the long-context side: performance degrades as input grows and needle-finding in repos stays imperfect — so "just give the model a huge window and skip retrieval entirely" is not the exit; something still has to choose what enters the window (E2).

4The index tax

Whatever an index buys, it costs along three axes. This section is the part of the chapter a client's platform team should read twice, because every line item lands on them.

Tax one: staleness — the index is a derived artifact that drifts

An embedding index is a second copy of the codebase in another representation. In I1's taxonomy it is a derived artifact; I3's law applies in full: every derived copy is drifting toward wrong the moment it is written. Cherny's list put "staleness" in the middle of the reasons to skip the whole problem; Sourcegraph's retreat named "keeping them up-to-date" as a core complexity. The engineering answer, where the index is kept, is real but nontrivial: Cursor's indexing pipeline (January 27, 2026 post) hashes every file into a Merkle tree ("a cryptographic hash of every file, along with hashes of each folder that are based on the hashes of its children"), re-embeds only changed chunks, and caches embeddings by chunk content — and even the bookkeeping is material: "In a workspace with fifty thousand files, just the filenames and SHA-256 hashes add up to roughly 3.2 MB." Grep's freshness, by contrast, is structural — it searches the bytes on disk at query time, so there is nothing to sync and no window in which the agent can be confidently wrong about code that changed a minute ago. For a brownfield client, put it in governance terms: an adopted index needs an owner, an automated sync, and a freshness check (I3's per-category policy) exactly like any other derived artifact — if nobody will own that, that alone decides §6.

Tax two: security — embeddings are recoverable code

The comforting intuition that a vector index is an abstract mathematical residue of the code is false. Vec2Text (Morris et al., EMNLP 2023, arXiv:2310.06816) showed an iterative inversion attack that "is able to recover 92% of 32-token text inputs exactly"; follow-on work relaxed the assumptions — transferable attacks that need no query access to the victim embedder (ACL 2024, arXiv:2406.10280) and few-shot variants (arXiv:2502.11308). The demonstrations are on text, but the mechanism is content-agnostic; treat an embedding store as a recoverable copy of the source and classify it accordingly (M7): where the vectors live, who can query them, and what leaves the building are now questions about the code itself. This was Sourcegraph's first stated reason (embedding meant code "has to be sent to a 3rd party… for processing"), and it is what Cursor's content-proof design is engineered against — the server stores the Merkle tree "as a set of content proofs" and "the client can never see results for code it doesn't already have." Note the limit of the public record: Cursor's fetchable pages do not state that plaintext code is never persisted server-side — verify current guarantees directly before an enterprise adoption, point-in-time. And agentic search is not tax-free here either: it inherits whatever file-access the agent has (I2's write-permission discipline is the control surface), it just adds no second store to defend.

Tax three: cost — the crossover arithmetic

Practitioner Harrison Guo frames the choice as one formula worth stealing for client conversations: total cost = build cost + maintenance × time + per-query cost × queries. Grep sets the first two terms to zero and pays per query in tokens and round-trips; an index pays heavily up front to make queries nearly free. The crossover is therefore about scale and query volume, and the extremes are documented. At the top: Augment's stated arithmetic — good embedding models "require 20+ bytes per LOC to store and 20+ nanoseconds per LOC to search," so a 100M-LOC codebase means ~2 GB of vectors and 2+ seconds per brute search, which is why they quantize (their reported 8× memory reduction and sub-200ms search are the vendor's own numbers). At planetary scale the argument inverts entirely: GitHub's code search (the Blackbird engine, February 2023) indexes 15.5B documents and 115 TB across ~45M repositories into a 25 TB lexical sparse-ngram index — because brute grep at that scale would need "2,048 CPU cores for 96 seconds to serve a single query." Two lessons hide in that number: corpus-scale search needs an index, full stop — and the biggest code-search operator on earth chose an inverted lexical index, not embeddings, to build it. Meanwhile the vector-DB industry runs the token-cost argument against the grep loop — Milvus/Zilliz: "Token usage dropped by over 40%, without any loss in recall" with their semantic plugin (August 2025) — a claim from the vendor selling the fix, quoted here as the named opposing voice, not as evidence; the independent Codebase-Memory measurement (§3) at least confirms the direction that structure saves tokens. The honest summary: on a normal repo with a task agent, the grep loop's token tax is real but the index's three taxes are realer; the regimes where that flips are §6's triggers.

5Knowledge graphs over code — the hype check

Lane D attracts the most consultant enthusiasm and has shipped the least. Hold three facts together and the picture is sober.

The most common miscitation in this topic: GraphRAG is not a code technique

Microsoft's GraphRAG (arXiv:2404.16130) — the paper that made "knowledge graph + RAG" a boardroom phrase — is about query-focused summarization over document corpora: podcast transcripts and news articles at ~1M tokens, where an LLM-extracted entity graph plus community summaries answers global "what are the main themes" questions. Its reported wins over vector RAG (comprehensiveness 72–83%, diversity 62–82%) are LLM-as-judge pairwise win rates — self-evaluated by a method with known biases, and on the wrong corpus type entirely. Citing GraphRAG to justify a code knowledge graph is a category error; do it in front of the wrong audience and it costs you the room.

The actual code-KG literature is real but self-reported. CodexGraph (arXiv:2408.03910) puts an LLM in front of a graph database of repo structure and labels itself "work in progress"; RepoGraph (arXiv:2410.14684) is a plug-in repo-level graph reporting SWE-bench-Lite improvements for the systems it augments; CGM (arXiv:2505.16901) and KG-guided repair (arXiv:2503.21710) continue the line. Every headline gain in that list is the authors' own evaluation — no independent replication, no adversarial benchmark (E7 files these where vendor claims go). The one measured head-to-head we have is §3's Codebase-Memory result: ~90% of the explorer's answer quality at a tenth of the tokens, better on graph-native questions, worse when the answer needs full source context — a genuine, quantified niche, not a revolution.

And the shipping test: as of mid-2026 there is no public evidence that any major production coding agent uses a code knowledge graph as its retrieval backbone (a hard-to-prove negative — state it as "no public evidence," not impossibility). The nearest shipped relatives are telling about what actually survives contact with production: Devin's DeepWiki (a generated wiki plus code graph — an onboarding and Q&A layer more than a retrieval backbone), and Greptile's AST-derived docstring-embedding graph — built for code review, a batch workload whose economics differ from an interactive agent's. The practical verdict for a client: the structural benefits a KG promises are mostly available today from lane C — LSP and tree-sitter tools, derived artifacts with toolchain-grade freshness and no graph database to feed. Put lane D on the watchlist, revisit when someone publishes an independent benchmark win plus a staleness story; do not put it in the harness.

6The escalation ladder

This rubric is our synthesis — assembled from the evidence above, not an industry standard; flag it the way D1–D5 are flagged. The default is set by the production consensus and the price list; each rung above it is bought only by a measured trigger, never by architecture enthusiasm.

L0

Agentic search + the map

the default — start every engagement here

Grep/glob/read driven by the model, over a repo whose always-loaded core is a map (I5): ARCHITECTURE.md for the hand-written topology (matklad's "10x more time to figure out where" is the cost being avoided), context files naming the load-bearing directories. The map is what makes search cheap — it turns the first three exploratory turns into zero turns. Cost: tokens per query. Prerequisites: none. This is what Claude Code and Codex ship, and it needs no procurement meeting.

L1

Derived structure tools

cheap, fresh, undersold

Add lane C: a repo map computed from tree-sitter (aider-style), LSP-backed symbol tools (Serena-class MCP servers) for definitions/references/call sites. These are derived artifacts the toolchain keeps fresh nearly for free, they answer exactly the questions grep answers worst, and they capture most of the KG promise without a database. For a brownfield client with a real language server already in CI, this rung is close to free — take it early.

L2

Hybrid: add an embedding index

bought by a trigger, run under governance

Adopt lane B only when a §-below trigger is measured, and adopt it with the full I-module governance: a named owner, automated Merkle-style sync, a security review of where vectors live and who queries them (§4, M7), and a before/after eval on the client's own query set — not the vendor's. Cursor's experience says the ceiling is real (+12.5% on their eval, with a custom-trained embedder); Sourcegraph's says the floor is real too.

L3

Code knowledge graph

watchlist — not a client recommendation

Experimental (§5). Revisit on independent benchmark evidence plus a credible freshness story. If a client is already being sold one, the questions that cut fastest: who re-extracts the graph on every merge, what did it score on a benchmark the vendor doesn't own, and what does lane C not give you?

TriggerWhat you observeEscalate to
scalerepo approaching the ~1M-LOC-plus regime where grep loops visibly thrash (Guo's crossover; Augment's 100M-LOC world)L1, then L2
query shaperecurring conceptual queries with little lexical overlap keep failing — the CodeRAG-Bench limit in the wildL2
latency classthe use case is millisecond-shaped: completions, real-time navigation, org-wide code Q&AL2 (this is §2's product-shape split)
corpus breadthsearch must span hundreds of repos the agent hasn't cloned — Blackbird's lesson: index yes, and lexical may sufficeL2 (consider lexical/Zoekt-class first)
token pressuremeasured search-loop token spend dominates session cost (the Codebase-Memory 10× direction)L1 first — structure is the cheap token fix
structural queries"who calls this / what breaks if I change this" dominates and grep chains sprawlL1 — this rung exists for exactly this
Measure before you buy — the twenty-query bench

Before any L2 conversation, collect ~20 real retrieval queries from the client's actual agent sessions — the searches that preceded wrong answers are the valuable ones — and run them against L0/L1 and a candidate index. Score answer quality, tokens, and wall-clock. This is E7's discipline applied locally: every number in §2 is a vendor's self-measurement on a private eval; the only benchmark that binds your client's decision is the one built from their queries on their code. It also answers this topic's standing open question — "at what repo size does plain grep start losing?" — empirically, for the one repo that matters.

7Week one at the client

The retrieval audit runs in parallel with I5's disclosure audit — same week, same interviews.

Do — in this order
  • 1 · Inventory the retrieval surface. For each agent in use: which lanes does it actually have — grep tools, an embedding index (whose? synced how?), symbol tools, MCP search servers? Most clients cannot answer this; the answer is the architecture.
  • 2 · Build the map before any index. ARCHITECTURE.md + context files naming load-bearing directories (I5). Cheapest retrieval upgrade that exists.
  • 3 · Take the free L1 rung. If the stack has a language server, wire symbol tools (repo map, LSP/MCP) into the harness.
  • 4 · Collect the twenty-query bench from real session transcripts (§6) and classify failures by trigger: scale, query shape, latency, structure.
  • 5 · If an index already exists, govern it: name an owner, verify sync automation, run the M7 questions (where do vectors live, who queries, what leaves) — and check it against I3 drift today.
Don't
  • let "we need a vector database" enter the SOW before the twenty-query bench exists
  • cite Cursor's 12.5%, Riptide's 3×, or Milvus's 40% as if they were independent — all vendor, all private evals (E7)
  • cite GraphRAG for code retrieval — wrong corpus, LLM-judged wins (§5)
  • treat an existing embedding index as harmless metadata — it is recoverable code (Vec2Text, M7)
  • promise the client grep "beats" embeddings on retrieval quality — the honest case is freshness, simplicity, privacy and iteration (§3)

8Where I6 sits in the framework

TopicThe connection
E1/E9Retrieval tools are harness surface: which lanes sit on the agent's belt is a tunable, eval-driven decision (E9), not a vendor default.
E2Search results are context: the grep loop's token tax and the index's millisecond answers are two ways of paying the same context bill.
E7Every §2 number is a vendor self-measurement on a private eval; the twenty-query bench is E7's answer at client scale.
I1Indexes, repo maps and graphs are derived artifacts — regenerated, never hand-edited, owned.
I3Index staleness is drift with a vector store attached; the freshness policy applies before the first query is served.
I4Memory retrieval is this chapter's question over the agent's own notes — and Momento's finding (agents over-trust stale memory) is the staleness tax in miniature.
I5The map layer is the retrieval accelerant: disclosure stages what is known to matter, search finds the rest — each makes the other cheaper.
M5Brownfield archaeology is retrieval under the worst conditions — undocumented code is exactly where the map is missing and search must carry everything.
M7The vector store is an asset class: embedding inversion makes it recoverable source; agentic search inherits the agent's file permissions (I2) instead.

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences

Production coding agents mostly answered the retrieval question against the 2023 folklore — no index, model-driven grep/glob/read — and the stated reasons are systems reasons (simplicity, freshness, privacy, reliability), not retrieval-quality reasons: on quality alone, dense code-trained embedders often beat lexical search, and the agent's iteration is what closes the gap. An index is a second copy of the code with three taxes — it drifts (I3), it is recoverable source (Vec2Text, M7), and it bills for build, sync and serving — so it must be bought by a measured trigger (scale, low-overlap queries, millisecond latency, corpus breadth), never by default. Our ladder — L0 search+map, L1 derived structure, L2 governed hybrid, L3 KG-watchlist — is this workbook's synthesis; the twenty-query bench on the client's own code is what moves anyone up a rung.

10Further reading

Boris Cherny — on Claude Code's retrieval (HN thread; later restatement)
news.ycombinator.com item 43164253 · February 2025 · first-person vendor

The primary quotes: "agentic search out-performed RAG for the kinds of things people use Code for," and the restated history — early RAG + local vector DB dropped for security, privacy, staleness, reliability.

The Pragmatic Engineer — Building Claude Code with Boris Cherny
newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com · March 4, 2026

The fuller story: local vector DBs and recursive model-based indexing tried and beaten by model-driven glob/grep; stale indexes and permission complexity named as the failure modes.

Cursor — Improving agent with semantic search · Securely indexing large codebases
cursor.com/blog · November 6, 2025 · January 27, 2026 · vendor-measured

The hybrid's case: +12.5% average QA accuracy (6.5–23.5% by model, internal Context Bench), a custom embedder trained on agent traces — and the Merkle-tree sync and content-proof design that a serious index commitment requires.

Sourcegraph — How Cody understands your codebase
sourcegraph.com/blog · February 15, 2024

The documented retreat: "we're leaving them behind (for now)" — third-party processing, freshness ops, and >100k-repo scale as the three reasons. (Cody Free/Pro later sunset July 2025; Enterprise continues — separate event.)

Cognition — DeepWiki & Devin's docs · Codeium — Riptide (migrated)
docs.devin.ai · cognition.com/blog · devin.ai/blog/codeium-dream-bigger (~Aug 2024) · vendor

The counterexamples: Devin auto-indexes repos into wikis and code graphs; Riptide runs an LLM as the retrieval reranker, claiming "3x the recall of state-of-the-art embedding systems."

Augment Code — quantized vector search for 100M+ line codebases
augmentcode.com/blog · June 11, 2025 (updated June 18, 2026) · vendor

The strongest embeddings-at-scale case: 20+ bytes and 20+ ns per LOC as the cost model, 8× memory reduction, sub-200ms search — for millisecond features on giant monorepos.

Jimenez et al. — SWE-bench · Xia et al. — Agentless
arXiv:2310.06770 (ICLR 2024) · arXiv:2407.01489 (2024)

The ceiling experiment (BM25 1.96% vs oracle 4.80%, Claude 2 — retrieval caps single-shot, and even the oracle stayed single-digit) and the hierarchical LLM-driven localization that needed no embeddings: 32.00% Lite at $0.70/issue.

Wang et al. — CodeRAG-Bench · CoIR benchmark
arXiv:2406.14497 (NAACL 2025 Findings) · arXiv:2407.02883

The evidence on retrieval quality for code: gains require high-quality contexts, retrievers struggle at low lexical overlap — and dense code-trained embedders frequently surpass BM25, the correction to grep folklore.

Vogel et al. — Codebase-Memory (tree-sitter KGs via MCP)
arXiv:2603.27277 · March 2026 · preprint — weight accordingly

The one measured lane-A-vs-lane-D head-to-head: 83% vs 92% answer quality at ten times fewer tokens and 2.1× fewer tool calls, across 31 repositories; better on graph-native queries, worse on full-source ones.

Edge et al. — GraphRAG · CodexGraph · RepoGraph
arXiv:2404.16130 · arXiv:2408.03910 · arXiv:2410.14684 · all self-evaluated

The KG shelf, honestly labeled: GraphRAG is query-focused summarization over documents (LLM-judged wins), not code; the code-graph papers are promising and self-reported; no production backbone mid-2026.

Morris et al. — Text Embeddings Reveal (Almost) As Much As Text
arXiv:2310.06816 · EMNLP 2023 · + arXiv:2406.10280 (ACL 2024), arXiv:2502.11308

The security tax: exact recovery of 92% of 32-token inputs from embeddings, with follow-on attacks needing no query access — the vector store is recoverable source (M7).

GitHub — The technology behind GitHub's new code search (Blackbird)
github.blog · February 6, 2023 · Timothy Clem

Corpus-scale ground truth: 15.5B documents, 115 TB, 45M repos in a 25 TB sparse-ngram lexical index — because brute grep would cost 2,048 cores for 96 seconds per query.

Liu — Why grep beat embeddings (Flaherty) · Turnbull — Agentic search is having a grep moment · Guo — Agent retrieval is a cost-curve problem
jxnl.co · September 11, 2025 · softwaredoug.com · April 6, 2026 · harrisonsec.com · May 25, 2026 · practitioner

The three practitioner pieces this chapter leans on: grep wasn't the SWE-bench bottleneck (and why), the two-loop harness as the real enabler, and the build/maintain/per-query cost formula with its crossover regimes.

Zhang — Why I'm against Claude Code's grep-only retrieval
milvus.io/blog · August 25, 2025 · vector-DB vendor — the opposing voice

The named rebuttal: "Token usage dropped by over 40%, without any loss in recall" via their semantic-search plugin. Quoted as the counter-position from the vendor selling it, not as independent evidence.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane B

The retrieval section this chapter expands; the four-lane framing and the escalation ladder are this workbook's synthesis, flagged as such.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I6-retrieval-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are I5 (the map that makes search cheap), I1/I3 (indexes as derived artifacts that drift), I4 (the same question over memory), E2 (the context bill both lanes pay), E7 (vendor numbers vs the twenty-query bench), E9 (retrieval tools as tunable harness surface), and M7 (the vector store as recoverable source). All product facts point-in-time mid-2026 — this is the fastest-moving chapter in M4; re-verify the vendor roster before any client meeting. M4 continues with I7: linking product and engineering artifacts.

I7 · DEEP DIVE

Linking Product ↔ Engineering: IDs, Maps & the MCP Bridge

0Why it matters

Every engagement has two databases that both claim to describe the same work. The product plane — Jira, Linear, Notion — holds intent: epics, stories, acceptance criteria, the priority call that made this ticket matter. The engineering plane — git and the forge — holds change: branches, commits, PRs, tests, releases. Neither owns the other, and nothing keeps them telling the same story except the discipline of linking. How weak that discipline is by default has been measured for seventeen years: Bird et al.'s classic bias study (ESEC/FSE 2009) counted how many fixed bugs could be connected to the commit that fixed them and got 49.6% in Apache, 41.5% in Eclipse — and 8.1% in OpenOffice. Worse than the averages was the direction of the bias: in Apache, 63% of minor-severity bugs were linked but only 15% of blockers — the more severe the work, the less traceable it was, a trend that held across the projects with manually verified data. Agents turn this from a research-dataset nuisance into an operational problem, because the link now carries live traffic in both directions: downward, the ticket has become the agent's prompt — Copilot, Devin and Cursor all accept work by ticket assignment and read the issue text as the task spec (§2); upward, the agent's branches, commits and PRs are the only progress signal the product side ever sees, and an unlinked agent commit is unattributable archaeology within a week (§6). This chapter is the plumbing manual for that link: one key stamped everywhere and enforced deterministically (§1), a system map so agents and humans can traverse from product concept to code location (§3), one master per artifact so the two planes stop forking the truth (§4), MCP as the runtime bridge — priced and threat-modeled, because the link itself is now an attack surface (§5), and provenance so the upward direction survives audit (§6).

What this chapter gives you
  • the unified ID scheme — platform mechanics for Jira/Linear/GitHub/GitLab and an enforcement ladder from convention to server-side rule (§1)
  • the ticket-as-prompt pattern: what agent↔tracker integrations actually do, and the evidence that ticket quality caps agent output (§2)
  • the system map: C4 + arc42 + ARCHITECTURE.md, with the derived-diagram rule (§3)
  • one master per artifact and "reference, don't copy" — our synthesis, flagged (§4)
  • the MCP bridge: the roster, the token bill, the lethal trifecta and three named incidents (§5)
  • provenance and the outward layer: trailers, the 30× census, Agent Trace, llms.txt honestly labeled (§6)
  • a week-one linking audit for a brownfield client (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • H3 — the in-repo half: requirement IDs, spec registry, suspect gate; this chapter extends the chain across the tool boundary
  • H1/H2 — what a good ticket contains; §2 is the evidence for why that matters
  • I1 — masters and derived artifacts; the map and the status roll-ups are derived
  • I3 — copies drift; "reference, don't copy" is the anti-drift rule at tool scale
  • I5/I6 — the map layer and retrieval; ARCHITECTURE.md serves both
  • E5 — advisory vs deterministic: the enforcement ladder is E5 applied to links
  • M7 — ticket text is untrusted input; §5's incidents are the proof

1The two planes and the one key

The cheapest piece of information architecture in this whole module is a string: the issue key. PROJ-123 in the branch name, the commit message, the PR description and the test name is a foreign key joining two databases that share no schema — and every tracker and forge on the market already knows how to dereference it. H3 built the in-repo half of this chain (the five-move ID scheme: registry, contract, stamping, gates, bootstrap); this section extends it across the tool boundary, where the mechanics differ per platform and the defaults are weaker than most teams assume.

What the platforms give you for free — and where they quietly don't

PlatformWhat links automaticallyThe catch
Jirasmart commits: PROJ-123 #comment fixed indent, #time 2h, #close act on the ticket from the commit message; the development panel shows branches/commits/PRs once the forge integration is linkedthe issue key must be in the commit message with the command — a key in the branch name alone triggers nothing; smart commits must be enabled on the integration
Lineara branch name containing the issue ID auto-links branch and PR to the issue ("Copy git branch name" exists precisely for this); magic words — Fixes ENG-123, Closes, Refs — work in PR titles and descriptions and move ticket statemagic words do not work in PR comments; closing vs non-closing word lists differ (ref/part of link without closing)
GitHubclosing keywords (fixes #123 and variants) auto-close issues from merged PRs; custom autolink references turn PROJ-\d+ into tracker links repo-wideauto-close is same-repo only (and the PR must target the default branch) — owner/repo#123 references cross-repo but closes nothing; custom autolinks need a paid plan (Pro/Team/Enterprise, not Free)
GitLabclosing patterns in commits/MRs; push rules can require a commit-message regex — "Messages must match the expression" — rejecting unlinked commits at the serverpush rules are Premium/Ultimate tier; the regex is only as good as the key format it demands

Two asymmetries in that table decide real rollouts. First, Linear links from the branch name; Jira does not. A team that standardizes type/PROJ-123-slug branch naming gets Linear's bidirectional links for free, but on Jira still needs the key repeated in the commit message — which is exactly what a prepare-commit-msg hook is for. Second, GitHub's auto-close stops at the repo boundary — in a multi-repo estate the "Closes" convention silently degrades to a plain mention, and ticket state stops advancing; teams discover this months later as a pile of "done but open" issues.

The enforcement ladder

E5's advisory-vs-deterministic split applies verbatim. A convention documented in CLAUDE.md ("branch req/012-*, trailer Refs: PROJ-123") is advisory — agents follow it more reliably than humans do, which is precisely why H3 calls stamping nearly free at agent speed, but nothing catches the exceptions. Client-side hooks are the next rung: prepare-commit-msg runs before the editor opens and can auto-fill the key parsed from the branch name (git's docs: it may "edit the message file in place"); commit-msg can reject — "exiting with a non-zero status causes the command to abort." Both are per-developer opt-in, not distributed with the repo, and commit-msg is one --no-verify away from bypassed — so hooks are convenience, not governance. The deterministic rungs live server-side and in CI: GitHub repository rulesets carry metadata restrictions that enforce commit-message patterns at push time; GitLab's push rules do the same on Premium; and a forge-agnostic CI gate (commitlint for Conventional-Commits shapes, a Danger rule or a plain grep for "PR body carries a valid key") is the safety net that works everywhere — the commitlint ecosystem's own framing is that the CI check exists to hold "even when local enforcement fails." The pattern to sell the client is the layering, not any single rung: convention for intent, hook for ergonomics, server rule + CI grep for the guarantee.

Measure it like E7 measures everything: % of commits (or PRs) carrying a valid key, baselined in week one, watched as a curve. There is industry precedent for the metric — LinearB surfaces unlinked branches as shadow work, and its published figure is blunt: "In 75% of teams, 31% of branches are unlinked" — so the client's likely starting point is far from 100%, and the severity inversion from Bird's study says the gap will be worst on the urgent work. The metric is also the honest test of the whole section: if the ladder works, the curve moves without anyone being nagged.

Keep the key boring

Everything in this section assumes the key is short, stable and greppable: PROJ-123, not a URL, not a title. Two corollaries clients trip on. Renaming the Jira project key breaks every historical link — treat the key namespace as append-only (I2's immutability instinct). And the key must survive copy-paste into plain text, because half its life is spent in commit messages, changelogs and agent prompts where markup dies; this is also why autolink references (turning bare keys into tracker links) beat pasting full URLs into code comments.

2The ticket is the prompt

The downward direction of the link stopped being metadata in 2025–26: for a growing class of work, the ticket is the input the agent executes. All three flagship integrations follow the same shape — everything in this paragraph is vendor documentation about the vendor's own product, point-in-time mid-2026. GitHub Copilot coding agent ↔ Jira (public preview March 5, 2026; GA June 25, 2026): assign a Jira issue to Copilot from the assignee dropdown and the agent reads the title, description, labels and comments, works in a GitHub Actions sandbox, opens a draft PR, and streams status back into the Jira ticket; the GA release added Confluence context via MCP. Devin ↔ Linear: assign the ticket to Devin and it reads the description, acceptance criteria and linked issues; playbook labels (!plan, !implement, !triage, !review) select behaviour, and ticket status auto-advances In Progress → In Review → Done through Linear's agent-session interface. Cursor ↔ Linear (August 2025): @-mention or assign @Cursor and a cloud agent pulls the issue description, comments and linked references, produces a branch and draft PR, and posts the link back to the issue. The pattern worth naming — this workbook's phrasing, not any vendor's — is that the product plane has become the agent's input queue and its output log: intent enters as issue text, progress exits as links and state transitions, and the quality of both rides on §1's key discipline.

Which makes ticket quality an engineering input with a measurable failure mode. The sharpest independent evidence comes from the SWE-bench audit line of work: Wang, Pradel and Liu (arXiv:2503.15223, ICSE 2026) re-examined "solved" issues on SWE-bench Verified and found that 29.6% of plausible, test-passing patches behaviourally diverge from the developer's actual fix — and when they traced the suspicious cases back, 66.2% of them were uncertain precisely because the issue statement under-specified the requirement. The agent did not fail to read the ticket; the ticket did not contain the answer, so the agent guessed, and a guess that passes the visible tests is worse than a failure because it merges. A human developer routes around a vague ticket with hallway context and a question in standup; H4 measured how rarely agents ask (HumanEvalComm: over 60% answer with code instead of a clarifying question). The practitioner discourse has converged on the same point in plainer language — the ticket-as-spec essays of the SDD wave all orbit the observation that vague tickets survived only because humans could infer intent from ambient context, and agents reading the same tickets inherit the vagueness without the context. The fix is not "write novels in Jira": it is H1's contract anatomy and H2's machine-checkable acceptance criteria applied at the ticket boundary, plus §1's links so the agent can traverse to the PRD, the spec and the ADRs when the ticket legitimately compresses (§4's excerpt-with-link rule).

Assignment is an autonomy decision

Assigning a ticket to an agent is not a workflow convenience — it is granting E6 autonomy with the product tool as the control surface. Two governance defaults follow. Restrict who can assign or @-mention the agent (and, where supported, whose issues it will act on — §5's GitLost incident turned exactly this knob's absence into a data leak). And treat the agent's ticket-side output — status transitions, comments, closing actions — as write operations under I2's permission matrix: an agent that can close tickets can mark its own guesses as Done.

3The system map

The key joins work items to changes. It cannot answer the prior question every traverse starts with: where in this system does the product concept live? That is the map's job, and the two standards worth teaching a client have conveniently opposite shapes. C4 (Simon Brown) is four zoom levels — system context → container → component → code — and its official framing is exactly the traverse metaphor: "a way to create 'maps of your code', at various levels of detail, in the same way you would use something like Google Maps to zoom in and out of an area you are interested in." arc42 (Gernot Starke & Peter Hruschka, CC BY-SA) is a twelve-section documentation template — context, constraints, solution strategy, building-block view, runtime view, deployment, cross-cutting concepts, decisions, quality, risks, glossary — that answers "where does each kind of architectural fact go"; in practice C4 diagrams slot into its building-block view (a pairing that is practitioner convention, not something either site mandates). They compose rather than compete: C4 is the picture language, arc42 the filing cabinet.

Three rules make the map agent-ready rather than shelf-ware. First, the entry point is a file, not a wiki page: ARCHITECTURE.md at the repo root, in matklad's original argument, because "the biggest difference between an occasional contributor and a core developer lies in the knowledge about the physical architecture of the project" — I5 already established this file as the map layer of progressive disclosure, and it is the first thing an agent's context assembly should surface. Second, the code level is derived (I1): C4's own guidance calls Level 4 "very much an optional level of detail" and recommends against hand-drawing it "because most IDEs can generate this level of detail on demand" — hand-drawn code diagrams are I3 drift with arrows. Generate what is derivable (docs-as-code toolchains: Structurizr DSL, PlantUML, docToolchain for arc42 — tool status point-in-time and currently shifting; Structurizr's cloud service is winding down toward self-hosted), hand-write only the two or three levels where human judgment decides what matters. Third, decisions live next to code: ADRs in source control — the ThoughtWorks Radar's long-standing recommendation ("a record that remains in sync with the code itself"), and I2's immutability rules — so the map explains not just what the containers are but why they are shaped that way, and the agent quoting an ADR quotes the current status line.

The map is for the traverse, not the wall

The test of a system map in an agentic SDLC is operational: given ticket text, can the agent (or the new hire) get from the product noun to the owning container and the entry-point files in two hops? An ARCHITECTURE.md that names the containers, their responsibilities and their top-level directories passes; a beautiful diagram exported to a wiki the agent cannot fetch does not. This is I6's lesson wearing map clothes — the map makes search cheap; it does not replace it.

4One master per artifact

Once traffic flows both ways across the link, the failure mode changes name: it stops being "missing link" and becomes "two truths." The PRD lives in Notion and a stale copy lives in docs/; the acceptance criteria live in Jira and a diverged paraphrase lives in the spec; the architecture lives in Confluence and a five-container sketch from 2023 lives in the README. Every copy is an I3 drift pair waiting to disagree, and an agent — which reads faster and wider than any human — is more likely to find the stale copy, not less. The rule this workbook recommends (our Report-3 synthesis, flagged as such) is one master per artifact, everything else a reference: each artifact class gets exactly one living home, chosen by who edits it, and every other appearance is a link — or, where the consumer genuinely needs inline text (a task spec an agent will execute offline), a summarized excerpt with a link to master, dated, so staleness is at least visible.

ArtifactMaster livesWhy thereEverything else is
PRD / product intentproduct tool (Notion/Confluence/Jira)product edits it; discussion threads attacha link from the spec; excerpt in the contract if needed (H1 component E)
Feature spec / ACrepo — specs/ (H3 registry)versioned with the code it constrains; PR-revieweda link from the ticket; the ticket summarizes
ADRrepo — docs/adr/immutable + superseded chain (I2)links from ARCHITECTURE.md and code seams
System maprepo — ARCHITECTURE.md (+ generated diagrams)agents and contributors start here (I5)exports; never a hand-maintained wiki twin
Status / progressthe tracker — derived from linksstate transitions ride §1 mechanicsroll-ups; never hand-authored status docs
Agent session tracestrace store (forge/vendor)append-only, queryable (§6)a commit-message link to the session

The row that surprises clients is status: in a linked estate, status is not written, it is computed — the branch exists, the PR is open, the checks are green, the deploy shipped; the tracker's job is to render that, which is exactly what the §1 integrations do with state transitions. Any hand-authored "status page" is a derived artifact pretending to be durable (I1) and will be wrong by Friday. The counter-habit to coach is the copy-paste reflex: the PM who pastes the full PRD into the epic description, the engineer who pastes the AC table into the PR — each paste feels helpful and creates a fork. "Reference, don't copy" is cheap to say and hard to live; the excerpt-with-link compromise (short, dated, linked) is what makes it liveable, and it is precisely the pattern E2 wants for context anyway: the agent's context window should hold the excerpt and fetch the master on demand, not carry three stale copies of the same intent.

5The MCP bridge

Links let humans traverse; MCP lets the agent traverse. All three major trackers now ship official hosted MCP servers — Atlassian's Rovo MCP Server, Linear's at mcp.linear.app (May 2025), Notion's hosted server — all OAuth-based, and Atlassian's identity model is the one to hold up in the I2 conversation: the agent inherits the invoking user's permissions ("all actions respect users' existing access controls and permissions"), so the ticket the agent can read is the ticket you can read, and admin-side toggles gate read/write/search separately. Roster and auth details are point-in-time mid-2026; re-verify before a client meeting. Two costs arrive with the bridge, and both are now measurable rather than folklore.

The token bill. Every connected server pre-loads tool definitions, and every tool result arrives as raw JSON in the context window; tracker payloads are notoriously padded (a Jira issue is kilobytes of custom fields around a paragraph of intent). Anthropic's own engineering numbers — vendor-measured, flagged as such — put the ceiling case vividly: a multi-server agent burning 150,000 tokens on tool definitions and intermediate results dropped to 2,000 when tool calls moved into code execution, "a time and cost saving of 98.7%," and the companion Tool Search work showed that fewer tool definitions in context improved accuracy while cutting tokens 85%. The independent corroboration is directional rather than threshold-shaped: on MCP-Universe, adding unrelated servers as distractors measurably degraded success rates. The practical rules for the product↔engineering bridge are modest: connect the servers the task actually needs, prefer summarized excerpts over full-payload dumps in anything durable (§4), and record project defaults (keys, board IDs, base URLs) in AGENTS.md so the agent does not re-discover them every session — a practitioner economy, not a benchmark result.

The injection surface. Ticket text is the textbook instance of Simon Willison's lethal trifecta (June 2025): an agent with "access to your private data," "exposure to untrusted content," and "the ability to externally communicate" can be steered by anyone who can write text it will read — and a public issue tracker is an open submission box for exactly that text. This is not hypothetical; the incident list is now three deep and all three are product↔engineering-link-shaped. Invariant Labs, May 2025 ("toxic agent flows"): a malicious issue in a public GitHub repo steers an agent connected to the GitHub MCP server into pulling private-repo data and publishing it in a PR — an architectural flaw of the tool combination, not a bug in any one server. General Analysis on Supabase's MCP, July 2025: injected support-ticket text made a Cursor agent with service_role credentials read the integration_tokens table and paste the secrets back into the ticket thread; Supabase's response — read-only mode, wrapping untrusted content, MCP off by default — is a defense catalogue in miniature. Noma Security's "GitLost", July 2026: GitHub's Agentic Workflows, configured to act on assigned issues, was steered by a public issue — filed by an unauthenticated attacker — into leaking a private repo's README into a public comment, with the built-in guardrails bypassed by prefixing the injected instructions with the single word "additionally," which the model parsed as a follow-on task rather than a new directive. Three incidents, one lesson: the link is the attack surface — the same channel that makes the ticket a prompt makes the ticket an exploit.

Defense defaults for the bridge

Write tools on "always ask"; read-only server modes until the workflow has earned writes (I2's matrix applied to MCP). Restrict which principals' tickets the agent acts on — GitLost's core mitigation — and scope tokens to single repos. Treat fetched ticket text as data, not instructions: wrap or quarantine it where the harness supports it. And audit the combination, not the servers: Invariant's toxic-flow analysis (and its mcp-scan tooling) exists precisely because each server can be individually safe while the trifecta emerges from their union (M7). None of this is exotic — it is the same E5 move as always: the defense that holds is the one enforced outside the context window.

6Provenance and the outward layer

The upward direction of the link has a new passenger: who — or what — did the work. The measurement that should scare any audit-minded client comes from the multi-method agent census (Khosravani & Mockus, arXiv:2606.24429, June 2026): across ~180 million repositories, multi-method detection identified 850,157 Claude Code commits in one snapshot, of which bot-account lookup — the signal most adoption studies rely on — recovered only 28,154, a 3.3% recall and a 30× gap; H3 drew the conclusion and it stands: if provenance is not stamped at write time, it cannot be reconstructed later, even by researchers trying hard. The stamping mechanics are pleasantly boring — git already has trailers (RFC-822-style lines that git interpret-trailers parses natively, the same footer position Conventional Commits formalizes) — but the convention is genuinely contested: Co-authored-by: makes the agent visible in forge UIs but implies co-authorship, which critics argue muddies CLA chains and personhood; the Assisted-by: camp keeps the human as sole author with the tool as annotation. The contest produced a real reversal: VS Code briefly flipped a default that added "Co-authored-by: Copilot" to commits (April 2026), drew immediate backlash — the trailer appeared even for users with Copilot disabled — and reverted to opt-in within weeks. Pick one convention per H3, write it into CLAUDE.md, enforce with a CI grep, and do not let the default of whatever tool arrives next pick it for you.

Above trailers, a trace layer is consolidating — point-in-time mid-2026, moving fast. Agent Trace (agent-trace.dev, v0.1.0 RFC, January 2026) is a Cursor-led, vendor-neutral spec — with Amp, Cognition, Cline and Cloudflare among the named collaborators — for JSON trace records linking code changes to the conversation and line ranges that produced them, storage-agnostic (files, git notes, a database), with explicit non-goals: it does not adjudicate ownership or code quality. GitHub shipped the same idea productized: every Copilot coding-agent commit carries a permanent link back to its full session logs (March 2026), and agent sessions can stream prompts, responses and tool calls to SIEM endpoints including Microsoft Purview (public preview, July 2026) — the session trace becoming a first-class, exportable SDLC artifact, which is exactly the tiered-artifact story I1 and H3 told: a durable decision-provenance spine with disposable payloads. For the EU-facing client, traj-compliance's clock applies — the AI Act's logging and record-keeping articles begin to bite in August 2026 — so "which sessions produced this release" is about to become a question with a legal deadline attached.

Last, the outward-facing corner of the link layer, kept honest: llms.txt (Jeremy Howard, September 2024) proposed a curated markdown index so LLMs can navigate a site's documentation. Mid-2026 status: publication is widespread because docs platforms (Mintlify, Fern, GitBook, Vercel) emit it by default — Anthropic's and Cursor's own docs carry one — but consumption has not materialized; Google's John Mueller: "FWIW no AI system currently uses llms.txt," and I1's Ahrefs study found 97% of published files received zero requests of any kind. The honest framing for a client: an llms.txt for your public developer docs is cheap and harmless, might help agent-fetched documentation at the margin (I5), and is not a discoverability strategy; inside the repo, AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md and ARCHITECTURE.md do the real indexing work.

7Week one at the client

The linking audit runs alongside I5's disclosure audit and I6's retrieval audit — same week, same interviews, and the cheapest of the three to act on.

Do — in this order
  • 1 · Baseline the link rate. One afternoon of git + tracker API: % of the last 500 commits/PRs carrying a valid issue key. Expect real gaps (LinearB: in 75% of teams, 31% of branches are unlinked); expect them to be worst on urgent work (Bird's severity inversion). This number is the E7 baseline the whole chapter improves.
  • 2 · Pick the key and ladder it. Branch pattern + trailer convention in CLAUDE.md; prepare-commit-msg auto-fill; then the deterministic rung — GitLab push rule, GitHub ruleset metadata restriction, or a CI grep — advisory first, enforced once the team stops noticing it.
  • 3 · Turn on what is free. Linear branch-name autolinks / Jira smart commits + development panel / GitHub custom autolinks for tracker keys. Ten minutes each; most estates have half of it off.
  • 4 · Establish masters. The §4 table as a one-hour workshop: for each artifact class, name the master, delete or link-ify the known twins (I3's two-truths pairs).
  • 5 · Bridge read-only. Connect the tracker MCP with reads only, writes "always ask," author restrictions on; record project defaults in AGENTS.md.
Don't
  • let anyone paste a PRD into the repo (or a spec into a ticket) — link it; excerpt-with-link where inline text is genuinely needed (§4)
  • trust "Closes PROJ-123" across GitHub repos — auto-close is same-repo only; multi-repo estates need the CI gate (§1)
  • let the agent act on tickets filed by arbitrary principals — GitLost is three weeks old at time of writing (§5)
  • cite the vendor integration claims (Copilot/Devin/Cursor §2) as measured outcomes — they are product documentation (E7)
  • hand-draw C4 Level 4 or hand-author status pages — both are derived artifacts (I1, §3–§4)
  • ship llms.txt as a "discoverability" line item — publish it if the docs platform emits it anyway, and expect nothing (§6)

8Where I7 sits in the framework

TopicThe connection
H1/H2The ticket the agent executes is a handoff contract in tracker clothes; component E (context links) is §1's key chain, and EARS-shaped AC are the §2 fix for under-specified issues.
H3The in-repo five-move ID scheme is the other half of this chapter; the % -linked metric extends H3's traceability coverage curve across the tool boundary.
H4Agents guess instead of asking (HumanEvalComm) — which is why §2's ticket quality matters and why the return channel (H4) must flow through the same links.
I1Masters vs copies is the taxonomy applied across tools; status pages and code-level diagrams are derived artifacts.
I3Every copy across the boundary is a drift pair; "reference, don't copy" is the freshness policy at estate scale.
I5/I6ARCHITECTURE.md is simultaneously the map layer (I5), a retrieval accelerant (I6) and §3's traverse entry point.
E2Raw MCP payloads are context spend; excerpts-with-links are the E2-shaped diet for cross-tool content.
E5The enforcement ladder is advisory-vs-deterministic applied to links: convention → hook → server rule + CI gate.
E6Ticket assignment is autonomy granting; the tracker becomes a control surface and needs the same gates.
E7% commits linked is the chapter's measurable; vendor integration claims stay in the vendor lane.
P2/P3The PRD and decomposition artifacts live on the product plane; decomposition quality (P3) is what makes tickets executable prompts.
M7Ticket text is untrusted input; the trifecta incidents are the security module's case studies for this chapter.

9Takeaways for our engagement

Three sentences

The product and engineering planes are two databases joined by one boring foreign key — the issue key in branches, commits, PRs and tests — and the default link rate is bad in the direction that hurts most (Bird 2009: the more severe the work, the less likely it is linked), so the key must be laddered from convention to hook to server-side rule and measured as % commits linked (E7). Agents raise the stakes in both directions: downward the ticket is now the prompt — and under-specified tickets measurably produce plausible-but-wrong patches — while upward, unstamped agent work is unrecoverable (the 30× census), so provenance trailers and session-trace links are stamped at write time or never. The bridge that makes this live — MCP into Jira/Linear/Notion — is paid in tokens and threatened through the very tickets it reads (three named incidents in fourteen months), so it ships read-only first, writes on always-ask, with one master per artifact and "reference, don't copy" keeping the two planes from forking the truth (our synthesis, flagged).

10Further reading

Bird, Bachmann, Aune, Duffy, Bernstein, Filkov, Devanbu — Fair and Balanced? Bias in Bug-Fix Datasets
ESEC/FSE 2009 · the classic missing-links measurement

Link rates from 8% (OpenOffice) to ~55% (NetBeans) — and the severity inversion: in Apache, 63% of minor bugs linked vs 15% of blockers. The empirical floor under this whole chapter.

Wang, Pradel, Liu — Are "Solved Issues" in SWE-bench Really Solved Correctly?
arXiv:2503.15223 · ICSE 2026 · independent

29.6% of plausible patches diverge from the real fix; 66.2% of suspicious cases trace to under-specified issue statements — the quantified case that ticket quality caps agent output.

Simon Brown — the C4 model · Starke & Hruschka — arc42
c4model.com (CC BY 4.0) · arc42.org (CC BY-SA 4.0)

The picture language and the filing cabinet: "maps of your code… Google Maps" zoom levels, and the twelve-section template; Level 4 explicitly optional and generatable — the derived-diagram rule from the source.

matklad — ARCHITECTURE.md
matklad.github.io · February 6, 2021

The entry-point argument: occasional contributors vs core developers differ in knowledge of the physical architecture — one file closes most of the gap.

Atlassian · Linear · GitHub · GitLab — the link mechanics, from the docs
smart commits · magic words & branch autolinks · closing keywords & autolink references · push rules · point-in-time mid-2026

The §1 table's primary sources — including the two asymmetries that decide rollouts: Linear links from branch names, Jira needs the key in the commit message; GitHub auto-close is same-repo only.
