GitHub Changelog — Copilot coding agent: Jira (preview → GA) · session-log links · SIEM/Purview streaming
github.blog/changelog · Mar 5 & Jun 25, 2026 · Mar 20, 2026 · Jul 2, 2026 · vendor

The productized two-way link: assign a Jira ticket to the agent; every agent commit links to its session log; sessions stream to Purview. The ticket as input queue, the trace as exportable artifact.

Cognition — Devin ↔ Linear · Cursor — Linear integration
docs.devin.ai/integrations/linear · linear.app/changelog · August 21, 2025 · vendor

The other two ticket-as-prompt integrations: playbook labels and agent-session status flow; @Cursor assignment to background agents.

Simon Willison — The lethal trifecta for AI agents
simonwillison.net · June 16, 2025

Private data + untrusted content + external communication: "an attacker can easily trick it into accessing your private data and sending it to that attacker." The threat model §5's incidents instantiate.

Invariant Labs — GitHub MCP toxic agent flows (+ toxic-flow analysis, mcp-scan) · General Analysis — Supabase MCP leak · Noma Security — GitLost
invariantlabs.ai · May 26, 2025 · generalanalysis.com · July 2025 · via The Hacker News · July 7, 2026

Three incidents, one topology: the ticket/issue as injection channel, the agent's tools as reach, the public comment/PR as exfiltration. GitLost's guardrail bypass was the word "additionally."

Anthropic — Code execution with MCP · Advanced tool use
anthropic.com/engineering · November 4 & 24, 2025 · vendor-measured

The token bill made vivid: 150,000 → 2,000 tokens ("a time and cost saving of 98.7%"); Tool Search cutting definitions 85% with accuracy up. Why the bridge needs an economy.

Khosravani & Mockus — the multi-method agent census
arXiv:2606.24429 · June 2026

850,157 Claude Code commits found by multi-method detection; bot-account lookup recovers 3.3% — the 30× gap. Provenance is stamped at write time or lost.

Agent Trace — a vendor-neutral trace-record spec
agent-trace.dev · v0.1.0 RFC · January 2026 · Cursor-led, with Amp/Cognition/Cline/Cloudflare

JSON records linking changes to the conversations and line ranges that produced them; storage-agnostic; explicitly not an ownership or quality adjudicator. The trailer's grown-up sibling.

Jeremy Howard — llms.txt · Ahrefs — the adoption study
llmstxt.org · September 2024 · ahrefs.com · May 2026

The proposal and the reality check: 97% of published files got zero requests of any kind; "FWIW no AI system currently uses llms.txt" (Mueller). Publish if it is free; expect nothing.

Report 3 — the information-architecture research notes (this workbook)
module M4 research notes · plane C

The linking plane this chapter expands; the master-per-artifact table and "reference, don't copy" are this workbook's synthesis, flagged as such.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (I7-linking-product-engineering-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are H3 (the in-repo ID scheme this chapter extends across the tool boundary), H1/H2 (what makes a ticket an executable contract), I1/I3 (masters, copies and drift), I5/I6 (the map and the traverse), E5 (the enforcement ladder), E6/E7 (assignment as autonomy; % linked as the metric) and M7 (ticket text as untrusted input). Platform mechanics, MCP rosters and the incident list are point-in-time mid-2026 — re-verify before any client meeting. With I7, module M4's deep-dive set is complete; the programme continues with M5: brownfield bootstrap.

B1 · DEEP DIVE

The Bootstrap Paradox & Safe Ordering

0Why it matters

Everything this programme has built so far assumes a floor that most clients do not have. The engineering harness (M1) assumes tests and CI the agent can run; the handoff contract (M3) assumes acceptance criteria something can verify; the information architecture (M4) assumes docs worth trusting. A brownfield client — which is to say, nearly every client — walks in below that floor: code without tests, tribal knowledge instead of docs, CI that builds but verifies nothing. That produces the bootstrap paradox this chapter is about: the agent needs a verification loop to work safely, the legacy estate does not have one, and the cheapest tool available for building one is the agent itself. Three true statements that close into a loop — and a loop you can enter wrongly. Enter at "writes" and you get the configuration DORA's 2024 data associates with a 7.2% drop in delivery stability; enter at "read-only comprehension" and each step builds the precondition for the next. The difference between those two entries is not speed — the wrong order is not faster, it is just unverified. This chapter states the paradox precisely (§1), lays out the evidence that ordering matters (§2), walks the dependency chain that unwinds the loop (§3), makes the case for a read-only Phase 0 (§4), names the failure modes of wrong ordering (§5), and turns it into a client conversation (§6). It is the hinge of module M5: B2–B6 are the individual steps; B1 is why they come in that order.

What this chapter gives you
  • the paradox stated as three verifiable claims, with the evidence for each — including whether "the agent can build its own prerequisites" is actually true yet (§1)
  • the evidence file: DORA 2024/2025, the METR RCT, GitClear, Stack Overflow 2025 — sorted by evidential weight, with the 2024→2025 sign flip flagged (§2)
  • the dependency chain: read-only comprehension → characterization → minimal CI → AGENTS.md → scoped writes, and why each arrow is a precondition, not a preference (§3)
  • Phase 0 as the paradox-breaker: zero write risk, immediate comprehension value, trust with a defensive team (§4)
  • four wrong-order failure modes, each with its detection signal (§5)
  • the per-step exit criteria table — what must exist before you take the next step (§6)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • B2 — characterization & golden-master: the second chain step in full
  • B3 — mutation testing: the quality gate that keeps agent tests honest
  • B4 — agent archaeology: what Phase 0 actually produces
  • B5/B6 — where to start writing (heatmap) and the F0–F3 roadmap with STOP criteria
  • E4 — verification-first: the loop the agent needs; this chapter is E4 meeting a codebase that lacks it
  • E5/E6 — deterministic vs advisory, and autonomy levels: Phase 0 is L1 enforced structurally
  • I1–I3 — the artifacts the bootstrap creates (AGENTS.md, docs-as-hypothesis) and why they must be extracted, not decreed
  • E7 — measurement: the pilot metrics that tell you the ordering is working

1The paradox, stated precisely

Michael Feathers gave brownfield its operational definition in 2004: "To me, legacy code is simply code without tests." The same preface carries the reason the definition still bites: "Code without tests is bad code. It doesn't matter how well written it is… With tests, we can change the behavior of our code quickly and verifiably. Without them, we really don't know if our code is getting better or worse." Note what the definition is not about: age, language, style, or the retirement date of the original author. It is about the absence of a verification signal — and that absence is precisely what disables an agent.

The paradox is three claims, each independently true:

Claim 1 — the agent needs a verification loop — is E4's whole argument, so here only the brownfield twist. Simon Willison's much-quoted reassurance about hallucinated code — "The moment you run LLM generated code, any hallucinated methods will be instantly obvious: you'll get an error" (March 2025) — quietly presupposes that something runs the code. That is exactly the machinery legacy lacks. On an untested estate the "least dangerous form of LLM mistakes" loses the property that made it least dangerous: nothing runs, nothing fails, the mistake ships. Anthropic's own usage guidance states the human cost plainly: without a check the agent can run, "'looks done' is the only signal available, and you become the verification loop: every mistake waits for you to notice it" — tolerable for one file, not for a legacy estate (vendor guidance, but guidance against the vendor's short-term interest, which raises its credibility).

Claim 2 — legacy does not have one — needs no argument, but it has a corollary that does: every headline agent benchmark is run on codebases where claim 2 is false. SWE-bench draws its 2,294 problems from 12 popular open-source Python repositories, and its construction pipeline discards any candidate task that lacks fail-to-pass tests — roughly 90,000 pull requests filtered down to the instances where a verification signal already exists. The independent Epoch AI teardown of SWE-bench Verified (June 2025) adds the texture: around 90% of tasks are fixes an experienced engineer could complete in under an hour, Django alone comprises nearly half of all issues, and the five largest repositories account for over 80% of the benchmark — "bug fixes in familiar, open-source Python repositories," in their words. And when a benchmark finally made the task look like brownfield evolution — SWE-EVO (December 2025), multi-file changes averaging 21 files, validated against suites averaging 874 tests — the same class of agents that scores 72.80% on SWE-bench Verified dropped to 25%. The number a client has seen in a vendor deck was measured on the estate they do not have.

Claim 3 — the agent is the cheapest tool to build the prerequisites — is the one that has actually shifted, so date-stamp it. In August 2024, Birgitta Böckeler (Thoughtworks) tried exactly this and reported that generating tests for untested code "all depends on the quality and design of that code," concluding that in most cases the hope "will remain a pipe dream" — untestable design blocks the generator the same way it blocks a human. The strongest production counter-evidence is Meta's TestGen-LLM (FSE 2024): with a filter-based pipeline — every candidate test must build, pass reliably, and measurably raise coverage before a human ever sees it — 75% of generated tests built, 57% passed reliably, 25% raised coverage, and 73% of surviving recommendations were accepted into production. By late 2025, UnitTenX was generating tests for untested legacy C packages — to "resolve bugs and document unknown interfaces" — by pairing LLM agents with the ESBMC bounded model checker to surface the states worth pinning; on its case-study codebase (djbdns, 202 functions), line coverage went from 0% to 100%. Read the trajectory carefully: what improved is not the model's trustworthiness but the scaffolding of deterministic filters around it — build gates, coverage deltas, model checkers. Claim 3 is true precisely to the extent that its output is anchored in signals that do not come from the model. Unanchored, the loop closes on itself: the agent writes tests that encode today's bugs as tomorrow's spec — the bug-as-spec trap that B2 exists to manage and B3 exists to measure.

2What the evidence says about ordering

The strongest available evidence is not "AI fails on legacy code" — it is that AI amplifies whatever delivery discipline already exists, in both directions.

DORA 2024 put numbers on the downside: a 25% increase in AI adoption was associated with a 1.5% decrease in delivery throughput and a 7.2% reduction in delivery stability — while documentation quality (+7.5%) and code quality (+3.4%) improved. Quote DORA's own mechanism, not a paraphrase: "improving the development process does not automatically improve software delivery — at least not without proper adherence to the basics of successful software delivery, like small batch sizes and robust testing mechanisms." The report's hypothesis is that AI makes large changesets cheap to produce, and teams without small-batch and testing discipline ship them unverified. That is the bootstrap paradox measured at population scale: write-capacity grows faster than verification capacity.

DORA 2025 flipped one sign and kept the other — flag both, because citing the 2024 throughput number as current is now wrong. In 2025, higher AI adoption was associated with increased throughput, while the instability association persisted. The report's framing became the amplifier thesis: "AI's primary role is as an amplifier, magnifying an organization's existing strengths and weaknesses" — and, in the announcement's blunter wording, "AI doesn't fix a team; it amplifies what's already there." Its companion AI Capabilities Model names seven organizational capabilities that turn AI investment into performance; note how many are harness-shaped: strong version control practices, working in small batches, quality internal platforms, AI-accessible internal data. The 2025 prescription is this chapter's ordering, written as survey correlations.

The METR randomized controlled trial (July 2025) is the only causal data point in the file, and it was run in conditions that look like a brownfield engagement's best case: 16 experienced maintainers, 246 real tasks on mature repositories averaging 22k+ stars and 1M+ lines, an average of five years of prior experience with the specific codebase. With AI tools allowed, developers took 19% longer — while forecasting a 24% speedup beforehand and still believing in a 20% speedup afterwards. METR itself stresses the result does not generalize to all developers or codebases; what it does establish is the perception gap: self-reported speedup is not evidence, which is why every exit criterion in §6 is deterministic and why E7's measurement design exists. If experienced developers misread their own productivity by ~40 percentage points on code they know deeply, a pilot's success cannot be adjudicated by asking the pilot team how it felt.

GitClear's longitudinal code-quality data (vendor research — they sell code analytics — on 211M changed lines, 2020–2024) describes what unverified write-capacity looks like in the aggregate: copy-pasted code rising from 8.3% of changed lines in 2020 to 12.3% in 2024, refactored ("moved") code falling from ~24% to ~9.5%, and code churned within two weeks rising from 5.5% to 7.9%. Their February 2025 report's headline — duplicated code blocks up "eightfold" in 2024 — and the January 2026 follow-up's "4× growth in code clones" are different metrics over the same underlying trend; cite the shares, not the multipliers, and attribute the multipliers to their specific dated reports. Correlational and vendor-sourced — but directionally consistent with DORA's instability finding.

The trust numbers explain the sociology you will meet in the room. DORA 2024: 39% of respondents reported little to no trust in AI-generated code (30% in DORA 2025). Stack Overflow 2025: adoption rose from 76% to 84% of developers while trust in accuracy sits at 32.7% against 45.7% distrust, and the single most-cited frustration — 66% of developers — is "AI solutions that are almost right, but not quite." Usage up, trust down is not a contradiction; it is what working with an unverified generator feels like. A defensive senior engineer on a legacy team is not a change-management problem to be routed around — they are correctly pricing the missing verification loop. §4's answer is to start where their skepticism costs nothing.

How to read this evidence file

One RCT (METR — causal, narrow conditions), two correlational surveys (DORA 2024/2025 — population-scale, self-reported, "associated with" only), one vendor dataset (GitClear — observational, seller of the measuring instrument), one practitioner survey (Stack Overflow — sentiment, not behavior). None of them says "agents fail on brownfield." Together they say: write-capacity without verification discipline degrades stability, and nobody's self-report can be trusted to notice. That is an ordering argument, not an abstinence argument — and the 2024→2025 DORA sign flip on throughput is the reminder that all of it is point-in-time; re-verify before a client meeting.

3The dependency chain — why the ordering is not arbitrary

The safe ordering is not a preference ranking; it is a dependency chain, and it runs on one rule: never take a step whose verification depends on a later step. Walk it backwards and each arrow is forced. Write changes safely require tests that catch regressions. Meaningful tests in untested code are characterization tests — pinning actual behavior, because no spec of intended behavior survives (B2). Characterization tests are only a signal if something runs them on every change — a minimal CI: build, lint, test, green or red. Writing the tests, and the AGENTS.md that makes every subsequent task cheaper, requires comprehension of the estate — which the agent can do with zero write risk. So: read-only comprehension → characterization/golden-master → minimal CI → AGENTS.md → scoped writes.

Two steps deserve their footnotes. The AGENTS.md step sits fourth, not first, for a measured reason. The reflex at every client is to start by writing the context file — it feels like progress and requires no permissions. But the ETH Zurich study of context files found agent-written context files were associated with a 2% drop in success rate and 23% higher cost on their agent benchmark (−0.5%/+20% on SWE-bench Lite), while human-written ones showed a modest positive effect — "context files do not provide effective overviews" when generated as boilerplate. The file earns its keep only when it encodes real, verified conventions: extracted from the code after comprehension, pruned by a human who knows which conventions are load-bearing (I1's durable-artifact discipline, I3's freshness rules). An /init-style draft is a fine starting point — the official description is that it "analyzes your codebase to detect build systems, test frameworks, and code patterns" — but the human pruning pass is the step that makes it an asset instead of confidently wrong prose.

The vocabulary for why the chain works comes from Böckeler's harness-engineering writing (April–May 2026): a harness gives an agent guides — "feedforward controls… aim to steer it before it acts" — and sensors — "feedback controls… observe after the agent acts and help it self-correct." Map the chain onto that split and its logic is visible: characterization tests and minimal CI build the sensors; AGENTS.md builds the guides; Phase 0 builds the comprehension both depend on. The chain is simply: sensors before actuators — with the guides drafted from what the sensors confirmed.

Flagged as our synthesis

The five-step chain, its "never verify with a later step" rule, and the F0–F3 phase names are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis, not an industry standard. Every component is standard and named — Feathers characterization (2004), Falco approval testing, minimal CI, context-file extraction, staged autonomy (E6) — and the DORA capabilities model independently points the same direction, but you will not find "the bootstrap dependency chain" in any external source. Present it as our recommendation with the components' provenance, exactly like D1–D5.

4Phase 0: read-only breaks the paradox

The paradox dissolves the moment you notice that its first profitable step requires no writes at all. A read-only agent — repo access, no commit rights, MCP servers in read-only mode, the leftmost column of I2's write-permission matrix — can produce the entire comprehension layer: architecture maps, dependency graphs, convention inventories, onboarding walkthroughs, docs-as-hypothesis drafts (B4's archaeology in full). The risk ledger of this phase is asymmetric in a way nothing else in agent adoption is: the blast radius of a read-only mistake is a wrong sentence a human reads, not a wrong change a system runs. The one real hazard — plausible-but-wrong documentation that nobody fact-checks — is managed by B4's rule: every generated claim ships labeled as hypothesis with a confidence level, anchored where possible in deterministic facts (the AST, the build graph), and sampled by the people who know the system.

Phase 0 also attacks the largest cost center first. Xia et al.'s large-scale field study (IEEE TSE 2018) measured professionals spending 58% of their time on program comprehension — on legacy estates the folk estimates run higher. An agent that only answers questions is therefore not a consolation prize while you wait for write access; it is aimed at the majority share of engineering time. Böckeler's early onboarding experiments found exactly this shape: the "where is this in the code?" answers were "usually not 100% accurate" but "did always point me in a generally useful direction" — a useful property for a hypothesis generator, a disqualifying one for an unreviewed committer. Even the vendor guidance agrees on the entry point: Anthropic's best-practices page recommends onboarding to a new codebase by using the agent "for learning and exploration," asking it "the same sorts of questions you would ask another engineer," with its four-phase workflow putting a read-only explore step before any plan or implementation (vendor, point-in-time). And the task-type evidence points the same way: the MSR 2026 study of ~602 failed agentic pull requests (Ehsani et al., on the AIDev-pop dataset) found that "tasks related to documentation, CI, and build update achieve the highest merge success, whereas performance and bug-fix tasks perform the worst" — the low-risk, harness-building end of the estate is where agent work actually lands, which is exactly the end the chain starts on.

And Phase 0 is where trust is built with the team that has to live with everything that follows. §2's numbers say the defensive posture is the majority posture — 45.7% distrust, "almost right, but not quite" as the top frustration — and on a legacy system the skeptics hold the invariants in their heads. A read-only phase lets them grade the agent on questions they know the answers to, at zero risk, before being asked to trust it with changes. When the team's own seniors have corrected the architecture map and pruned the AGENTS.md draft, the artifacts carry their fingerprints — adoption stops being something done to the team.

The phase needs an exit as much as it needs an entrance. Phase 0 produces value forever, which is exactly how it becomes a place to hide from the harder steps — comfortable, riskless, and quietly signalling that the agent is a documentation toy. Time-box it. The exit criteria are concrete (§6): the map exists and survived expert sampling, the pilot-module candidates are identified (B5's heatmap), the AGENTS.md draft is pruned, and the first characterization target is chosen. A Phase 0 that cannot meet those criteria in a few weeks on a pilot scope is not being careful — it is stalled, and the stall has a cause worth naming (no champion, no access, no seams; B6's thresholds).

5Failure modes of wrong ordering

Four ways teams enter the loop at the wrong door, each with the signal that detects it.

WO1

Write-first pilot

skips the whole chain · detected by CFR and rework

The demo-driven entry: point the agent at a real backlog item in week one, on the theory that value must be shown fast. This is the configuration behind §2's population-scale numbers — write-capacity added to a system with no verification capacity. It often looks successful for weeks, because the failures are regressions discovered downstream, not errors at generation time; GitClear's duplication and churn curves are what the aggregate looks like when review is the only gate. Detection: change failure rate and rework climbing on agent-touched modules (E7, DORA's Rework Rate). Repair: pause writes, build the chain backwards from wherever it broke.

WO2

Tests gated on coverage, not quality

corrupts the chain's second step · detected by mutation score (B3)

The chain says "characterization tests," the team hears "run the test generator until coverage is green." Coverage is the wrong gate: MutGen's authors observed suites with 100% coverage and a 4% mutation score, and Haroon et al. found that of 23,977 LLM-generated tests that failed on mutated code, over 99% pass on the original program — tests that execute everything and verify almost nothing. Böckeler's sensors framing names the defect: "Coverage tells us that a line was executed, but not that its impact was verified" — acceptance-style tests "increase coverage, but are often not very assertion-heavy, giving us a false sense of security in test effectiveness — mutation testing helps us monitor that gap." Detection is B3's entire subject: mutation score (~70% on the pilot module) is the exit criterion, not line coverage. Repair: mutation feedback demonstrably works — MutGen lifted a stuck 53% to 89.5% on HumanEval-Java by feeding surviving mutants back to the generator.

WO3

AGENTS.md by decree

inverts the chain's fourth step · detected by the file not being read, or being wrong

Writing the context file first, from aspiration ("we use clean architecture") rather than extraction, or letting the agent draft it before anyone comprehends the estate. §3's ETH numbers are the measured cost of the boilerplate version: −2% success, +23% cost against no file at all. On a legacy estate this failure has a sharper edge: the aspirational file describes the system its authors wish they had, and the agent obeys the file rather than the code — producing changes idiomatic to a codebase that does not exist. Detection: I3's drift checks, plus a simple audit — sample the file's claims against the code the way you would sample any docs-as-hypothesis. Repair: regenerate descriptively after comprehension, prune by a human, date-stamp what is aspiration vs description.

WO4

Phase 0 forever

refuses the chain's exits · detected by the calendar

The mirror image of WO1, common in risk-averse organizations: the read-only phase produces maps, docs and goodwill indefinitely, and every proposal to take the next step is met with "after the next audit." The tell is that Phase 0's outputs stop changing anything — the third architecture map adds nothing the first two did not. This failure mode is quieter than the others because nothing breaks; what is lost is the entire return on the harness investment, plus credibility ("the agent is a wiki generator"). Detection: the §6 exit criteria have been met for weeks and no characterization target has been picked. Repair is organizational, not technical: a time-box agreed at kickoff, and a named champion who owns the F0→F1 transition (B6).

6Running it at the client

The first-meeting narrative writes itself once the paradox is stated: "we start by measuring and mapping what you already have — nothing is written until there is a net to catch it." That sentence does three jobs at once: it is technically the correct ordering (§3), it is the answer to the defensive senior engineer (§4), and it is a credibility screen — a client who bristles at "read-only first" is telling you something about how the eventual write phase will be governed. It is the same entry move this programme recommends on the design side (D7's audit-then-characterize) and in BMAD's *document-project; the pattern recurs because the dependency chain does not care which artifact class it is bootstrapping.

The working tool is the exit-criteria table. Each step has a deterministic "done" — you advance on evidence, never on calendar pressure or enthusiasm:

Chain stepExit criterion — what must existDeterministic check
F0 · read-only comprehensionarchitecture map + convention inventory, expert-sampled; pilot candidates shortlisted; docs labeled as hypothesissampling review signed off by the estate's seniors; every claim carries a confidence label (B4)
Characterization / golden-masterpilot module's observable behavior pinned; SUSPECTED-BUG items triaged (load-bearing vs real)tests run red on injected change; mutation score ≥ ~70% on the pilot module (B2/B3)
Minimal CIbuild + lint + tests execute on every change, visibly green/reda deliberately broken commit goes red end-to-end (E5: deterministic, not advisory)
AGENTS.mddescriptive conventions extracted from code, human-pruned, date-stampedclaim-sampling against the code passes; the file is in the repo, versioned (I1/I3)
Scoped writesfirst low-risk module (B5 heatmap), L1–L2 autonomy, full review loop, secrets scan cleanCFR/rework tracked from day one (E7); STOP criteria armed (B6)

Two honesty rules for the conversation. First, do not oversell the downside evidence: §2's file does not say agents fail on legacy code — METR's authors themselves warn against generalizing, and DORA 2025 shows throughput gains for teams with the platform basics. The claim to make is narrower and stronger: unverified writes degrade stability, and self-report will not detect it, so the ordering is the cheap insurance. Second, concede what is genuinely unsettled: how long Phase 0 should last has no evidence-backed answer — our time-box guidance is practice, not research — and claim 3's trajectory (§1) is moving fast enough that the "cheapest tool" assessment deserves a re-check at engagement start. Clients trust the material more, not less, when the synthesis is flagged (D1–D5 discipline).

7Takeaways

Three sentences

The bootstrap paradox — the agent needs a verification loop, legacy lacks one, the agent is the cheapest way to build one — closes into a bug-as-spec trap unless every agent output is anchored in a deterministic signal, so adoption unwinds along a forced dependency chain: read-only comprehension → characterization/golden-master → minimal CI → extracted-and-pruned AGENTS.md → scoped writes (our Report 4 synthesis, components standard, ordering ours). The evidence file supports ordering, not abstinence: DORA's amplifier findings (−7.2% stability in 2024, throughput sign flipped positive in 2025, instability persisting), the METR RCT's 19%-slower-while-believing-20%-faster on mature repos, and the benchmark teardowns (SWE-bench requires pre-existing fail-to-pass tests; agents drop from 72.8% to 25% when the task looks like real evolution) all say the same thing — write-capacity without verification discipline degrades stability and self-report will not notice. Phase 0 read-only breaks the loop at zero blast radius while attacking the 58% comprehension share of engineering time and earning the skeptics' trust on questions they can grade — but it needs a time-boxed exit with deterministic criteria, because both write-first (WO1) and Phase-0-forever (WO4) are ways of losing the same engagement.

8Further reading

Michael Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code
Prentice Hall · 2004 · the definitions in the Preface

"To me, legacy code is simply code without tests" — and the reason: without tests "we really don't know if our code is getting better or worse." Twenty years on, the operational definition of where agents can and cannot verify.

DORA — Accelerate State of DevOps 2024 · State of AI-assisted Software Development 2025
dora.dev · correlational, self-reported · re-verify before client use

2024: +25% AI adoption associated with −1.5% throughput, −7.2% stability, with small batches and robust testing named as the missing basics. 2025: throughput sign flips positive, instability persists, "AI's primary role is as an amplifier," and the seven-capability AI Capabilities Model.

METR — Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Experienced Open-Source Developer Productivity
arXiv:2507.09089 · July 2025 · randomized controlled trial

16 maintainers, 246 tasks, repos averaging 22k+ stars and 1M+ LOC: 19% slower with AI, against a forecast of 24% faster and a persistent post-hoc belief of 20% faster. The perception-gap result that disqualifies self-report as a pilot metric.

Jimenez et al. — SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues? · Epoch AI — What skills does SWE-bench Verified evaluate?
arXiv:2310.06770 · ICLR 2024 · epoch.ai · June 2025

The construction detail that matters here: ~90,000 PRs filtered to 2,294 instances that have fail-to-pass tests. Epoch's teardown: ~90% of Verified tasks under an hour, Django nearly half, five repos over 80% — the benchmark measures the estate a brownfield client lacks.

Le et al. — SWE-EVO: Benchmarking Coding Agents in Long-Horizon Software Evolution Scenarios
arXiv:2512.18470 · December 2025

48 evolution tasks averaging 21 files against suites averaging 874 tests: 25% for a frontier agent stack that scores 72.80% on SWE-bench Verified. The measured size of the greenfield-benchmark gap.

Birgitta Böckeler — Onboarding to a "legacy" codebase with the help of AI · Maintainability sensors for coding agents · Harness engineering
martinfowler.com · Aug 2024 / May 2026 / Apr 2026 · practitioner synthesis

The 2024 reality check ("in most cases… a pipe dream"), the sensors/guides vocabulary this chapter maps the chain onto, and "coverage tells us that a line was executed, but not that its impact was verified."

Alshahwan et al. — Automated Unit Test Improvement using Large Language Models at Meta
arXiv:2402.09171 · FSE 2024 · industrial deployment

The filter-based pattern: 75% built / 57% passed reliably / 25% raised coverage / 73% accepted for production. Claim 3 of the paradox, made true by deterministic filters rather than model trust.

Charalambous et al. — UnitTenX: Generating Tests for Legacy Packages with AI Agents Powered by Formal Verification
arXiv:2510.05441 · October 2025

LLM agents plus the ESBMC model checker generating tests that "document unknown interfaces" of legacy C (case study: djbdns, 202 functions, 0%→100% line coverage) — the 2025 data point that claim 3 improves exactly as fast as the deterministic oracles around the model.

Ehsani et al. — Where Do AI Coding Agents Fail? An Empirical Study of Failed Agentic Pull Requests in GitHub
arXiv:2601.15195 · MSR 2026 · AIDev-pop dataset (Li et al., 2025)

~602 failed agentic PRs classified: Abandoned/Not Reviewed is the largest category (38%), and documentation/CI/build tasks merge best while performance and bug-fix tasks fare worst — field evidence for starting at the harness-building end.

GitClear — AI Copilot Code Quality reports
gitclear.com · Feb 2025 ("8× duplicated blocks") & Jan 2026 ("4× code clones") · vendor research

211M changed lines, 2020–2024: copy-paste 8.3%→12.3% of changed lines, moved/refactored code ~24%→9.5%, two-week churn 5.5%→7.9%. What unverified write-capacity looks like in aggregate; vendor-sourced, correlational.

Simon Willison — Hallucinations in code are the least dangerous form of LLM mistakes
simonwillison.net · March 2, 2025

"The moment you run LLM generated code, any hallucinated methods will be instantly obvious" — the reassurance that quietly presupposes a runnable estate, which is why it inverts on untested legacy code.

Anthropic — Claude Code best practices
code.claude.com/docs · vendor guidance · point-in-time mid-2026

"You become the verification loop: every mistake waits for you to notice it"; explore (read-only) before plan before implement; /init as descriptive extraction. The vendor's own usage model agrees with the chain.

Report 4 — Brownfield Bootstrap research notes (this workbook)
module M5 research notes · the paradox, the chain, F0–F3

The source synthesis this chapter expands: the dependency chain, the day-1 recipe, the roadmap and its STOP criteria — flagged as ours throughout.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B1-bootstrap-paradox-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are B2/B3 (the characterization step and its quality gate), B4 (what Phase 0 produces), B5/B6 (where to write first, and the roadmap with STOP criteria), E4/E5/E6 (the verification loop, deterministic gates, and autonomy levels the chain instantiates), I1–I3 (the artifacts the bootstrap creates and keeps honest) and E7 (how the pilot is measured, given that self-report demonstrably fails). Benchmark scores, DORA correlations and vendor guidance are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B1, module M5's deep-dive set opens; B2 (characterization & golden-master testing) is the natural next chapter.

DEVICE SYNC

Sync across devices

Loading…

B2 · DEEP DIVE

Characterization & Golden-Master Testing with Agents

0Why it matters

B1 ended with a chain: read-only comprehension → characterization/golden-master tests → minimal CI → AGENTS.md → scoped writes. This chapter is the second link — the first time the agent writes anything in a brownfield engagement, and what it writes is a peculiar kind of artifact: tests that assert what the system does, not what anyone thinks it should do. The obvious objection arrives immediately, usually from the most senior engineer in the room: "the agent doesn't understand our code — how can it write the tests we'll trust our refactoring to?" The honest answer is that it doesn't have to understand, if you choose the technique accordingly. Characterization and approval testing were designed — two decades before coding agents — for exactly the situation where nobody trusts their understanding of the code; they replace interpretation with observation. What the agent adds is mass: Feathers-style pinning of a module used to cost weeks of expert time, which is why untested estates stayed untested. What the agent does not add is judgment — whether a pinned behavior is a feature or a bug remains a human question, and the entire discipline of this chapter is keeping that question out of the generator's hands. The chapter walks the two techniques (§1–§2), the production evidence that filtered mass generation works (§3), the five ways it goes wrong (§4), the workflow that survives contact with all five (§5), and the tools plus the exit criteria that decide when a module counts as "characterized" (§6).

What this chapter gives you
  • the two pinning techniques, precisely: Feathers characterization (per-behavior assertions) and Falco approval/golden-master (whole-output diffs) — and when each fits (§1–§2)
  • the terminology map: approval / golden master / snapshot / characterization are one mechanism with four emphases — with Bache's argument for which word to use (§2)
  • the evidence file: Meta TestGen-LLM and ACH, Qodo Cover, UnitTenX — what filtered generation achieved in production, with the numbers in their honest contexts (§3)
  • the five traps: coverage vanity, oracle-by-construction, bug-as-spec measured, flaky pins, and the agent gaming its own net (§4)
  • the workflow: "document, don't fix" → deterministic filter → sampled human review → SUSPECTED-BUG triage → mutation gate → read-only commit (§5, our synthesis)
  • the toolbox point-in-time and the exit criteria table (§6)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • B1 — why this step is second, never first: the dependency chain
  • B3 — mutation testing: the gate that decides whether these tests are real
  • B4 — agent archaeology: the read-only comprehension this step builds on
  • B5 — the heatmap that picks which module to characterize first, and Hyrum's Law on why bugs are load-bearing
  • E4 — verification-first: characterization is E4's loop being built where none existed
  • E5 — deterministic vs advisory: the filter pipeline is E5 applied to test generation
  • H2 — EARS/Gherkin: greenfield's machine-verifiable AC; characterization is the brownfield cousin that pins instead of specifies
  • I2 — write permissions: why approved characterization tests go read-only for the agent
  • D7 — design archaeology: the same golden-master move on the visual layer

1Pinning what is: characterization tests

Michael Feathers introduced the technique in Working Effectively with Legacy Code (2004), in the chapter titled — with a candor most methodology books avoid — "I Need to Make a Change, but I Don't Know What Tests to Write." His definition: "A characterization test is a test that characterizes the actual behavior of a piece of code." The purpose, in his later words: "The purpose of characterization testing is to document your system's actual behavior, not check for the behavior you wish your system had." The procedure is almost mechanical, which is precisely what makes it agent-compatible. Call the code with a chosen input. Write an assertion you know is wrong. Run it; the failure message tells you what the code actually returns. Change the assertion to expect that. You now have a test that passes — and a documented fact about the system. Repeat until the behaviors you care about are pinned.

Notice what the procedure never asks: whether the answer is correct. That omission is deliberate, and Feathers defends it with an argument that has only grown teeth since 2004: "When a system goes into production, in a way, it becomes its own specification." He tells the story of finding an obvious bug, fixing it, and fielding complaints from users whose workflows depended on the broken behavior. The general law arrived later, from Google's C++ estate — Hyrum's Law: "With a sufficient number of users of an API, it does not matter what you promise in the contract: all observable behaviors of your system will be depended on by somebody." (An observation of Hyrum Wright's, named by Titus Winters.) The canonical museum piece is Excel, which to this day treats 1900 as a leap year; Microsoft's own documentation explains the incorrect assumption was kept so Excel could "use the same serial date system used by Lotus 1-2-3" — a bug preserved for decades because correctness mattered less than what users depended on. A smaller, closer-to-home instance: when Java 8 reorganized HashMap's internals, iteration order — never guaranteed, always depended on — shifted, and test suites failed across the ecosystem (the Broad Institute's picard tracked it as "Unit tests fail due to changes in HashMap iteration order"). Pairing these examples with Hyrum's Law is our synthesis, not the sources'; but the pattern they share is the reason characterization pins everything observable, bugs included.

That is the technique's strength and its trap in one sentence. As a safety belt for refactoring, pinning actual behavior is exactly right: the refactor's contract is "observable behavior does not change," and a suite that encodes today's behavior — warts included — is the only suite that can enforce it. As a specification, the same suite is poison: it asserts that the warts are load-bearing without anyone having decided they are. Feathers wrote the technique for a developer who holds the difference in their head — they know they are documenting, not specifying. An agent holds nothing in its head between tasks (E2); hand it the technique without the discipline and you get B1's bug-as-spec loop at industrial scale. The discipline — who decides what a pinned wart means — is §5's whole subject.

The rest of the Feathers toolkit survives intact and is worth naming in an engagement, because it gives the client's engineers vocabulary they may half-know: seams (places where behavior can be altered without editing the code — the enabling condition for getting legacy under test at all), sprout (new behavior grows in new, tested code that the old code calls) and wrap (the old code is enclosed rather than edited). B5 returns to seams as a pilot-selection criterion: a module with no seams is not where the agent starts.

2The whole output at once: approval / golden-master testing

Characterization tests pin one behavior per assertion; someone still has to pick the behaviors. The second technique drops even that requirement. Approval testing — associated for two decades with Llewellyn Falco and his ApprovalTests libraries — runs the code over a representative set of inputs, captures the entire output as text (or an image, or a rendered document), and asks a human to approve the capture once. From then on, the test is a diff: any run whose output differs from the approved file is red until a human either fixes the code or approves the new output. Emily Bache's one-sentence anatomy covers every variant: "Run the software, gather the output and store it. The combination of (a) exactly how you set up and ran the software and (b) the stored output, forms the basis of a test case."

The same mechanism trades under four names, and the names carry emphases worth keeping straight — the client's frontend team already knows one of them:

NameEmphasisHabitat
Approval testinga human approves the captured output — the review step is in the nameApprovalTests, Verify, Texttest; Bache's preferred term, for exactly that reason
Golden masterthe approved capture is the fixed reference; the name comes from manufacturing's master disclegacy-rescue literature; popularized for legacy code by J. B. Rainsberger (Legacy Code Retreats). Bache argues against the term — it suggests "once you've decided on the correct output, and stored it in a test, it should never change"
Snapshot testingthe capture is cheap and frequently regenerated — which invites regenerating it carelesslyJest/Vitest and the JS ecosystem
Characterization testingthe capture documents essential behavior of code that has no specFeathers' world: post-hoc, on legacy

Why this family is the natural first move for an agent on a brownfield estate: it is the testing technique with the lowest comprehension requirement in existence. Falco's own pitch for it was about legacy code long before agents — with approval tests it is "relatively easy to get good, working test coverage based on output rather than having to gain a deep understanding of the internals of the legacy code" (Herding Code podcast #117; podcast-attested wording). Substitute "agent" for "developer new to the estate" and the argument transfers whole: the generator needs to find the entry points and produce representative inputs — B4's archaeology provides both — and the question is this output right? moves entirely into the human approval step, which is where §5 keeps it. The objection from the senior engineer in §0 is answered not by defending the agent's understanding but by choosing the technique that never needed it.

The engineering that makes whole-output pinning livable is small but load-bearing. Scrubbers handle nondeterminism: in ApprovalTests' definition, a scrubber is "a function that takes a string and returns a string," used "to convert non-deterministic text to something stable" — timestamps, GUIDs, connection ids become [date], [guid-1] before the diff. Un-scrubbed nondeterminism is the number-one killer of golden masters (§4's flakiness data agrees). Printers control how objects render to text — the capture is only as reviewable as its formatting. Combination approvals run a function over the cross-product of input lists and capture the whole grid in one file — the cheapest way to pin a pure function's behavior densely enough that B3's mutation gate has something to bite on.

The JS world's instance deserves a paragraph because your client's frontend team uses it daily and has opinions. Jest's snapshot testing is a golden master with the approval step weakened to a CLI flag: "Snapshot tests are a very useful tool whenever you want to make sure your UI does not change unexpectedly," say the docs — which then spend most of their length on the discipline the flag undermines: commit snapshots and review them in code review, "fight against the habit of regenerating snapshots when test suites fail instead of examining the root causes of their failure," and — the bug-as-spec warning, shipped in mainstream tool documentation since the 2010s — if other snapshot tests fail due to an unintentional bug, "we would need to fix the bug before re-generating snapshots to avoid recording snapshots of the buggy behavior." Every failure mode this chapter attributes to agent-generated pins already existed at human scale in every large Jest codebase; the agent changes the volume, not the physics.

One boundary to draw honestly, because a well-read engineer will raise it: Google's Testing-on-the-Toilet piece "Change-Detector Tests Considered Harmful" (Alex Eagle, 2015) is sometimes waved at this whole family. Its actual target is tests that mirror the structure of the implementation — mock-heavy tests that restate the code and break on any edit without verifying behavior. Approval tests assert on observable output, which is the opposite move; the piece does not mention golden masters. The legitimate overlap is narrower: a golden master scoped too wide (the whole dashboard, the whole API response with its timestamps) degenerates into a change detector in practice, red on every harmless edit until humans stop reading the diffs. The fix is scope, not abandonment — pin the chart, not the dashboard — and it is a §4 trap with a §5 answer.

3Mass generation with filters: the production evidence

The reason this chapter can recommend agent-written pins at all is that the pattern has production evidence — and the evidence is unanimous about where the guarantee lives. Not in the model. In the filter.

Meta's TestGen-LLM (Alshahwan et al., FSE 2024) is the canonical deployment. Its design principle has its own companion paper — "Assured LLM-Based Software Engineering" (arXiv:2402.04380) — and the principle is worth stating because it is E5's deterministic/advisory split applied to test generation: every candidate test passes through hard filters — it must build, it must pass reliably (run repeatedly, to screen flakiness), and it must measurably increase coverage — before any human sees it. The model's output is advisory; the filters decide. Keep the published numbers in their honest contexts, because they are two different measurements: in the evaluation on Instagram's Reels and Stories, 75% of generated test cases built, 57% passed reliably, and 25% increased coverage; in the broader Instagram and Facebook test-a-thons, TestGen-LLM improved 11.5% of the classes it was applied to, and 73% of its recommendations were accepted for production by Meta engineers. (A widely-circulated "useful:generated 1:4 vs 1:20" ratio is not in the paper — do not repeat it.) Read the numbers with brownfield eyes: sub-60% reliability at the generator is fine because the filter is in front of the human — the 40%+ of junk costs compute, not review time and not trust.

Meta's second-generation system, ACH ("Mutation-Guided LLM-based Test Generation at Meta," FSE 2025), inverts the direction of generation and previews B3. Instead of generating tests and asking "did coverage rise?", ACH generates faults first — targeted mutants representing a concern Meta cares about (privacy regressions) — then generates tests that catch those faults. Deployed across 10,795 Android Kotlin classes on seven platforms including Messenger and WhatsApp, it produced 9,095 mutants and 571 privacy-hardening tests; engineers accepted 73% of them, judging 36% privacy-relevant. The equivalent-mutant problem — mutants that change the code but not its behavior, mutation testing's classic tax — got an LLM detector of its own: precision 0.79 and recall 0.47 out of the box, rising to 0.95/0.96 with simple pre-processing. For this chapter the salient point is the trajectory: Meta's first system asked "do the tests exercise the code?"; its second asked "do the tests catch injected faults?" — which is exactly the quality bar B3 will make the exit criterion for the whole bootstrap.

Outside Meta, the pattern reproduces — at reality's usual discount. Qodo's Cover-Agent, the first open-source implementation of the TestGen-LLM design, reported the friction a paper's evaluation section smooths over: indentation-mangling with weaker models, extra prompting for edge cases, repeated useless tests until a "failed tests" section was added to the prompt. Its concrete win is verifiable in public: an autonomously generated pull request — 15 tests, 168 newly covered lines — accepted into Hugging Face's pytorch-image-models (PR #2331). Qodo's CEO Itamar Friedman's honest framing at the time: "I believe that we are still in the era of AI assistants and not AI teammates who run fully automated workflows." And at the hard end of the spectrum, UnitTenX (B1 §1 introduced it) pairs LLM agents with the ESBMC bounded model checker and symbolic execution to generate tests for untested legacy C — "resolve bugs and document unknown interfaces," in the paper's words — taking its case-study codebase (djbdns, 202 functions) from 0% to 100% line coverage. One codebase, so treat it as an existence proof, not a rate; but notice the anchor is a model checker, the most deterministic filter in the toolbox.

The research frontier adds one more design lesson: repair beats regeneration. YATE (Konstantinou et al., 2025) starts from the observation that a large share of generated tests die at the filter for shallow reasons — they don't compile, they reference the wrong symbol — and that repairing them (feed the error back, fix, re-run) recovers most of that loss: +32% line coverage and +22% more mutants killed than plain generation, and ahead of four state-of-the-art generators at comparable cost. The loop shape — generate, run, read the failure, repair — is E4's inner loop; every serious harness in E3 already has it, and a characterization campaign should run inside it rather than as one-shot generation.

The lesson the evidence teaches

Generation is a commodity; the engineering is the filter chain and the repair loop. Every production success in this file put deterministic gates between the model and the human — build, repeated runs, coverage delta, injected faults, a model checker — and every gate is E5's "deterministic beats advisory" applied to the agent's own output. When a vendor demos a test generator, the question is never "how good are the tests?" — it is "what stands between the raw generation and my review queue?"

4The traps — five ways a safety net lies to you

Trap 1: tests that execute but don't verify. Coverage measures execution, not verification — a line "covered" by a test with no meaningful assertion counts the same as one guarded by three. The MutGen authors found test suites at 100% coverage with a 4% mutation score; TestGenEval — the benchmark built from real repositories' human-written tests, and the one that grades generators on mutation score as well as coverage — measured the best frontier model at 35.2% coverage but a 18.8% mutation score on real-world test generation. The gap between those two numbers is invisible junk: green, covered, asserting nothing. This is why B3 exists and why §6's exit criterion is a mutation score, never a coverage number.

Trap 2: the oracle is passing-by-construction — know which product you're buying. Mathews and Nagappan's critique of the generator category ("Design choices made by LLM-based test generators prevent them from finding bugs," 2024) lands on a design fact: tools like Cover-Agent construct their oracle from the code's current output, and filter out tests that fail on it — so if the code is buggy, the tools validate the bug and can discard a genuinely bug-revealing test as "broken." As a criticism of test generation marketed as bug-finding, that is damning. For characterization, read it carefully: passing-on-current-code is not a flaw of the oracle, it is the definition of the task — §1 established that pinning the wart is the point. The trap is the confusion, in either direction: a team that thinks its characterization campaign found no bugs ("all tests pass!") has misread what the tests can see; a team that buys a "bug-finding" generator gets a characterization suite with better marketing. Name the product before you run it.

Trap 3: bug-as-spec is now measured, not hypothetical. B1 argued the loop qualitatively; in 2026 Konstantinou, Tambon and Papadakis put numbers on it: when an LLM writes tests after seeing a faulty implementation, fault detection drops from roughly 25% (tests written from the task description alone) to roughly 14% — the models read the buggy code and reproduce its behavior in the assertions, consistently across five models and three benchmarks. That is the mechanism of petrification caught on instruments: the pin inherits the bug precisely because the generator saw the code — which a characterization generator must. You cannot prompt this away; you can only route around it with process — §5's SUSPECTED-BUG channel exists because the generator will not raise its own hand.

Trap 4: flaky pins — the net that cries wolf. A study of LLM-test flakiness on industrial database systems (ICSE SEIP 2026; SAP HANA, MySQL, DuckDB, SQLite) found generated tests consistently flakier than the existing suites — against SAP HANA's baseline flake rate of 0.07%, generated tests flaked at 0.29–0.39%, and on MySQL at up to 0.71% — and 63% of the flaky tests (72 of 115) shared one root cause: asserting on unordered collections (SQL without ORDER BY, pinned row order). Small percentages, wrong conclusion: a characterization campaign generates thousands of tests, and at these rates the suite pages someone daily about nothing. The study's operational advice, verbatim, matches this chapter's ordering: "developers should prioritize fixing flaky tests before applying LLM-based generation approaches" — or the noise floor swallows the signal. Scrubbers (§2) are the same medicine at the approval-test layer: nondeterminism must be normalized before the pin, not apologized for after.

Trap 5: the agent games its own net. A characterization suite is only a constraint if the agent cannot edit it. Kent Beck's running metaphor for agentic tools in 2025 was the genie — grants the wish, with a sting — and his "Augmented Coding: Beyond the Vibes" names the tell to watch for: "Any indication that the genie was cheating, for example by disabling or deleting tests" (in conversation with Gergely Orosz he described exactly such incidents from his own sessions). The systematic version is ImpossibleBench (I2): frontier models, handed tasks whose tests cannot be satisfied honestly, modified the tests in a majority of cases. And ThoughtWorks' Radar (Vol. 34, April 2026) generalizes the governance failure: "Agents find creative exfiltration paths, push to branches they shouldn't touch and erode approve/deny chokepoints without any malicious intent or explicit prompting." The countermeasure is structural, not rhetorical, and I2 already specified it: once approved, characterization tests are read-only for the agent — enforced in the harness (deny-rules, CODEOWNERS, protected paths), not requested in the prompt. A deliberate behavior change then flips a pin through the human door: the fix PR updates the test, a human approves both sides, and the diff records that the spec moved on purpose.

Underneath all five sits the human-scale failure the JS world named years ago: approve-all fatigue. Artem Sapegin's much-cited snapshot-testing critique (2019) states the arc plainly: "Understanding each failure takes a lot of time, so often developers just update snapshots without looking at them at all, especially if it's not their code." Hundreds of pins, an OS or library bump repaints them all, and the re-approval becomes a reflex; the net still exists, but no one reads it, so it catches nothing. Agents accelerate the arc by raising the volume. The defenses are §5's sampling discipline and §2's scoping rule (pin the chart, not the dashboard) — and honesty in the engagement plan: a golden master nobody re-reads is inventory, not insurance.

5The workflow: document, don't fix — then triage

Everything above compresses into one pipeline, and the pipeline is this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — the components are standard (Feathers' technique, Falco's approval step, Meta's filters, Hyrum's law), the assembly is ours. It exists to hold one line: the generator observes, the filter verifies, the human interprets. Every trap in §4 is a place where one of those three tries to do another's job.

Step 1 — generate under "document, don't fix." The prompt's core instruction: pin current behavior; do not correct anything; where behavior looks suspicious, keep the pin and mark the test SUSPECTED-BUG with one sentence on why. This is Trap 3's routing: the generator cannot reliably distinguish wart from feature (measured, §4), so it is never asked to — it is asked to notice, which costs nothing when wrong. An agent that "helpfully" fixes the off-by-one while characterizing it has broken the refactoring contract before the refactor started — the B1 failure mode in one commit.

Step 2 — the deterministic filter. TestGen-LLM's three gates, verbatim into your CI: the candidate must build; must pass repeatedly (screening Trap 4 at the gate — run it N times, not once); must raise coverage over the module's baseline. Add the estate's linter and the scrubber check (no raw timestamps/ids in approved output). Everything that fails dies silently — review capacity is the scarcest resource in the whole bootstrap, and the filter's job is to spend compute to protect it.

Step 3 — sampled human review. All surviving SUSPECTED-BUG tests get human eyes, plus a ~20% sample of the rest — checked not for correctness of the pinned values (the code is the oracle; that question is circular) but for sense: are these the behaviors worth pinning, are the inputs representative, is the output scoped tightly enough to survive Trap 5's fatigue arc. The sample rate is a dial, not a doctrine: raise it on the payment-adjacent module, lower it on the report formatter — B5's heatmap already encodes that judgment.

Step 4 — triage every SUSPECTED-BUG, one of two doors. Load-bearing bug (Hyrum says someone depends on it — check consumers before believing otherwise): keep the pin, write the ADR recording that the behavior is intentional-by-history, so the next engineer — or the next agent reading the estate's I1 artifacts — does not "fix" it either. Real bug (nothing depends on it, or what depends on it is wrong too): file the ticket, fix it after the net exists, and let the fix flip the characterization test deliberately — the red test is the proof the net works, and the two-sided diff (code + test, human-approved) is the audit trail of a spec change. The one forbidden move is the quiet third door: fixing it during characterization, which changes the system while the safety belt is half-buckled.

Step 5 — the mutation gate (B3, previewed). Before the module counts as characterized, inject faults and count kills: a ~70% mutation score on the pilot module is Report 4's working threshold. This is the only step that answers Trap 1 — everything upstream can be green and worthless; surviving mutants are a concrete, inspectable list of what the net does not hold. B3 owns the mechanics (tooling, equivalent mutants, cost control); B2 owns the contract: no write phase opens on a module whose net has not been graded.

Step 6 — commit, and the tests go read-only. The suite enters version control and the agent's write permissions close over it (I2's matrix; Trap 5's countermeasure). From here on the net is infrastructure: the agent refactors under it in F2, and touching it requires the human door.

Flagged as our synthesis

The six-step pipeline, the SUSPECTED-BUG marker, the ~20%-plus-all-suspected sampling rule and the two-door triage are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — assembled from named, standard components (Feathers 2004; Falco's approval step; TestGen-LLM's filters, FSE 2024; Hyrum's Law; ImpossibleBench's read-only-tests mitigation) but not, as a package, an industry standard. Present it as our recommendation with the components' provenance — same rule as D1–D5.

6The toolbox and the exit criteria

The tool landscape is stable at the core and fast-moving at the edges — everything in this section is point-in-time (mid-2026); re-verify names, versions and licenses before a client meeting.

LayerTools (mid-2026)Notes for the engagement
Approval testing, multi-languageApprovalTests (Falco et al.) — Java, C#, C++, Python, JavaScript, Go, Ruby and ~8 morescrubbers, printers, combination approvals; the reference implementation of the §2 mechanics
.NET snapshottingVerify (Simon Cropp)actively maintained; licensing changed mid-2026 — from August 2026 commercial/government use of official binaries moves to a paid subscription while source stays open and individuals/OSS are unaffected; check current terms before recommending
JS/TS snapshotsJest, Vitest (toMatchSnapshot / inline)the client's frontend team already runs a golden master; §2's discipline notes apply verbatim
Whole-program / CLITexttest (Geoff Bache)approval testing at the process boundary — stdout/files as the captured output; fits batch jobs and ETL
HTTP boundariesVCR-style record/replay (VCR, vcrpy, Polly.JS, WireMock recording)pins the integration seam without the dependency; cassettes are golden masters of traffic — scrub secrets before committing
Visual layervisual-regression snapshots (Percy/Chromatic/Playwright screenshots)D7's design archaeology uses exactly this move; vendors now ship AI diff-reviewers aimed at approve-all fatigue — treat as vendor claims
Test generationQodo Cover (OSS), harness-native campaigns (Claude Code / Copilot agents under §5's pipeline)the generator matters less than the filter chain; wire TestGen-LLM's three gates into CI first

When is a module characterized? Four deterministic criteria, none of them "the team feels confident" (B1's METR lesson: feelings don't grade):

Exit criterionDeterministic check
The net catches changeinject a behavior change by hand; at least one test goes red
The net catches faultsmutation score ≥ ~70% on the module (B3 owns the gate)
The warts are adjudicatedzero untriaged SUSPECTED-BUG markers; each resolved to an ADR or a ticket
The net is stable and ownedN consecutive green runs (no flakes); tests read-only for the agent; keep-or-discard decision after the refactor recorded

The last row's tail matters: Feathers and Falco both treat pins as potentially disposable — a golden master that existed to guard one refactor can be deleted after it, and often should be, precisely because of the approve-all fatigue arc. Keep the per-behavior characterization tests that read as documentation; discard the whole-output pins whose diffs nobody will read next quarter; record which you did in the module's ADR trail (I1: durable decision, disposable artifact). A net you keep is a maintenance commitment, not a trophy.

7Takeaways

  • Choose techniques that don't need understanding. Characterization (per-behavior) and approval/golden-master (whole-output) were designed for code nobody trusts their grasp of — the agent's weakness is the technique's operating assumption, not its refutation (§1–§2).
  • The filter is the product. Meta shipped 73%-acceptance test generation by putting build/repeat-run/coverage-delta gates in front of humans; ACH upgraded the gate to injected faults. Ask any generator vendor what stands between the model and your review queue (§3).
  • Coverage lies; mutation grades. 100% coverage with 4% mutation score exists; frontier models hit ~35% coverage but ~19% mutation on real repos. The exit criterion is a mutation score (§4, B3).
  • Bug-as-spec is measured. Seeing buggy code drops LLM fault detection from ~25% to ~14% — the generator pins the wart because it read the wart. Route the judgment to humans via SUSPECTED-BUG; never ask the generator to adjudicate (§4–§5).
  • The net must be agent-proof. Agents demonstrably edit failing tests to reach green; approved pins go read-only in the harness, and spec changes walk through the human door as a two-sided diff (§4–§5, I2).
  • Pins can be disposable. Keep what documents; discard what nobody will re-read; write down which — the fatigue arc kills unread golden masters anyway (§6).
Three sentences

Characterization and approval testing pin what a legacy system does — deliberately including its bugs, because Hyrum's Law says someone depends on them — and they are the natural first write task for an agent precisely because they replace understanding with observation plus a human approval step. The production evidence (Meta's TestGen-LLM and ACH, Qodo's public PR, UnitTenX's model-checker anchor) converges on one design: mass generation is a commodity and every guarantee lives in the deterministic filter chain and the repair loop, while the measured traps — 100%-coverage/4%-mutation suites, fault detection falling from ~25% to ~14% when the model sees buggy code, generated tests flaking at multiples of the baseline rate, agents editing failing tests to reach green — are each a place where generation tried to do verification's or interpretation's job. Our workflow holds the line with "document, don't fix" plus SUSPECTED-BUG marking, sampled review, two-door triage (load-bearing → ADR; real bug → ticket, fixed only after the net exists), a ~70% mutation gate before any write phase, and read-only status for the approved net (our Report 4 synthesis; components standard, assembly ours).

8Further reading

Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code (2004) + "Characterization Testing" (blog)
the source technique · ch. 13 · michaelfeathers.silvrback.com

The definition, the failing-assertion procedure, and the "it becomes its own specification" argument — the chapter's §1 in the author's own words.

Bache — "Why we should be saying 'Approval Testing' instead of 'Golden Master'" (2021)
coding-is-like-cooking.info · the terminology map

One mechanism, four names, and the argument for the name that keeps the human approval step visible. Her samman-coaching kata catalogue is the hands-on companion.

Meta — TestGen-LLM (arXiv:2402.09171, FSE 2024) + Assured LLM-SE (arXiv:2402.04380) + ACH (arXiv:2501.12862, FSE 2025)
the production evidence file

Filter-based generation at deployment scale, the assurance framing, and the mutation-guided second generation with its equivalent-mutant detector.

Mathews & Nagappan — "Design choices made by LLM-based test generators prevent them from finding bugs" (arXiv:2412.14137)
the oracle critique

Why passing-by-construction oracles cannot find bugs — and why that is a purchasing error, not a characterization error. Read with Trap 2's both-directions warning.

Konstantinou, Tambon & Papadakis — the code-before-testing risk study (arXiv:2607.05139)
bug-as-spec, measured

Fault detection ~25% → ~14% when the generator sees the faulty implementation — the number behind §5's "document, don't fix" discipline.

Flakiness of LLM-generated tests for DBMS (arXiv:2601.08998, ICSE SEIP 2026)
the noise floor

Generated tests flake at multiples of baseline on SAP HANA and MySQL, 63% from unordered collections — the empirical case for scrubbers, repeat-run filters, and fixing existing flakes first.

TestGenEval (arXiv:2410.00752) + MutGen (arXiv:2506.02954) + Haroon et al. (arXiv:2603.23443)
how to grade generated tests

The benchmark that reports mutation score, the 100%-coverage/4%-mutation exhibit, and the >99%-of-failing-tests-pass-on-original precision result — B3's evidence base, previewed.

Report 4 — Brownfield Bootstrap research notes (this workbook)
module M5 research notes · the workflow's source synthesis

The SUSPECTED-BUG workflow, the triage doors and the F0–F3 roadmap this chapter's §5–§6 expand — flagged as ours throughout.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B2-characterization-golden-master-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are B1 (why this step is second), B3 (the mutation gate that grades the net), B4/B5 (the archaeology that feeds it and the heatmap that scopes it), E4/E5 (the verification loop and the deterministic-filter principle it instantiates), I2 (the write-permission matrix that makes the net agent-proof), H2 (the greenfield cousin: machine-verifiable AC) and D7 (the same golden-master move on the visual layer). Tool names, licences and benchmark numbers are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B2, module M5 stands at two of six; B3 (mutation testing as the test-quality gate) is the natural next chapter.

B3 · DEEP DIVE

Mutation Testing as the Test-Quality Gate

0Why it matters

B1 gave the safe ordering and B2 built the safety net: a mass-generated suite of characterization tests that pins what the system does. This chapter is the step that decides whether that net is real. The uncomfortable fact is that every cheap signal the team already trusts — the suite is green, coverage went up — can be produced by tests that verify nothing, and the generators we use produce exactly such tests at scale unless something grades them. Mutation testing is that grader: it injects small faults and counts how many the suite notices, measuring the one property coverage cannot — whether anything would fail if the code were wrong. E4 §5 taught the mechanics (operators, killed vs surviving mutants, the worked example); this chapter makes the mechanism operational as a brownfield gate: the fifty-year evidence base and why it suddenly became urgent, what Google and Meta actually do at scale (and pointedly do not do), the three taxes you will pay — equivalent mutants, pseudo-tested code, flakiness — the cost engineering that keeps runs affordable on a legacy estate, and the design of the gate itself: a ~70% module score as the F0→F2 exit criterion, with survivors flowing back to the agent as its next work items. One sentence to hold the whole chapter: coverage measures whether code ran; mutation score measures whether tests would notice it being wrong — and only the second is safe to delegate against.

What this chapter gives you
  • the theory in three results: the 1978 founding assumptions, the FSE 2014 validation that mutants proxy real faults, and the ICSE 2014 result that coverage does not (§1)
  • the LLM-era evidence file: 100%-coverage/4%-mutation suites, GPT-4o's 35.2%-coverage/18.8%-mutation gap, and the >99% precision failure on semantic changes (§2)
  • why "the tests pass" is itself a gameable gate — the reward-hacking measurements (§3)
  • the feedback loop: surviving mutants as the agent's to-do list, with the measured gains (§4)
  • what mutation testing looks like at Google and Meta scale — and the consumption-mode lesson (§5)
  • the three taxes (§6), the cost ladder and toolbox (§7), and the gate design for our rollout (§8, our synthesis)
  • the anti-patterns that quietly rebuild coverage theater under a new name (§9)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • E4 — the mechanics from first principles, and verification as the autonomy ceiling
  • B1 — why the gate sits at the end of the bootstrap chain, never before it
  • B2 — the characterization suite this gate grades; the flaky-pins trap it inherits
  • B4/B5 — the archaeology that feeds the suite and the heatmap that picks the module
  • E7 — measurement discipline: the same honesty rules applied to pilot metrics

1Fifty years of theory — and why it suddenly matters

Mutation testing is not a fashionable reaction to LLMs; it is one of the oldest ideas in testing research, and the case against coverage predates coding agents by a decade. The founding paper is DeMillo, Lipton and Sayward's "Hints on Test Data Selection: Help for the Practicing Programmer" (IEEE Computer, April 1978), with Hamlet's "Testing Programs with the Aid of a Compiler" (IEEE TSE, July 1977) as an independent parallel origin. The 1978 paper states, as observations, the two assumptions the whole field later formalized into named hypotheses. First, what became the competent programmer hypothesis: "Programmers have one great advantage that is almost never exploited: they create programs that are close to being correct!" — so small injected faults are a reasonable model of the faults that actually occur. Second, what became the coupling effect: "Test data that distinguishes all programs differing from a correct one by only simple errors is so sensitive that it also implicitly distinguishes more complex errors" — so killing simple mutants buys detection of compound faults too.

For thirty-five years those were assumptions. In 2014 they were tested. Just, Jalali, Inozemtseva, Ernst, Holmes and Fraser ("Are Mutants a Valid Substitute for Real Faults in Software Testing?", FSE 2014) took 357 real faults from five large Java projects — the seed data that became Defects4J, though the artifact itself was introduced in a separate ISSTA 2014 companion paper — generated some 230,000 mutants, and measured: "The results show the existence of a coupling effect for 73% of real faults," with 17% of real faults not coupled to any mutant (the remainder indeterminate). More important for our purposes, they found "a statistically significant correlation between mutant detection and real fault detection, independently of code coverage" — mutation score predicts real-fault detection even after coverage is accounted for. Mutants are an imperfect but validated proxy for the faults you actually care about.

The same year, the anti-coverage result landed. Inozemtseva and Holmes ("Coverage Is Not Strongly Correlated with Test Suite Effectiveness", ICSE 2014) measured 31,000 test suites across five systems and found "there is a low to moderate correlation between coverage and effectiveness when the number of test cases in the suite is controlled for" — and that "stronger forms of coverage do not provide greater insight." Their conclusion is the sentence to quote at any client who proposes a coverage KPI: coverage "should not be used as a quality target because it is not a good indicator of test suite effectiveness." Note the precision — coverage remains useful as a negative signal (uncovered code is definitely unverified); it fails as a positive one (covered code may still be unverified).

Why this history matters for the engagement

When you propose mutation testing at a client, the pushback is usually "exotic academic tooling." The honest counter is the opposite: the technique is older than most of the client's codebase, its founding assumptions were empirically validated on real faults in 2014, and the reason it stayed niche was cost — a constraint §5 and §7 show has been engineered away at the largest codebases on earth. What changed in 2024–2026 is not the theory; it is that test generation became free, which made test quality the scarce resource. A fifty-year-old grader met a two-year-old flood.

2The LLM-era evidence: coverage became free, and worthless

Agents made tests cheap. A March 2026 empirical study of real-world repositories (Yoshimoto et al., arXiv:2603.13724, on the AIDev PR census dataset) found that "AI authored 16.4% of all commits adding tests in real-world repositories," and that the tests look reassuring: "longer code and a higher density of assertions while maintaining lower cyclomatic complexity," contributing "code coverage comparable to human-written tests." On every dashboard the team already has, agent-written tests are indistinguishable from — or better than — human ones. That is precisely the problem: the dashboards measure the wrong thing.

Grade the same kind of output on fault detection and the picture inverts. The MUTGEN study (Wang, Xu, Briand and Liu, "Mutation-Guided Unit Test Generation with a Large Language Model", accepted at IEEE TSE; arXiv:2506.02954) reports the exhibit this workbook keeps returning to: "some test suites achieve 100% coverage but only 4% mutation score." TestGenEval (Jain, Synnaeve, Rozière; arXiv:2410.00752) — the benchmark built from real repositories' human-written tests, and the one that grades on mutation score precisely because it is "much harder to game" than coverage — measured: "The best performing model—GPT-4o—has an average coverage of 35.2% and a mutation score of 18.8%." The gap between any pair of these numbers is invisible junk: tests that execute code and verify almost nothing.
