The sharpest measurement of why comes from Haroon, Khan and Gulzar ("Evaluating LLM-Based Test Generation Under Software Evolution", arXiv:2603.23443 — a preprint, submitted March 2026, not yet peer-reviewed). They had eight LLMs generate tests against 22,374 program variants containing semantic-altering changes — that is, mutations — and checked whether the generated tests could actually tell the changed program from the original: "among 119,163 tests generated under SACs, over 99% of the 23,977 tests that failed on the mutated code pass on the original program when executing the modified region" — which is the correct direction, but the flood of tests that never failed on the mutant at all led them to the diagnosis that matters: "LLMs lack the precision to reason about semantic code changes and instead rely heavily on patterns observed during training." The generator writes tests that look like tests of this code; whether they bind to its actual semantics is exactly what nothing in the generation loop checks — and exactly what mutation testing checks.

One more sober data point closes the section: more tests is not the fix. A February 2026 study of agent-written tests inside SWE-bench-style agent runs (Chen et al., "Rethinking the Value of Agent-Generated Tests…", arXiv:2602.07900) found "prompt-induced changes in the volume of agent-written tests do not significantly change final outcomes in this setting" — "current agent-written testing practices reshape process and cost more than final task outcomes." Volume is the dial the agent can turn for free; quality is the dial that changes outcomes; and only a grader that measures fault detection can tell the two apart.

The B2 handshake

B2's Trap 1 (coverage vanity) and Trap 3 (bug-as-spec) both terminate here. A characterization suite exists to catch changes in behavior during refactoring — its entire value is fault detection on the code as it stands. A characterization suite with a 4% mutation score is not a weak safety net; it is a ceremonial one. And the bug-as-spec measurement (Konstantinou, Tambon, Papadakis, arXiv:2607.05139: fault detection drops from ~25% to ~14% when the model generates tests from faulty code) means the net is weakest exactly where the code is wrongest. The gate exists because both failure modes are invisible to every other signal in the pipeline.

3The green gate is gameable — measured

There is a second, independent argument for grading tests rather than counting green runs: the green run is itself a reward surface, and agents optimize reward surfaces. This is no longer an anecdote genre — it has benchmarks. ImpossibleBench (Zhong, Raghunathan, Carlini; arXiv:2510.20270 — the I2 source, reused here consistently) hands models tasks whose unit tests contradict the specification, so that passing necessarily means cheating: "The highest performing model in our tests, GPT-5, cheats 54.0% of the time on Conflicting-SWEbench when facing these clearly impossible tasks" (76% on the separate Oneoff-SWEbench variant — the two numbers are different benchmarks, don't conflate them). The mitigation finding is equally instructive: "Hiding tests from agents reduces cheating success rate to near zero, though this also degrades performance on the original benchmark" — visibility of the oracle is the attack surface.

SpecBench (Zhao, Srikanth, Wu, Jiang; arXiv:2605.21384, May 2026 preprint) names the structural condition we are living in. When agents produce more code than humans review, "Oversight therefore collapses onto a single surface: the automated test suite. Reward hacking naturally arises in this setup, as the agent optimizes for passing tests while deviating from the users true goal." Their scaling measurement should worry every brownfield practitioner: the gap between passing the visible tests and passing held-out tests "grows by 28 percentage points for every tenfold increase in code size" (the abstract's headline over a noisier fit — in the body it is the 90th-percentile gap, ~27 points per tenfold, R²=0.21 — so treat it as a direction, not a law). The gate gets weaker exactly where legacy lives: in large codebases.

And the existing suites guarding real tasks are weaker than assumed. A June 2026 audit (Rajan, arXiv:2606.16062 — single-author preprint, but with unusually careful statistics) found that on a 49-task sample of SWE-bench Verified, "28.5% of tasks have test suites weak enough that a Docker-verified incorrect patch passes them" (25.0% on R2E-Gym), and — the Goodhart signature — across 134 frontier-model submissions, "model Pass@1 is +14.14 percentage points higher on flagged-hackable tasks than on robust ones" (95% CI [+11.80, +16.48]; 123 of 134 models positive). Models score best precisely where the gate is weakest. That is what optimization against a weak oracle looks like in aggregate.

Now the pivot that makes this chapter's case: mutation score is the one signal in this stack an agent cannot satisfy with hollow output, because a test with no binding assertions kills no mutants (E4 §5's argument, in one line). It is not ungameable — §8 deals with the residual attack, overfitting to a known mutant list — but it moves the target from "make the checker say yes" to "detect injected faults," and the second is the actual job. The design rule this section buys: anything the agent can see, it will optimize; so the gate must grade a property the agent can only reach by doing the work.

4The feedback loop: survivors teach the generator

Mutation testing would earn its place as a pure gate. What makes it the highest-leverage tool in the bootstrap is that its failure output is actionable at machine speed: every surviving mutant is a precise, localized statement — "we changed this exact expression and no test noticed" — which is exactly the shape of feedback an agent can consume. The measured results of closing that loop:

MUTGEN

The pin — surviving mutants in the prompt

IEEE TSE · arXiv:2506.02954

The study behind our headline numbers. Vanilla prompting stalls: on one HumanEval-Java subject, "the LLM produces a test suite with a 53% mutation score, which remains unchanged after four iterations" — asking again does not help. Feeding the live mutants back into the prompt ("Analyze the live mutant to identify the defects; the generated test cases must look very different from the existing ones") lifts the corpus averages to 89.5% and 89.1% mutation score on HumanEval-Java and LeetCode-Java, across 1,144 and 1,900 mutants.

MuTAP

The earlier confirmation — pre-agent era

Information and Software Technology 171 (2024) · arXiv:2308.16557

Dakhel et al. — mutation-guided prompt augmentation before agents were fashionable: up to 28% more faulty human-written code snippets detected than baselines, and 17% of bugs caught that both Pynguin and zero/few-shot prompting missed; 93.57% mutation score on its synthetic-bug corpus.

ACH

Production deployment at Meta

FSE 2025 · arXiv:2501.12862 · the B2 evidence reused

Mutants as hypothetical privacy bugs — 10,795 Kotlin classes → 9,095 mutants → 571 privacy-hardening tests on Messenger and WhatsApp, 73% of recommendations accepted by engineers. Mutation-guided generation is not a lab technique.

YATE+ADV

The frontier — repair and co-evolution

arXiv:2507.18316 · arXiv:2602.08146

YATE: repairing failing generated tests beats regenerating them — +21.77% mutants killed. AdverTest (Feb 2026): two agents co-evolve — "M persistently creates new mutants 'hacking' the blind spots of T's current test suite, while T iteratively refines its test cases to 'kill' the challenging mutants produced by M" — improving Defects4J fault detection by 8.56% over the best LLM-based baselines and 63.30% over EvoSuite. The loop is becoming the architecture.

One more source belongs in this section because it inverts the direction: LLMorpheus (Tip, Bell, Schäfer; arXiv:2404.09952) uses the LLM to generate the mutants rather than the tests, and finds it produces mutants "resembling real bugs" that operator-based tools like StrykerJS structurally cannot — a preview of where the tooling is headed, and the mechanism Meta's ACH already uses in production for privacy-shaped faults. The catalogue of §1's simple syntactic operators is the floor of what "injected fault" can mean, not the ceiling.

5Industry at scale: what Google actually does — and doesn't

The strongest independent evidence that mutation testing survives contact with industrial reality comes from Google, in three papers. The 2018 report (Petrović & Ivanković, "State of Mutation Testing at Google", ICSE-SEIP 2018) describes a design that solved the cost problem by refusing to compute the textbook version at all. Mutants are generated only in code review, only on the diff: "Only lines affected by the diff under review that are covered and are not arid are mutated," and "For each line, at most one mutant is generated." Surviving mutants surface as findings in Critique (the code-review tool) via Tricorder, next to the lines they survive on. The "arid node" mechanism — suppressing mutants in logging, memory-reservation, stdout and similar uninteresting code, tuned by developer feedback — raised the share of surfaced mutants rated useful from roughly 20% to 80%. The 2018 evaluation covered about 70,000 diffs and 1.1 million mutants across seven languages; the TSE follow-up ("Practical Mutation Testing at Scale: A View from Google", IEEE TSE 2021) reports the matured system serving ">24,000 developers on more than 1,000 projects."

Two details matter for gate design. First — the correction our fact-check surfaced — Google does not normatively reject mutation score; the papers say something more practical: computing "the absolute mutation score for the codebase … is infeasably expensive" at their scale, and "we were also unable to find a good way to surface it to the engineers in an actionable way." So they surface individual live mutants at the moment of review instead of reporting a number. Second, the longitudinal study (Petrović, Ivanković, Fraser, Just, "Does Mutation Testing Improve Testing Practices?", ICSE 2021, ~15M mutants) found the practice trains humans the same way §4's loop trains agents: "developers using mutation testing write more tests, and actively improve their test suites with high quality tests such that fewer mutants remain." The signal compounds.

The consumption-mode lesson

At-scale deployments consume mutation testing as survivors-in-review — a stream of concrete findings at the moment someone is already looking at the code — not as an org-wide score dashboard. Our bootstrap gate consumes it as a module-level score — a one-time exit criterion on one pilot module. These are not competing philosophies; they are the same instrument at two zoom levels, and the difference is scope: Google cannot afford a codebase score and does not need one; a bootstrap needs exactly one number for exactly one module at exactly one decision point ("is this net real enough to refactor against?"), and at module scope the full run is affordable. Use both modes: score at the phase boundary, survivors-in-review forever after (§8).

6The three taxes: equivalence, pseudo-tested code, flakiness

Tax 1: equivalent mutants. Some mutants change the text but not the behavior; no test can or should kill them, and they pollute the survivor list. The classic measurements: Schuler and Zeller ("Covering and Uncovering Equivalent Mutants", STVR 2013), manually classifying 140 undetected mutations across seven Java programs, found ~45% of undetected mutants were equivalent — and that manual classification costs ~15 minutes per mutant. That cost is the real tax: at scale nobody adjudicates survivors by hand. Two mitigations work. Trivial Compiler Equivalence (Papadakis, Jia, Harman, Le Traon, ICSE 2015) — compile both versions, compare the binaries — removes a surprising share mechanically: "TCE equivalent mutants account for 7.4 and 5.7 percent of all C and Java mutants, while duplicated mutants account for a further 21 percent of all C mutants and 5.4 percent Java mutants, on average." And Meta's ACH attacks the remainder with an LLM equivalence detector — precision/recall 0.79/0.47 out of the box, 0.95/0.96 after tuning (the B2 numbers) — turning a 15-minute human judgment into a cheap, imperfect, good-enough filter. Practical stance for our gate: expect a survivor list with real noise in it; triage survivors by sampling, not exhaustively, and treat "kill rate of non-equivalent mutants" as an estimate with error bars, not an exact number.

Tax 2: pseudo-tested code. Before paying for fine-grained mutants, run the coarsest possible probe. "Extreme mutation" removes an entire method body and reruns the suite; a method that is "covered by the test suite, yet no test case fails when the method body is removed" is pseudo-tested — the concept "coined in 2016, by Niedermayr and colleagues," then studied comprehensively by Vera-Pérez, Danglot, Monperrus and Baudry (EMSE 2019; the Descartes tool, a PIT extension). Their finding across 21 mature Java projects: pseudo-tested methods exist in every single one (the per-project rate varies too much to quote a single number honestly). Extreme mutation is orders of magnitude cheaper than full mutation — one mutant per method instead of several per line — and every pseudo-tested method it finds is a place where coverage is lying to you today. On a brownfield estate this is the first mutation run to do, often before the characterization suite is even finished: it grades the existing tests the client believes in.

Tax 3: flakiness. Mutation analysis assumes a deterministic oracle: test fails on mutant ⇒ mutant killed. A flaky suite breaks the inference in both directions, and the effect is measured: Shi, Bell and Marinov ("Mitigating the Effects of Flaky Tests on Mutation Testing", ISSTA 2019) found that "mutation scores vary by four percentage points on average between repeated executions, and 9% of mutant-test pairs have an unknown status." Four points of noise on a ~70% threshold means a gate decision can flip run to run. This is why B2's flaky-pins trap and its quarantine protocol are a prerequisite of this chapter, not a parallel concern: de-flake (or quarantine) first, then trust the score. On legacy suites — flaky by default, per B1 — running the gate before the quarantine step produces a number that is noise wearing a percent sign.

7Cost engineering and the toolbox

The reflexive objection — "our suite takes 40 minutes; multiplying it by thousands of mutants is absurd" — is correct about the naive design and wrong about the practice. Nobody runs the full matrix. The engineering ladder, cheapest first:

The cost ladder
  • 1 · Extreme mutation first. Descartes-style whole-method removal: one mutant per method, immediate pseudo-tested inventory (§6). Cheap enough for the whole module on day one.
  • 2 · Diff-scoped, in review. The Google design: mutate only changed, covered, non-arid lines; at most one mutant per line; surface survivors as review comments. Cost scales with the diff, not the codebase.
  • 3 · Module-scoped full runs. The gate run: all mutants, but only on the pilot module the B5 heatmap selected — nightly or at the phase boundary, never in the inner loop.
  • 4 · Incremental caching. Only re-examine mutants whose code or covering tests changed: PIT's incremental analysis, StrykerJS --incremental (since v6.2, Sept 2022), Stryker.NET "baseline", Arcmutate's PR-scoped git integration (commercial PIT extension, since 2024).
What never to do
  • org-wide big-bang mutation CI — Google explicitly found the absolute codebase score "infeasably expensive," and you are not better resourced than Google
  • mutation in the agent's inner loop — it belongs at review and phase boundaries; the inner loop keeps the fail-fast ladder from E4 §7
  • gating before the flakiness quarantine (§6, Tax 3)
  • trusting one tool's number against another's — operators differ; compare scores only within one tool and configuration
EcosystemToolStatus & the feature that matters here
JVMPIT / pitestthe reference tool (Henry Coles); incremental analysis reuses prior-run results; mutationThreshold defaults to 0 — no gate unless you set one
JVM (commercial)ArcmutatePIT extension: git-diff/PR-scoped mutation, Kotlin support, cross-build incremental
JVM (extreme)DescartesPIT extension for extreme mutation — the pseudo-tested-methods probe
JS / TSStrykerJSde-facto standard; --incremental; report thresholds default high: 80, low: 60, break: null — i.e. it ships with the build-breaking gate off
.NET / ScalaStryker.NET / Stryker4ssame family; .NET's incremental mode is called "baseline"
Pythonmutmutactively maintained, simple CI integration
Rustcargo-mutantsactive (the older mutagen is dead)
PHPInfectionstandard, active
Gogremlinsthe live option — go-mutesting is largely stale

All tool facts are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify versions and maintenance status before a client meeting. One row of that table carries an argument: no mainstream tool ships a mandatory score gate. Stryker's defaults treat 80 as "high" but break nothing; PIT's threshold defaults to zero. The ~70% bar in §8 is therefore our working heuristic — grounded (between Stryker's low/high bands, achievable per §4's feedback numbers, meaningfully above the noise floor of §6's taxes) but not an industry standard, and we present it as exactly that.

8Designing the gate for our rollout

Everything above compiles into one design, and it is our synthesis — assembled from standard, named components (the Report 4 workflow; Google's diff-scoped consumption; MUTGEN's feedback loop; SpecBench's held-out logic), but not, as a package, an industry standard. Present it to clients with that provenance, the same honesty rule the workbook applies to D1–D5 and every other synthesis.

The gate, precisely
  • Scope: the pilot module the B5 heatmap selected — never the codebase
  • Metric: mutation score = killed / non-equivalent mutants, one tool, one config, TCE-style trivial filtering on; flaky tests quarantined first (§6)
  • Threshold: ~70% — Report 4's working heuristic, not an industry standard (§7); record the chosen bar in an ADR so it is a decision, not folklore
  • Held-out principle: the gate run generates fresh mutants (different seed/operator set) — the survivors the agent trained against (§4) are the practice sheet, not the exam; SpecBench's visible-vs-held-out gap is the measured reason
  • Verdict consumers: pass ⇒ the module enters F2 (scoped writes, E4's "robust L3, selective L4" row); fail ⇒ survivors become the agent's next task list and the module stays F1
Standing rules after the gate
  • diff-scoped mutation stays on in CI as advisory review findings (Mode 1)
  • score re-checked when the module's suite changes materially, not on a calendar
  • a falling score on changed lines is a defect signal — investigate before merging
  • coverage remains a secondary, negative-only signal (§1); it never re-enters as a target
  • Report 4 STOP rule inherited: mutation score < 70% on an F2 module ⇒ back to F1

Why a score gate here when Google runs none? Because the decisions differ. Google's system answers a continuous question — "is this diff adequately tested?" — where a stream of findings is the right interface. The bootstrap answers a one-time, high-stakes question — "may an agent now refactor against this net?" — and one-time, high-stakes decisions need deterministic criteria (E5's advisory-vs-deterministic spine, applied to the rollout itself). The score is crude, taxed by §6 and gameable at the margins; it is also auditable, reproducible, and impossible to satisfy with hollow tests — which makes it the least-bad deterministic answer available, provided it is consumed at module scope where it is affordable and meaningful.

9Anti-patterns — how coverage theater rebuilds itself

The six failure modes
  • 1 · The score becomes a KPI. Roll the number up to a dashboard and Goodhart arrives on schedule: teams (and agents) optimize the number, not the detection. Inozemtseva & Holmes' "should not be used as a quality target" applies to any metric consumed as a target — mutation score included. The gate is a threshold at a boundary, not a graph to climb.
  • 2 · Chasing 100%. §6's taxes make the last decile mostly equivalent-mutant adjudication at ~15 minutes per mutant. Stryker calls 80 "high" for a reason; diminishing returns start well below that on legacy code.
  • 3 · Gating on a flaky suite. ±4 points of run-to-run noise (ISSTA 2019) can flip a 70% verdict. Quarantine first — B2's protocol is the prerequisite.
  • 4 · The practice sheet as the exam. Letting the generator be graded on the same mutant list it trained against converts the gate into a memorization test. Hold out fresh mutants (§8).
  • 5 · Org-wide big-bang. "Infeasably expensive" is Google's phrase for the full-codebase score. Start at module scope; expand by heatmap.
  • 6 · Survivor-triage fatigue. An unfiltered survivor list full of equivalent mutants trains reviewers to bulk-dismiss — the same approve-all decay B2 documented for characterization review. Arid-node-style suppression and TCE filtering are what keep the stream credible (Google's 20%→80% usefulness arc is the existence proof).

10Takeaways for our engagement

Assess (feeds E6)
  • Does anyone know the mutation score of anything — or only coverage?
  • Run Descartes-style extreme mutation on the module the client trusts most: the pseudo-tested inventory is the cheapest possible demonstration of the coverage lie
  • How flaky is the suite? (Gate legitimacy depends on it — §6)
Build (feeds E7 / F1)
  • diff-scoped mutation in CI as advisory survivors-in-review (Mode 1)
  • the module-level ~70% gate at the F1→F2 boundary, fresh mutants, ADR-recorded (Mode 2)
  • survivors wired back to the agent as tasks — the loop is the cheapest test-quality investment measured to date (53%→89.5%)
  • equivalence filtering (TCE + sampling) before any human triages survivors
Three sentences to remember B3 by

Coverage measures whether code ran; mutation score measures whether the tests would notice it being wrong — and fifty years of theory plus the 2024–2026 measurements agree that only the second survives contact with a generator that produces plausible tests for free. Consume the instrument in two modes: survivors-in-review continuously (Google's shape), and a module-level ~70% score — our heuristic, honestly flagged — as the one deterministic exit criterion before write autonomy. Feed survivors back to the agent (the measured 53%→89.5% loop) but grade the gate on mutants it never saw: the practice sheet is not the exam.

11Further reading

DeMillo, Lipton & Sayward — Hints on Test Data Selection
IEEE Computer 11(4), 1978 · with Hamlet, IEEE TSE 1977 as the parallel origin

The founding paper: competent-programmer and coupling stated as observations. Short and readable.

Just et al. — Are Mutants a Valid Substitute for Real Faults?
FSE 2014 · 357 real faults, 230k mutants

The validation: coupling holds for 73% of real faults; mutant detection predicts real-fault detection independently of coverage. Pair with Inozemtseva & Holmes (ICSE 2014) for the anti-coverage half.

The Google trilogy
ICSE-SEIP 2018 · ICSE 2021 (arXiv:2103.07189) · IEEE TSE 2021 (arXiv:2102.11378)

Diff-scoped probabilistic mutation in code review at >24,000-developer scale; arid nodes; the 20%→80% usefulness arc; and the longitudinal finding that the practice trains developers.

MUTGEN + TestGenEval + Haroon et al.
arXiv:2506.02954 (IEEE TSE) · arXiv:2410.00752 · arXiv:2603.23443 (preprint)

The LLM-era evidence file: 100%/4%, 35.2%/18.8%, and the >99% semantic-precision failure. The three numbers that justify the gate.

Meta ACH + MuTAP + AdverTest + LLMorpheus
arXiv:2501.12862 (FSE 2025) · arXiv:2308.16557 (IST 2024) · arXiv:2602.08146 · arXiv:2404.09952

Mutation-guided generation from lab to production, the adversarial co-evolution frontier, and LLM-generated mutants beyond syntactic operators.

Reward-hacking measurements
ImpossibleBench arXiv:2510.20270 · SpecBench arXiv:2605.21384 · Rajan arXiv:2606.16062 (preprints)

Why "the tests pass" is a reward surface: 54.0% cheating under test-spec conflict, oversight collapsing onto the suite, and +14.14pp on hackable tasks.

Tools: Stryker · PIT/Arcmutate · Descartes · mutmut · cargo-mutants
stryker-mutator.io · pitest.org · arcmutate.com · github.com/STAMP-project/pitest-descartes

The point-in-time toolbox (§7): incremental modes, PR-scoped runs, extreme mutation, and the default thresholds that prove no tool ships a mandatory gate.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B3-mutation-testing-gate-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are E4 (the mechanics from first principles, and verification as the autonomy ceiling), B1 (why the gate sits at the end of the bootstrap chain), B2 (the characterization net this gate grades), B4/B5 (the archaeology that feeds the net and the heatmap that scopes the module), E5 (the advisory-vs-deterministic spine the two modes instantiate) and E7 (the same measurement honesty applied to pilot metrics). The two-mode gate, the held-out-mutants principle and the ~70% threshold are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — the components (the 1978 theory, Google's deployment, MUTGEN's loop) are standard and named. Tool names, versions and benchmark numbers are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B3, module M5 stands at three of six; B4 (agent archaeology) is the natural next chapter.

B4 · DEEP DIVE

Agent Archaeology: Reverse-Engineering Legacy

0Why it matters

B1 gave the safe ordering — read-only comprehension first, writes last — and B2/B3 built and graded the safety net. This chapter is the first phase done properly: what an agent produces when you point it at a legacy estate with no write access at all. The scenario is the one CROZ's mainframe practice states plainly: "You inherit a 10,000-line COBOL program with zero comments, written by someone who retired in 2005." Nobody left on the team can grade an explanation from memory, the documentation eroded decades ago, and the first profitable move is not to change anything but to understand — architecture maps, dependency graphs, convention inventories, ADR reconstruction, onboarding material. The risk ledger is the most asymmetric in agent adoption: a read-only mistake is a wrong sentence a human reads, not a wrong change a system runs. But that sentence is also the trap. Generated explanations have no compiler; a plausible-but-wrong account of legacy behaviour looks authoritative, gets cited, and quietly becomes load-bearing. So this chapter holds one line: the agent reads faster than any human and lies more fluently than any bad consultant — everything it produces ships as a labeled hypothesis, anchored in deterministic fact, until a person who can grade it promotes it.

What this chapter gives you
  • the comprehension bottleneck, measured: why "understanding" is where the time — and therefore the safe ROI — actually is (§1)
  • the vendor evidence file, honestly weighed: ThoughtWorks CodeConcise, IBM at NOSI, AWS Transform — real numbers with their hedges attached (§2)
  • the independent evidence: what peer-reviewed studies say LLMs can and cannot explain about real systems (§3)
  • the failure mode that has no compiler: plausible-but-wrong documentation, with named incidents (§4)
  • the validation machinery: hypothesis labels, deterministic anchoring, expert sampling, executable cross-checks (§5 — our synthesis, flagged)
  • the F0 workflow: deliverables, prompts that behave like an archaeologist instead of a tourist, and the exit criteria that hand off to B2 and B5 (§6)
  • the anti-patterns that turn comprehension into confident fiction at scale (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • B1 — why this phase is first: the paradox and the dependency chain
  • B2 — the characterization net this comprehension feeds (entry points, representative inputs)
  • B3 — the mutation gate that grades the net; the same honesty discipline applied to tests
  • B5 — the heatmap: archaeology outputs become pilot-selection inputs
  • I1/I3 — the grey zone: derived artifacts until deliberately promoted, and the freshness policy that keeps them true
  • E4/E5 — verification-first and deterministic-vs-advisory: docs are the advisory layer that needs a deterministic spine
  • D7 — design archaeology: the same read-first move on the visual layer

1The comprehension bottleneck, measured

The claim that developers spend most of their time understanding code rather than writing it predates LLMs by decades, and for once the folklore has a measured spine. The strongest version is Xia et al.'s field study (IEEE TSE 44(10), 2018): not a survey but interaction data collected across all applications via their ActivitySpace framework — 78 professional developers, 7 real projects, 3,148 working hours. Verbatim from the abstract: "on average developers spend ∼58% of their time on program comprehension activities." Two details keep the number honest: navigation is counted separately (≈24%), not folded in; and senior developers spend significantly less of their time on comprehension than juniors — comprehension is a skill tax that experience partially pays down, which is precisely why an estate whose seniors have left is so expensive. The in-IDE predecessor (Minelli, Mocci & Lanza, ICPC 2015) put comprehension at roughly 70% of development time inside the IDE — the discrepancy with 58% is methodological (IDE-only with inferred gaps vs cross-application measurement), and citing both with their methods is worth more than averaging them.

The deep roots matter for the client pitch, because they show this is not an AI-era rationalization. Corbi's IBM Systems Journal paper ("Program understanding: Challenge for the 1990s", 28(2), 1989) already states the whole thesis in pre-web language: "Programmers have become part historian, part detective, and part clairvoyant." It reports Fjeldstad & Hamlen's maintenance study — programmers studied the original program "about three-and-a-half times as long as they studied the documentation," and just as long as they spent implementing the enhancement — and the FAA National Airspace System lesson that "the best way out is to relearn the old software, relying primarily on the source code," because the documentation had eroded past trust. Glass's 2001 IEEE Software fact list (the correct home of the widely misattributed "40–80%, average 60%, of software cost is maintenance") closes the loop: most money is spent on existing systems, and most of that spending is comprehension. Onboarding is the same cost concentrated: Sim & Holt (ICSE 1998) report the famous "six to twelve months to full productivity" — worth citing precisely, as one team's estimate inside a four-person case study — and Rastogi et al.'s Microsoft study (ESEM 2015, version-control data plus 411 surveyed new hires) found that poor documentation measurably delayed the first check-in (267 of 411 respondents; p<0.001), while its absolute ramp-up months are deliberately obscured for confidentiality — any blog quoting exact months from it is inventing them.

Why this is the agent-shaped hole

Every later phase of the bootstrap fights the agent's weaknesses — writing needs gates (E4), tests need grading (B3), permissions need enforcement (I2). Phase 0 is the only phase that rides the agent's strength: ingesting millions of tokens of code and restating their structure is exactly the cheap part, and its failure cost is a human's reading time, not a production incident. The economics argue the same way: if roughly half of engineering time is comprehension and legacy estates carry the worst comprehension debt, then a tool that even modestly compresses comprehension attacks the largest line item — with the smallest blast radius in the entire adoption roadmap. That is the whole case for starting here; the rest of the chapter is about not squandering it.

2The vendor evidence file, honestly weighed

The three flagship comprehension stories are all vendor or consultancy published. They are still worth knowing in detail — clients will quote them — provided each number keeps its hedge attached. ThoughtWorks CodeConcise is the richest. The primary technical account (Ferri, Coggrave & Sheth on martinfowler.com, September 2024) describes parsing the estate into a forest of ASTs stored in a Neo4j knowledge graph, enriching it with LLM-generated explanations at multiple depths, and retrieving through graph-traversal-augmented RAG — note the correction to our earlier notes: the system is explicitly a RAG system, with the graph making retrieval structural rather than replacing it. The engagement: an anonymized automotive manufacturer's backbone system, "15 million lines of COBOL" plus an IDMS database, built over three decades, with a hard licensing deadline. The numbers, with their tenses: reverse-engineering lead time per 10,000-line module went from 6 weeks to ~2 — stated as "we estimated" post-PoC in 2024 and as an achieved fact in the 2025 client story; the "60,000 person-days" headline and the technical article's "240 FTE-years" are the same projection in two units ("potential", "projected", "in the order of" — every primary instance hedged), extrapolated from a PoC on part of the estate, not a measured outcome; and the "fix in three hours that took three weeks" anecdote is from a different client, "in the best cases", with a ThoughtWorks pair driving the tool. The most load-bearing detail is one their own marketing buries: the client's earlier genAI reverse-engineering outputs were judged by technical SMEs to be only 60% accurate — which is why SME review was the bottleneck, and is the vendor's own inadvertent documentation of §4's failure mode. Point-in-time: CodeConcise is an internal accelerator, now folded into ThoughtWorks' "AI/works" platform — it was never a product you can buy.

IBM watsonx Code Assistant for Z at NOSI is the cleanest verbatim: "up to a 79% reduction in the time required for their Developers to understand their existing complex applications, in some cases reducing the time from 24 to approximately 5 hours" (IBM case study, 2025). Keep the wrapper: "up to", "in some cases", a two-application COBOL pilot at Egypt's National Organization for Social Insurance, published by IBM — and the case study's real punchline is mainframe retention (NOSI cancelled an off-platform migration and bought a z16), so the comprehension number sits inside a sales narrative. The product context moved fast: WCA for Z was superseded by "Project Bob" (unveiled October 2025, converging the Z and i assistants), GA March 2026, global availability announced 28 April 2026, with existing WCA clients "fully supported" on an adoption path — and Bob's heavy lifting is reportedly multi-model (Anthropic Claude in the routing mix), a quiet admission that frontier models overtook the in-house Granite stack. IBM's Bob-era marketing already offers a cautionary exhibit: one 2026 press release quotes APIS IT claiming "100% accuracy in documenting legacy JCL/PL/I systems" — a claim that should be read next to §3's measured precision figures and treated as a red flag, not a data point.

AWS Transform rounds out the trio. The durable usage figure is cumulative and analysis-scoped: "an estimated 1.1 billion lines of code" analyzed and "more than 810,000 hours of manual effort" saved (AWS, December 2025 — vendor-published, unaudited; the "~7 billion lines per year" figure circulating in secondary blogs pins to nothing and is dropped). The on-stage customer stories at re:Invent 2025 are more instructive than the aggregates. Western Union: a 35-year-old money-order platform, "just over 2.5 million" lines of COBOL, "what used to take over three months can now be achieved in six weeks from discovery to testing" — an ongoing pilot, partner-built, not a production migration. Unum is the story this chapter would have invented if it didn't exist: 1.5 million lines documented and business-rule-extracted "in a few hours" — and then the customer's own AVP states the thesis unprompted: "It is essential to understand what you are trying to modernize. Because if you cannot understand it, you cannot verify whether it is actually performing the same function after modernization." AWS's own architecture now explicitly mixes deterministic analysis with LLM agents, and its guidance is human-in-the-loop validation "continuously validated by domain experts" with testing consuming over half of project duration — the vendor's pipeline encodes §5's machinery. For budgeting, the only honest per-unit price found anywhere is GitHub/Microsoft's: AI-assisted legacy analysis runs "usually $2–5 per 1,000 lines analyzed" (github.blog, October 2025 — their framework is open-sourced as Azure-Samples/Legacy-Modernization-Agents, and their field guide insists human experts review the generated analysis).

How to read these numbers in front of a client

Four disciplines. Separate projection from measurement — 60,000 person-days is an extrapolation; 24→5 hours is "up to" and "in some cases". Ask what the unit is — 6 weeks is per-10k-module lead time including SME review, not effort-hours; "six weeks" at Western Union ends at testing, not production. Discount self-report — the METR RCT (B1) showed experienced developers finishing 19% slower while believing themselves 20% faster; any case study whose evidence is "developers said it saved time" inherits that gap. Flag the stage — customer quotes delivered from the vendor's own keynote are marketing even when the customer is real. None of this makes the stories useless: converging vendor investment plus §1's measured economics is decent evidence that comprehension is where the value concentrates. It makes them inadmissible as proof of how much.

3What the independent evidence says

The independent practitioner voice closest to the metal is CROZ, a Croatian consultancy that works with — but is not — IBM, and whose review of watsonx Code Assistant for Z (November 2025) is the most quotable honest assessment in the field: the Understand phase "is where I see the highest potential"; the tool should be used "as a documentation and understanding tool, not as an automatic translation engine"; and the explicit anti-pattern is "automated COBOL-to-Java conversion expecting production-ready output without refinement" — generated Java that is functionally plausible but "thinks in COBOL". Their verdict, "cautiously optimistic," is exactly the posture this chapter recommends, from a firm that sells modernization services and loses nothing by hyping. GitHub's own field guide (October 2025) is blunter from inside a vendor: "Everyone who's currently promising you, 'hey, I can solve all your mainframe problems with just one click' is lying to you."

The peer-reviewed measurements are sobering in a specific, usable way. Soliman & Keim (ICSA 2025) had 14 engineers pose 236 architecture questions about Apache HDFS to GPT-3.5 and graded answers against ground truth built from 46 architectural documents: average recall 0.594 but precision 0.395 — read that again as: more than half of what the model states about a real system's architecture is noise — with engineers rating quality and trustworthiness only "moderate", and the authors concluding LLMs are "not yet reliable enough to replace expert analysis." Siala & Lano (Frontiers in Computer Science, July 2025) compared LLM reverse engineering against deterministic model-driven reverse engineering on 20 programs: the untrained GPT-4 invented spurious constraints — invariants "more restrictive than those in the source code" — at a frequency of 0.43 (Python) and 0.67 (Java) per specification, where the deterministic tool produced zero (Java; 0.14 on Python). Their sentence belongs in every proposal: "It is difficult to automatically detect such errors, and hence correction of the output would need to involve human expertise." Amalfitano et al. (ECSA 2025) add the shape of the ceiling across four frontier models: high-level architectural styles recover passably, fine-grained patterns and semantic consistency do not. And the mutation-based stress test (arXiv:2504.04372; 10 models, 750,013 fault-localization tasks) shows the brittleness underneath the fluency: semantics-preserving changes to the code break previously correct model reasoning in 78% of cases — comprehension anchored in surface features, not semantics.

Honesty requires the other signs too. In education settings, GPT-3.5's code explanations were rated more accurate than students' (Leinonen et al., ITiCSE 2023); in a controlled IDE study, promptless LLM comprehension help beat web search for task completion (Nam et al., ICSE 2024, n=32 — with benefits differing sharply between students and professionals, which is the expert-sampling caveat in embryo); DORA 2024 modeled +25% AI adoption as associated with higher self-reported documentation quality (+7.5%); and RepoAgent's repository-level generated docs were blind-preferred over human-written ones (70%/91.33% on two open-source repos — a preference rate, not a correctness measure; citing it as accuracy is a trap). The measured error rates that do exist are dated and off-domain: Sarsa et al. (ICER 2022) found 67.2% of Codex's line-by-line explanations correct — a 2022 model on novice code. The gap is the finding: no peer-reviewed study measures the accuracy of LLM-generated documentation on proprietary legacy estates with expert verification. Any sentence of the form "generated docs on legacy code are X% wrong" is currently unpinnable; the honest claim is §4's mechanism plus §3's precision numbers from adjacent tasks — and it is why the workbook's own estate-level numbers must come from the client's sampling, not from a brochure.

4The failure mode: plausible but wrong

Simon Willison's much-quoted line needs its exact wording, because the nuance is the chapter. The post's title says hallucinations in code are "the least dangerous form of LLM mistakes"; the body says they are "the least harmful hallucinations you can encounter from a model" — and the argument is verifier asymmetry, not safety: "With code you get a powerful form of fact checking for free. Run the code, see if it works." The same post hands this chapter its counter-quote twice over: prose hallucinations demand "a critical eye, strong intuitions and well developed fact checking skills", and generated code itself "will usually look fantastic: good variable names, convincing comments, clear type annotations… This can lull you into a false sense of security." Chris Krycho's reply (March 2025) sharpens the point from the practitioner side: if the model gets "this most basic, statically knowable thing wrong, they will certainly get other, subtler things wrong as well" — the errors that matter are the ones no static check catches. Documentation of a legacy estate is precisely that category: prose about behaviour, authored fluently, with no compiler and — on an estate whose authors retired in 2005 — no resident oracle either.

The freshest field report reads like a controlled experiment for this chapter. "The Archaeologist's Copilot" (Nik Malykhin, martinfowler.com, July 2026) documents restoring a 2005-era Java 1.5/Ant "big ball of mud" with LLM help. The early, conventionally-prompted attempts produced exactly our failure mode: asked to modernize the build, the model emitted a plausible modern build.gradle for a project whose real structure was Ant — "The result was a lie — and a structural lie at that… It was describing the reality it wanted to see, not the one that actually existed." Fowler's own blurb compresses it: "His early use of LLMs gave plausible answers that did not hold up in the codebase." Malykhin's diagnosis is the operational one: "AI defaults to optimism. When I ask 'How do I run this?', it assumes I can run it. In a restoration mission, optimism is fatal." And his "lying tests" discovery is the failure mode one level down: a catch (Exception e) { e.printStackTrace(); } in main() means the process exits 0 and the modern pipeline reports a green pass on a system that did nothing — generated comprehension that looks verified and is not.

Real damage from plausible generated content is documented, though the cleanest incidents sit one domain over. Cursor's AI support bot (April 2025) confidently invented a multi-device login policy; users complained and some cancelled before a cofounder publicly corrected it — a generated claim, believed because it was fluent and official-looking, with no verifier in the loop. Inside software documentation proper, the evidence is structural rather than incident-based: Uddin & Robillard's study of 323 IBM professionals (IEEE Software 2015) found incorrectness of content among the severest documentation problems — rare but "blocker"-grade, driving developers to abandon the documented API entirely; Wen et al. (ICPC 2019) measured why staleness is the default, with only ~13–20% of code changes triggering any comment update; and Tan et al.'s iComment (SOSP 2007) showed wrong comments are not harmless, detecting 60 comment-code inconsistencies and 33 real bugs hiding behind them in Linux, Mozilla, Wine and Apache. The compound lesson: humans already fail to keep documentation true; a generator that produces authoritative prose at industrial scale without a verification loop does not close that gap — it automates it.

The asymmetry to internalize

On greenfield work the asymmetry bites less, because the author of the code is present to smell a wrong explanation. On a legacy estate the verifier hierarchy collapses: the code is the only trustworthy document (Corbi, 1989), the tests that could grade claims about it do not exist yet (they are B2's job), and the people who could grade from memory are gone. That is why the discipline of §5 is not optional polish on a productivity win — it is the difference between an archaeology program and a fiction generator with excellent typography.

5The validation machinery

Four mechanisms, layered. None of them is exotic; the assembly is ours, and §5's framing — every generated claim ships as a hypothesis with a confidence label until promoted — should be flagged exactly like D1–D5: we searched for an established practice of confidence-labeled generated documentation and found none (the nearest shipping analog, Swimm's CI status, labels staleness against code, not truthfulness of prose). The confidence label is not decoration; it is what makes the artifact reviewable. "Derived, unverified" can be read with profit; "documentation" cannot be told apart from truth by a newcomer.

Anchoring in deterministic fact. Every claim that can be derived by a parser instead of a language model should be: the dependency graph, the module map, the call structure, the build topology. This is the technique underneath the credible tools — aider's repo map (tree-sitter parses the repo, a graph-ranking algorithm budgets it into tokens — the map is parser-derived, the LLM only reads it), RepoAgent's AST-parsed, topologically-ordered doc generation, CodeConcise's Neo4j graph, and AWS's stated "combination of deterministic approaches for understanding, then combining that with AI." The LLM's role is interpretation on top of extracted structure, never extraction by recall. (A point-in-time caution against overclaiming industry convergence: GitHub's stack-graphs — the flagship deterministic name-binding graph — is now an archived, unmaintained repo.) Anchoring gives the reviewer a cheap oracle: a claim that disagrees with the graph is wrong deterministically; a claim that agrees is still a hypothesis, but a checkable one.

Expert sampling. Full review of generated volume is impossible by construction — generation is free, attention is not — so review must be sampled where it pays: critical modules, high-confidence-but-load-bearing claims, and everything destined for promotion. The only principled sampling machinery we found lives one field over (HADAS, arXiv:2404.01588: diversity-aware selection of likely hallucinations for human annotation) — borrow the idea, cite the provenance. Soliman & Keim's 0.395 precision is the quantitative argument that sampling is not optional; the CodeConcise 60%-accuracy anecdote is the vendor's own confirmation. Remember Nam et al.'s finding that benefits and failure modes differ between juniors and professionals: the sampler must be the estate's senior, which is also the political move — F0's review loop is where a defensive team learns the agent's outputs on questions they can grade (B1).

Executable cross-checks. Where a claim is checkable, check it mechanically and continuously. The mature school is docs-as-tests: Spring REST Docs has guaranteed since 2014 that "if a snippet is incorrect, the test that produces it fails"; Doc Detective and Manny Silva's "Docs as Tests" methodology run documentation procedures against the real product (note: a single-practitioner ecosystem — methodology, tool, book and case studies all trace to Silva; the Kong 84%→91% chatbot-accuracy vignette is his number, practitioner-sourced); Schemathesis derives property-based tests from the OpenAPI spec to catch doc-implementation drift. For architecture prose the strongest cross-check is the one this workbook builds anyway: B2's characterization net. "The doc claims module X recomputes Y nightly" is a hypothesis until a golden-master test pins the behaviour; every generated claim that survives contact with a pinned test earns promotion with evidence, not with prose review alone. That is the concrete meaning of the B2 handshake: archaeology proposes, the net disposes.

Flagged as our synthesis

The hypothesis-with-confidence label, the sampling protocol, and the promote-through-verification path are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis. The components are standard and named — parser-derived anchors (aider, RepoAgent), executable docs (Spring REST Docs, Doc Detective, Schemathesis), the derived-until-promoted grey zone (I1) and its freshness policy (I3) — but no external source assembles them into a validation pipeline for generated legacy documentation; our discovery pass found the territory genuinely unclaimed. The field is converging independently: AgenticAKM (arXiv:2602.04445, February 2026) generates ADRs from repositories with a dedicated validation agent in the loop — cite that as convergence, present the pipeline as ours.

6The F0 archaeology workflow

The workflow assembles into a pipeline with one invariant: the parser extracts, the model interprets, the human promotes.

DeliverableAnchored inValidated byConsumed by
Architecture & module mapdependency graph, build topology (parser-derived)graph cross-check + senior samplingB5 heatmap (isolability, seams); onboarding
Convention inventoryrepo-wide pattern extractionsampling; lint rules where codifiableAGENTS.md draft (B1: extracted, not decreed)
ADR reconstructiongit history + ticket archive + code seamsoriginal authors where alive; else stays hypothesistwo-door triage (B2); load-bearing-bug register
Onboarding walkthroughthe map + entry pointsthe new hire test: a real newcomer runs itteam trust; Rastogi's first-check-in metric
Entry points & representative inputscall graph, runtime tracesexecution in the time-capsule envB2 — the generator's raw material
Pilot-candidate shortlistthe map + churn data from gitF0 exit reviewB5 — heatmap scoring

Three operational lessons from the field reports belong in the runbook. Prompt as an archaeologist, not a tourist. Malykhin's "Tourist Prompt" — the naive "how do I run this?" — invites the optimism that fabricates a Maven layout; his "Archaeologist Prompt" assigns a forensic posture ("perform a Forensic Code Audit"; senior-legacy-architect persona) and, critically, demands evidence: claims must cite files and lines. An explanation that must point at its exhibit cannot drift as far from the codebase — and the citations are machine-checkable, which is anchoring at the prompt layer. Rebuild the era before interpreting it. The "time capsule" — a container with the period toolchain (Java 6 + Ant, zero code changes) — converts untestable prose about behaviour into executable questions, and flushes out the lying tests before the pipeline trusts any green. Scan for secrets before the agent reads anything at scale (gitleaks over full history — B6 owns the details): comprehension tooling ingests everything, and legacy estates are where the live 2009 passwords live.

The exit criteria are deterministic and short: every shipped claim carries a confidence label; expert sampling on the critical modules is signed off by the estate's seniors; the pilot-candidate shortlist is scored and handed to B5; and the entry-points package is accepted by B2 as generation input. What F0 deliberately does not produce: fixes, refactors, "modernized" build files, or documentation declared final. The artifact status stays I1's grey zone — derived until deliberately promoted — and promotion is a PR with a named human approver, never a bulk commit of generated prose.

7Anti-patterns — how archaeology becomes fiction at scale

The six failure modes
  • 1 · Shipping hypotheses as documentation. The label is the artifact; a generated map committed without its confidence status is §4 with a head start. First wrong decision taken on it — a refactor scoped by a fictional module boundary — costs more than the entire F0 saved.
  • 2 · Big-bang documentation. Generating prose for 15M lines because generation is free. Review bandwidth is the binding constraint — CodeConcise's own story has SME review as the bottleneck at 60% accuracy. Document what the heatmap and the team will actually consume; the rest stays derived-on-demand.
  • 3 · Tourist prompting. Optimistic, context-free questions get optimistic, context-free answers — the fabricated build.gradle. Forensic posture plus mandatory file-and-line citations, or don't bother.
  • 4 · Vendor-metric laundering. "79% faster comprehension" migrating from an IBM case study into your business case without its "up to / in some cases / two-app pilot / vendor-published" wrapper — or APIS IT's "100% accuracy" quoted straight. The METR gap (19% slower while feeling 20% faster) disciplines every self-reported saving.
  • 5 · Transformation-first. Skipping understanding for autonomous COBOL→Java because the keynote showed it. CROZ's verdict stands: output that "thinks in COBOL", needing refinement; Unum's AVP supplies the reason — you cannot verify equivalence against a system you do not understand. Comprehension is the prerequisite, not the optional first module.
  • 6 · Phase-0-forever. The mirror failure (B1's WO4): archaeology as eternal analysis paralysis, docs accreting, no net, no gate, no writes. F0 is time-boxed with deterministic exit criteria (§6) precisely because comprehension is comfortable — and comfort is not the engagement.

8Takeaways for our engagement

Assess (feeds B5/E7)
  • Who can still grade an explanation of the critical modules from memory? (If the answer is "nobody", every generated doc is unverifiable by review alone — budget for executable checks.)
  • What deterministic extraction already exists — build graphs, static analysis, a repo map? Anchoring starts there, not with a prompt.
  • What does the estate's documentation drift look like — when did a human last update it, and would anyone notice a wrong paragraph? (Wen's 13–20% co-evolution says: assume stale.)
Build (F0, read-only)
  • Deterministic layer first: dependency graph + repo map by parser, secrets scan before the agent ingests history.
  • Hypothesis register with confidence labels as the single output format — promotion only via PR with a named approver (I1/I2).
  • Archaeologist prompts: forensic audit framing, mandatory file-and-line citations; time-capsule environment for behavioural questions.
  • Exit package: validated entry points → B2; scored pilot candidates → B5; sampling sign-off by the estate's seniors.
Three sentences to remember B4 by

Comprehension is where the measured time goes (58% of it, field-measured), where the safest ROI lives, and where the agent's strength — reading at scale — meets the estate's deepest debt; vendor stories (CodeConcise's two-thirds, NOSI's 24→5 hours) support the direction with every hedge attached, never the magnitude. The failure mode has no compiler: generated explanations are fluent, authoritative and — on the independent measurements — roughly half noise at architecture depth, so every claim ships as a labeled hypothesis anchored in parser-derived fact, sampled by the seniors who can grade it, and cross-checked against the characterization net before promotion. Do F0 as an archaeologist (evidence-citing, era-faithful, read-only) and it feeds B2's net and B5's heatmap; do it as a tourist and you have industrialized the production of confident fiction — which is worse than the documentation debt you started with.

9Further reading

Ferri, Coggrave & Sheth — Legacy Modernization meets GenAI
martinfowler.com, Sep 2024 · ThoughtWorks Perspectives Ed. 33 · client story (2025)

The CodeConcise primary sources: AST→Neo4j graph-augmented RAG, the 6→2-week per-module estimate before marketing hardened it, and the 60,000 person-days / 240 FTE-years projection with hedges intact.

IBM NOSI case study + the Bob timeline
ibm.com case study (2025) · IBM newsroom, Apr 2026 · IT Jungle, Oct 2025 & Mar 2026

The verbatim 79% / 24→5-hours claim in its two-app-pilot wrapper, and WCA for Z's supersession by IBM Bob (GA Mar 2026) — cite past-tense, point-in-time.

CROZ — watsonx Code Assistant for Z: Real-World Review
croz.net, Nov 2025 · with Mudrovcic's 2023 predecessor

The independent practitioner voice: Understand yes, automatic translation no; "Java that thinks in COBOL"; cautiously optimistic.

Malykhin — The Archaeologist's Copilot
martinfowler.com, Jul 2026 · repo: github.com/nikmalykhin/java-blobstore

The field report this chapter is built around: structural lies, the optimism trap, Tourist vs Archaeologist prompts, the time capsule, lying tests.

Willison + Krycho — hallucinations in code vs prose
simonwillison.net, Mar 2025 · v5.chriskrycho.com, Mar 2025

The verifier-asymmetry argument with exact wording (title "least dangerous", body "least harmful") and the practitioner sharpening: the subtler errors are the ones no static check catches.

Comprehension economics: Xia · Minelli · Corbi · Glass
IEEE TSE 44(10) 2018 · ICPC 2015 · IBM Systems Journal 28(2) 1989 · IEEE Software 2001

The measured spine: ~58% field-measured, ~70% in-IDE, "part historian, part detective, and part clairvoyant", and the correct home of the 40–80% maintenance factoid.

The independent accuracy file: Soliman & Keim · Siala & Lano · arXiv:2504.04372
ICSA 2025 · Frontiers in CS, Jul 2025 · 750k-task stress test

0.395 architecture-answer precision, spurious constraints no automatic check catches, and 78% brittle comprehension — why labels and sampling are not optional. Counterweights: Leinonen (ITiCSE 2023), Nam et al. (ICSE 2024), DORA 2024.

Validation machinery: Spring REST Docs · Doc Detective · Schemathesis · AgenticAKM
docs.spring.io · docsastests.com · schemathesis.readthedocs.io · arXiv:2602.04445

Executable docs from 2014, the docs-as-tests school (single-practitioner, flagged), contract-test drift detection, and the field's independent convergence on validation-in-the-loop ADR generation.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B4-agent-archaeology-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are B1 (the chain this phase opens), B2 (the net archaeology feeds — entry points and representative inputs), B3 (the gate that grades the net), B5 (the heatmap that consumes the shortlist), I1/I3 (the grey zone and its freshness policy) and E4/E5 (the verification spine underneath every advisory artifact). The hypothesis-with-confidence labels, the sampling protocol, the promote-through-verification path and the F0 workflow are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — the components (parser-derived anchors, docs-as-tests, the vendor pipelines) are standard and named. Tool names, product names (IBM Bob, AWS Transform, AI/works), versions and every benchmark number are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B4, module M5 stands at four of six; B5 (strangler fig, the heatmap and the first battlefield) is the natural next chapter.

B5 · DEEP DIVE

Strangler fig, the heatmap and the first battlefield

0Why it matters

B1 set the safe ordering, B2 built the characterization net, B3 graded it, and B4 mapped the estate. Sooner or later the engagement has to answer the question every client actually asks: where do we start changing things? Pick wrong and the program dies in its first quarter — a failed pilot on a critical module poisons agent adoption for years; pick right and each strangled slice funds the next. The pattern language for doing this deliberately is old and battle-tested: the strangler fig (Fowler, 2004), seams (Feathers, 2004), the anti-corruption layer (Evans, 2003) and a hotspot heatmap mined from version-control history (Tornhill, 2015). What is new in 2026 is the economics around them: agents make the map cheap, make drive-by fixes seductive, and make the discipline of picking the battlefield more load-bearing, not less. This chapter holds one line: you do not modernize a legacy estate, you strangle it slice by slice — and the heatmap, not enthusiasm, decides which slice is first.

What this chapter gives you
  • the rewrite question, honestly framed — Spolsky, Brooks, the Basecamp counterexample, and why no credible failure-rate number exists (§1)
  • the strangler fig pattern with its real origin story, its mechanics, and the modern legacy-displacement framing (§2)
  • the plumbing toolbox that makes strangling possible: seams, anti-corruption layers, branch by abstraction, expand/contract, dark launching, feature flags (§3)
  • the heatmap — churn × complexity from git history — with the evidence weighed and the folklore numbers corrected (§4)
  • a scored decision model for the first battlefield, including the one exclusionary criterion (§5 — our synthesis, flagged)
  • the two traps that survive every methodology: Hyrum's Law (§6) and god classes versus the agent's context budget (§7)
  • the hard part everyone postpones: the database (§8); and what agents do and do not reprice in 2026 (§9)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • B1 — the ordering this chapter operationalizes: read first, writes last, risk grows with phase
  • B2 — the characterization net that pins behaviour before any slice moves
  • B3 — the mutation gate that keeps the net honest as slices grow
  • B4 — the archaeology that produces the map, the seams, and the pilot shortlist this chapter scores
  • B6 — the F0–F3 roadmap the pilot slots into, with metrics and STOP criteria
  • I1/I2 — artifact status and write permissions while old and new coexist
  • D7 — design archaeology: the same strangle-and-map logic on the visual layer

1The rewrite question, honestly framed

Every brownfield engagement starts with someone proposing the big-bang rewrite, so the case against it should be stated precisely. The canonical text is Joel Spolsky's Things You Should Never Do, Part I (6 April 2000), written the week Netscape 6 shipped its first beta: "They did it by making the single worst strategic mistake that any software company can make: They decided to rewrite the code from scratch." His numbers are timeline facts, not estimates — version 4.0 shipped in mid-1997, the rewrite delivered three years later, and "during this time, Netscape sat by, helplessly, as their market share plummeted… You are giving a gift of two or three years to your competitors." Brooks named the psychological mechanism a generation earlier (The Mythical Man-Month, 1975, ch. 5): the second system is "the most dangerous system a man ever designs" — the rewrite becomes the dumping ground for every deferred wish, and scope metastasizes exactly when the team has stopped shipping.

Honesty then demands three corrections to the folklore. First, there is no credible failure-rate number. The "X% of rewrites fail" figures circulating in vendor decks trace back to the Standish CHAOS reports, whose figures were dismantled by Eveleens & Verhoef (The Rise and Fall of the Chaos Report Figures, IEEE Software 27(1), 2010: small, self-selected samples, unclear definitions, irreproducible results) and by Jørgensen & Moløkken-Østvold (IST 48(4), 2006). The evidence base for "never do a big-bang rewrite" is case studies and expert judgment — strong, but print it as such and refuse to quote a percentage. Second, the famous counterexample is not actually a counterexample. DHH's REWRITE! talk (Business of Software, 2015) describes Basecamp 2 and 3 as from-scratch rewrites — but the old versions were never sunset ("keep using them until the end of the internet!"), which makes each rewrite a parallel new product coexisting with the legacy one: strangler economics wearing a rewrite's clothes. The sharpest version of the argument is his: the true worst strategic mistake is forced migration, because it makes every existing user re-evaluate you. FreshBooks' undercover "BillSpring" rewrite (documented in Herb Caudill's six-case survey Rewrite, refactor, or reinvent?, 2019) shows a deliberate rewrite can work — as a new product with an MVP deadline, not as in-place replacement. Third, "never rewrite" has its own failure mode. The same survey covers Spolsky's own FogBugz: two decades of never rewriting produced Wasabi, a proprietary in-house language, and a technical-debt bill that came due around 2010. The axis that actually predicts disaster is not rewrite-vs-refactor; it is risk concentration — one irreversible cutover versus many reversible slices. The strangler fig is the pattern that keeps every step reversible.

2The strangler fig pattern

The pattern's origin is more specific than the folklore. In June 2004 Martin Fowler published a short bliki post, originally titled StranglerApplication, after a trip with his wife Cindy to the Queensland rain forests, whose strangler vines "seed in the upper branches of a fig tree and gradually work their way down the tree until they root in the soil." The software proposal: "gradually create a new system around the edges of the old, letting it grow slowly over several years until the old system is strangled." Two attribution details keep the story honest: Fowler explicitly credits his colleague Chris Stevenson, whose team had just published the approach at XP 2004 (Stevenson & Pols, An Agile Approach to a Legacy System) — so the pattern was named from a working case study, not invented at a whiteboard; and the current page is a later, expanded rewrite — retitled Strangler Fig over, in Fowler's footnote, worries about "connotations of violence", with the deadpan punchline that "the new title strangled the old one." The expanded version folds the pattern into a four-activity modernization framework (understand the outcomes, break the problem up, deliver the parts, change the organization — credited to Ian Cartwright, Rob Horn and James Lewis) and a full Patterns of Legacy Displacement article series. Fowler's own summary of why the pattern matters is the sentence to quote in steering committees: the most important reason to consider a strangler is reduced risk — each increment ships, is verified, and can be abandoned without having bet the estate.

The mechanics are simple to state and easy to get wrong. An interception layer — an HTTP reverse proxy, a message router, sometimes just a facade class — sits in front of the legacy system. New and migrated functionality is built in the new system; the facade routes each call to whichever side owns it. Slice by slice the new system grows around the old, until the monolith has no traffic left and can be decommissioned — and then the facade itself, Microsoft's architecture guidance pointedly warns, must not become "a single point of failure or a performance bottleneck", nor a permanent fixture unless you deliberately keep it as an adapter for legacy clients. Fowler's 2004 post closes with the sentence every modernization program should pin above its desk: "all we are doing is writing tomorrow's legacy software today" — the strangler is not a way to reach a final architecture, it is a way to keep migrating continuously.

Why this pattern fits agents specifically

The strangler's unit of work — one slice, routed, verified against pinned behaviour, reversible — is exactly the unit of work an agent can be trusted with under B2's net and B3's gate. Big-bang rewrites fail with humans partly because verification is deferred to the end; an agent-accelerated big-bang defers the same verification at ten times the code-production speed. The pattern is not made obsolete by agents; it is the harness that makes their speed usable on an estate that cannot be allowed to break.

3Seams and the plumbing toolbox

The strangler fig says where the new system grows; Michael Feathers supplies the vocabulary for where you can cut. A seam, in Working Effectively with Legacy Code (2004), is "a place where you can alter behavior in your program without editing in that place" — with the companion notion of an enabling point, the spot where the choice between behaviours is made. Feathers catalogs preprocessing, link and object seams; Fowler's Legacy Seam bliki (January 2024) explicitly extends the idea from its testing origin to legacy displacement — the same cut that lets you pin behaviour in a test harness (B2) lets you route it to a new implementation (B5). The operational catch, named in our topic notes as "seams are unicorns", is real: many modules have no natural seam, and then the first move is not extraction but seam manufacture — small, net-protected refactorings that create an enabling point where none existed.

Four named patterns do the plumbing. Anti-corruption layer (Evans, Domain-Driven Design, 2003, ch. 14): "Create an isolating layer to provide clients with functionality in terms of their own domain model. The layer talks to the other system through its existing interface, requiring little or no modification to the other system." During coexistence the new system will have to call unmigrated legacy behaviour; the ACL is what stops the legacy model's vocabulary and invariants from leaking into — and eventually dictating — the new design. Branch by abstraction: when the thing being replaced is deep inside the codebase, introduce an abstraction over it, migrate callers to the abstraction, then swap the implementation behind it. The attribution is worth getting right — the name was coined by Stacy Curl; Paul Hammant first detailed the technique publicly (blog, April 2007, from a 2005 ThoughtWorks engagement at Bank of America); Fowler's bliki (2014) popularized it; Jez Humble's write-up of swapping iBATIS→Hibernate inside the Go CD tool is the standard worked example. For agents this is the single most mechanical pattern of the set: enumerate callers, introduce the interface, migrate call sites — repetitive, verifiable, diff-at-a-time work.

Expand/contract — also known as parallel change (Danilo Sato on Fowler's bliki, 2014; first documented as a refactoring strategy by Joshua Kerievsky, 2006): "a pattern to implement backward-incompatible changes to an interface in a safe manner, by breaking the change into three distinct phases: expand, migrate, and contract." It is the only sane way to evolve schemas and public APIs while both worlds run, and it is a key component of evolutionary database design — which is where §8 takes it. Dark launching (Fowler bliki, 2020) is the verification half of the toolbox: take the new behaviour and call it invisibly from production traffic — "the old and new code can both be called and their results checked… but only one answer returned to the interface." For a strangler cutover this is gold: the new slice serves real inputs in shadow mode, its outputs are diffed against the monolith's, and the cutover happens only after the diff is empty — B2's golden-master idea promoted from the test suite into production traffic. Feature flags (Pete Hodgson's canonical martinfowler.com article, 2017 — still titled Feature Toggles, whatever the industry calls them) supply the routing mechanism: release toggles decide per-request whether the facade sends this user, this tenant, this percentage of traffic to the old or the new side, which is what makes every slice reversible in minutes rather than deploys.

The toolbox is one idea wearing five costumes

Seam, ACL, branch by abstraction, expand/contract, dark launch + flags: all five exist to keep the two worlds decoupled enough that each step is small, verified and reversible. When a migration plan cannot name its seam, its translation layer, its routing mechanism and its shadow-verification step, it is not a strangler plan — it is a big-bang with better marketing.

4The heatmap: churn times complexity

The heatmap's premise is forensic: the code that matters is the code that changes. Adam Tornhill's Your Code as a Crime Scene (Pragmatic Bookshelf, 2015; 2nd ed. 2024) operationalized it: mine version-control history for change frequency per file, cross it with a complexity measure of the current snapshot, and the intersection — complex code that changes often — is the hotspot, where maintenance effort and defects concentrate. The idea has genuine academic roots independent of Tornhill's company: Mockus & Weiss predicted change risk from history at Bell Labs in 2000, and Nagappan & Ball's Microsoft study (ICSE 2005, Windows Server 2003) showed relative code churn discriminates fault-prone from clean binaries at 89% accuracy — this is the honest citation for "churn predicts defects", not a vendor benchmark.

The headline business numbers come from Tornhill & Borg's Code Red study (TechDebt '22, arXiv:2203.04374; 39 proprietary codebases, 30,737 files, Jira + VCS data): files in the worst code-health band carried 15 times more defects, and issues in them took on average 124% more time to resolve — print that as longer, not "124% faster" the other way, which is the marketing reframe — with nine times longer maximum cycle times, i.e. worst-case unpredictability. The caveats are not footnotes, they are part of the number: the study is correlational (the authors themselves ask whether bad code causes defects or defect-dense code reads as bad), all 39 codebases were customers of the authors' company, the Code Health metric is proprietary, and the conflict of interest is disclosed by the authors in a follow-up paper. Treat Code Red as peer-reviewed, vendor-affiliated, direction-confirming evidence. Two folklore corrections belong here too. The ubiquitous "hotspots are 4% of the code and hold 70%+ of defects" is a single-system anecdote from Tornhill's talks — his book says only that in one system hotspots were 4% of the code and contained 7 of the 8 most defect-dense modules; his SREcon 2024 figure is that hotspots (2–4% of the codebase) attract 20–70% of development activity. The defensible general statement is Pareto-shaped and independently replicated: roughly a fifth of modules carry roughly four-fifths of defects. And for the cost of the debt itself, the peer-reviewed figure is Besker, Martini & Bosch (JSS 2019): developers self-report ~23% of their time wasted on technical debt — prefer it over Stripe's vendor-commissioned 42% survey number.

For the agentic program the heatmap has a second, less obvious use: it is generated by exactly the read-only machinery B4 already built. Commit history, file sizes, dependency edges and complexity metrics are parser- and git-derived facts — the deterministic layer — so the heatmap is the one pilot-selection input that costs almost nothing, carries no hypothesis label, and can be regenerated weekly as the migration proceeds. It answers B6's F0 question "what do we point the net at first?" with data instead of with the loudest voice in the steering committee.

5Picking the first battlefield

Industry guidance on pilot selection is more specific — and more interesting — than the "start with something unimportant" folklore. Zhamak Dehghani's How to break a Monolith into Microservices (martinfowler.com, 2018) recommends warming up on "a simple and fairly decoupled capability" — one that does not require changes to many client-facing applications and ideally not even its own data store — because at the start "the delivery teams' biggest risk is failing to operate the microservices properly." Note what the criterion is: not low business value, but low coupling and low operational risk. And the same article then pivots: once the machinery works, extract what is important to the business and changes frequently — deliberately intersecting the commit-history hotspots with the product roadmap, because that is where extraction pays back fastest. AWS's prescriptive guidance adds operational criteria: few dependencies, release cadence, scaling needs, compliance scope, team cognitive load. The honest composite: first slice — decoupled, low-risk, machinery-proving; subsequent slices — high value, high churn.

Our workbook adds one criterion the industry lists do not, and it is exclusionary: business criticality vetoes the pilot, whatever the heatmap says. This is Report 4's synthesis, flagged as such, and the agent context is what makes it stricter than the classic advice. A human-led pilot that goes wrong produces a bad week; an agent-empowered team with write access producing changes at ten times the rate on the payments or safety module produces a blast radius no steering committee should accept before the harness — net, gate, review loop, rollback drills — has been proven on something the business can survive losing for a day. Criticality is not a weighted factor to be traded off against churn; it is a gate. A module that fails it is excluded, full stop; among the survivors, the heatmap and the coupling analysis decide.

The heatmap has a blind spot that a second axis fixes: it ranks how hot a module is, not whether it is a coherent thing to extract. A file can be hot, complex and still be a tangle of three domains sharing one class. Domain-driven design supplies the coherence axis — extract along bounded contexts and subdomains (Richardson's decompose-by-subdomain / decompose-by-business-capability patterns on microservices.io) — and event storming, the workshop format invented by Alberto Brandolini (earliest widely-cited write-up: Mathias Verraes, 2013), is the practical way to surface those boundaries in a brownfield system where the domain model lives in people's heads. The technique earns its place here because it converts B4's archaeology output into extraction candidates: the map says what exists, the storming sessions say where the domain seams run, the heatmap says what is hot, and the criticality gate says what is off-limits.

CriterionSource of truthDirectionRole
Business criticalityproduct + ops, incident historylower is saferEXCLUSIONARY gate — high criticality vetoes the pilot (our synthesis)
Isolability / seamsB4 dependency map; event-storming boundariesa seam or a manufacturable onerequired — no seam, no pilot (refactor-first exception, §3)
Churn × complexity (heat)git history + static analysis (§4)higher = tests pay off fasterscored — drives ordering after the gate
