Existing test coveragecoverage + B3 mutation scoresome is enough; zero means B2 firstscored — pins the cost of the safety net
SizeLOC, context-budget fit (§7)fits a review loop + agent contextscored — god-class modules shrink, not disappear, as candidates
Data couplingschema ownership, shared tables (§8)movable data preferredscored — shared-DB modules drop sharply
Operational riskDehghani: "can the team operate it?"proves the machineryscored — the first slice exists to exercise the pipeline

The decision rule assembles: run the criticality gate first; among survivors, require a seam (or a budgeted seam-manufacturing step); then rank by the weighted score, with heat and value pulling up and data coupling pulling down. The output is a shortlist of two or three candidates taken to the F0 exit review (B6), where the estate's seniors — the same people who graded B4's hypotheses — get the veto they have earned. A worked example: an invoicing-adjacent reporting module (hot, medium size, one database view, low criticality) beats the auth service (hot, small, but criticality-failing) and beats the pricing engine (hottest, but criticality-failing and a shared-schema tangle). The heatmap made the pricing engine visible; the gate and the second axis kept the program alive.

6Hyrum's Law and load-bearing bugs

The strangler's deepest trap is not architectural, it is epistemic: you cannot migrate a behaviour you do not know someone depends on. Hyrum's Law states it exactly (hyrumsław.com, observation by Hyrum Wright; named and popularized by Titus Winters; canonized in Software Engineering at Google, 2020): "With a sufficient number of users of an API, it does not matter what you promise in the contract: all observable behaviors of your system will be depended on by somebody." The essay is undated (online since at least 2017; widely cited as c. 2012 — no dated primary source exists, so cite it that way), and it names the two dependence classes that matter for migration: bug-for-bug compatibility — consumers depend on the bugs — and performance as an implicit interface, where consumers depend on timing the contract never promised. The obligatory cartoon is xkcd 1172 ("Workflow", 2013): every change breaks somebody's workflow. And the term "load-bearing bug" is engineering slang, not literature — use it freely, attribute it never.

The canonical war stories show the law operating at industrial scale. When Microsoft built Windows 95, SimCity's retail version used memory after freeing it — a bug that happened to work on DOS — so Windows 95 shipped an allocator that detected SimCity running and switched to a permissive mode (told by Spolsky in How Microsoft Lost the API War, 2004; corroborated by Raymond Chen's compatibility-lab accounts, where he took any application failing on Windows 95 as a personal failure). The Linux kernel enshrined the principle as its first rule: when a 2012 media-subsystem "fix" broke PulseAudio, Linus Torvalds overrode the maintainer's blame-the-userspace argument with the famous "WE DO NOT BREAK USERSPACE!" — and reverted, even though the userspace code was genuinely buggy. The lesson for a migration is symmetric: on a legacy estate, the consumers are often unknown integrations, cron jobs, Excel macros and other teams' workarounds — and every "obvious bug" is potentially somebody's dependency.

What Hyrum's Law implies for agent-driven change

Agents make drive-by fixes nearly free — "while you're in there, this null check is obviously wrong" — and Hyrum's Law makes drive-by fixes nearly uninsurable. The discipline: characterize before you fix (B2's net pins the current behaviour, bug included); check the consumers (B4's map shows who calls in); and ship the behaviour change as a deliberate, communicated migration with a flag and a rollback (§3), never as a side effect of an unrelated commit. The bug gets fixed on a Tuesday on purpose, with the integration teams warned — or it does not get fixed this quarter at all.

7God classes and the agent's context budget

The classic evidence against god classes predates LLMs and is stronger than most people realize. Khomh, Di Penta, Guéhéneuc & Antoniol (EMSE 2012; 54 releases of ArgoUML, Eclipse, Mylyn, Rhino) found that classes participating in antipatterns including the Blob/God Class are significantly more change- and fault-prone — and, crucially for the "it's just because they're big" objection, the effect persists after controlling for size. Abbes et al. (CSMR 2011) measured the comprehension side in controlled experiments: developers needed significantly more time and effort to complete comprehension tasks on Blob and spaghetti-code systems. God classes are where defects and comprehension cost concentrate — for humans, which is exactly why they also sit at the top of every heatmap.

For agents there is a second, newer penalty, and it should be stated with its evidence class attached. Liu et al., Lost in the Middle (TACL 2024): model performance "is often highest when relevant information occurs at the beginning or end of the input context, and significantly degrades when models must access relevant information in the middle" — drops of 20–30% and worse, in some configurations below the no-context baseline; the honest caveats are that these were retrieval/QA tasks on 2023-era models, and "U-shaped curve" is the field's shorthand, not the paper's phrase. Chroma's Context Rot technical report (July 2025 — vendor research, hedged, but with an open replication kit and directionally consistent with the peer-reviewed RULER and Levy et al., ACL 2024) generalized it across 18 models: "model performance consistently degrades with increasing input length", a single distractor measurably hurts, and the effect amplifies with length. Anthropic's own context-engineering guidance (September 2025, vendor) draws the operational conclusion: context is a "finite resource with diminishing marginal returns", and the goal is "the smallest possible set of high-signal tokens" — just-in-time retrieval, not whole-file stuffing. What does not exist, despite confident blog posts, is a peer-reviewed study correlating agent benchmark failures with target-file size — do not imply one; the argument is the synthesis: god classes force exactly the reading pattern (relevant lines mid-context, surrounded by distractors) that the long-context literature says models handle worst.

The practical consequence lands back on the heatmap and the pilot criteria. A 10,000-line god class is a triple liability: fault-concentrating (Khomh), comprehension-taxing for the human reviewers who must grade the agent's diffs (Abbes), and context-hostile for the agent itself (Liu/Chroma, with caveats). It should score high on the heatmap — it changes often, it hurts — but its size and tangle push it out of pilot territory and into a preparatory track: B4 archaeology to map it, B2 net to pin it, seam-manufacturing refactorings to split it (§3), and only then extraction. "Refactor the god class first" is not perfectionism; it is resizing the work to fit both the human review loop and the agent's context budget.

8The database is the hard part

Every strangler plan is elegant until it reaches the schema. Routing HTTP calls through a facade is a solved problem; splitting the data underneath them is not, because data has gravity, history and consumers nobody listed. Sam Newman's Monolith to Microservices (2019) is blunt about where the strangler's reversibility breaks down: the moment a migrated slice manages data that must be kept in sync between the monolith and the new service, each step stops being cleanly reversible, and the escape hatches are all compromises — a temporary shared database, a database view, a database-wrapping service, or change data capture. Chris Richardson's pattern catalog names the default failure: the shared database, whose costs are development-time coupling ("coordinate schema changes with the developers of other services") and runtime coupling — every service one slow query away from every other's outage. Dehghani's warning is the one to quote when a migration plan looks suspiciously clean: avoid the anti-pattern of only decoupling the facades and never decoupling the data — without decoupling the data, the architecture is not microservices, it is a monolith with network latency (and §10's distributed monolith is the name on the toe tag).

The mechanics of moving data safely are well documented. The dual-write problem (Gunnar Morling's canonical Debezium post, 2019): a service that writes to its database and publishes an event has no shared transaction across the two, so a crash between the writes corrupts the world silently; the outbox pattern fixes it by writing state and event into one table in one ACID transaction, with change data capture (Debezium tailing the write-ahead log) publishing asynchronously — CDC thus doubles as a database-level seam, letting the old and new worlds stay synchronized during coexistence without either knowing about the other. The full playbook is Stripe's Online migrations at scale (2017), the four-phase discipline Dehghani explicitly recommends: dual-write (via outbox), backfill history, run both and compare results, then cut over. Notice the family resemblance to §3: expand/contract for schemas, dark launching for reads, outbox for writes — the database is where all of §3's patterns get applied simultaneously, under less forgiving constraints. The pilot-selection consequence is already in §5's table: a module whose data cannot move (shared tables with twenty consumers) drops out of pilot territory no matter how hot it is; the ideal first battlefield owns its data or reads it through a single, severable view.

9What agents change in 2026 — and what they do not

The loudest recent claim in this space is Anthropic's How AI helps break the cost barrier to COBOL modernization (February 2026, vendor, point-in-time): modernization programs that took armies of consultants years can now run "in quarters instead of years", because the binding cost was never typing new code but understanding the old — and understanding is what agents reprice (the B4 thesis at industry scale). The market treated it as credible: IBM's stock dropped 13.2% the day it published, its worst single-day fall since 2000. Two details matter more than the headline. First, the post itself prescribes exactly this chapter's discipline — "start with a single component or workflow that has clear boundaries and moderate complexity… implement incrementally with testing at each step"; the strongest "AI changes the economics" source on record does not argue for big-bang anything. Second, the incumbents' rebuttal names precisely the work agents do not automate: IBM's Rob Thomas countered that "translation captures almost none of the actual complexity" — the hardware-software integration, data models, operations and behavioural equivalence that §6 and §8 are about. AWS Transform's re:Invent 2025 numbers (1.1 billion lines analyzed, 810,000 hours saved, customers at 1.5M lines/month) belong in the same bucket as B4's vendor file: unaudited, direction-confirming, magnitude-unproven.

The counterweight dataset is GitClear's analysis of 211 million changed lines (2020–2024, vendor but independent of the agent vendors): the share of changed lines that were moved/refactored collapsed from ~25% (2021) to under 10% (2024), copy-pasted lines rose from 8.3% to 12.3%, and churn climbed from 3.1% to 5.7%. Correlational, not causal — but the shape of the warning is clear: agents accelerate the production of new legacy at least as reliably as they accelerate migration. A strangler program without decommissioning discipline now accumulates zombie code faster than ever, which upgrades "the old system serves no traffic" from a nice-to-have to the program's central metric. The synthesis this chapter offers: agents make the heatmap cheap, not the big-bang safe. Discovery, dependency mapping, draft documentation and seam analysis — the inputs to §4 and §5 — collapse in cost; behavioural parity under Hyrum's Law, data migration, and organizational change do not. And one discovery finding closes the loop back to the harness: fitness functions (Ford, Parsons & Kua, Building Evolutionary Architectures, 2017) — automated architectural checks in CI — with a ratchet (ArchUnit's FreezingArchRule records existing violations, fails only on new ones, and shrinks the allowance as violations are fixed) keep the not-yet-migrated monolith from rotting further while the migration runs. A rule executable in CI is a rule an agent's every diff can be graded against; the ratchet is how you let agents touch the legacy code during a years-long strangler without letting them make it worse.

10Anti-patterns — how strangling goes wrong

The six failure modes
  • 1 · Piloting on the crown jewels. The payments/safety module is hot, visible and politically attractive — and one agent-accelerated bad week there ends the program. Criticality is a gate, not a weight (§5); the first battlefield is chosen to be survivable.
  • 2 · The permanent facade. The routing layer accretes logic, becomes a single point of failure and a performance bottleneck (Microsoft's own warning), and quietly turns into the new monolith's front half. Transitional architecture needs a decommissioning date, or a deliberate decision to keep it as a legacy-client adapter.
  • 3 · The strangler that never finishes. Practitioner folklore with no canonical source but universal recognition: the migration stalls at 60%, the dual-stack becomes permanent, and the organization now pays for two systems plus the sync glue between them. GitClear's rot data (§9) is the 2026 accelerant — agents produce new code faster than anyone decommissions old code. The metric that prevents it is decommissioned traffic and deleted lines, not migrated features.
  • 4 · The distributed monolith. Extracting along technical seams — shared database, chatty synchronous calls, lockstep deploys — yields a monolith with network latency (Newman). The bounded-context axis and the data-coupling criterion exist precisely to catch this before the first cut.
  • 5 · Drive-by fixes. "While the agent was in there, it fixed an obvious bug" — and Hyrum's Law collects the invoice from an integration nobody knew existed. Characterize first, migrate deliberately (§6); the bug fix ships as its own flagged, communicated change or not at all.
  • 6 · Facade-only decoupling. Clean API routing over a forever-shared database (Dehghani's named anti-pattern) — the diagram looks strangled, the schema says otherwise. No pilot whose data cannot move; outbox/CDC for the coexistence window; "database per service" as the explicit end state.

11Takeaways for our engagement

Assess (feeds B6/E7)
  • Is the pilot shortlist derived from the git-history heatmap plus B4's map — or from whoever argues loudest in the steering committee?
  • Which candidate modules fail the criticality gate outright? (Name them now, before they become tempting.)
  • For the surviving candidates: does each have a seam or a budgeted seam-manufacturing plan, and can its data actually move?
  • Do the domain boundaries exist on paper (bounded contexts, event-storming output), or is "the module" just a directory name?
Build (F1–F2)
  • The facade and the flags first: routing with per-request release toggles, so every slice is reversible in minutes (§3).
  • Dark-launch verification as the cutover gate: new slice shadows production traffic, outputs diffed, cutover only when the diff is empty.
  • Outbox + CDC for any module that must sync data during coexistence; shared database flagged as transitional with an exit date (§8).
  • Fitness-function ratchet in CI over the not-yet-migrated monolith: no new coupling to the parts being strangled, no new violations, agent diffs graded against the same rules (§9).
  • The program metric on the wall: old-system traffic and deleted lines trending to zero — not features shipped (§10).
Three sentences to remember B5 by

Big-bang rewrites concentrate risk into one irreversible cutover — Spolsky's Netscape is the canonical corpse and no honest failure-rate number exists — so the estate is strangled instead: a facade routes traffic, slices migrate one at a time behind flags, and every step ships verified against the old behaviour and stays reversible. The first battlefield is chosen by data, not enthusiasm: the git-history heatmap (churn × complexity, honestly caveated) finds what is hot, bounded contexts decide what is coherent, seams and movable data decide what is possible, and business criticality — our flagged synthesis — vetoes what is too dangerous for an agent-empowered pilot. Agents reprice the map, not the risk: they make archaeology, heatmaps and seam analysis nearly free (Anthropic's own COBOL playbook prescribes incremental migration), while Hyrum's Law, the database and the strangler-that-never-finishes remain exactly as dangerous as they were in 2004 — which is why the program measures decommissioned lines, and why the net, the gate and the ratchet come before the first cut.

12Further reading

Fowler — Strangler Fig Application (2004, expanded later)
martinfowler.com/bliki/StranglerFigApplication.html · 2004 original via web.archive.org · Patterns of Legacy Displacement series

The origin post with the Queensland fig trees, the Stevenson/XP-2004 credit, "writing tomorrow's legacy software today", and the modern four-activity framing (Cartwright, Horn, Lewis).

Spolsky — Things You Should Never Do · Caudill — Rewrite, refactor, or reinvent? · DHH — REWRITE!
joelonsoftware.com, Apr 2000 · herbcaudill.com, 2019 · Business of Software, 2015

The canonical case against big-bang, the six-case survey that complicates it (Basecamp, FreshBooks/BillSpring, FogBugz/Wasabi), and the counterexample that turns out to be strangler economics in disguise.

Feathers — Working Effectively with Legacy Code · Fowler — Legacy Seam, Branch by Abstraction, Parallel Change, Dark Launching · Hodgson — Feature Toggles
Prentice Hall 2004 · martinfowler.com bliki 2014–2024 · martinfowler.com, 2017

The plumbing toolbox: the seam definition and its displacement-era extension, branch by abstraction (Curl/Hammant attribution), expand/contract, shadow-traffic verification, and the flags that route it.

Tornhill — Your Code as a Crime Scene · Tornhill & Borg — Code Red · Nagappan & Ball (ICSE 2005)
Pragmatic 2015/2024 · arXiv:2203.04374 (TechDebt '22) · DOI 10.1145/1062455.1062514

The hotspot method, its vendor-affiliated business numbers with caveats attached, and the independent Microsoft evidence that churn predicts defects. Besker et al. (JSS 2019) for the 23%-time-wasted figure.

hyrumsław.com · Spolsky — How Microsoft Lost the API War · LKML "WE DO NOT BREAK USERSPACE!" · xkcd 1172
Wright/Winters, undated (c. 2012) · joelonsoftware.com, 2004 · lkml.org, Dec 2012 · xkcd.com/1172

The implicit-interface law with correct attribution, the SimCity bug-for-bug war story, the kernel's first rule, and the obligatory cartoon.

Dehghani — How to break a Monolith into Microservices · Newman — Monolith to Microservices · Richardson — microservices.io data patterns
martinfowler.com, 2018 · O'Reilly, 2019 · microservices.io

Pilot selection as published guidance (decoupled-first, then high-value/high-churn), the book-length treatment of database decomposition, and the shared-database / database-per-service pattern pair.

Morling — Outbox pattern · Stripe — Online migrations at scale
debezium.io, 2019 · stripe.com/blog/online-migrations, 2017

The dual-write problem and its CDC fix, and the four-phase dual-write/backfill/compare/cutover playbook for moving data without downtime.

Liu et al. — Lost in the Middle · Chroma — Context Rot · Khomh et al. (EMSE 2012) · Abbes et al. (CSMR 2011)
TACL 2024 · research.trychroma.com, Jul 2025 (vendor) · DOI 10.1007/s10664-011-9171-y · DOI 10.1109/CSMR.2011.24

The god-class double penalty: antipatterns concentrate faults beyond size effects and tax human comprehension; long contexts degrade model performance exactly where big files put the relevant lines.

Anthropic — COBOL modernization cost barrier · GitClear 2025 · Ford, Parsons & Kua — Building Evolutionary Architectures · ArchUnit FreezingArchRule
claude.com/blog, Feb 2026 (vendor) · gitclear.com, 2025 (vendor data) · O'Reilly, 2017 · archunit.org

The 2026 economics file: the vendor claim that repriced a market (and prescribes incremental migration anyway), the rot counterweight, and the fitness-function ratchet that keeps agents from making the old world worse while the new one grows.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B5-strangler-heatmap-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are B4 (the archaeology that produces the map and the shortlist), B2 (the net that pins behaviour before any slice moves), B3 (the gate that grades the net), B6 (the F0–F3 roadmap and STOP criteria the pilot slots into), I1/I2 (artifact status and write permissions during coexistence) and D7 (the same strangle-and-map logic on the design layer). The exclusionary criticality gate, the scoring assembly and the "AI makes the heatmap cheap, not the big-bang safe" framing are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — the patterns (strangler fig, seams, ACL, branch by abstraction, expand/contract, dark launching, outbox) and the evidence base (Nagappan & Ball, Code Red, Khomh, Liu, Lost-in-the-Middle follow-ups) are standard and named, with vendor claims (Code Red's affiliations, AWS Transform, Anthropic, Chroma, GitClear) hedged as such. Product names, market reactions and every 2024–2026 number are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B5, module M5 stands at five of six; B6 (the F0–F3 roadmap, metrics and STOP criteria) closes the module.

B6 · DEEP DIVE

Roadmap F0–F3, Metrics and STOP Criteria

0Why it matters

Module M5 has so far produced techniques: B1 the safe ordering and its evidence, B2 the characterization net, B3 the gate that grades the net, B4 the read-only archaeology that starts everything, B5 the heatmap that picks the first battlefield. What a client actually asks for is none of these — it is a plan: where do we start on Monday, what do we promise the board, and how do we know when to widen access and when to pull back? This chapter is that assembly. Four phases (F0–F3), each defined by what the agent is allowed to touch, gates between them defined by measured exit criteria rather than calendar dates, a small set of metrics that survive contact with the measurement literature, a secrets discipline that starts before the first write, and — the part most rollout decks omit — explicit STOP criteria: the conditions under which the honest recommendation is to halt, shrink, or never start. The roadmap and its thresholds are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis, not an industry standard; we checked in July 2026, and no published canonical equivalent exists. What does exist is convergent support — DORA's AI Capabilities Model wants a clear organizational stance, strong version control and small batches; Anthropic's enterprise guidance rolls out in waves with tiered permissions and a read-only plan mode; ThoughtWorks' Böckeler starts legacy engagements with reverse engineering; and the nearest published cousins (Sharma's D³, GAIE's oversight tiers) stage oversight per task, not access over time. The phased-permission ladder itself remains ours to defend, which this chapter does.

What this chapter gives you
  • the four phases with per-phase permissions, artifacts and autonomy levels (§1)
  • exit criteria as deterministic gates — the table, and who signs off (§2)
  • the metrics that survive scrutiny: delivery health vs bootstrap progress, with the self-report trap and the vendor-telemetry file (§3)
  • secrets from day 0: the 2026 evidence that agent-assisted commits leak more, and the toolbox (§4)
  • STOP criteria and back-arrows: never-start conditions, automatic demotion, and the human factor (§5)
  • the day-1 recipe and the master plan that merges Reports 1–3 and 6 into one table (§6, our synthesis)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • B1 — the dependency chain these phases operationalize, and why the ordering is not optional
  • B2/B3 — what F1 builds (the net) and the gate that grades it
  • B4 — F0's workflow in full: archaeologist prompts, hypothesis registers, sampling
  • B5 — the heatmap that picks F2's modules and drives F3's expansion
  • E5/E6 — deterministic vs advisory, and the L1–L5 autonomy scale the phases map onto
  • E7/T2 — how the pilot inside F2 is measured; baseline discipline
  • I2 — write permissions as governance: the enforcement layer under every phase
  • M7 — the security module this roadmap inherits its blast-radius logic from

1The four phases — permissions, not quarters

The first thing to say about F0–F3 is what the F stands not for: not "fiscal", not a schedule. A phase is a permission tier plus a proof obligation — the agent's write scope stays fixed until the exit criteria are met, however long that takes, and widens only when a gate opens (§2). This is the same logic the write-permission matrix gave individual artifacts in I2, applied to the engagement as a whole; it is also why the roadmap survives the awkward fact, established in B1, that agent throughput is worthless while the verification loop beneath it is missing. Phases are cheap to describe and expensive to skip: every failure pattern catalogued in B1 — the bug-as-spec loop, the review pile-up, the deleted-tests incident — is a phase boundary crossed without its proof.

F0

Read-only: earn the map and the trust

autonomy L1 · write scope: nothing

The agent reads everything and changes nothing. Its outputs are B4's artifacts — a hypothesis register with confidence labels, validated entry points, reconstructed decision history — plus B5's scored heatmap draft. Two things are being built and only one of them is technical: the map, and the maintaining team's willingness to believe the map. The team that says "the agent doesn't understand our system" is often right (B4's independent accuracy file says roughly half of architecture-level claims are noise), which is why F0's discipline — every claim labeled, anchored and sampled by the seniors who can grade it — is also the trust-building program. F0 ends when the seniors have signed off on a sampled register, not when a week has passed.

F1

Verification infrastructure: build the loop before the writes

autonomy L1 · write scope: tests, CI config, docs — never production code

The first writes are a peculiar kind: characterization and golden-master tests that pin what the pilot modules actually do (B2's six-step workflow), graded by the mutation gate (B3's two-mode design, with ~70% on the pilot module as Report 4's working heuristic — no tool ships a mandatory threshold), a minimal CI that runs them (~20 lines: build, lint, test — the empty test job on day 1 is deliberate, it makes the loop exist before it has content), an AGENTS.md extracted descriptively from code and pruned of hallucinations by a human, and a full-history secrets scan (§4). Everything the agent writes in F1 is meta-code; production behavior does not change. That is the point: when F2 starts, every future change lands inside an already-running verification loop.

F2

Scoped writes: the pilot, measured

autonomy L1–L2 · write scope: heatmap-selected low-risk modules

The agent now changes production code — in the modules B5's heatmap scored as isolable, characterized and non-critical, at L1–L2 (human reviews everything; the agent proposes), through the full loop: handoff contract in, verified diff out (M3). Batches stay small — DORA's own capability wording, and its 2024 report's mechanism for teams that kept stability while adopting AI, is adherence to "small batch sizes and robust testing mechanisms". Swarmia's Holkeri, mapping five autonomy levels in March 2026, locates the industry's honest position exactly here: "Most teams hit a ceiling somewhere between Level 2 and Level 3, and the reason is usually not the tool, but how purposefully they're using it." F2 is also where the baseline discipline of E7 becomes non-negotiable: the pilot's success criteria are pre-registered before the first task, because self-report will not save you (§3).

F3

Expansion: scale what the numbers earned

autonomy per module, promoted on evidence · write scope: widening by heatmap

Expansion is heatmap-driven, module by module — each new module gets the F1 treatment (characterize, gate, then write) before the agent touches it, while genuinely new code starts agent-first behind an anti-corruption layer (B5's strangler fig). Two findings should shape how F3 is run. First, diffusion is social: Microsoft's study of its own early-2026 CLI-agent rollout — tens of thousands of engineers, independent and instrumented, not surveyed — found "first use spread primarily through social networks, retention was associated more with engineers' coding activity than with demographics," and concludes "organizations should treat visible peer use as central to rollout strategy." The same study supplies F3's headline metric: adopters "merged roughly 24% more pull requests than they would have otherwise," a lift that "persists across our four-month window" — sustained, not a novelty spike. Second, the ceiling is review capacity: Report 1's rule — never scale agent count beyond code-review capacity — binds hardest in F3, and §3's vendor telemetry shows what ignoring it looks like.

2Gates, not vibes: exit criteria as deterministic checks

E5 drew the line this workbook keeps returning to: advisory guidance bends under pressure, deterministic checks do not. Applied to the rollout itself, the principle says each gate should be decided by checkable facts — a mutation score, a green CI run, a clean scan, a signed sample — not by "the team feels ready" or, worse, "the quarter ended." A gate that cannot be stated as a check is a gate that will be argued open the first time a deadline leans on it. The table states each gate both ways: the criterion, and the check that decides it.

GateOpens whenDeterministic checkCloses again when (back-trigger)
F0 → F1The map is trusted: hypothesis register sampled and signed by the estate's seniors; entry points validated; heatmap scored (B5)Sampling sign-off recorded; every register claim carries a confidence label and a file-and-line anchor (B4)Sampled error rate too high → re-run archaeology with tighter anchoring; team still defensive → extend F0, find a champion (§5)
F1 → F2The loop exists: characterization tests on the pilot modules pass the mutation gate; CI runs on every push; AGENTS.md pruned; secrets history clean or rotatedMutation score ≥ ~70% on pilot modules (Report 4 heuristic, B3); CI green; gitleaks/TruffleHog full-history report closed outMutation score falls below threshold; suite flakiness rises (B2's flaky-pin trap); an active credential surfaces → STOP writes, rotate
F2 → F3The pilot measured well: pre-registered success criteria met; rework/change-fail stable against the F1 baseline; review load sustainableBaseline-vs-pilot comparison on the §3 metrics (not self-report); review-time and no-review-merge share within agreed boundsRework or change-fail rate rising → shrink batch size, hold scope; review queue saturating → fewer agents, not more reviewers on paper
F3 steady stateExpansion continues module-by-module, each through its own F1 treatment firstHeatmap re-scored on a cadence; per-module mutation and rework tracked; autonomy promotions recorded with a named approverAny module's numbers regress → automatic demotion of that module's autonomy tier — rollback needs no meeting (§5)

Who signs off matters as much as what is checked. Two 2026 preprints — both flagged as such, neither peer-reviewed — supply useful precedents for making sign-off proportional rather than uniform. GAIE (Kang, arXiv:2606.22484) routes oversight by task risk across four dimensions — regulatory impact, customer proximity, reversibility, data sensitivity — into three tiers (human-in-the-loop, human-over-the-loop, automated-with-monitoring), reframing the question as "not 'should agents code autonomously?' but 'what level of oversight is appropriate for this code's regulatory context?'" The Digital Apprentice (Weber & Taneja, arXiv:2606.04321) treats autonomy as a per-skill state machine with a dual-key promotion — "Promotion from one tier to the next requires both empirical evidence of competence on that skill and a recorded human authorization" — and, the detail worth stealing outright, asymmetric demotion: "the system rolls a skill back automatically when quality degrades, without waiting for human action." Our back-triggers in the table are exactly that mechanism, driven by the mutation and rework numbers.

The honest analogue check (July 2026)

We looked for a published equivalent of F0–F3 before claiming it as ours. GitHub's Engineering System Success Playbook is a continuous-improvement loop (identify → evaluate → implement) over twelve metrics in four zones — useful for §3, but not a phase model. Anthropic's enterprise guidance recommends wave-based rollout, tiered access levels and a read-only plan mode — the ingredients, not the ladder (vendor). Sharma's D³ — "Beyond Greenfield," arXiv:2512.01155, single-author, n=52 self-report survey, 26.9% perceived gain, with the author's own caveat that results are "preliminary evidence of practitioner sentiment rather than causal effects" — stages the work of one task (Discover → Define → Deliver, no relation to this workbook's D-scale), and GAIE stages oversight per task. None stages access over time against measured exit criteria. F0–F3 therefore stays flagged as Report 4's synthesis: assembled from named, standard components, but ours.

3What to measure — and what measurement lies to you

Two metric families, deliberately kept apart. Delivery health is DORA's territory, and worth citing with its current vocabulary: the canonical set is now five — change lead time, deployment frequency, change fail rate ("the ratio of deployments that require immediate intervention following a deployment"), failed deployment recovery time, and deployment rework rate ("the ratio of deployments that are unplanned but happen as a result of an incident in production"). A dating correction this workbook itself needed: rework rate was introduced in the 2024 report — DORA's metrics history calls 2024 "the most significant structural change to the metrics", added to test whether change failure rate was proxying rework — and carried into the 2025 benchmarks. Rework rate is the roadmap's load-bearing delivery metric precisely because AI-era throughput can look healthy while instability accumulates beneath it: DORA 2025 found the throughput relationship turned positive (2024's small negative did not persist) while the instability relationship remained negative — a shape rework rate is designed to catch.

Bootstrap progress is this module's own territory, and its metrics come from the preceding chapters: mutation score on characterized modules (primary — B3, diff-scoped advisory plus module-level exit scores), the verified share of the hypothesis register (B4's promote-through-verification path, a real documentation-coverage number rather than a page count), onboarding time, and sustained per-developer PR-merge lift. The last two now have independent anchors: DX's Q4 2025 impact report (135,000 developers, 425 organizations; practitioner research, method-transparent) measured onboarding — time to tenth PR, across six multinationals — falling "from 91 days with no AI usage to just 49 days with daily use"; Microsoft's instrumented rollout study put the sustained merge lift at roughly 24% (§1). The two 24%-ish PR findings arrive by different methods from different populations — cite both, never merge them into one number.

The measurement traps, in evidence order

Self-report is disqualified. The METR RCT (arXiv:2507.09089): 16 experienced developers, 246 real tasks on mature repos — AI increased completion time 19% while participants forecast a 24% speedup and, after experiencing the slowdown, still believed they had been ~20% faster. Every F2 exit decision therefore compares instrumented baseline against instrumented pilot; developer sentiment is a health signal, not an exit criterion (E7, T2).

Vendor telemetry shows the failure shape. Faros AI's 2025 telemetry (10,000+ developers) found +21% tasks completed and +98% PRs merged per developer against +91% median review time, +154% PR size and flat organizational delivery — the "AI Productivity Paradox." Its 2026 follow-up ("The Acceleration Whiplash," ~22,000 developers, 4,000+ teams, analysis as of March 2026) revised the shape sharper: +33.7% tasks, +16.2% PRs merged, median review time up 441.5% (their summary: increased 5×), +51.3% PR size — and 31.3% more PRs "merging without any review". Vendor numbers from a self-selected customer base, flagged as such — but the mechanism (production outruns verification until verification is skipped) is exactly what the F2→F3 gate exists to catch.

The boardroom number needs handling. "95% of AI pilots fail" — MIT NANDA's "The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025" (Challapally et al., July 2025, not peer-reviewed) actually says "95% of organizations are getting zero return" and "Just 5% of integrated AI pilots are extracting millions in value, while the vast majority remain stuck with no measurable P&L impact" — measured on enterprise GenAI initiatives broadly (methodology: "a systematic review of over 300 publicly disclosed AI initiatives, structured interviews with representatives from 52 organizations, and survey responses from 153 senior leaders" — the widely-circulated "150 interviews / 350 employees" version is a misquote), with P&L impact as the bar and nothing coding-agent-specific in it. Do not cite it as evidence about coding tools; do let it argue for the thing it genuinely supports — pre-registered success criteria, because "zero measurable return" is what a pilot without a baseline reports by default. KPMG's 2026 diagnosis of why scale stalls lands closer to this module's thesis: "Enterprise AI doesn't stall because pilots fail. It stalls because the IT readiness required for AI scale was never fully in place" — and its readiness gaps (data infrastructure, governance, legacy architecture built for human workflows) are what F1 exists to close. DORA 2025's amplifier finding points the same way: without user-centric focus AI adoption's team-level effect turns negative, and a high-quality internal platform correlates directly with the ability to unlock AI's value — infrastructure first is not our idiosyncrasy.

4Secrets from day 0

Legacy git history is an archive of every credential anyone ever pasted, and the agent is about to read all of it — then write commits at machine speed. The 2026 numbers make this concrete. GitGuardian's State of Secrets Sprawl 2026 (vendor measurement of public GitHub, March 2026): "28.65 million new hardcoded secrets were added to public GitHub commits in 2025 alone, a 34% increase year over year"; AI-service credentials were the fastest-growing category ("in 2025, AI service secrets reached 1,275,105, up 81% year over year"); and — the finding that names this section — "Claude Code-assisted commits showed a 3.2% secret-leak rate, versus a 1.5% baseline across all public GitHub commits." Roughly 2×, replacing the older and weaker Copilot correlation (2025 report: 6.4% of Copilot-active repos leaked vs 4.6% baseline — +40%). Leaks also do not age out: 70% of secrets confirmed valid in 2022 were still valid in January 2025, and "when we retested that same dataset in January 2026, the validity rate was still above 64%." One more 2026 surface belongs in this module's threat model: "24,008 unique secrets exposed in MCP-related configuration files across public GitHub, including 2,117 unique valid credentials" — the agent's own configuration layer is now a leak path (M7, sec-surfaces).

The discipline is cheap and front-loaded. Before any write access — during F1's day-1 recipe, and before the agent's context ever ingests history — run a full-history scan; every hit gets triaged; a verified-active production credential is a STOP, not a ticket: writes halt until it is rotated, because from that moment the credential exists in scan output, agent context and possibly generated text. From day 1 the scan also runs in CI and, where available, at push time, so the ~2× generation-side rate meets a deterministic gate instead of a reviewer's attention span (E5).

ToolWhat it doesLicense / costPoint-in-time note (mid-2026)
gitleaksFast regex/entropy detection over working tree and full historyCLI/binary MIT — free for any usev8.30.x; feature-complete, security patches only. The separate gitleaks-action is commercial-EULA since v2: free on public repos, license key for org/private repos — run the MIT binary directly to stay free
TruffleHogDetection plus live verification — calls the issuing service to flag which secrets are currently active; 800+ detectors; scans beyond git (S3, CI, images)AGPL-3.0 open source; commercial enterprise tierThe verification step is the differentiator: it turns "1,400 historical hits" into "12 active credentials to rotate today"
GitHub push protectionBlocks pushes containing detectable secrets at the boundaryFree and on by default for public repos (since 2024)Private repos need the paid Secret Protection product (standalone since April 2025); a free one-time Secret Risk Assessment exists

Pair the pipeline with the boundary: the scanners find what leaked, push protection stops the next one, and the I2 write-permission matrix keeps .env and credential stores out of the agent's readable scope in the first place. All three tool rows are fast-moving facts — re-verify before a client meeting.

5STOP criteria, back-arrows and the human factor

A roadmap that cannot say "no" is a sales document. Three conditions mean the engagement stays at F0 — read-only, indefinitely — because the later phases' preconditions cannot exist:

No verification loop is buildable. If the system cannot be put under CI at all — no reproducible build, no environment that can run tests, coupling so total that B5's heatmap finds no seam anywhere — then F1 has no substrate, and every agent write is an unverified write. B1 established what that produces; the answer to "can we skip the tests part?" is this STOP rule.

Safety-critical scope without provenance. Regulated safety standards — ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), DO-178C (aerospace), IEC 61508 — mandate bidirectional traceability from requirement through test to code; compliance-tooling vendors' guidance (Ketryx, Parasoft — vendor readings of the standards, flagged as such) converges on the operational consequence: AI-generated artifacts without recorded provenance — prompt, model version, inputs, approver — cannot serve as safety-case evidence, because they cannot be reproduced for an auditor. Until the provenance layer exists (H3's traceability machinery; EU AI Act Articles 12/19 impose the logging cousin of the same duty from August 2026), the agent contributes comprehension, not commits.

No audit trail. Same logic, general form: if the organization cannot say after the fact what the agent did and who approved it, autonomy has no substrate to be earned against — I2's matrix and the session-trace discipline (I1) come first.

Below STOP sit the back-arrows — cheaper, automatic, and used far more often. Mutation score sinking below the working threshold on a module sends that module back to F1: the net degraded, so writes against it stop counting as verified. Rework or change-fail rate rising against baseline shrinks batch size before it shrinks scope — DORA's stability mechanism, applied as a dial. And the whole point of borrowing the Digital Apprentice's asymmetry (§2) is that demotion needs no meeting: thresholds are agreed at gate-opening, so pulling back is the default consequence of a number, not a confrontation with whoever championed the tool.

The last back-arrow is human. A defensive maintaining team is listed in Report 4 as a threshold condition — extend F0, find a champion — and 2026 gave that advice an evidence base. DX's report is blunt about the mechanism: "Simply handing out licenses is not enough" — organizations that invested in structured enablement (training, office hours, named champions, internal patterns) saw a modeled 25% enablement increase correlate with +10.6% change confidence, +8.0% maintainability, −16.1% knowledge gaps and −18.2% time loss. Microsoft's rollout study says who actually spreads adoption: peer networks, not mandates — make the champion's wins visible, since "visible peer use" is the study's own recommended lever. Both findings convert "find a champion" from folklore into the cheapest intervention on this page.

6Day 1, and the master plan

The roadmap compresses to a first week that a client can start without ceremony — Report 4's day-1 recipe, every step read-only or meta-code:

StepActionOutputFeeds
1Map the structure read-only — parser-derived dependency graph and repo map before any promptThe deterministic anchor layerB4 archaeology; F0
2Draft AGENTS.md descriptively from the code; a human prunes hallucinations the same dayFirst advisory artifact, verifiedF1; I3's freshness policy
3Stand up the ~20-line CI: build, lint, empty test jobThe loop exists, awaiting contentF1; E4
4First golden-master test on the most isolated pure moduleThe net's first knotB2; the F1→F2 gate
5gitleaks/TruffleHog full-history scan; triage; rotate anything verified activeClean (or honest) secrets baseline§4; the STOP rule

And it composes. Each research report in this workbook carries its own staged introduction — the engineering harness's maturity ladder, the product layer's P-scale, the information architecture's artifact lifecycle, the design harness's F0–F4, security's staged rollout. The topic's open question asked how they merge; the answer is that F0–F3 is the spine and the others are rails — each report's stages map onto the same four permission tiers, so the client gets one plan, not six:

PhaseEngineering harness (R1)Product & handoff (R2)Information architecture (R3)Security (R6)
F0Read-only tools; plan-mode-style sessions; no hooks yetPM reads generated analyses; no agent-facing specs yetHypothesis register; provenance from the first session (I1)Secrets scan; threat model of the estate; deny-by-default
F1CI as the feedback loop; deterministic gates before autonomy (E4/E5)First handoff-contract templates drafted against pilot modules (H1)AGENTS.md + write-permission matrix agreed (I2)Scanning in CI; push protection; agent-readable scope excludes credential stores
F2L1–L2 on pilot modules; hooks enforce the loopContracts in use; EARS/Gherkin AC on pilot tasks (H2)Session traces linked to tickets (I7); freshness checks live (I3)Blast-radius containment on write paths; incident playbook tested
F3Autonomy per module, promoted on evidence (E6)P-scale rises with the modules; PM as editor (P4)Retrieval and memory layers earn their keep (I4–I6)Continuous verification; audit trail feeding compliance (Art. 12/19)

The mapping is this workbook's synthesis — the rows are standard components from the named chapters; the assembly into one table is ours, and it is the artifact to put in front of a client when the six reports would be five too many.

7Takeaways for our engagement

Assess (before proposing dates)
  • Can a verification loop exist here at all — build, test environment, one seam? If not, the proposal is F0-only and says so.
  • Is any scope safety-regulated or audit-bound? Then provenance machinery precedes write access, whatever the demo showed.
  • What does the full-history secrets scan return — and is anyone prepared for the answer to be "active production credentials"?
  • Who would the champion be, and would their wins be visible to peers?
Commit (the plan itself)
  • Phases as permission tiers with proof obligations; gates stated as deterministic checks with named sign-off; dates estimated, criteria fixed.
  • Baseline instrumented before F2; success criteria pre-registered; self-report demoted to a health signal.
  • Back-triggers agreed at gate-opening so demotion is automatic — a number acts, no meeting needed.
  • One master table (§6) as the client-facing plan; the six reports behind it as appendices.
Three sentences to remember B6 by

The roadmap is a permission ladder, not a schedule: each phase fixes what the agent may touch, and the gate out of it is a measured exit criterion — mutation score, clean scan, stable rework rate — checked deterministically and signed by a named human, with demotion automatic when the numbers regress. The evidence file says infrastructure decides outcomes — METR disqualified self-report, Faros' telemetry shows production outrunning review until review is skipped, DORA's amplifier findings and KPMG's stall diagnosis both point at readiness rather than models, and GitGuardian's 2026 measurement (agent-assisted commits leaking at ~2× baseline) makes the secrets scan a day-1 gate rather than a hardening task. F0–F3 has no published canonical equivalent as of July 2026 — its cousins stage oversight per task, not access over time — so it ships flagged as Report 4's synthesis: assembled from named, standard components, defensible piece by piece, and ours.

8Further reading

DORA — metrics history, the five metrics, and the AI Capabilities Model
dora.dev/insights/dora-metrics-history · dora.dev/guides/dora-metrics-four-keys · 2025 AI Capabilities Model PDF

The current vocabulary (change fail rate, deployment rework rate — introduced 2024), verbatim definitions, and the seven capabilities; stance, version control and small batches are the roadmap-relevant three.

Murphy-Hill, Butler & Savelieva — CLI coding agents at Microsoft
arXiv:2607.01418 · July 2026 · independent, instrumented

Tens of thousands of engineers: ~24% sustained PR-merge lift over four months, adoption spreading through peer networks — the F3 diffusion evidence and the anti-mandate argument.

DX — AI-Assisted Engineering: Q4 Impact Report
getdx.com · Laura Tacho · Nov 2025 · 135,000 developers / 425 organizations

Structured enablement's measured effects, onboarding 91→49 days (time to tenth PR, six multinationals), 22% of merged code AI-authored; practitioner research, method-transparent.

Faros AI — the Productivity Paradox and the Acceleration Whiplash
faros.ai · 2025 telemetry + AI Engineering Report 2026 (Apr 2026)

The failure shape in vendor telemetry: review time up 441.5%, PR size up 51.3%, 31.3% more PRs merging without any review — what the F2→F3 gate exists to catch. Flag as vendor.

METR — Measuring the Impact of Early-2025 AI on Developer Productivity
arXiv:2507.09089 · the RCT

19% slower against a believed 20% speedup — the study that disqualifies self-report as an exit criterion and mandates instrumented baselines (E7, T2).

GitGuardian — State of Secrets Sprawl 2026 (and 2025)
blog.gitguardian.com · Mar 2026 · vendor measurement of public GitHub

Claude Code-assisted commits at 3.2% vs 1.5% baseline; 28.65M new secrets (+34%); AI-service secrets +81%; MCP config files as a leak surface (24,008 secrets, 2,117 valid); the 70%-still-active persistence line.

Kang — Governed AI-Assisted Engineering · Weber & Taneja — The Digital Apprentice
arXiv:2606.22484 · arXiv:2606.04321 · 2026 preprints, flagged

Risk-routed oversight tiers, and the per-skill autonomy state machine with dual-key promotion and automatic demotion — the two mechanisms §2 borrows.

Sharma — Beyond Greenfield: the D³ framework
arXiv:2512.01155 · Dec 2025 · practitioner preprint, n=52 self-report

The nearest published cousin, honestly weighed: a per-task Discover→Define→Deliver workflow with human gates — not a permission-staged rollout; its own caveat says sentiment, not causal effect.

Holkeri — Five levels of AI coding agent autonomy (Swarmia)
swarmia.com/blog · Mar 2026 · practitioner

The L2–L3 ceiling observation, and levels-as-tradeoffs rather than a ladder to climb — the practitioner frame for F2's restraint.

KPMG (Muir) — Why Enterprise AI Stalls After Pilot Success · MIT NANDA — The GenAI Divide
kpmg.com, 2026 · mlq.ai PDF, Jul 2025 (not peer-reviewed)

The stall mechanism (IT readiness, "legacy architecture built for human workflows") and the boardroom's 95% number with its real wording, methodology and limits — cite the mechanism, handle the number.

Report 4 — Brownfield Bootstrap research notes (this workbook)
module M5 research notes · roadmap, thresholds, day-1 recipe

The source synthesis this chapter operationalizes — F0–F3, the ~70% working threshold, the STOP list and the recipe — flagged as ours throughout.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (B6-roadmap-f0-f3-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are B1–B5 (the chain, the net, the gate, the archaeology and the map this plan assembles), E5/E6 (deterministic gates and the autonomy scale the phases climb), E7/T2 (the measurement discipline the F2 gate depends on), I2 (the write-permission matrix under every phase) and M7 (the security rollout this roadmap inherits). The F0–F3 ladder, the gate table, the back-triggers and the §6 master plan are this workbook's Report 4 synthesis — the components are standard and named; the assembly is ours. Tool rows, vendor telemetry and every benchmark number are point-in-time (mid-2026) — re-verify before any client meeting. With B6, module M5's deep-dive set completes alongside B5 — the brownfield bootstrap now runs read-only comprehension to measured expansion with a companion at every step.

D2 · DEEP DIVE

Design Tokens as the Visual Contract (DTCG)

Study companion · module M6

0Why it matters

D1 argued that agent handoff needs a third artifact — the design system as machine-readable infrastructure — and was honest that most of that artifact is only partially checkable. This chapter zooms into the one layer where the checkability is total: design tokens. A token is a named design decision serialized as data — color.action.primary instead of #2A4AD0 — and that single move changes its nature: a hex value in a stylesheet is an anonymous fact, while a token reference is a citable commitment that a linter can verify, a build can resolve, and an agent can be held to. That makes tokens the visual layer's closest counterpart to EARS/Gherkin acceptance criteria (H2): a constrained, named vocabulary that turns "follow the brand" from advisory prose into a deterministic pass/fail gate (E5). The W3C Design Tokens Community Group gave this contract a vendor-neutral serialization — the DTCG format, first stable version October 2025 — and an ecosystem of translators, sync bridges and lint rules grew around it. This chapter covers the contract itself: what a token is (§1), what the spec actually stabilized and what it only promises (§2), the pipeline that moves tokens from design tool to production (§3), the sync-direction decision that quietly determines governance (§4), how the contract binds agents specifically (§5), what the evidence says (§6), and how a brownfield client's pile of ad-hoc SCSS variables becomes a token contract without a big-bang rewrite (§7).

What this chapter gives you
  • token anatomy and the tier model — and why the semantic tier is the contract (§1)
  • the DTCG spec with its honest status: what 2025.10 stabilized, what the Resolver draft defers (§2)
  • the pipeline, tool by tool, point-in-time for mid-2026 (§3)
  • code-first vs design-first as a governance decision, not a tooling preference (§4)
  • the enforcement stack and the agent-specific reading channels, incl. DESIGN.md (§5)
  • the evidence file with its do-not-cite list (§6)
  • a six-step SCSS-to-DTCG migration, flagged as our synthesis (§7)
Where its neighbours pick up
  • D1 — the third-artifact thesis this chapter instantiates at its most machine-readable
  • D3 — how agents consume the wider design context beyond tokens
  • D4/I1/I2 — SSOT, durable-vs-derived, and write permissions as governance
  • D5 — the verification loop these tokens feed (visual regression, axe, lint)
  • H2 — EARS/Gherkin: the analogy this chapter borrows and bounds
  • B2/B5 — characterization and strangler fig: §7 is their design-layer twin
  • E5 — advisory vs deterministic, the distinction §5 runs on

1Anatomy of a token — and the tier that carries the contract

In the DTCG format a token is a JSON object whose reserved properties carry a $ prefix: $value (required), $type (color, dimension, fontWeight, duration…), optional $description. The dollar sign is deliberate namespace hygiene — the spec reserves it for its own properties, present and future, and forbids token or group names from starting with it, so user vocabulary and spec vocabulary can never collide. Tokens nest inside groups ("a set of tokens belonging to a specific category"), and one token can reference another by alias: a $value of "{color.blue.600}" resolves to the target token's $value. Files carry the recommended extensions .tokens or .tokens.json and the media type application/design-tokens+json. That is nearly the whole format — and the smallness is the point: it is a data interchange for named design decisions, not a styling language.

The structure teams build with aliases is not in the spec, and it matters more than the spec. Community practice organizes tokens into tiers: primitives (raw values with no meaning attached — color.blue.600), semantic tokens (intent — color.action.primary, aliased to a primitive), and optionally component tokens (button.primary.background, aliased to a semantic). The lineage is worth crediting precisely: Nathan Curtis's 2016 "Tokens in Design Systems" drew the original two-tier line between options (the values you could use) and decisions (the values you did choose, with context attached); the three-tier form is a later community elaboration, standard in Tokens Studio's docs and most 2026 practice — but it is convention, not DTCG normative text. Attribute it accordingly. In zeroheight's 2026 survey data the convention is visibly mainstream: only around one team in ten still runs a single flat layer, and just over half run the full three tiers.

Why does the middle tier carry the contract? Because it is the only tier whose names encode intent, and intent is what survives change. A rebrand edits primitives and touches nothing else; a dark theme re-resolves the semantic tier against a different primitive set; a component redesign moves component tokens. Code and agents that cite semantic names are insulated from all three. This is also the reason the naming discipline matters more than the JSON syntax: Curtis's companion piece "Naming Tokens in Design Systems" (2020) decomposes names into ordered levels — namespace, category, concept, property, variant, scale — and that grammar, not the file format, is what makes ten thousand tokens navigable by a human or retrievable by an agent.

2The spec — what 2025.10 stabilized, and what it only promises

The vocabulary is a decade older than the standard. Jina Anne coined "design tokens" at Salesforce around 2014, where the Lightning Design System distributed brand decisions across platforms through the open-source tool Theo; Curtis's 2016 article gave the idea its conceptual frame; and in 2019 Anne and Kaelig Deloumeau-Prigent founded the W3C Design Tokens Community Group to standardize the serialization. The group took six years to ship — a running joke in the community until it stopped being one: on 28 October 2025 the DTCG announced the first stable version, 2025.10, developed by (verbatim) "more than 20 editors and authors" with a roster spanning 24 named organizations — Adobe, Amazon, Google, Microsoft, Meta, Figma, Sketch, Salesforce, Shopify, Tokens Studio, Penpot, Supernova, zeroheight and others (the 24 is our count of the announcement's list, not a figure the group published). One governance note for anyone tracking the group itself: in June 2026 Anne and Val Head stepped back to chairs emerita, leaving Deloumeau-Prigent as chair.

Precision about what "stable" covers, because the announcement itself blurs it. The 2025.10 release comprises two stable modules: the Format Module (tokens, groups, types, aliases — everything in §1) and the Color Module (modern color spaces: Display P3, Oklch, CSS Color 4 alignment). Both self-describe as "considered stable." Theming, modes and multi-brand are not in either. They live in a third document, the Resolver Module — sets of base tokens plus modifiers for contexts like light/dark, composed via .resolver.json files — and that document is a draft carrying an unusually blunt warning: "Do not attempt to implement this version of the specification. Do not reference this version as authoritative in any way." Yet the W3C announcement headlines "theming and multi-brand support" as a 2025.10 capability. Both statements come from the same organization; only one is normative.

Read the status line, not the press release

The DTCG's own announcement markets theming as shipped while the module that specifies it says "do not implement." This is the E5 lesson in standards form: the press release is advisory, the status-of-this-document section is deterministic. The same discipline applies one level up — the spec itself, verbatim, "is not a W3C Standard nor is it on the W3C Standards Track"; it is a Community Group Candidate Recommendation published under the W3C Community Final Specification Agreement. That is a real, useful thing — a patent-licensed, multi-vendor stable format — but a client slide that says "the W3C standard for design tokens" is overclaiming, and a theming architecture built on the Resolver draft is building on text its own authors disown. As of July 2026 there is no 2026.x release; 2025.10 remains current and the Resolver Module remains the active frontier.

The spec also has a citable dissenter, and the disagreement is instructive rather than dismissable. Donnie D'Amato — design-systems practitioner and author of the "Avoiding tokens" essay — argues the group is "scope-creeping into territories that will make it difficult for it to ever be finished" and worries that tool-oriented formats invite "vendor lock-in where you might only be able to edit design tokens from tools"; his deeper point is that the nested-JSON format optimizes for machine parsing over human authorship, and that CSS custom properties already provide a web-native token medium. For this workbook's purposes the criticism sharpens rather than undermines the thesis: we want tokens precisely because they are machine-first — the agent and the linter are the new readers — but D'Amato is the reminder that the format is contested, that Figma conspicuously chose "variables" over "tokens" as its product vocabulary, and that betting a client's architecture on any single serialization deserves the same point-in-time humility as every tool fact in this module.

3The pipeline — from decision to production, tool by tool

A token file does nothing by itself; the contract is only as real as the pipeline that resolves it into every surface the product ships. The reference shape, mid-2026: decisions serialized as DTCG JSON in git; a translator resolves aliases and emits platform outputs (CSS custom properties, SCSS, JS/TS, iOS, Android) as a CI build step; design tools consume or contribute through a sync bridge. Every tool fact below is point-in-time — this is the fastest-moving layer of module M6, and each item should be re-verified before a client meeting.

TRANSLATOR

Style Dictionary

the default

Created at Amazon by Danny Banks; since a 2023 handover co-maintained with Tokens Studio, with Joren Broekema as core maintainer (the canonical repo now lives at style-dictionary/style-dictionary). v4 (April 2024) brought first-class DTCG-format support. v5 is released and stable — v5.5.0 shipped June 2026 — but full compliance with the 2025.10 spec is still an open workstream (tracked in issue #1590: color, border and shadow types done; gradients and durations in progress; the Resolver draft deliberately not implemented). The one-line status a client should hear: the tool is production-grade, the spec coverage is converging, and nobody serious implements the theming draft yet.

TRANSLATOR

Terrazzo

the alternative

Cobalt UI 2.0, renamed. DTCG-native from the start rather than retrofitted, MIT-licensed, with a CLI that emits CSS, Sass, JS/TS, JSON and Tailwind presets and a plugin API pitched as cleaner than Style Dictionary's. Worth shortlisting for greenfield token builds; references to "Cobalt UI" in older material are this project.

BRIDGE

Tokens Studio

Figma ↔ git

The de-facto bridge: a Figma plugin (now also a standalone platform) that stores tokens as JSON and syncs them to GitHub/GitLab, including via GitHub Actions — the mechanism that makes "Figma proposes, git disposes" practical. Note the commercial reality: git sync at team grade (branching, multi-file) sits in the paid tier (≈39€/month per editor at mid-2026 pricing — point-in-time), and teammates without a licence read tokens but cannot write. Budget it as infrastructure, not as a free plugin.

DESIGN TOOLS

Figma variables · Penpot

the endpoints

Figma's variables are the design-side representation, but two constraints define mid-2026 practice: the Variables REST API "requires an Enterprise plan" (verbatim from Figma's docs), and native export is not DTCG-clean — alias resolution generally needs a custom parser, and both the REST path and the MCP tools return only default mode values. Figma has announced native DTCG-conformant import/export, reported for late 2026 — not GA as of this writing, so any "Figma can/cannot export DTCG" sentence needs a date on it. Penpot, by contrast, shipped native DTCG tokens in 2025 in collaboration with Tokens Studio — the first open-source design tool to do so, and the existence proof that the format works as a native design-tool primitive rather than a plugin afterthought.

4Sync direction is the governance question

Teams argue about translators and bridges; the decision that actually shapes behavior is which copy is allowed to win. Three models exist. Design-first treats Figma as master and exports toward code — the default posture of design teams, and the drift machine: overrides and detached styles accumulate in the canvas with no gate, exports lag, and the "source of truth" quietly becomes "whatever the last export said." Bidirectional sync promises both sides an editable copy and costs the most machinery while producing the most conflicts — a team must earn it with mature review discipline on both sides, and few have. Code-first — tokens as versioned DTCG JSON in the repo, Figma importing as a consumer — is the recommended default of this workbook and of a converging practitioner line: Artur Sopelnik's February 2026 essay states it as a mental shift, verbatim — "design tokens are code, not design files" — and draws the consequence that "Figma should not be the source of truth for design tokens. Instead, it should be a consumer." Builder.io's governance essay (Steve Sewell, September 2025) makes the same call from the vendor side under the title "Code is the source of truth (not Figma)" — "Figma is, at best, a set of suggestions"; "All that matters is what makes it into code."

The reasons are the unglamorous ones that decide governance questions everywhere in this workbook. A git-resident token file gets CI validation (schema, alias resolution, lint), a reviewable diff, a PR gate with a named approver, and tool independence — the DTCG file outlives any particular design tool's export format. In I1's taxonomy: the JSON is durable, every generated output is derived, and canvas experiments are disposable; in I2's terms, the write-permission matrix has one master cell and it sits in the repo. And code-first does not demote designers to consumers of engineering — the strongest counterexample-shaped confirmation is Adobe Spectrum, whose token data is a public git repository explicitly described as the canonical dataset, while (per Adobe's Garth Braithwaite, verbatim) "we have some authoring experience that we allow the designers to author these design tokens in figma, and we use some plugins and some custom workflows to then bring that into our public GitHub repository". Designer-first authoring, code-resident truth: the author experience and the system of record are separate decisions, and only the second one is the contract.

modelmaster copywhat enforces itcharacteristic failure
code-firstDTCG JSON in gitPR gate + CI schema/lint + regenerated outputsdesign tool lags until the import runs — visible, boring, recoverable
design-firstFigma libraryconvention and export discipline (advisory only)silent drift via overrides/detaches; exports become archaeology
bidirectionalcontestedmerge tooling on both sidesconflicting edits, last-write-wins, provenance lost

For the agentic thesis the choice is even less symmetric than for humans: an agent can read, diff, validate and cite a JSON file in its own repository natively, while a design canvas is reachable only through rate-limited, plan-gated APIs that flatten modes and aliases (§3). Putting the master where the agent's hands already are is not a style preference; it is what makes §5 possible at all.

5The contract agents can be held to

Why do tokens matter more in agentic development than they did before it? Because generation without constraint samples the training-data mean — D1's slop evidence — and a token vocabulary is the cheapest effective constraint: it replaces an open universe of values with a closed, named set. Practitioner Hardik Pandya's July 2026 write-up states the mechanism in one line: the token layer gives the model "a closed set of values instead of fabricating new ones." The agent does not need taste to pick color.action.primary; it needs the vocabulary in context and a gate that rejects everything outside it. That splits the work into the two halves this module keeps insisting on: supply (make the vocabulary readable) and enforcement (make violations fail deterministically).

Supply — how the vocabulary reaches the agent. The baseline channel is the one §4 already built: the DTCG JSON and its generated outputs live in the repo the agent works in, referenced from the project's context file ("use tokens from tokens/; never hardcode a color") — advisory, but cheap and always present. Above that, mid-2026 practice offers three sharper channels, all point-in-time. Figma's Dev Mode MCP server exposes get_variable_defs, returning the variables and styles used in a selection so generated code can reference the real token names — launched in beta, and it currently returns only default mode values. Vibe-tool constraint files — Lovable's design-system.json (D3) and the theming registries of the shadcn ecosystem — are the same idea in tool-specific dress. And in April 2026 Google Labs open-sourced DESIGN.md, the most direct instantiation of this chapter's thesis to date: a repo-root file combining machine-readable design tokens in YAML front matter with human-readable design rationale in markdown prose, a lint CLI that validates structure and checks WCAG contrast and emits agent-actionable structured JSON, and exporters to Tailwind config and W3C DTCG. It is deliberately tool-agnostic — the spec addresses "coding agents" generically, and the vendor lists in circulating write-ups are third-party commentary, not spec text — and uptake requests appeared in other vendors' ecosystems within a day of release. Its spec is honestly versioned alpha, which earns it the same flag as the Resolver draft: watch it, pilot it, do not architect on it yet.

DESIGN.md, read through this workbook's lenses

What makes DESIGN.md interesting is not novelty — it is convergence. It is tokens-as-code (§4) with the durable/derived split intact (the DTCG and Tailwind exports are derived); it is progressive disclosure (I5) — front-matter data for machines, prose rationale for the humans and the judgment calls; and it ships with its own deterministic gate (the lint CLI) rather than trusting the prose — E5 applied by a vendor who never read Report 1. A Google-shipped spec being requested in Anthropic's skills ecosystem within a day is also the clearest signal yet that the "agent-readable design contract" is becoming a layer every vendor expects to exist. Point-in-time, alpha, re-verify — but this is the direction of travel.

Enforcement — the deterministic half. The advisory instruction "use tokens" becomes law through a short, boring stack, every piece of which predates agents: Stylelint's built-in color-no-hex (disallow hex colors outright) and declaration-property-value-allowed-list (allow-list var(--…) patterns per property); the purpose-built stylelint-declaration-strict-value plugin (rule id scale-unlimited/declaration-strict-value), which forces a variable, function or keyword for named properties — the single most token-shaped lint rule in the ecosystem; ESLint's no-restricted-imports with a message pointing at the design-system component, keeping agents off raw UI libraries; and a drift check comparing extracted reality against the committed baseline — the young open-source CLI Dembrandt is built exactly for this, failing CI on drift with a machine-readable JSON report and honest exit codes. CI blocks the merge; the agent iterates against the failure output before a human ever reviews (E4's loop, D5's gate). One security note that costs a sentence and buys a habit: token files, DESIGN.md and their kin are agent-read context, so they inherit the M7 rule — provenance and review before an agent consumes them; there is no known token-file injection incident as of July 2026, and the write-gate from I2 is precisely what keeps it that way.

And the boundary, restated once because every chapter in this module must: the gate verifies conformance — the value came from the vocabulary, the import came from the system, the contrast clears the threshold — never quality. Whether color.action.primary was the right token for this button in this flow is D1's judgment question, and it stays behind the designer's gate (D5, D6). Tokens are the Gherkin of the visual layer precisely this far and no further.

6What the evidence shows — and the do-not-cite list

The honest evidence file for tokens is thinner than the confidence of the ecosystem suggests, and the gap itself is worth showing a client. What holds up: adoption is real and steep. zeroheight's Design Systems Report 2025 (n=294 practitioners, surveyed late 2024) reports, verbatim, "Design tokens have gone from 56% in 2024 to 84% this year, which shows that design tokens are at a point of mass adoption in design systems"; the 2026 edition (n=147 — note the smaller sample) has tokens in 86% of systems — "almost ubiquitous", said jointly of tokens and documentation. Cite the jump with the 2025 report's dates, not as a 2026 number — and carry the same report's cold shower alongside it: only about 40% of teams report any token pipeline, so for the majority the contract exists but the plumbing of §3 does not.

On agents specifically, the rigorous evidence sits one level up from tokens. A CHI 2026 extended abstract ("Design System-Compliant User Interface Generation with LLM Agents") compares strategies for feeding a design system to an agent and finds registry-based supply — the agent assembles pre-built, system-compliant components rather than generating free-form — reached the highest design-system compliance, 95.08%, with a small pilot (n=9) reporting the work felt easier. Read it for the direction, not the decimals: it is an extended abstract with a tiny pilot, its metric is compliance rather than general code quality, and it constrains at the component level, not the token level. The sharper honest statement: as of July 2026 no rigorous study isolates design tokens as the causal variable in agent output quality. The mechanism argument of §5 stands on the D1 slop evidence plus the general constraint literature, and this workbook says so rather than borrowing decimals from adjacent studies. One practical trap for anyone searching this literature: "token" in the LLM papers means context tokens — the arXiv results for "design tokens + LLM" are dominated by token-economics work that has nothing to do with design, and at least one plausible-looking citation in circulation is exactly this collision.

The do-not-cite list for this topic

Circulating numbers we could not pin to a primary source, mostly traceable to third-hand agency aggregations: Atlassian's "67% reduction in duplicate UI code" and "consistency 58→89"; GitHub Primer's "WCAG compliance 71→96%"; "31–47% development time saved with tokens"; and the Spotify rebrand folklore ("months before tokens, minutes after") — repeated everywhere, sourced nowhere we could find. If a deck needs a rebrand number, the best available is vendor self-report with the flag showing: Netguru's Silk case study claims its 2024 rebrand's core redesigns completed in two days, the full process in about three working days, and estimates it would have taken an additional four days without the system — their own design system, their own estimate, tokens not isolated as the variable. Weak, and honestly labelled beats strong and unpinned.

7Brownfield: from ad-hoc SCSS to a token contract without a big-bang

The client in this engagement does not have a token file; they have nine years of SCSS variables, four near-identical grays and a $blue-new-final-2. The move to DTCG is a strangler-fig migration (B5) with a characterization step (B2), and it never requires freezing feature work. Six steps, each with an exit condition — the sequence is this workbook's synthesis, assembled from the named tools and the module's own gates:

stepwhat happensexit condition
1 · inventoryextract the de-facto tokens from shipped CSS — Project Wallace's CSS Design Tokens Analyzer emits a spec-compliant token set with per-value usage counts (you might find 47 colors with 80% of usage in six of them — the counts are the consolidation map); CSS Stats and Superposition triangulatede-facto token report reviewed with the designer
2 · characterizegolden-master the current UI: visual-regression snapshots of key screens as the baseline (B2's move, D5's tooling) — the migration must change nothing visually until a human chooses tobaseline green in CI
3 · consolidatedesigner collapses the inventory into primitives + a semantic tier (§1), naming per Curtis's grammar; every collapse (four grays → one) is a reviewed, intentional diff against step 2's baselinetokens.json in git, PR-gated (I2)
4 · bridgetranslator emits CSS custom properties; legacy SCSS variables are re-pointed at them ($brand-blue: var(--color-action-primary)) so every existing stylesheet compiles unchanged — the CMS Design System's Sass-to-CSS guide documents this incremental path, and Halodoc's engineering blog shows the same JSON→SCSS bridge in productionold code compiles against new tokens; baseline still green
5 · ratchetturn on the §5 lint stack one property category at a time — colors first, then spacing, then type — new violations fail CI while the legacy count only goes down (the same one-way ratchet as B2's coverage exit criteria)zero hardcoded values in the gated categories
