6 · open the gateonly now do agents generate UI against the vocabulary, and Figma imports the tokens as a consumer (Tokens Studio bridge) — supply and enforcement both exist, so D2-level generation (D6 scale) is safe to pilotagent PRs passing the token gate unassisted

The order is the load-bearing part, and it is B1's dependency-chain argument in the design layer: enforcement before generation, characterization before change. A team that starts by letting an agent "clean up the styles" runs the bootstrap paradox again — changes with no baseline to verify against; a team that starts by writing the perfect token taxonomy in a workshop ships a document, not a contract. Start from shipped CSS (what is), collapse toward intent (what should be), keep the golden master green throughout, and let the ratchet — not a mandate — retire the legacy values.

8Takeaways for our engagement

Assess (first client conversations)
  • Where do design decisions live today — SCSS variables, a Figma library, someone's memory? Run the Wallace inventory before proposing anything.
  • Which sync direction is implicitly in force, and who currently wins a conflict between Figma and code?
  • Does any CI gate reference design at all? If not, §5's lint stack is the cheapest first win in the whole design module.
  • Figma plan tier — the Variables API is Enterprise-gated, which changes the available bridges.
Commit (the defaults we bring)
  • Code-first: DTCG JSON in git as the single master, PR + human gate, everything else derived.
  • Stable-spec only: Format + Color modules; treat the Resolver draft and DESIGN.md as watch-list, not foundation.
  • Enforcement before generation: the §7 ratchet, colors first; agents pilot only after the gate holds.
  • Evidence honesty: the 56→84 jump with its real dates, the 40% pipeline gap, the do-not-cite list — and the admission that token-specific causal evidence does not yet exist.
Three sentences to remember D2 by

A design token turns a design decision into a named, machine-checkable commitment, and the DTCG's 2025.10 release gave that commitment a stable, vendor-neutral serialization — Format and Color modules only, with theming still an explicitly do-not-implement draft and the whole thing a Community Group report, not a W3C standard. Put the tokens in git as the master copy and they become the one layer of the design system an agent can be held to deterministically: a closed vocabulary supplied through context and MCP, enforced by a lint-and-drift gate that CI runs before any human reviews — conformance always, judgment never. Adoption is genuinely mainstream (56%→84% across zeroheight's 2024→2025 surveys, ~86% in 2026) but the plumbing is not (~40% have pipelines), which is exactly the gap a brownfield engagement fills with the six-step inventory→characterize→consolidate→bridge→ratchet→generate migration — enforcement before generation, always.

9Further reading

DTCG — Design Tokens Format Module & Color Module 2025.10
designtokens.org/tr/2025.10/format · /color · announcement w3.org/community/design-tokens · 28 Oct 2025

The stable spec itself: $value/$type/$description, groups, aliases, color spaces — and the status section ("not a W3C Standard nor on the W3C Standards Track") worth quoting precisely.

DTCG — Resolver Module (draft)
designtokens.org/tr/drafts/resolver · draft as of Jul 2026

Where theming/modes actually live: sets + modifiers + .resolver.json — behind a verbatim "do not implement" warning. The gap between this and the announcement's theming headline is §2's lesson.

Style Dictionary — DTCG docs & the 2025.10 tracking issue
styledictionary.com/info/dtcg · github.com/style-dictionary/style-dictionary issue #1590 · v5.5.0 Jun 2026

The default translator: v4 brought first-class DTCG, v5 is stable, full 2025.10 coverage converging type-by-type. Amazon origin, Tokens Studio co-maintenance.

Artur Sopelnik — Figma Is Not the Source of Truth for Design Tokens
artursopelnik.de · 22 Feb 2026 · practitioner

The code-first argument in its cleanest form: "design tokens are code, not design files"; Figma as consumer. Pair with Builder.io's "Code is the source of truth (not Figma)".

Nathan Curtis — Tokens in Design Systems · Naming Tokens in Design Systems
medium.com/eightshapes-llc · 2016 · 2020

The conceptual root (options vs decisions — the two-tier ancestor of today's three tiers) and the naming grammar (namespace/category/concept/property/variant/scale) that makes a token set navigable.

zeroheight — Design Systems Report 2025 & 2026
zeroheight.com · n=294 (fielded late 2024) · n=147 (2026)

The adoption evidence: 56%→84% verbatim, ~86% in 2026, the ~40% pipeline gap, tier-structure distribution. Small samples, self-selected practitioners — cite with n and dates attached.

CHI 2026 EA — Design System-Compliant UI Generation with LLM Agents
Cha, Jo, Shin & Seo · DOI 10.1145/3772363.3798616 · extended abstract, pilot n=9

Registry-based supply hits 95.08% design-system compliance — the best rigorous signal that structured constraint beats prose instruction, one level above tokens. Direction, not decimals.

Google Labs — DESIGN.md
github.com/google-labs-code/design.md · Apr 2026 · spec version alpha

The agent-native design contract: DTCG-exportable tokens in front matter, prose rationale, a lint CLI emitting agent-actionable JSON. Cross-vendor uptake within days; alpha-flagged like the Resolver draft.

Donnie D'Amato — Avoiding tokens
blog.damato.design · practitioner dissent

The citable counter-voice: DTCG scope creep, tool lock-in worry, CSS-native alternative. Read to keep the module's enthusiasm honest.

Adobe Spectrum design data · Garth Braithwaite (Knapsack interview)
github.com/adobe/spectrum-design-data · knapsack.cloud · May 2025

Enterprise-scale code-first in the wild: designers author in Figma, the public git repo holds the canonical dataset, JSON Schema is the handoff format.

Project Wallace — CSS Design Tokens Analyzer · Dembrandt
projectwallace.com/design-tokens · github.com/dembrandt/dembrandt · young tooling, point-in-time

The brownfield pair: extract de-facto tokens with usage counts from shipped CSS; fail CI on drift against the committed baseline with machine-readable reports.

Report 5 — the design-harness research notes (this workbook)
module M6 research notes · section 1

The synthesis this deep dive expands — with this chapter's corrections applied: theming is Resolver-draft territory, Style Dictionary v5 is shipped, and the adoption stat now carries its true dates.

This deep dive is also available as a standalone downloadable file (D2-design-tokens-dtcg-deepdive.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are D1 (the third-artifact thesis tokens make machine-checkable), D3 (the consumption channels), D4/I1/I2 (SSOT, durable-vs-derived and write permissions), D5 (the verification loop the token gate feeds), H2 (the EARS/Gherkin analogy and its limits), and B2/B5 (characterization and strangler fig, reused in §7). The tier reading of the contract, the sync-direction table and the six-step migration are this workbook's synthesis; the D1–D5 scale it serves remains our original framing, not an industry standard. Every tool fact — spec module status, Style Dictionary coverage, Figma plan gating, Tokens Studio pricing, DESIGN.md's alpha — is point-in-time (mid-2026) and marked for re-verification before any client meeting.

PB1 · RUNBOOK

Assess: Client Maturity & the F0 Entry Gate

0Why this runbook exists

PB1 is the entry point of every engagement: you assess the surrounding system before building anything on it, because AI amplifies that system rather than fixing it. The evidence is independent and directional — DORA 2024 associates a 25% increase in AI adoption with −7.2% delivery stability, and a METR randomised controlled trial found experienced developers were 19% slower while believing they were about 20% faster. PB1 exists to replace optimism with measured facts before the F0 gate.

What this is not

This is not an audit produced for the client's benefit alone. The output decides our entry decision at the F0 gate — whether we proceed, what preconditions we attach, and where the first real risk lives.

The theory behind the checklist lives in the workbook topics this runbook distils — especially E7 Metrics & anti-patterns for the delivery baseline and the review-capacity argument, and B1 Safe ordering for why verification must come before delegation.

1How to run it

Run PB1 with one person, 2–4 focused days, read access to the repository, CI, deploy logs and the incident system, one team interview, and a client sponsor empowered to sign the F0 decision. Every step below is written as ČO (the action), PREČO (the theory link), and DONE (the exit criterion). Check steps off as you go; the progress chip updates automatically.

Progress is saved locally in this workbook (same store as topic progress). The standalone copy of this runbook does not persist checkbox state.

2Phase A — Verification base & delivery baseline

Run the full suite three times from a clean checkout. Record: one-command invocation, wall-clock runtime, pass rate, whether it finishes under ~15 min, and the list of flaky tests. Why: the suite is the external truth the whole loop stands on — without a trusted, fast signal there is nothing for an agent to converge on (E4 Verification-first, B1 Safe ordering).

DONE = runtime, pass rate, under-15-min status and flaky list recorded in report §2; headless one-command status recorded as a fact (feeds questionnaire A2).

Insert a deliberately broken test and let CI run. Confirm the failure is machine-readable: a non-zero exit code and a parseable log or status payload. Why: an agent that cannot read CI cannot self-correct; a red/green dot is not enough (E4 Verification-first, E7 Metrics & anti-patterns).

DONE = broken-test experiment run; failure artifact linked in report §2; whether the red build blocks merge recorded (feeds questionnaire A3).

Compute the DORA four keys plus rework rate retroactively from git and CI history, add cost per merged PR (review hours, CI minutes, seat licences — the token column is added by PB5), agree the before/after window with the client, and confirm access to git history, deploy logs, and the incident system. The baseline is captured now, at assess time — a pilot without it can be neither proven nor disproven (the METR perception gap), so it cannot wait for PB5 (E7 day-0 DORA checklist).

DONE = baseline numbers recorded in report §3; window + data access confirmed in writing.

3Phase B — Information architecture

List architecture docs, ADRs, specs, READMEs, and runbooks; record owner, last-touched date, and whether a newcomer can retrieve each from the repo. Why: an agent cannot compensate for missing or buried truth; the loop will hallucinate consensus where none exists (I1 Artifact taxonomy, I3 Consistency drift).

DONE = artifact table in report §4 with gaps explicitly listed.

4Phase C — Governance minimum

Prove production credentials are unreachable from the development environment, then run a secrets scan across the repo history. Why: environment separation and secrets hygiene are hard boundaries, not maturity shades — the Replit production-DB incident (July 2025) showed what happens when they fail. GitGuardian 2026 vendor telemetry reports AI-assisted commits leak secrets at 3.2% versus a 1.5% baseline; treat that as a vendor claim, not independent evidence (E5 Governance, B6 Roadmap F0–F3; see also module M7 Security).

DONE = reachability tested and result recorded; scan report attached; if prod credentials are reachable → questionnaire C1 scored 0 and logged as an F0 precondition.

Document branch protection, CODEOWNERS, who and what can merge, and where an agent's write access would stop today. Why: delegation without a permission boundary is just automation of mistakes; the write-permission matrix is the deterministic half of governance and the RBAC/least-privilege check (I2 Lifecycle & write permissions, E5 Governance).

DONE = draft write-permission matrix in report §5 (skeleton below).
# Write-permission matrix — draft
Who / what        | Push to default | Merge PR | Release | Prod access
------------------|-----------------|----------|---------|------------
Human developer   |                 |          |         |
CI bot            |                 |          |         |
Agent (today)     |                 |          |         |
Agent (proposed)  |                 |          |         |

Rules: default branch protected? ____  CODEOWNERS enforced? ____  Review required? ____

5Phase D — Team readiness

Identify who will curate agent config and review agent PRs, and secure a concrete weekly review budget in hours. Why: agent output scales faster than review; Faros AI vendor telemetry shows +98% PR volume and +91% review time when assistive tools land without capacity planning (P4 The PM role, H4 Feedback-loop anti-patterns, E7 Metrics & anti-patterns).

DONE = named owner(s) + weekly review budget recorded in report §6, or absence recorded as questionnaire D2 = 0.
# Team interview — guide
Current PR wait time (hours/days): ____
Who reviews today & spare hours/week: ____
Who would own agent config & review agent PRs: ____
Where do docs for a given feature actually live: ____
A recent incident story (what happened, how it was found): ____

6Phase E — Evaluation & the F0 gate

Fill the 24-point questionnaire below, mark the four [GATE] items, and map the result to the recommendation table. Why: the score is a structured way to surface gaps, not a disguise for gut feel; the thresholds are our synthesis, calibrated against the theory in E6 Autonomy levels.

DONE = filled questionnaire archived with the engagement notes; recommended level written into report §7.

Produce the assessment report from the template, convene the F0 gate, and record a go / no-go / go-with-preconditions decision together with the STOP criteria that would pull the cord. Why: the gate is the only thing that turns assessment into action; without a recorded decision and triggers, the report becomes shelfware (B6 Roadmap F0–F3).

DONE = decision (go / no-go / go-with-preconditions) recorded with date, criteria and signatories.

The questionnaire stays at 24 points. The delivery baseline captured in step A3 is measurement data, not a scored maturity dimension — scoring it would double-count evidence the gates already cover and would break the calibrated thresholds in the recommendation table.

# Agentic-readiness assessment — questionnaire (v1)
Client: ____________   Assessor: ____________   Date: ____________
Score each 0 (absent) / 1 (partial) / 2 (solid). Score 2 only for what is in place today;
agreed or planned remediation = 1. Gate criteria marked [GATE] are non-compensable:
any [GATE] at 0 caps the recommendation at L1 regardless of total. A gate counts as
passed only at 2; a gate at 1 passes but must appear as a precondition in report §7.

## A — Verification base
A1 [GATE] Trusted automated test suite exists and passes ........... 0 1 2
A2 [GATE] Full suite runs headless in under ~15 min ................ 0 1 2
A3 CI runs on every PR and blocks merge on red ..................... 0 1 2
A4 [GATE] CI failure output is machine-readable (agent-consumable) . 0 1 2

## B — Information architecture
B1 Architecture/decision docs exist and are findable from the repo . 0 1 2
B2 Docs freshness: last-touched within ~6 months for core docs ..... 0 1 2
B3 A newcomer can locate the source of truth for a feature ......... 0 1 2

## C — Governance minimum
C1 [GATE] Dev/prod separated; prod creds unreachable from dev ...... 0 1 2
C2 Secrets scan clean (or remediation plan agreed) ................. 0 1 2
C3 Branch protection + review requirement on the default branch .... 0 1 2

## D — Team readiness
D1 A named owner will curate agent config & review agent PRs ....... 0 1 2
D2 Review capacity exists (agent PRs will not queue for days) ...... 0 1 2

TOTAL ____ / 24        GATES PASSED ____ / 4

# Agentic-readiness assessment — report
Client / system: ____________     Date: ____________     Assessor: ____________

## 1 Executive summary
Recommendation: L__ entry, F0 scope: ____________. Go / No-go / Go with preconditions.

## 2 Verification base (steps A1–A2)
Suite command: ____  Runtime: ____  Pass rate: ____  Flaky tests: ____
Under 15 min: ____  Headless one-command: ____  Merge blocked on red: ____
CI signal experiment result: ____________

## 3 Delivery baseline (step A3)
Deploy frequency: ____  Lead time: ____  Change-fail rate: ____  Recovery time: ____  Rework rate: ____
Cost per merged PR (review hours / CI minutes / seats): ____
Before/after window agreed: ____________  Data access confirmed (git / CI / incidents): ____

## 4 Information architecture (step B1)
| Artifact | Owner | Last touched | Retrievable from repo? |
|---|---|---|---|

## 5 Governance minimum (steps C1–C2)
Dev/prod separation: ____________   Secrets scan: ____________
Write-permission matrix (draft): ____________

## 6 Team readiness (step D1)
Editor/curator: ____________   Review budget: ____ h/week

## 7 Score & recommendation (steps E1–E2)
Total ____ / 24 · Gates ____ / 4 → recommended level L__ (rationale: ...)
Decision signatories: ____________
Preconditions before F1: ____________

## 8 STOP criteria agreed for F0
(from B6: e.g. stability drop beyond agreed threshold, secrets leak,
unreviewed-merge incident — list the concrete triggers + who pulls the cord)

The mapping below is our synthesis, not an industry standard. It converts the questionnaire into an entry recommendation; the non-compensable gate criteria are what make it conservative.

ScoreGatesRecommendation
< 10anyL1 only; engagement starts by fixing the base (PB2 becomes the deliverable, not a prerequisite)
10–164/4L2 entry; F0 read-only archaeology can start (B4)
≥ 174/4L3 pilot candidate; proceed to PB2 with the F0→F1 exit criteria from B6
any< 4/4no L2+ autonomy until the failed gate is fixed — non-compensable

The table tops out at L3 deliberately — L4 is a per-slice decision for narrow deterministic tasks and L5 is not achieved (E6); a high score buys a pilot, not a higher ceiling.

STOP criteria for F0 are taken from B6 and adapted to the client's context. Typical triggers include a delivery-stability drop beyond the agreed threshold, a secrets leak or production credential reachable from dev, and any unreviewed merge. Each trigger names who pulls the cord and how.

7Exit gate & takeaways

Exit criteria

Report delivered; delivery baseline recorded (report §3 is the pilot's "before" snapshot — PB5 compares against it); go/no-go recorded; if GO → proceed to PB2 with the report's §2 numbers as the bootstrap baseline.

Three sentences to remember PB1 by

Assess before build, because AI amplifies the system it lands on — good or bad. The delivery baseline and the four non-compensable gates are the guardrails that keep the assessment honest; a high average with one gate failed is exactly the false-confidence case PB1 exists to catch. The F0 decision is an action, not a report: go, no-go, or go-with-preconditions, each with a named owner and STOP criteria.

Sources and caveats for the empirical claims: DORA 2024/2025 reports (dora.dev) — note the 2025 update partially reverses the throughput finding while instability stays elevated; METR RCT (arXiv:2507.09089) and the 2026 follow-up (metr.org) — the follow-up weakens the slowdown estimate but the perception-gap lesson stands; GitGuardian State of Secrets Sprawl 2026 — vendor telemetry, not independent evidence; Replit incident — July 2025.

This runbook is also available as a standalone file (PB1-assess-runbook.html). Its natural neighbours in the workbook are E6 Autonomy levels, E7 Metrics & anti-patterns, B1 Safe ordering, and B6 Roadmap F0–F3. The scoring model and gate criteria are this workbook's synthesis; every point-in-time fact in the report should be marked for re-verification before the next gate.

v1.49 · 2026-07-18 ·

  • Report 1 — "Adapting Classical SDLC to Agentic Development with LLM Coding Agents: Conceptual Framework 2025–2026"

Extension rule: new topics get new stable IDs; existing IDs never change, so saved progress survives. The full changelog (v0.1 → current) lives in the version-history modal — open it from the top bar or the link above.
